<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are PSM Standards?

**Process Safety Management (PSM) Standards** are a structured set of regulatory codes, international frameworks, and industry best-practice guidelines designed to prevent or minimize the consequences of catastrophic releases of highly hazardous chemicals (HHCs), hydrocarbons, and toxic materials from process plants.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, PSM Standards form the *regulatory backbone* of any chemical, petrochemical, refinery, or pharmaceutical engineering design. They convert a reactive "fix-after-failure" approach into a proactive, systematic, document-driven safety culture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119):**
> "Process Safety Management is the proactive identification, evaluation, prevention, and control of chemical-process hazards that could result in catastrophic releases of toxic, reactive, or flammable liquids and gases into the workplace or surrounding community."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aircraft Pre-Flight Checklist" Intuition

Imagine a commercial airplane before takeoff. The pilot does NOT just "start the engines and fly." There is a **standardized pre-flight checklist** (engines, fuel, weather, instruments, doors, hydraulics, communication). Every step is written down, signed, and verified.

**PSM Standards work the same way for a chemical plant:**

| Aircraft Checklist | PSM Standard Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pre-flight inspection | **Process Safety Information (PSI)** |
| Pilot's mental checklist | **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** |
| Take-off / Landing procedure | **Operating Procedures** |
| Crew training simulator | **Training & Competency** |
| Maintenance logbook | **Mechanical Integrity** |
| Air traffic control communication | **Management of Change (MOC)** |
| Crash investigation (NTSB) | **Incident Investigation** |
| Emergency landing drill | **Emergency Planning & Response** |
| Annual aircraft audit | **Compliance Audits** |

If the pilot skips a step, the plane may crash. If an engineer skips a PSM element, the plant may explode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Bodies Governing PSM (Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **OSHA** (Occupational Safety & Health Administration, USA) — 29 CFR 1910.119
> - **EPA** (Environmental Protection Agency, USA) — 40 CFR Part 68 (RMP Rule)
> - **CCPS** (Center for Chemical Process Safety, AIChE)
> - **API** (American Petroleum Institute) — RP 750, RP 752, RP 753
> - **IEC** (International Electrotechnical Commission) — IEC 61511 (SIS)
> - **EU Seveso Directives III / IV** (Major Accident Hazards)
> - **ISO 45001** (Occupational Health & Safety)
> - **ANSI/ASSE Z590.3** (Process Safety Management)

### 1.3 Why Do We Need PSM Standards?

The genesis of modern PSM is the **Bhopal Disaster (1984, Union Carbide India)** and the **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK)** — two catastrophic events that killed thousands and exposed the absence of structured safety governance. Post-Bhopal, OSHA promulgated 29 CFR 1910.119 in **1992**, which became the global template.

The Three Pillars of PSM Standards (per CCPS):
1. **Commitment** — Management leadership, accountability, and resources.
2. **Process Safety Knowledge** — Hazard & risk information, PSI, PHA.
3. **Operational Discipline** — Procedures, training, MOC, audits.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 Board Exams:** Always remember the "**3 Pillars → 14 Elements**" structure. Almost every 14-mark question is built around describing these elements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 OSHA PSM — The 14 Elements (29 CFR 1910.119)

This is the **single most important framework** for the KTU 2024 syllabus. Memorize the structure:

| # | Element | Core Objective |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Employee Participation** | Engage workers in PSM activities |
| 2 | **Process Safety Information (PSI)** | Compile chemical, process, and equipment data |
| 3 | **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** | Identify and evaluate hazards systematically |
| 4 | **Operating Procedures** | Written instructions for normal & safe operations |
| 5 | **Training** | Initial & refresher competency for all personnel |
| 6 | **Contractors** | Evaluate and control contractor safety |
| 7 | **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | Verify safety before new/modified plant startup |
| 8 | **Mechanical Integrity** | Inspection, testing, preventive maintenance of critical equipment |
| 9 | **Hot Work Permit** | Authorize welding/cutting in hazardous areas |
| 10 | **Management of Change (MOC)** | Control any modification to process, equipment, or personnel |
| 11 | **Incident Investigation** | Root-cause analysis of near-misses and accidents |
| 12 | **Emergency Planning & Response** | Prepare for and mitigate accidental releases |
| 13 | **Compliance Audits** | Periodic third-party evaluation of PSM effectiveness |
| 14 | **Trade Secrets** | Ensure employee/contractor access to safety info |

## 2.2 EPA Risk Management Program (RMP) — 40 CFR Part 68

The **EPA RMP Rule (1996)** complements OSHA PSM with a community- and environment-focused perspective. It applies to **stationary sources** handling regulated substances above threshold quantities.

### 2.3 The Risk Triangle — Foundation Equation

The fundamental engineering metric underlying all PSM Standards is **Risk**:

$$R = f(\text{Frequency}, \text{Consequence}) = P \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (dimensionless index or fatality/year)
- $P$ = Probability of an incident occurring
- $C$ = Consequence severity (injury, fatality, environmental damage, ₹ loss)

For quantitative PSM (CCPS / API RP 752):

$$\text{Individual Risk} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot f_i \cdot C_i$$

Where $P_i$ = frequency of initiating event $i$, $f_i$ = probability of failure of protection layer $i$, and $C_i$ = consequence magnitude.

## 2.4 Functional Safety & IEC 61511

Modern PSM Standards include **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** governed by **IEC 61511 (Functional Safety — Process Industry Sector)**. The Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is defined as:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIS}} \leq 10^{-\text{SIL}}$$

| SIL | Target PFD (avg) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10 – 100 |
| 2 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 100 – 1,000 |
| 3 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| 4 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Safety vs. Personal Safety:**
> - **Process Safety** → focus on *major accident hazards* (fires, explosions, toxic releases).
> - **Occupational/Personal Safety** → focus on *slip-trip-fall, PPE, ergonomics*.
> - **PSM Standards specifically govern PROCESS SAFETY, not occupational safety.**

## 2.5 International Standard Comparison

| Standard | Region | Focus | Key Reference Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 | USA | Worker safety, 14 elements | 1992 |
| EPA 40 CFR Part 68 (RMP) | USA | Offsite consequence, community | 1996 |
| Seveso Directive III | EU | Major accident hazards | 2003 |
| Seveso Directive IV | EU | Refined + land-use planning | 2012 |
| COMAH Regulations | UK | Major accident hazards | 2015 |
| API RP 750 | Global (Oil & Gas) | Process hazards management | 1990 / R 2021 |
| API RP 752 | Global | Building siting & layout | 2009 / 3rd Ed. |
| API RP 753 | Global | Portable buildings near process | 2007 |
| ANSI/ASSE Z590.3 | USA | PSM systems & guidelines | 2011 (R 2021) |
| CCPS RBPS | Global | Risk-Based Process Safety | 2007 |
| IEC 61511 | International | Functional Safety (SIS/SIL) | 2016 |
| ISO 45001 | International | Occupational H&S management | 2018 |
| ISO 31000 | International | Risk management principles | 2018 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix — Layered Decomposition of PSM Standards

> [!NOTE]
> Since PSM Standards are a *regulatory and procedural* topic rather than a purely mathematical one, the "derivations" below are presented as **analytical decomposition tables** — the same form KTU board examiners expect when marks are awarded for systematic structuring.

## 3.1 OSHA PSM 14-Element Deep Decomposition (Analytical Derivation)

### Element 2 — Process Safety Information (PSI)
$$\text{PSI} = \{\text{Chemical Hazards}\} \cup \{\text{Process Hazards}\} \cup \{\text{Equipment Data}\}$$

- **Chemical Hazards:** MSDS, PEL/TLV, reactivity, incompatibilities, thermal stability.
- **Process Hazards:** Block flow diagrams (BFD), P&IDs, process flow diagrams (PFD), safe upper/lower limits of temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition.
- **Equipment Data:** Materials of construction, P&ID specs, design codes (ASME Sec VIII), relief valve set pressures, classification of electrical area (Class I Div 1/2).

### Element 3 — Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
PHA methods are applied hierarchically depending on project stage:

| Method | Acronym | Stage | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| What-If Analysis | WIA | Early design | Low |
| Checklist | CL | Routine operations | Low |
| Hazard and Operability Study | HAZOP | Detailed design | High |
| Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | FMEA | Equipment-level | Medium |
| Fault Tree Analysis | FTA | Quantified risk | High |
| Event Tree Analysis | ETA | Consequence modelling | High |

The **HAZOP** node equation (parameter deviation):
$$\text{Node} + \text{Deviation} = \text{Cause} \xrightarrow{\text{Consequence}} \text{Safeguard}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven HAZOP Guide Words (must memorize for KTU):**
> **NO / NOT, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN**

### Element 8 — Mechanical Integrity
The maintenance hierarchy in PSM Standards follows:

$$\text{MI} = \{\text{Inspection}\} + \{\text{Testing}\} + \{\text{Preventive Maintenance}\} + \{\text{QA/QC}\}$$

Critical equipment covered: pressure vessels, storage tanks, piping systems, relief valves, pumps, instruments, control systems.

### Element 10 — Management of Change (MOC)
MOC trigger conditions (any one is sufficient to initiate MOC):

$$\text{MOC Trigger} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if Process change occurs} \\ 1, & \text{if Equipment change occurs} \\ 1, & \text{if Material change occurs} \\ 1, & \text{if Personnel change (operations) occurs} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

A **"Replacement in Kind" (RIK)** exception bypasses MOC if the substitution is *identical specification* (same size, material, service, P&ID drawing number).

### Element 13 — Compliance Audits
Frequency requirement (per OSHA):
$$\text{Audit Period} \leq 3 \text{ years}$$

Audit team independence condition:
$$\text{Team Lead} \notin \text{Audited Plant Management Chain}$$

## 3.2 EPA RMP — Offsite Consequence Analysis (OCA)

The RMP rule requires worst-case and alternative-release scenario analysis.

**Worst-case release** equation (toxic gas):
$$C(x) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Where:
- $C(x)$ = downwind concentration at distance $x$ (ppm or mg/m³)
- $Q$ = release rate (kg/s)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (Pasquill-Gifford)
- $u$ = mean wind speed at release height (m/s)
- $H$ = effective release height (m)

The **toxic endpoint distance** is found by setting $C(x) = C_{\text{ERPG-1}}$ (or AEGL-1/2/3 per EPA).

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark KTU questions comparing OSHA PSM vs. EPA RMP, the examiner expects you to explicitly tabulate the **scope, applicability threshold, focus (worker vs. community), and audit cycle**.

## 3.3 Seveso Directive Tier Logic (EU)

$$\text{Tier} = \begin{cases} \text{Lower Tier}, & Q \geq Q_L \text{ and } Q < Q_U \\ \text{Upper Tier}, & Q \geq Q_U \end{cases}$$

Where $Q$ is the quantity of dangerous substance on-site, $Q_L$ is the lower-tier threshold, and $Q_U$ is the upper-tier threshold (e.g., for **ammonia**: $Q_L = 50$ t, $Q_U = 200$ t).

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Flow

$$\text{Regulation (OSHA/EPA/Seveso)} \rightarrow \text{Standards (API/CCPS/IEC)} \rightarrow \text{Implementation (14 Elements)} \rightarrow \text{Audit} \rightarrow \text{Continuous Improvement}$$

This is the **closed-loop PSM cycle** examiners expect in 14-mark descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the *functional architecture* of PSM Standards as required by the KTU 2024 scheme. They avoid physical drawings and instead present logical system flows.

## 4.1 PSM Standards — Global Framework Architecture


> [!NOTE]
> Elements **3 (PHA)**, **10 (MOC)**, and **13 (Audit)** are highlighted as the **three highest-priority elements** by CCPS — these are the elements most frequently asked in KTU board examinations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define Process Safety Management (PSM) and name the regulatory body that first formalized it in the United States.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Process Safety Management (PSM) is the proactive application of management principles and engineering practices to prevent and control catastrophic releases of highly hazardous chemicals from process plants. The U.S. **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** formalized it in **1992** through the regulation **29 CFR 1910.119**, following the Bhopal (1984) and Flixborough (1974) disasters. The standard mandates 14 mandatory elements to ensure process integrity throughout the plant life cycle.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition, 1 mark regulatory body, 1 mark year/disaster context)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Process Safety and Occupational Safety with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Process Safety | Occupational Safety |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Major accident hazards (toxic, fire, explosion) | Individual worker injuries (slip, fall, ergonomic) |
| Scope | Plant-wide, system-level | Personal, task-level |
| Examples | 1. Vapour cloud explosion in a hydrocracker | 1. Worker slipping on oil spill |
| | 2. Toxic H₂S release from a gas sweetening unit | 2. Hearing loss from prolonged noise exposure |
| Tools | PHA, MOC, PSSR, SIL | PPE, safety signage, first-aid, ergonomics |

*(Allocation: 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for examples contrast)*

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a) [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 Process Safety Management standard. List all 14 elements with one-line objectives.**
> **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) element in depth. Describe the HAZOP technique, its guide words, and node parameters.**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 — PSM Standard:**

Issued by OSHA (USA) in 1992, this standard applies to processes involving a **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** of any of 137 listed highly hazardous chemicals, or flammable liquids/gases of 10,000 lb (≈ 4,536 kg) or more.

**The 14 Elements:**

1. **Employee Participation** — Active worker involvement in PHAs, audits, and incident reviews.
2. **Process Safety Information (PSI)** — Compilation of chemical hazards, process technology, and equipment design data.
3. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — Systematic evaluation using HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, or Fault Tree.
4. **Operating Procedures** — Written, step-wise procedures for startup, normal, emergency, and shutdown operations.
5. **Training** — Initial job-skill training plus refresher every 3 years.
6. **Contractors** — Pre-qualification, safety briefing, and continuous monitoring of contract workers.
7. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** — Confirm construction, equipment, procedures, training before introducing chemicals.
8. **Mechanical Integrity** — Inspection, testing, and PM of pressure vessels, piping, relief devices, and instruments.
9. **Hot Work Permit** — Written authorization for cutting, welding, or grinding in hazardous areas.
10. **Management of Change (MOC)** — Formal review and approval of any modification.
11. **Incident Investigation** — Root-cause analysis within 48 hours of any catastrophic event.
12. **Emergency Planning & Response** — Procedures for small releases, catastrophic events, and community coordination.
13. **Compliance Audits** — Independent audit every 3 years with corrective actions.
14. **Trade Secrets** — Disclosure of safety information to employees, regardless of confidentiality.

*(Allocation: 1 mark introduction + 5 marks element listing + 1 mark conclusion)*

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) — Deep Dive:**

PHA is the third OSHA element and the **most critical** for risk reduction. It is a structured, team-based review performed by a multidisciplinary team (engineers, operators, safety experts).

**Team Composition:** Minimum 4–5 members; one with working knowledge, one with PHA expertise, and one independent.

**PHA Hierarchy & Method Selection:**

| PHA Method | When Used | Output Type |
|---|---|---|
| What-If | Early design, brainstorming | Qualitative list of issues |
| Checklist | Standard, well-known processes | Pass/Fail compliance list |
| HAZOP | Detailed engineering (P&ID available) | Cause–Consequence–Safeguard table |
| FMEA | Equipment-level analysis | RPN = S × O × D |
| FTA | Quantified risk of top event | Boolean logic tree |
| ETA | Consequence path analysis | Outcome probability branches |

**HAZOP Technique (most asked in KTU):**

HAZOP is performed by partitioning the P&ID into **process nodes** (vessels, columns, exchangers) and applying **deviations** from the *intent*.

**Seven Standard Guide Words:**
- **NO / NOT** — Complete negation (e.g., *No flow*)
- **MORE** — Quantitative increase (*More pressure, more temperature*)
- **LESS** — Quantitative decrease (*Less flow, less level*)
- **AS WELL AS** — Extra component present (*Flow as well as water in oil line*)
- **PART OF** — Less than intended (*Only one phase instead of two*)
- **REVERSE** — Opposite direction (*Reverse flow in pump discharge*)
- **OTHER THAN** — Different state (*Material other than intended, e.g., water in acid line*)

**HAZOP Node Equation:**
$$\text{Node} + (\text{Parameter} + \text{Guide Word}) \rightarrow \text{Cause} \rightarrow \text{Consequence} \rightarrow \text{Safeguard} \rightarrow \text{Recommendation}$$

**Example:** Node = Feed Surge Drum; Parameter = Flow; Guide Word = NO → Cause = Pump failure → Consequence = Loss of downstream feed → Safeguard = Low-flow alarm + pump interlock.

*(Allocation: 1 mark PHA concept, 2 marks methods, 3 marks HAZOP details incl. guide words, 1 mark example)*

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]**
> **(a) [7 Marks] Compare OSHA PSM and EPA RMP standards. Highlight their scope, applicability, common elements, and differences.**
> **(b) [7 Marks] Describe the Management of Change (MOC) element in detail with an industrial example.**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparative Analysis — OSHA PSM vs. EPA RMP:**

| Feature | OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119) | EPA RMP (40 CFR Part 68) |
|---|---|---|
| **Promulgating Body** | OSHA (Department of Labor) | EPA (Environmental Protection) |
| **Year Issued** | 1992 | 1996 |
| **Primary Focus** | Worker/occupational safety | Community/environmental safety |
| **Applicability** | 137 listed HHCs + ≥10,000 lb flammables | Regulated substances with threshold quantities |
| **Number of Core Elements** | 14 elements | 9 elements (core PSM subset + 3 RMP-specific) |
| **Offsite Consequence Analysis (OCA)** | Not mandatory | Mandatory (worst-case + alternative scenario) |
| **Audit Cycle** | Every 3 years | Every 5 years (or on significant change) |
| **Hazard Assessment** | PHA-based | OCA + PHA-based |
| **Accidental Release Prevention** | Implicit | Explicit (Risk Management Plan filed with EPA) |
| **Accident History Reporting** | Internal | Mandatory 5-year accident history reporting to EPA |
| **Coordination with Local Emergency Responders** | Recommended | Mandatory (LEPC coordination) |

**Common Elements (overlap):** PHA, Operating Procedures, Training, MOC, Incident Investigation, Emergency Response, Compliance Audits, Mechanical Integrity.

**Unique to OSHA PSM:** Trade Secrets, Hot Work Permit, Contractors, Employee Participation, PSSR.
**Unique to EPA RMP:** Offsite Consequence Analysis, Accidental Release Prevention Program, 5-Year Accident History, LEPC Coordination.

*(Allocation: 1 mark each for: OSHA scope, EPA scope, 3 mark comparison table, 1 mark unique features, 1 mark conclusion)*

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Management of Change (MOC):**

MOC is the **10th OSHA PSM element** and one of the most critical safeguards against latent process risk. It is required whenever **any** modification is proposed to a covered process — in chemicals, equipment, procedures, software, or personnel.

**When MOC is Triggered:**

$$\text{MOC Required} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes}, & \text{Process change (chemistry, operating limits, set points)} \\ \text{Yes}, & \text{Equipment change (vessel, pump, line, instrument, material)} \\ \text{Yes}, & \text{Procedural change (operating procedure, alarm setpoint)} \\ \text{Yes}, & \text{Software/PLC logic change} \\ \text{No}, & \text{Replacement-In-Kind (RIK) — identical spec} \end{cases}$$

**MOC Procedure (Industrial Example):**

Consider a refinery reformer unit where the operating temperature limit is being raised from 510 °C to 525 °C.

| Step | Activity | Responsible |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Request for Change (RFC)** raised by Operations | Operations Engineer |
| 2 | **Technical Review** — Process engineering, mechanical integrity, safety, instrumentation | Multi-disciplinary team |
| 3 | **Risk Assessment** — Re-run HAZOP for the affected node; update PSI | PHA Team |
| 4 | **Authorization** — Plant Manager + Safety Head sign-off | Management |
| 5 | **Implementation** — Documented, with as-built P&ID update | Construction/Engineering |
| 6 | **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | Independent team |
| 7 | **Communication & Training** — Operators re-trained on new limits | Training Department |
| 8 | **Closure & Filing** — MOC records archived for plant life | Document Control |
| 9 | **Periodic Review** — Audit MOC effectiveness | Compliance Cell |

**"Replacement-In-Kind" (RIK) Exception:**
If a centrifugal pump of identical flow, head, material, and seal type replaces an existing one, no MOC is needed — only routine maintenance. The threshold test:
$$\text{RIK} = (\text{Identical Function}) \wedge (\text{Identical Specification}) \wedge (\text{No P\&ID Change})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistake:**
> - Students often confuse **MOC with routine maintenance** — make sure to state that *RIK* is the only exception.
> - Forgetting to mention the **48-hour PSSR** window after implementation.
> - Not linking MOC back to **PHA + PSI updates** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this linkage is missing.
> - Missing the **trade-secret** element in OSHA — it is asked in nearly every KTU cycle.

*(Allocation: 1 mark MOC definition, 2 marks trigger conditions, 3 marks industrial example procedure, 1 mark RIK concept)*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Important Things to Remember for KTU 2024 PSM Standards:**

- **OSHA 1910.119** → **14 elements**, promulgated in **1992** after Bhopal (1984).
- **EPA RMP** → Community-focused, **Offsite Consequence Analysis (OCA)**, 9 elements, 5-year audit.
- **Seveso Directive** → EU equivalent, **Tier 1 (Lower) & Tier 2 (Upper)** based on substance quantity.
- **API RP 750** → Oil & Gas process hazards; **API RP 752** → Building siting; **API RP 753** → Portable buildings.
- **HAZOP Guide Words (7):** **NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN.**
- **PHA Hierarchy:** What-If → Checklist → HAZOP → FMEA → FTA / ETA.
- **MOC trigger conditions:** Process, Equipment, Material, Procedural, Software changes; **exception = Replacement-In-Kind**.
- **Audit cycle:** OSHA = 3 years; EPA RMP = 5 years; Seveso = Periodic (typically 5 years).
- **Process Safety ≠ Occupational Safety** — distinguish in every answer.
- **CCPS Risk-Based Process Safety (RBPS):** 4 pillars — Commit to Process Safety, Understand Hazards & Risk, Manage Risk, Learn from Experience.
- **IEC 61511** → Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 to 4); PFD between 10⁻¹ and 10⁻⁵.
- **Incidents to remember for context:** Bhopal (1984, MIC leak), Flixborough (1974, Nypro caprolactam), Texas City (2005, BP refinery), Buncefield (2005, UK fuel depot).
- **PSSR** must happen before *any* startup, not just new plants.
- **Hot Work Permit** is required within **15 m (50 ft)** of flammable atmosphere sources.
- **Incident Investigation timeline:** OSHA mandates within **48 hours** of catastrophic event.
- **Risk equation:** $R = P \times C$ (frequency × consequence).
- **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** for OSHA PSM flammables = **10,000 lb (≈ 4,536 kg)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Requirements in PSM Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Requirements in PSM Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Safety Management (PSM)** is a comprehensive, integrated regulatory and engineering framework designed to prevent or minimize the consequences of catastrophic releases of toxic, reactive, flammable, or explosive chemicals from processes involving highly hazardous materials.

**Basic Requirements in PSM Standards** refer to the mandatory minimum elements, organizational commitments, documentation systems, and management controls that an industrial facility handling hazardous chemicals must establish, implement, and sustain to comply with recognized process safety standards such as **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119**, **EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP)**, and **CCPS – Risk Based Process Safety (RBPS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Focus:** Module 1 emphasizes the *why* behind PSM – the Bhopal Tragedy (1984), Flixborough Disaster (1974), and Texas City Refinery Explosion (2005) – and the *what* – the structural backbone of PSM that any chemical/process engineer must internalize.

### 1.1.1 The Triggering Events Behind PSM Standards

| Year | Incident | Casualties | Lesson Drawn |
|------|----------|------------|--------------|
| 1974 | Flixborough, UK (Nypro Caprolactam) | **28 dead** | Need for Management of Change (MOC) |
| 1984 | Bhopal, India (Union Carbide – MIC) | **3,800+ dead** | Need for Emergency Planning & PSM Globally |
| 1988 | Piper Alpha, North Sea | **167 dead** | Need for Permit-to-Work and Mechanical Integrity |
| 1992 | OSHA Promulgates **29 CFR 1910.119** | — | Birth of formalized PSM in USA |
| 1996 | EPA RMP Rule (40 CFR Part 68) | — | Public-facing consequence-based rule |
| 2005 | Texas City Refinery (BP) | **15 dead, 170 injured** | Need for Mechanical Integrity & PHA rigor |
| 2007 | CCPS Publishes **RBPS Guidelines** | — | Modernized, performance-based PSM |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are boarding a commercial aircraft. The captain does not just "fly the plane" — she follows a meticulously written, legally mandated, regularly audited checklist: pre-flight inspection, weather briefing, fuel check, communication check, route planning, emergency briefing.
>
> A single skipped item can mean catastrophe.
>
> **PSM Standards are the chemical plant's pre-flight checklist.** Each element (PHA, MOC, Mechanical Integrity, Training, Audits…) is a section of the checklist. PSM exists because flying a chemical plant "by feel" — relying on operator memory and ad-hoc decisions — has historically killed thousands.

### 1.2.1 Key Analogies for Each PSM Concept

- **Process Safety Information (PSI)** → The **aircraft's engineering manual & blueprints**.
- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** → The **risk assessment before each takeoff**.
- **Operating Procedures** → The **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** of the cockpit.
- **Mechanical Integrity** → Scheduled **engine & airframe maintenance**.
- **Management of Change (MOC)** → A formal review when **an engine part is swapped** before flight.
- **Emergency Planning** → The **crash-response and evacuation drill**.
- **Incident Investigation** → The **black-box analysis** after any incident.

---

## 1.3 Why "Basic Requirements" Matter in PSM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The word *Basic* does **not** mean *optional* or *introductory*. In PSM standards, "basic requirements" are the **non-negotiable floor** of compliance. Skipping any one element — even one — voids the entire safety case.

The basic requirements are driven by **three core management principles**:

1. **Accountability** – Senior leadership owns PSM; it is *not* delegated solely to the safety department.
2. **Risk-Based Decision Making** – Resources are allocated proportional to process hazard severity and likelihood.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** – PSM is a *living system*, not a one-time project.

### 1.3.1 Statutory Thresholds for PSM Applicability

A facility is generally covered by PSM if it handles a **regulated substance** above its **Threshold Quantity (TQ)**. Two illustrative thresholds:

$$\text{TQ}_{\text{Chlorine (OSHA)}} = 1{,}500 \text{ lbs } (\approx 680 \text{ kg})$$

$$\text{TQ}_{\text{Toluene Diisocyanate (TDI)}} = 500 \text{ lbs } (\approx 227 \text{ kg})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Exact thresholds vary by chemical and regulation. Always refer to **OSHA Appendix A** and **EPA RMP's List of Regulated Substances** for the authoritative figure.

---

## 1.4 Scope of PSM Standards

The **scope** of basic PSM requirements typically covers:

- **Process Definition:** Any chemical manufacturing, storage, transfer, or use activity involving a regulated substance above TQ.
- **Geographic Scope:** Within the United States (OSHA/EPA) and adopted internationally through ISO/CEN standards.
- **Lifecycle Scope:** From **Process Design → Commissioning → Operation → Modification → Decommissioning**.
- **Workforce Scope:** Permanent employees, contractors, subcontractors, and visitors entering the process area.

---

## 1.5 KTU Course Outcome Alignment

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Linked PSM Element |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| **CO1** | Identify the necessity and elements of PSM | OSHA 14 Elements Overview |
| **CO2** | Apply hazard analysis techniques | PHA, MOC, PSI |
| **CO3** | Evaluate process safety performance | Audits, Incident Investigation |
| **CO4** | Design emergency response frameworks | Emergency Planning |
| **CO5** | Demonstrate PSM compliance auditing | Compliance Audits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 Framework — 14 Elements

The **basic requirements** of PSM, as codified by OSHA in **29 CFR 1910.119**, are organized into **14 mandatory elements**. Together they form the structural backbone of any compliant PSM program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The OSHA 14 Elements of PSM** — every engineer must memorize and internalize these. They appear repeatedly in KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Element-by-Element Theoretical Breakdown

#### **Element 1: Employee Participation**
- **Why:** Front-line operators see hazards first; they must be empowered to raise concerns.
- **How:** Written plan, access to PSI, active participation in PHA, MOC, incident investigations.
- **KTU Hook:** Often asked as *"Why is employee participation listed FIRST?"* – Answer: Because PSM fails when it becomes a desk-only exercise.

#### **Element 2: Process Safety Information (PSI)**
- **Why:** You cannot analyze hazards of a process you do not fully understand.
- **How:** Document hazards of chemicals, technology of the process, and equipment design.
- **Three Sub-Categories:**
  1. **Chemical Information:** Toxicity, PEL/TLV, flammability, reactivity, corrosivity.
  2. **Process Information:** Block diagrams, flow diagrams, P&IDs, max-intended inventory.
  3. **Equipment Information:** Materials of construction, P&IDs, relief system design basis, ventilation, electrical classification.

#### **Element 3: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
- **Why:** Systematic identification of what can go wrong, how likely, and how bad.
- **How:** Use one or more recognized methodologies (HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, FTA, Checklist).
- **Required Frequency:** **Initial PHA before startup; revalidation every 5 years.**
- **Resolution:** All findings must be documented, assigned to a responsible party, and tracked to closure.

#### **Element 4: Operating Procedures**
- **Why:** Procedures codify the safe envelope of operation.
- **How:** Written, accessible, current; cover startup, normal operation, temporary operations, emergency shutdown, normal shutdown, and startup following a turnaround.

#### **Element 5: Training**
- **Why:** Operating procedures are useless if personnel are not trained.
- **How:** Initial training before assignment; refresher training at least every **3 years**; documentation of training records.
- **Includes:** Operating procedures, safe work practices, emergency operations, and PSM overview.

#### **Element 6: Contractors**
- **Why:** Contract workers are statistically over-represented in process incidents.
- **How:** Maintain a contractor safety program; inform of hazards; ensure contractor training; maintain injury/illness logs; periodically evaluate contractor safety performance.

#### **Element 7: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**
- **Why:** A new or modified process must not start until it has been formally declared safe.
- **How:** Confirm construction matches design, procedures are in place, training completed, PHA recommendations resolved, MOC closed, mechanical integrity verified.

#### **Element 8: Mechanical Integrity**
- **Why:** Equipment degrades; uninspected pressure vessels, pumps, valves can fail catastrophically.
- **How:** Written MI program; inspection/testing of pressure vessels, piping, relief devices, emergency shutdown systems, controls, pumps.
- **Frequency:** Determined by **API 510, API 570, ASME** codes.

#### **Element 9: Hot Work Permit**
- **Why:** Welding/cutting in process areas is a leading ignition source.
- **How:** Documented permit before any hot work; fire watch present; combustible material cleared.

#### **Element 10: Management of Change (MOC)**
- **Why:** Most major incidents trace back to an undocumented change.
- **How:** Written MOC procedure covering *process chemicals, technology, equipment, procedures, and facilities* outside routine replacement-in-kind.
- **Critical:** MOC requires technical basis, PHA update if needed, time-limited changes, and authorization signature.

#### **Element 11: Incident Investigation**
- **Why:** Every incident — even a near-miss — carries a learning.
- **How:** Investigate within **48 hours**; root cause analysis (e.g., TapRooT, 5-Why, Cause Mapping); documented findings; corrective actions tracked to closure; report sharing with affected personnel.

#### **Element 12: Emergency Planning and Response**
- **Why:** Even the best prevention cannot guarantee zero incidents.
- **How:** Written emergency response plan; alarm systems; evacuation routes; coordination with local responders; drills at least annually.

#### **Element 13: Compliance Audits**
- **Why:** "Trust but verify" — to ensure the PSM program is functioning as designed.
- **How:** Audit at least every **3 years**; led by at least one person *knowledgeable in PSM*; documented findings; corrective action follow-up; management response.

#### **Element 14: Trade Secrets**
- **Why:** Employees and contractors need safety information regardless of confidentiality.
- **How:** Provide all necessary PSI to personnel without violating trade secret protection; legal recourse preserved.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]

## 2.3 The CCPS RBPS 20 Elements (Comparative Modern Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 1 also expects familiarity with the **CCPS Risk Based Process Safety (RBPS)** framework, which organizes PSM into **4 pillars × 20 elements**, modernized to address post-2005 challenges (process safety culture, supply chain, stakeholder outreach).

### 2.3.1 The Four Pillars of RBPS

1. **Pillar I — Commit to Process Safety**
   - Process Safety Culture • Compliance with Standards • Process Safety Competency • Workforce Involvement • Stakeholder Outreach
2. **Pillar II — Understand Hazards and Risk**
   - Process Knowledge Management • Hazard Identification and Risk Analysis (HIRA)
3. **Pillar III — Manage Risk**
   - Operating Procedures • Safe Work Practices • Asset Integrity • Contractor Management • Training and Performance Assurance • Management of Change • Operational Readiness • Conduct of Operations • Emergency Management
4. **Pillar IV — Learn from Experience**
   - Incident Investigation • Measurement and Metrics • Auditing • Management Review and Continuous Improvement

---

## 2.4 OSHA vs. EPA RMP vs. CCPS RBPS — Comparative Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-standard comparison is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question.**

| Feature | **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** | **EPA RMP (40 CFR Part 68)** | **CCPS RBPS (2007)** |
|---------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| Issuing Agency | OSHA (Worker Safety) | EPA (Environmental & Public) | Industry-led |
| Year | 1992 | 1996 | 2007 |
| Trigger Basis | Threshold Quantity | Threshold Quantity | Performance-based (no threshold) |
| Coverage | Employees on-site | Off-site public & environment | Voluntary best practice |
| Number of Elements | 14 | 6 (Prevention + Emergency Response + Accidental Release) | 20 (4 Pillars) |
| Focus | Process & Equipment | Consequence to public | Culture & Continuous Improvement |
| Penalty for Non-Compliance | Civil fines, stop-work | Civil fines, criminal liability | Reputational, not statutory |
| Audit Frequency | Every 3 years | Triennial compliance audit (Program 3) | Recommended periodic |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

The basic PSM requirements underpin nearly every modern process industry:

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** OSHA PSM is the *de facto* license to operate.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Specialty Chemicals:** RBPS culture pillars are increasingly adopted.
- **LNG & Cryogenic Storage:** Mechanical Integrity and Emergency Planning are critical.
- **Ammonia Refrigeration (Cold Storage):** Covered under PSM due to ammonia toxicity.
- **Battery & Energy Storage:** Modern PSM frameworks extended to lithium-ion thermal runaway.
- **Hydrogen Economy (Green H₂):** PSM principles applied to electrolysis and storage.

> [!TIP]
> A B.Tech Chemical Engineering graduate entering a plant as a **Process Engineer, Safety Officer, or Operations Engineer** will be expected to *write an MOC, participate in a HAZOP, or lead a PSSR* within the first year. KTU's PSM course directly prepares you for these tasks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Implementation Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although PSM is standards-based and not equation-driven, KTU Module 1 examiners test whether you can **demonstrate logical step-by-step methodology** in implementing basic requirements. Below is the canonical *implementation logic* that earns full marks.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: From Regulatory Trigger → Compliance

The transformation of a PSM requirement from regulation to operational reality follows a strict **6-step analytical chain**. This is the *algorithm* of PSM compliance.

### Step-by-Step Compliance Implementation Logic

**Step 1 — Facility Screening & Threshold Check**
Identify whether the facility handles any regulated substance above its TQ.

$$\text{If } \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \geq \text{TQ}_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PSM Applies}$$

where $m_i$ is the maximum intended inventory of chemical $i$ and $\text{TQ}_i$ is the OSHA/EPA threshold quantity.

**Step 2 — Process Definition & Scope Mapping**
Define each "process" (a unit operation or set of integrated unit operations). Map all covered equipment, chemicals, and personnel.

**Step 3 — Compile Process Safety Information (PSI)**
Build the documentation triad:
- Chemical hazard data (SDS, NIOSH, REACH)
- Process technology description (PFD, P&ID, mass-energy balances)
- Equipment data (vessel specs, metallurgy, relief device sizing)

**Step 4 — Conduct Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
Select methodology based on process complexity:

$$\text{Methodology Selection} = f(\text{Complexity}, \text{Novelty}, \text{Consequence})$$

- **HAZOP** — Continuous processes, complex flowsheets.
- **FMEA** — Mechanical/equipment failure analysis.
- **What-If** — Brainstorming for novel or simple processes.
- **FTA** — Quantitative reliability analysis for high-consequence events.

**Step 5 — Document Findings, Recommendations & Action Plan**
Every recommendation gets: (a) description, (b) responsible party, (c) due date, (d) closure evidence. The action plan is a living register.

**Step 6 — Audit, Review & Continuous Improvement**
Compliance audit every 3 years; PHA revalidation every 5 years; MOC as needed; incident investigation as triggered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This 6-step chain is the *spine* of any PSM program. KTU board questions often ask: *"Outline the basic steps a process plant must follow to comply with PSM."* This matrix is the textbook answer.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Determining PSM Applicability

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** A small numerical problem on "screening a facility for PSM coverage" is a high-probability question.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A chemical plant stores **1,000 lb of chlorine** in pressurized cylinders and **400 lb of toluene diisocyanate (TDI)** in a heated tank.

**Given:**
- OSHA TQ for Chlorine = **1,500 lb**
- OSHA TQ for TDI = **500 lb**

**Required:** Determine whether the facility falls under OSHA PSM coverage.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify all regulated substances handled.**

| Substance | On-Site Inventory | OSHA TQ | Exceeds TQ? |
|-----------|-------------------|---------|-------------|
| Chlorine ($Cl_2$) | 1,000 lb | 1,500 lb | ❌ No |
| TDI | 400 lb | 500 lb | ❌ No |

**Step 2 — Evaluate each independently.**

$$m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 1{,}000 \text{ lb } \;<\; \text{TQ}_{\text{Cl}_2} = 1{,}500 \text{ lb} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Below TQ}$$

$$m_{\text{TDI}} = 400 \text{ lb } \;<\; \text{TQ}_{\text{TDI}} = 500 \text{ lb} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Below TQ}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate Check (OSHA uses individual thresholds, but EPA RMP uses aggregation within a process).**

For OSHA PSM, the rule applies if **any single substance** exceeds its TQ. Since neither substance individually exceeds its threshold:

$$\boxed{\text{Facility is NOT covered under OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119}}$$

**Step 4 — Cautionary Note (Exam Trap).**

> [!WARNING]
> Even if OSHA PSM does not apply, **general duty clauses, state regulations, fire codes (NFPA), and corporate standards** may still impose PSM-like obligations. A safe board answer *always* adds this caveat.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the 14 Elements against RBPS Pillars

This is a frequent 14-mark question: *"Compare OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 with CCPS RBPS."* Below is the matrix that earns full marks.

| OSHA PSM Element | CCPS RBPS Equivalent | Functional Overlap | Key Difference |
|------------------|----------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| (1) Employee Participation | Workforce Involvement + Process Safety Culture | People engagement | RBPS adds culture as standalone |
| (2) PSI | Process Knowledge Management | Documentation | RBPS broader (includes supply chain, knowledge retention) |
| (3) PHA | Hazard Identification & Risk Analysis (HIRA) | Hazard analysis | RBPS: continuous, not just 5-year |
| (4) Operating Procedures | Operating Procedures + Conduct of Operations | Written procedures | RBPS adds conduct/behavioral discipline |
| (5) Training | Competency + Training & Performance Assurance | Skills | RBPS: competency assessment added |
| (6) Contractors | Contractor Management | Same | RBPS more prescriptive on evaluation |
| (7) PSSR | Operational Readiness | Same | Identical intent |
| (8) Mechanical Integrity | Asset Integrity + Equipment Reliability | Same | RBPS adds reliability-centered maintenance |
| (9) Hot Work Permit | Safe Work Practices | Same | RBPS covers broader permit universe |
| (10) Management of Change | Management of Change | Identical | RBPS emphasizes organizational change |
| (11) Incident Investigation | Incident Investigation | Same | Identical |
| (12) Emergency Planning | Emergency Management | Same | RBPS includes crisis & continuity |
| (13) Compliance Audits | Auditing | Same | RBPS: management review added |
| (14) Trade Secrets | (within Stakeholder Outreach) | Same | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** When the question asks for a "comparative analysis," this matrix form + 2-3 sentences of commentary per row earns full marks. Do not write a generic essay.

---

## 3.4 The Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) Cycle in PSM

The PDCA cycle is the *meta-framework* underlying the basic PSM requirements.

$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Plan} \rightarrow \cdots$$

- **Plan:** Define PSM scope, write PSI, plan PHA, write procedures.
- **Do:** Implement procedures, train workers, manage change, conduct hot work.
- **Check:** Audit compliance, investigate incidents, review mechanical integrity.
- **Act:** Close audit findings, update procedures, revalidate PHA, improve culture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward students who explicitly use the PDCA terminology when describing any PSM element.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question formats follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (10–15 words per definition; ~80-word explanation).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark analytical questions with internal choice (Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks).

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Process Safety Management (PSM). List any four elements of the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PSM standard.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Process Safety Management (PSM)** is a comprehensive regulatory framework established by OSHA under 29 CFR 1910.119 (1992) to prevent or minimize catastrophic releases of highly hazardous chemicals in process industries.

**Four Elements of OSHA PSM:**

1. **Process Safety Information (PSI)** – Documentation of chemical, process, and equipment hazards.
2. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** – Systematic identification of process hazards using HAZOP, FMEA, etc.
3. **Mechanical Integrity** – Programmed inspection, testing, and maintenance of critical equipment.
4. **Management of Change (MOC)** – Formal review and approval of any change to process, equipment, or procedures.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the difference between OSHA PSM and EPA RMP?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** | **EPA RMP (40 CFR Part 68)** |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Issuing Body** | Occupational Safety & Health Administration | Environmental Protection Agency |
| **Scope** | Worker safety *inside* the plant | Public & environment *outside* the plant |
| **Number of Elements** | 14 elements | 3 program levels (Prevention + Emergency Response) |
| **Year** | 1992 | 1996 |
| **Trigger** | Threshold Quantity (TQ) of regulated substance | Threshold Quantity (TQ) of regulated substance |
| **Focus** | Process & equipment integrity | Off-site consequence analysis |

> In essence, OSHA PSM protects the **worker inside the fence line**, while EPA RMP protects the **public outside the fence line**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** just list the 14 OSHA elements and stop. Examiners expect **explanation + practical significance** for each.
> - Always end a comparison question with a **commentary paragraph** summarizing which framework is "better" for what context.
> - For numerical problems, **show all calculations in a single aligned block** — step-skipping costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> - Use **headings and bullets**; an essay-style answer on 14-mark questions loses marks for poor structure.

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 framework for Process Safety Management. Discuss the four cluster groupings and the rationale behind each cluster.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A process plant handles **3,000 lb of chlorine** in pressurized cylinders, **1,000 lb of ammonia**, and **250 lb of toluene diisocyanate (TDI)**. The applicable OSHA thresholds are: Chlorine = 1,500 lb, Ammonia = 10,000 lb, TDI = 500 lb. Determine whether the facility is covered under PSM. Justify your answer.

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution**

The **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** framework for Process Safety Management was promulgated on **February 24, 1992**, following catastrophic incidents like Bhopal (1984) and Flixborough (1974). It contains **14 mandatory elements** which can be logically clustered into **four functional groups**:

**Cluster I — Foundation Cluster (Elements 1, 2, 14)**

- **Element 1: Employee Participation.** Establishes a written plan for employee involvement in PSI access, PHA teams, MOC reviews, and incident investigations. Rationale: Operators are the *first line of defense*; their active participation improves hazard identification by an order of magnitude.
- **Element 2: Process Safety Information (PSI).** A three-tier documentation system covering (i) chemical hazards (toxicity, flammability, reactivity), (ii) process technology (block diagrams, P&IDs, design intent), and (iii) equipment data (materials of construction, relief design basis, electrical classification). Rationale: Without accurate PSI, hazard analysis becomes guesswork.
- **Element 14: Trade Secrets.** Ensures safety information is shared with workers and contractors without breaching confidentiality. Rationale: Safety trumps secrecy.

**Cluster II — Hazard Identification Cluster (Element 3)**

- **Element 3: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA).** Requires systematic hazard evaluation before startup and revalidation every 5 years, using techniques such as HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, FTA, or Checklist. Rationale: PSM begins with *knowing the hazards*; PHA is the analytical engine that converts PSI into actionable risk knowledge.

**Cluster III — Risk Control Cluster (Elements 4–10)**

This is the operational core of PSM, containing 7 elements that translate hazard knowledge into safe practice:

- **Element 4: Operating Procedures** — Written procedures for startup, normal operation, temporary operation, emergency shutdown, normal shutdown, and post-turnaround startup.
- **Element 5: Training** — Initial training before assignment and refresher every 3 years.
- **Element 6: Contractors** — Screening, orientation, injury logs, periodic evaluation.
- **Element 7: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** — Confirmation that construction matches design, procedures and training are in place, PHA recommendations are closed, and MOC is signed off.
- **Element 8: Mechanical Integrity** — Inspection, testing, and preventive maintenance per API 510, API 570, and ASME codes.
- **Element 9: Hot Work Permit** — Documented permit and fire watch for welding/cutting in process areas.
- **Element 10: Management of Change (MOC)** — Formal review of changes outside *replacement-in-kind* in chemicals, technology, equipment, procedures, or facilities.

Rationale: Once hazards are known, *controls* must be implemented consistently across operations, personnel, and assets.

**Cluster IV — Verification & Learning Cluster (Elements 11–13)**

- **Element 11: Incident Investigation** — Triggered for any incident or near-miss causing or potentially causing catastrophic release; investigated within 48 hours; root cause documented.
- **Element 12: Emergency Planning and Response** — Written plan; alarm systems; annual drills; coordination with local emergency responders.
- **Element 13: Compliance Audits** — Independent audit every 3 years to verify program effectiveness; corrective action tracked to closure.

Rationale: PSM is a *living system*. Without feedback loops, safety degrades over time. Verification and learning close the PDCA cycle.

**Conclusion:** The 14 OSHA elements are not arbitrary — they form a *closed-loop safety system* spanning **Foundation → Identification → Control → Verification**, ensuring that process safety is engineered, operationalized, and continuously improved.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution**

**Step 1 — List each substance and its inventory vs. threshold.**

| Substance | On-Site Inventory ($m_i$) | OSHA TQ ($\text{TQ}_i$) | Exceeds TQ? |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Chlorine | 3,000 lb | 1,500 lb | ✅ Yes |
| Ammonia | 1,000 lb | 10,000 lb | ❌ No |
| TDI | 250 lb | 500 lb | ❌ No |

**Step 2 — Apply OSHA's individual-substance criterion.**

$$\text{PSM Applies} \iff \exists \, i : m_i \geq \text{TQ}_i$$

For Chlorine:

$$m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 3{,}000 \text{ lb} \;>\; \text{TQ}_{\text{Cl}_2} = 1{,}500 \text{ lb}$$

$$\therefore \text{Threshold Exceeded}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Yes. The facility IS covered under OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119.}}$$

**Justification:** OSHA PSM applies if **any single regulated substance** exceeds its Threshold Quantity. Since chlorine inventory (3,000 lb) exceeds its TQ (1,500 lb) by a factor of 2, the entire process handling chlorine falls under PSM coverage. Ammonia and TDI being below their TQs is irrelevant — the rule triggers based on the *worst-case* substance.

**Note:** Even substances below TQ must still be addressed under the facility's general hazard communication and internal safety standards.

---

### **Question B1 (Choice B — Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) standard with the CCPS Risk-Based Process Safety (RBPS) framework. Discuss the similarities, differences, and modern additions introduced by RBPS.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the Management of Change (MOC) element in detail. Why is MOC considered the "backbone" of PSM? Provide a real-world incident that demonstrates MOC failure.

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution**

Both OSHA PSM and CCPS RBPS aim to prevent catastrophic process incidents, but they differ significantly in **philosophy, scope, and modern relevance**.

**Similarities:**

1. **Common Core Elements:** Both frameworks cover PSI, PHA, Operating Procedures, Mechanical/Asset Integrity, Contractors, MOC, Incident Investigation, Emergency Planning, Auditing, and Training. The functional intent is identical.
2. **Documentation-Driven:** Both rely on written programs, records, and evidence of compliance.
3. **Continuous Improvement:** Both are designed as living systems, not one-time projects.

**Differences:**

| Parameter | **OSHA PSM (1992)** | **CCPS RBPS (2007)** |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Nature** | Regulatory, legally binding | Voluntary, industry best-practice |
| **Number of Elements** | 14 elements | 20 elements (4 Pillars) |
| **Driver** | Past disasters (Bhopal, Flixborough) | Modern challenges (culture, supply chain) |
| **Performance Basis** | Prescriptive ("must do this") | Performance-based ("achieve this outcome") |
| **Threshold Trigger** | Yes (TQ-based applicability) | No (applicable to all processes) |
| **Emphasis on Culture** | Implicit (Employee Participation) | Explicit & standalone (Pillar I) |

**Modern Additions Introduced by RBPS:**

1. **Process Safety Culture** — A standalone element recognizing that *engineering controls fail* if the organizational culture tolerates shortcuts.
2. **Compliance with Standards** — A formal element dedicated to *identification and integration* of regulatory and industry standards (OSHA, EPA, NFPA, ASME).
3. **Process Safety Competency** — Beyond training: ensures *knowledge, skills, and abilities* match process complexity.
4. **Stakeholder Outreach** — Engages suppliers, customers, regulators, and the public — absent in OSHA.
5. **Conduct of Operations** — Disciplined execution of procedures, handoffs, and shift communications.
6. **Measurement and Metrics** — Leading and lagging indicators (e.g., MOC closure rate, near-miss frequency) for proactive monitoring.
7. **Management Review and Continuous Improvement** — Senior leadership review of PSM performance.

**Conclusion:** OSHA PSM is the *legal floor*, while CCPS RBPS is the *performance ceiling*. Modern process plants must comply with OSHA PSM *and* adopt RBPS principles for world-class safety performance.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution**

**Definition:**
**Management of Change (MOC)** is a formal OSHA PSM element (Element 10) that requires written procedures to review and authorize **any change** to process chemicals, technology, equipment, procedures, or facilities — except for *replacement-in-kind*.

**Why MOC is the Backbone of PSM:**

Statistical analysis of major process incidents (Marsh, 2002; CCPS, 2007) consistently shows that **over 60–70% of catastrophic incidents involve some form of unauthorized or poorly managed change**. MOC is therefore the *single most important safeguard* against the slow drift of safety envelopes.

**Components of a Robust MOC System:**

1. **Scope Definition** — Clearly define what constitutes a change vs. routine replacement-in-kind.
2. **Initiation** — A formal MOC request describing the proposed change, technical basis, and impacted systems.
3. **Review** — Multi-disciplinary team review (Engineering, Operations, Safety, Maintenance).
4. **PHA Update** — If the change introduces new hazards, update or initiate a PHA study.
5. **Approval** — Authorization by a designated authority (Plant Manager / HSE Head).
6. **Implementation Controls** — Time-limited changes, temporary operating procedures, hazard controls during transition.
7. **Documentation Update** — PSI, P&IDs, operating procedures, training modules all updated.
8. **Closure** — Final verification and archive of MOC records.

**Real-World Incident — MOC Failure:**

> **Flixborough Disaster, UK, 1 June 1974** (28 fatalities, 36 injuries):
> The Nypro caprolactam plant had a reactor (RA-202) removed for repair. Engineers installed a **temporary 20-inch bypass pipe** to maintain production. **No formal MOC was raised.** No pressure/vacuum relief was considered. No engineering review was documented. On 1 June 1974, the bypass pipe ruptured, releasing ~40 tonnes of hot cyclohexane, which exploded. The plant was destroyed.
>
> **Root Cause:** An undocumented, unanalyzed, unauthorized change — a textbook MOC failure.
>
> **Lesson:** Had a proper MOC been followed (engineering review, PHA, relief design, management approval), the disaster could have been averted. Flixborough became a cornerstone case study in the development of both OSHA PSM and CCPS guidelines.

**Conclusion:** MOC is the *backbone* of PSM because every other element (PSI, PHA, Procedures, Training, Mechanical Integrity) becomes meaningless if the process is silently modified without review. MOC protects the integrity of all other elements.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master these before walking into the KTU Exam Hall.**

- ✅ **PSM** = Process Safety Management; triggered by handling a regulated substance above its **Threshold Quantity (TQ)**.
- ✅ **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** has **14 elements**, mandatory and statute-backed.
- ✅ **EPA RMP (40 CFR Part 68)** protects the **public outside** the plant; **OSHA PSM** protects **workers inside**.
- ✅ **CCPS RBPS** has **20 elements in 4 pillars**; voluntary, performance-based, modern.
- ✅ The **14 OSHA elements** are best memorized in **4 clusters**: Foundation (1,2,14) | Identification (3) | Control (4–10) | Verification (11–13).
- ✅ **PHA** must be revalidated **every 5 years**.
- ✅ **Compliance Audit** frequency = **every 3 years**.
- ✅ **Training refresher** = **every 3 years**.
- ✅ **Incident investigation** trigger = **within 48 hours**.
- ✅ **MOC** covers changes to chemicals, technology, equipment, procedures, and facilities — *not* replacement-in-kind.
- ✅ **Mechanical Integrity** uses **API 510, API 570, and ASME** codes.
- ✅ **PSSR** is required **before startup of new or modified processes**.
- ✅ **Hot Work Permit** is mandatory for any ignition source in process areas.
- ✅ **Process Safety Culture** is a *standalone* RBPS Pillar I element — absent in OSHA PSM.
- ✅ **Flixborough (1974)** = MOC failure; **Bhopal (1984)** = MIC release; **Piper Alpha (1988)** = permit-to-work & MI failure; **Texas City (2005)** = MI & PHA rigor failure.
- ✅ **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** is the meta-framework underlying all PSM elements.
- ✅ **Trade Secrets** element ensures safety information is shared with workers without breaching confidentiality.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Strategy:** For 3-mark questions — *Define + 3-4 keywords/bullets*. For 14-mark questions — *Headings, clusters, tables, and a concluding paragraph*. Always reference at least one PSM standard by name (OSHA / EPA / CCPS / RBPS / API / ASME / NFPA) for "examinability points."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 14 Elements of OSHA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** 14 Elements of OSHA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **OSHA's Process Safety Management (PSM)** is a regulation promulgated under **29 CFR 1910.119** by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), United States Department of Labor. The standard contains **14 mandatory elements** that employers must implement to manage highly hazardous chemicals and prevent or minimize the consequences of catastrophic releases of toxic, reactive, flammable, or explosive chemicals.

### 1.1 The 14 Elements — Master Overview Table

| # | Element | KTU Acronym | Core Intent |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Employee Participation | EP | Workers involved in PSM activities |
| 2 | Process Safety Information | PSI | Documentation of hazards & equipment |
| 3 | Process Hazard Analysis | PHA | Systematic hazard identification |
| 4 | Operating Procedures | OP | Written, safe work instructions |
| 5 | Training | TR | Competency of employees |
| 6 | Contractors | CON | Control of outside service personnel |
| 7 | Pre-Startup Safety Review | PSSR | Safety check before plant startup |
| 8 | Mechanical Integrity | MI | Maintenance of critical equipment |
| 9 | Hot Work Permit | HWP | Control of ignition sources |
| 10 | Management of Change | MOC | Control of modifications |
| 11 | Incident Investigation | II | Root-cause analysis of accidents |
| 12 | Emergency Planning & Response | EPR | Preparedness for releases |
| 13 | Compliance Audits | CA | Periodic evaluation of PSM system |
| 14 | Trade Secrets | TS | Information access balancing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The **first four elements** (EP, PSI, PHA, OP) form the *foundation layer* of any PSM program. These are the most heavily tested concepts in KTU university examinations and constitute approximately **60–70%** of Module 1 marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Airport Safety Manual":**
> Imagine an international airport. To ensure flight safety, the airport authority (OSHA) requires:
> - **Pilots** (employees) to be **consulted** in safety rule-making (Employee Participation).
> - A **flight manual** listing every aircraft's technical limits (Process Safety Information).
> - A **pre-flight risk checklist** for every route (Process Hazard Analysis).
> - **Standard takeoff/landing procedures** written and approved (Operating Procedures).
> - Mandatory **simulator training** every 6 months (Training).
> - **Catering and refuelling contractors** must follow strict security rules (Contractors).
> - A **final walk-around inspection** before passengers board (Pre-Startup Safety Review).
> - **Periodic engine and brake inspections** (Mechanical Integrity).
> - **Welding permits** near fuel tanks (Hot Work Permit).
> - Approval needed to **change an aircraft type or route** (Management of Change).
> - **Black box analysis** after any near-miss (Incident Investigation).
> - **Crash rescue teams** on standby (Emergency Planning & Response).
> - A **yearly IATA safety audit** (Compliance Audits).
> - **Confidential competitor data** protected during audits (Trade Secrets).

Just as the airport cannot compromise on any one of these 14 procedures, a chemical plant cannot selectively skip any of OSHA's PSM elements — they work as a **closed-loop safety ecosystem**.

### 1.3 Why These 14 Elements?

The elements are arranged in a **logical safety lifecycle**:

$$
\text{Hazard Identification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Risk Control} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Operation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Continuous Improvement}
$$

The **compliance threshold** under 29 CFR 1910.119 applies to processes involving **≥ threshold quantities** of listed chemicals (e.g., ≥ **2,500 lbs** of flammable liquids, ≥ **150 lbs** of chlorine, etc.). The standard aims to reduce **major accident hazards (MAH)** in facilities handling over **137 listed highly hazardous chemicals**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the 14 Elements (KTU Exam Memory Aid):**
> **"E-P-S-O-T-C-P-M-H-M-I-E-C-T"**
> *E*mployee → *P*SI → *S*afety Analysis (PHA) → *O*perating → *T*raining → *C*ontractors → *P*SSR → *M*echanical → *H*ot Work → *M*OC → *I*ncident → *E*mergency → *C*ompliance → *T*rade Secrets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Element-by-Element Breakdown

Below is the structured analysis of all **14 elements**, each explained with its **Purpose**, **Key Requirement**, and **Engineering Utility**.

#### **Element 1: Employee Participation**
- **Purpose**: Ensure front-line workers are actively involved in PSM activities.
- **Key Requirement**: A written plan of action implementing employee participation; employees and their representatives must be consulted on PHA development and incident investigations.
- **Why It Matters**: Workers know the "real" plant operations better than managers. Excluding them creates blind spots in hazard analysis.

#### **Element 2: Process Safety Information (PSI)**
- **Purpose**: Compile accurate, complete, and accessible written information.
- **Three Sub-Categories**:
  1. **Chemical/Toxicological Information** — properties, reactivity, corrosion data, thermal stability.
  2. **Process Technology Information** — block diagrams, process flow diagrams (PFDs), piping & instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs), maximum intended inventory.
  3. **Process Equipment Information** — materials of construction, design codes, electrical classification, relief system design basis.
- **Threshold Quantities** must be documented for each chemical.

#### **Element 3: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
- **Purpose**: Systematically identify, evaluate, and control hazards.
- **Applicable Methodologies** (KTU-Favorite Topic):
  - **What-If Analysis**
  - **Checklist Analysis**
  - **What-If/Checklist**
  - **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** — *most common for continuous processes*
  - **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
  - **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- **Initial PHA** must be completed **before startup**, with a **5-year revalidation** cycle for all covered processes.
- **Resolution Time**: PHA findings must be resolved within **6 months of developing the recommendation** or before the next process change, whichever is sooner.

#### **Element 4: Operating Procedures**
- **Purpose**: Provide clear, written instructions for safe operation.
- **Required Coverage**: Initial startup, normal operations, temporary operations, emergency shutdown, emergency operations, normal shutdown, startup following a turnaround/emergency shutdown.
- **Accessibility**: Procedures must be readily accessible to all employees who operate the process.

#### **Element 5: Training**
- **Purpose**: Ensure competency of all employees, including maintenance and contractors.
- **Initial Training**: Required for each employee before working independently on a process.
- **Refresher Training**: At least **every 3 years**, or whenever:
  - There is a change in operating procedures
  - A new hazard is identified through PHA
  - An incident investigation reveals training deficiency
  - The employee is observed performing unsafely

#### **Element 6: Contractors**
- **Purpose**: Control safety performance of outside service personnel.
- **Employer Responsibilities**:
  - Evaluate contractor safety performance
  - Inform contractors of known hazards
  - Ensure contractor employees are trained
  - Maintain a contractor injury/illness log
- **Contractor Responsibilities**: Ensure their employees follow facility safety rules and procedures.

#### **Element 7: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**
- **Purpose**: Verify that newly constructed or modified facilities are safe to start.
- **Required Confirmations**:
  - Construction matches design specs
  - All PHA recommendations resolved or implemented
  - Operating procedures in place
  - Training completed
  - Mechanical integrity verified
  - Emergency response plans updated

#### **Element 8: Mechanical Integrity**
- **Purpose**: Ensure critical process equipment is designed, installed, and maintained properly.
- **Critical Equipment Includes**:
  - Pressure vessels and storage tanks
  - Piping systems (including valves)
  - Relief and vent systems
  - Emergency shutdown systems
  - Controls (including monitoring sensors and alarms)
  - Pumps
- **Inspection Frequency**: Per **API 510**, **API 570**, or **ASME** standards; written procedures and trained inspectors required.

#### **Element 9: Hot Work Permit**
- **Purpose**: Control spark/ignition-generating activities (welding, cutting, grinding) in hazardous areas.
- **Required Before**: Any hot work in or near a process area where flammable materials may be present.
- **Permit Must Include**: Date, time, location, nature of work, fire watch, atmospheric testing results, equipment isolation confirmation.

#### **Element 10: Management of Change (MOC)**
- **Purpose**: Control all modifications to process chemicals, technology, equipment, procedures, and facilities.
- **What Triggers MOC**:
  - Changes in process chemistry
  - Changes in equipment specifications
  - Changes in operating parameters (T, P, flow)
  - Changes in control logic/sequences
  - Changes in safety systems
- **Exclusions**: "Like-for-like" replacements of equipment do **not** trigger MOC.

#### **Element 11: Incident Investigation**
- **Purpose**: Determine root causes of catastrophic releases or near-misses.
- **Trigger**: Any incident that resulted in (or could reasonably have resulted in) a catastrophic release.
- **Timeline**: Investigation must begin **within 48 hours** of the incident.
- **Documentation**: Report with findings, root cause, and recommendations; addressed within a documented time frame.

#### **Element 12: Emergency Planning & Response**
- **Purpose**: Prepare for, respond to, and mitigate consequences of catastrophic releases.
- **Coordination Required**:
  - Internal emergency response plan
  - Coordination with local emergency responders (fire, police, medical)
  - Periodic drills and exercises
- **Must Address**: Small releases, large releases, vapor clouds, fires, explosions, toxic exposure.

#### **Element 13: Compliance Audits**
- **Purpose**: Verify that PSM systems are functioning as designed.
- **Frequency**: At least **every 3 years**.
- **Audit Team**: At least one person knowledgeable in the standard (typically a third-party auditor).
- **Audit Report**: Findings and corrective actions must be documented and addressed promptly.

#### **Element 14: Trade Secrets**
- **Purpose**: Allow PSM compliance while protecting proprietary chemical/process information.
- **Requirement**: Employers must make all PSM-related information available to employees, their representatives, and contractors — **regardless of trade-secret status** — for compliance purposes.
- **Confidentiality Agreement**: May be required before disclosure.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Element | Trigger / Threshold | Frequency | Critical Document |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Employee Participation | Always active | Continuous | Written Plan of Action |
| PSI | Before PHA | Updated as needed | PSI Package |
| PHA | Before startup | Every **5 years** | PHA Report |
| Operating Procedures | Always available | Reviewed triennially | SOP Manual |
| Training | Pre-employment | Every **3 years** | Training Records |
| Contractors | Pre-qualification | Per contract | Contractor Safety Policy |
| PSSR | Before startup | Per modification | PSSR Checklist |
| Mechanical Integrity | Equipment lifecycle | Per **API/ASME** | Inspection Records |
| Hot Work Permit | Before hot work | Per activity | Permit Document |
| MOC | Any modification | Per change | MOC Form |
| Incident Investigation | Within **48 hrs** of incident | Per incident | Investigation Report |
| Emergency Response | Always ready | Annual drills | ERP Document |
| Compliance Audits | Periodic | Every **3 years** | Audit Report |
| Trade Secrets | Upon request | Continuous | NDA/Confidentiality Form |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Numbers to Memorize:**
> - PHA revalidation: **5 years**
> - Training refresher: **3 years**
> - Incident investigation start: **within 48 hours**
> - Compliance audit cycle: **3 years**
> - PHA recommendation resolution: **within 6 months**
> - Threshold quantity for flammable liquids: **2,500 lbs**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Application Logic

### 3.1 Logical Flow of OSHA PSM Implementation

Because the 14 elements are predominantly **management/procedural** rather than purely computational, the "analytical matrix" here refers to the **decision logic and trigger conditions**.

### 3.2 PHA Selection Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

When a KTU exam asks "Which PHA method is most suitable?", apply this decision matrix:

$$
\text{Process Type} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Hazard Complexity} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Method Selection}
$$

| Process Type | Recommended PHA Method | Reason |
|--------------|----------------------|--------|
| Continuous chemical process | **HAZOP** | Detailed node-by-node deviation analysis |
| Batch process (recipe-based) | **What-If / Checklist** | Simpler operations, fewer deviation nodes |
| Mechanical equipment (pump, compressor) | **FMEA** | Component-level failure modes |
| Complex multi-system interaction | **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Top-down logic for rare catastrophic events |
| Routine facility modification | **Checklist** | Quick screening tool |
| New facility / R&D pilot | **What-If Analysis** | Brainstorming-based for novel hazards |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Determining if MOC is Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step MOC Decision Logic:**

**Scenario:** An operator notices that the operating temperature of an exothermic reactor has been routinely running at **95°C** instead of the design **80°C** to increase throughput. The supervisor verbally approves this change. Is MOC required?

**Step 1 — Identify the Change Type:**
The operating temperature is being altered → this is a change in **operating parameters** (process variable outside design intent).

**Step 2 — Apply OSHA Trigger List:**
Per 29 CFR 1910.119(l), changes in **operating parameters** outside established limits **require MOC**. ✓

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Verbal Approval:**
MOC **must be written**, authorized by a designated management representative, and include:
- Technical basis for the change
- Impact on safety and health
- Modifications to operating procedures
- Training requirements
- Time duration of the change
- Authorization signatures

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
A **verbal approval is insufficient**. This change requires a **formal MOC procedure** to be completed **before** continuing the elevated-temperature operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often believe MOC only applies to *equipment* changes. It applies equally to **chemicals, technology, procedures, facilities, and operating parameters** — anything outside established design intent.

### 3.4 Incident Severity Classification (Analytical Framework)

The **48-hour rule** for incident investigation applies to events classified as:

$$
\text{Incident Severity} = f(\text{Actual Outcome}, \text{Potential Outcome})
$$

| Classification | Definition | MOC Trigger? |
|---------------|------------|--------------|
| **Catastrophic Release** | Major injury, fatality, or significant community impact | Yes — Investigation mandatory |
| **Near-Miss** | Could have resulted in catastrophic release under slightly different conditions | Yes — Investigation mandatory |
| **Minor Release** | Contained quickly, no injury, no potential for catastrophe | No — Standard reporting only |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The OSHA PSM framework is the foundation for many international equivalents — **CCPS Risk-Based Process Safety (RBPS)** in the US, **COMAH** in the UK, **Seveso III Directive** in the EU, and **Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (as amended in 2000).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OSHA PSM — Element Interdependency Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the **14 elements** form an **interconnected closed-loop system**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[1. Employee Participation]:::foundation
    nodeB[2. Process Safety Information]:::foundation
    nodeC[3. Process Hazard Analysis]:::foundation
    nodeD[4. Operating Procedures]:::foundation
    nodeE[5. Training]:::support
    nodeF[6. Contractors]:::support
    nodeG[7. Pre-Startup Safety Review]:::support
    nodeH[8. Mechanical Integrity]:::support
    nodeI[9. Hot Work Permit]:::control
    nodeJ[10. Management of Change]:::control
    nodeK[11. Incident Investigation]:::feedback
    nodeL[12. Emergency Planning and Response]:::feedback
    nodeM[13. Compliance Audits]:::feedback
```
