```mermaid
    nodeN[14. Trade Secrets]:::control
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeA
    
    classDef foundation fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 PHA Methodology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[New Process or Modification Identified]:::start
    nodeQ1{Process Type?}:::decision
    nodeA1[Continuous Chemical Process]:::branch
    nodeA2[Batch Process]:::branch
    nodeA3[Mechanical Equipment]:::branch
    nodeA4[Complex System Interaction]:::branch
    nodeR1[Apply HAZOP]:::method
    nodeR2[Apply What-If and Checklist]:::method
    nodeR3[Apply FMEA]:::method
    nodeR4[Apply Fault Tree Analysis]:::method
    nodeFinal[Document Findings in PHA Report]:::output
    
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|Continuous| nodeA1 --> nodeR1 --> nodeFinal
    nodeQ1 -->|Batch| nodeA2 --> nodeR2 --> nodeFinal
    nodeQ1 -->|Mechanical| nodeA3 --> nodeR3 --> nodeFinal
    nodeQ1 -->|Complex Systems| nodeA4 --> nodeR4 --> nodeFinal
    
    classDef start fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef decision fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef output fill:#9D4EDD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MOC Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[CHANGE REQUEST INITIATED]:::input
    blockB[TECHNICAL REVIEW BY ENGINEERING]:::process
    blockC[SAFETY AND HEALTH IMPACT ASSESSMENT]:::process
    blockD[OPERATING PROCEDURE UPDATE]:::process
    blockE[TRAINING OF AFFECTED EMPLOYEES]:::process
    blockF[MANAGEMENT AUTHORIZATION SIGNATURE]:::decision
    blockG[IMPLEMENTATION OF CHANGE]:::output
    blockH[POST-IMPLEMENTATION REVIEW]:::feedback
    blockI[UPDATE PSI AND PHA RECORDS]:::output
    
    blockA --> blockB --> blockC --> blockD --> blockE --> blockF --> blockG --> blockH --> blockI
    blockI -.-> blockA
    
    classDef input fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The MOC workflow is a *closed-loop* system. Step I ("Update PSI and PHA Records") feeds back into the Change Request initiation, ensuring that lessons learned become part of the facility's permanent knowledge base — a core principle of the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** safety cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six elements of OSHA's Process Safety Management (PSM) standard.**

**Model Answer:**
The six elements of OSHA's PSM standard (29 CFR 1910.119) are:

1. **Employee Participation** — involving workers in PSM activities.
2. **Process Safety Information (PSI)** — documentation of hazards, process technology, and equipment.
3. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — systematic identification and evaluation of hazards.
4. **Operating Procedures** — written instructions for safe operation under all modes.
5. **Training** — initial and refresher training for employees handling hazardous processes.
6. **Mechanical Integrity** — inspection, testing, and maintenance of critical process equipment.

*(Any six of the 14 elements accepted; ½ mark each.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why must a Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) be revalidated every five years under OSHA PSM?**

**Model Answer:**
Under 29 CFR 1910.119(e)(6), **PHA revalidation every 5 years** is mandatory to ensure that:

1. The PHA remains **current and consistent with present operating conditions**.
2. Any **newly identified hazards** (from incident investigations, MOC records, or updated scientific data) are incorporated.
3. The facility's **modifications and process changes** since the last PHA are reflected.
4. **Recommendations from the previous PHA** have been resolved or are still being addressed.

This revalidation requirement guarantees that the hazard analysis does not become obsolete and continues to serve its purpose of preventing catastrophic releases.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Option 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain in detail the Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) element of OSHA's PSM standard. Include its purpose, applicable methodologies, and team composition requirements.**

##### **Part (a) — Purpose, Applicability, and Team Composition (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition and Purpose:**
Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a **systematic, organized, and comprehensive review** of the process to identify, evaluate, and control hazards associated with the chemicals, equipment, and operations. Its purpose is to **prevent or minimize the consequences of catastrophic releases** of highly hazardous chemicals.

**Step 2 — Applicability:**
- PHA must be completed **before the initial startup** of any new process.
- PHA must be **updated and revalidated at least every 5 years** thereafter.
- A PHA is also required following any **Management of Change (MOC)** that alters process safety information.

**Step 3 — Team Composition Requirements (per 1910.119(e)(4)):**

The PHA team must consist of at least:
1. **One person with expertise in the process** being analyzed (typically a process engineer).
2. **One person knowledgeable in the PHA methodology** being used.
3. **One person with expertise in process operations** (typically an experienced operator).
4. **Other members** as needed for technical depth — e.g., maintenance, instrumentation, safety specialists.
5. A **team leader** who is independent of the facility being analyzed (for objectivity).
6. **Employee participation** is required — workers must be represented on the team.

**Step 4 — Priority Ranking of Recommendations:**
All recommendations arising from the PHA must be **ranked by priority**, addressed in a timely manner, and **documented with a written schedule for resolution**.

---

##### **Part (b) — PHA Methodologies and Resolution Timelines (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Recognized Methodologies (per OSHA 1910.119 Appendix):**

| Methodology | Best Suited For |
|-------------|----------------|
| What-If Analysis | New processes, brainstorming scenarios |
| Checklist | Routine, well-understood operations |
| What-If/Checklist | Hybrid scenarios |
| HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) | Continuous chemical processes |
| FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) | Mechanical/equipment failure analysis |
| FTA (Fault Tree Analysis) | Complex systems, rare catastrophic events |

**Step 2 — Hierarchy of Methods:**
OSHA recommends (in order of preference for complex processes): **HAZOP > FMEA > What-If > Checklist**.

**Step 3 — Resolution Timeline:**
- Recommendations must be **resolved within 6 months** of developing the recommendation.
- If not resolved in 6 months, implementation must occur **before the next process change** or, for urgent safety issues, **immediately**.
- All PHA records must be **retained for the life of the process**.

**Step 4 — Engineering Utility:**
PHA findings directly feed into **Operating Procedures**, **Training**, and **Mechanical Integrity** programs — making PHA the **central nervous system** of OSHA's PSM framework.

---

#### **Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Discuss the Management of Change (MOC) and Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) elements of OSHA's PSM standard, with emphasis on their interconnection.**

##### **Part (a) — Management of Change (MOC) (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
MOC is a formal procedure to evaluate and authorize **any modification** to a process — including changes to chemicals, technology, equipment, procedures, and operating parameters — that fall outside the established design intent.

**Step 2 — Triggers for MOC:**

| Trigger Category | Example |
|-----------------|---------|
| Chemical change | Substitution of feedstock |
| Technology change | New control system |
| Equipment change | Replacement of pump with different capacity |
| Procedural change | New startup sequence |
| Operating parameter change | Temperature/pressure limits increased |
| Facility change | Addition of new piping tie-in |

**Step 3 — Exclusions:**
*"Like-for-like" replacements* (e.g., replacing a damaged valve with the same model) **do not** require MOC.

**Step 4 — Required Elements of a MOC Procedure:**
1. **Technical basis** for the proposed change.
2. **Safety and health impact analysis**.
3. **Modification of operating procedures** as needed.
4. **Training** requirements for affected employees.
5. **Authorization signature** from designated management representative.
6. **Time-limited or permanent** designation.
7. **Update to Process Safety Information (PSI)** and PHA records.

**Step 5 — Engineering Utility:**
MOC prevents the silent accumulation of "drift" in plant operations that historically has caused catastrophic incidents (e.g., **Bhopal 1984**, **Texas City 2005**).

---

##### **Part (b) — Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
PSSR is a **systematic safety review** conducted **before introducing highly hazardous chemicals** into a new or modified process facility.

**Step 2 — Applicable Situations:**
- Commissioning of **newly constructed** process facilities.
- Startup after **major modifications** (those requiring MOC).
- Startup after **process shutdowns for maintenance** of significant duration (employer-defined).

**Step 3 — PSSR Confirmation Checklist:**
1. Construction and equipment **match design specifications**.
2. All **PHA recommendations resolved or scheduled** for completion.
3. **Operating procedures** are in place and updated.
4. **Training** of operating personnel is complete.
5. **Mechanical integrity** inspections are completed.
6. **Emergency response procedures** are updated and communicated.
7. **Management of Change** documentation is complete for any modifications.
8. **Pre-startup alarm and interlock testing** has been performed.

**Step 4 — Interconnection Between MOC and PSSR:**
$$
\text{MOC Approval} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Implementation}}\; \text{Physical Modification} \;\xrightarrow{\text{PSSR}}\; \text{Safe Startup}
$$

PSSR is the **verification gate** that ensures the MOC-approved change has been physically and procedurally implemented correctly. Together, they form a **closed-loop assurance system**.

**Step 5 — Engineering Utility:**
PSSR acts as the **last checkpoint** before hazardous chemicals are introduced, preventing many historical incidents where modifications were completed without verifying safety interlock integrity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "14 Elements of OSHA" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PHA revalidation (5 years) with Compliance Audits (3 years)** — these are two different elements with two different cycles. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Omitting employee participation** — students often skip this "soft" element while focusing on technical ones, but it carries full marks weightage.
> 3. **Missing the "5 W's" of MOC** — students fail to mention *Why* (technical basis), *What* (impact), *Who* (authorization), *When* (time-limited vs permanent), and *How* (procedure updates).
> 4. **Mixing PSSR with Mechanical Integrity** — PSSR is a *one-time* pre-startup review; Mechanical Integrity is *continuous* throughout equipment life.
> 5. **Forgetting Hot Work Permit specifics** — many students describe the permit requirement but forget to mention the **fire watch** and **atmospheric testing** components.
> 6. **Trade Secrets misuse** — examiners expect students to know that *trade-secret status does NOT exempt an employer from PSM compliance disclosure to employees/contractors*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 14 Elements of OSHA PSM**

- [ ] **Standard Reference:** 29 CFR 1910.119
- [ ] **Total Elements:** 14 (memorize all names)
- [ ] **Key Numbers:**
  - PHA cycle: **5 years**
  - Training cycle: **3 years**
  - Compliance audit cycle: **3 years**
  - Incident investigation start: **within 48 hours**
  - PHA recommendation resolution: **within 6 months**
- [ ] **Foundation Elements (Module 1 focus):** Employee Participation, PSI, PHA, Operating Procedures
- [ ] **PHA Methods (in OSHA's preference order):** HAZOP > FMEA > What-If > Checklist
- [ ] **MOC Triggers:** Chemicals, Technology, Equipment, Procedures, Facilities — **NOT like-for-like replacement**
- [ ] **PSSR Scope:** Required for new construction, MOC modifications, major shutdown restarts
- [ ] **Mechanical Integrity Equipment:** Pressure vessels, piping, relief systems, ESD systems, pumps, controls
- [ ] **Hot Work Permit Essentials:** Fire watch + atmospheric testing + isolation confirmation
- [ ] **Incident Investigation:** Required for **catastrophic releases AND near-misses**
- [ ] **Trade Secret Rule:** Cannot block employee/contractor access to PSM-relevant information
- [ ] **Engineering Connections:** OSHA PSM → Seveso III (EU), COMAH (UK), Indian MSIHC Rules
- [ ] **Logical Sequence:** Identify Hazards → Document → Analyze → Operate → Audit → Improve → Close the Loop

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain any 14 elements" or "list and briefly describe" the elements, structure your answer using a **bullet/tabular format** with the element name, one-line purpose, and one-line key requirement. This format is examiner-friendly and **maximizes marks per minute of writing time**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools Used to Implement PSM Standards
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tools Used to Implement PSM Standards

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Safety Management (PSM)** is a comprehensive, integrated framework mandated by regulatory bodies (e.g., OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, EPA Risk Management Program) that requires employers to anticipate, prevent, and control the catastrophic release of highly hazardous chemicals from covered processes. The **Tools Used to Implement PSM Standards** refer to the systematic, structured, and quantitative methodologies that operationalize the abstract elements of the PSM standard into measurable, auditable engineering and management practices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, these tools are categorized into three functional tiers:

1. **Analytical Tools** — Identify and quantify hazards (e.g., HAZOP, FMEA, Fault Tree, QRA).
2. **Protective Layer Tools** — Reduce risk to acceptable levels (e.g., LOPA, SIL determination, Safety Instrumented Systems).
3. **Operational Tools** — Sustain PSM performance over the plant lifecycle (e.g., Management of Change, Mechanical Integrity, PSSR, Audits).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, students are expected to know *what* each tool does, *when* to apply it across the plant lifecycle, and *how* to interpret its output for risk-based decision making.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a hospital treating a critically ill patient. The "PSM Standard" is the hospital's policy that *no patient should die from preventable medical error*. Now, the **PSM Tools** are the actual instruments the doctors use:

- **Stethoscope & Blood Tests** = PHA / HAZOP (diagnosis of the problem)
- **Surgery Protocol Checklist** = LOPA / SIL (deciding the right protective procedure)
- **ICU Monitoring** = Mechanical Integrity & Operating Procedures (continuous care)
- **Discharge Review** = Pre-Startup Safety Review (verifying recovery before resuming normal life)
- **Post-Mortem Report** = Incident Investigation (learning from failure)

Just as a doctor uses *multiple tools in sequence* to save a patient, a process safety engineer uses multiple PSM tools across the lifecycle of a chemical plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** No single PSM tool is sufficient. PSM implementation requires an **integrated suite** of tools applied at the correct lifecycle stage — design, construction, operation, modification, and decommissioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of PSM Implementation Tools

The KTU 2024 Scheme organizes PSM tools around three lifecycle phases:

#### A. Tools for Hazard Identification (Applied During Design & Periodic Review)

**1. Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**
- A structured, team-based brainstorming technique that applies guidewords (`NO`, `MORE`, `LESS`, `AS WELL AS`, `PART OF`, `REVERSE`, `OTHER THAN`) to process nodes.
- Identifies deviations from design intent, their causes, consequences, and safeguards.
- Best suited for **continuous process plants** (refineries, petrochemicals).

**2. Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
- Bottom-up inductive method. Each component is asked: *"How can it fail?"*
- Produces a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** to rank failure modes.

**3. Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- Top-down deductive method. Starts with the undesired TOP event and uses Boolean logic (AND/OR gates) to find combinations of basic failures.

**4. What-If Analysis**
- Informal, open-ended questioning technique. Low cost, used in early design stages.

**5. Checklist Analysis**
- Lists standard items based on regulations, codes, and past experience.

#### B. Tools for Risk Assessment & Protective Layer Determination

**6. Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**
- Semi-quantitative method. Counts **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** between a scenario and its consequence.
- Determines the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** required for instrumented functions.

**7. Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**
- Uses failure rate databases, event tree analysis (ETA), and consequence modeling to compute individual and societal risk.
- Outputs include **F-N curves**, **Individual Risk (IR)**, and **Potential Loss of Life (PLL)**.

#### C. Tools for Operational Discipline & Continuous Improvement

**8. Management of Change (MOC)**
- Formal written procedure to evaluate and authorize *any* change in process, equipment, procedures, or personnel that could affect safety.

**9. Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**
- A structured audit before introducing highly hazardous chemicals into a new or modified facility, confirming that all design, construction, and procedural safeguards are in place.

**10. Mechanical Integrity Program (MIP)**
- Systematic inspection, testing, and maintenance of critical equipment (vessels, piping, relief devices, instrumentation).

**11. Process Safety Audits**
- Periodic, independent assessments of PSM system compliance with the standard.

**12. Hot Work / Confined Space / Line Breaking Permits**
- Administrative control tools used during maintenance to prevent ignition, asphyxiation, and unintended releases.

**13. Incident Investigation**
- Root-cause methodologies: **5-Why**, **Fishbone (Ishikawa)**, **Bow-Tie**, **TapRooT®**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas** — Master these for Part B derivations and numerical problems.

**Table 2.1 — Core Equations for PSM Tools**

| Tool | Equation / Concept | Variables & Units |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|
| FMEA | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10) |
| FTA (OR Gate, n inputs) | $Q_{top} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | $\lambda_i$ = failure rate of basic event $i$ (failures/year) |
| FTA (AND Gate, n inputs) | $Q_{top} \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot t$ | $t$ = mission time (hours) |
| LOPA | $Mitigated\,Frequency = Initiator\,Freq \times \prod_{j=1}^{n} PFD_j$ | $PFD_j$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of $j$-th IPL |
| SIL Target | $PFD_{SIL} \leq \dfrac{Tolerable\,Freq}{Initiator\,Freq \times \prod PFD_{other\,IPLs}}$ | Frequency in events/year |
| QRA — Individual Risk | $IR_i = \sum_{k} f_k \cdot p_{k,i}$ | $f_k$ = frequency of scenario $k$; $p_{k,i}$ = probability of fatality at location $i$ |
| QRA — PLL | $PLL = \sum_{k} f_k \cdot N_k$ | $N_k$ = expected fatalities in scenario $k$ |
| SIL vs PFD | $SIL = -\log_{10}(PFD)$ | SIL 1: $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, **vertical absolute-value bars** (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) have been replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. If a student encounters a problem involving absolute quantities, the LaTeX form $x = \vert v \vert$ is preferred over writing `|v|` inside a row.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Sector | PSM Tool Most Commonly Applied | Why |
|--------|-------------------------------|-----|
| Oil & Gas Refining | HAZOP + LOPA + SIL | High inventories, complex process interactions |
| Pharmaceutical | FMEA + QRA | Batch processes, high consequence of contamination |
| LNG Storage | QRA + Fault Tree | Domino effect potential, off-site population risk |
| Pipelines | What-If + Mechanical Integrity | Long linear assets, aging infrastructure |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection by Lifecycle Stage

| Lifecycle Stage | Primary PSM Tools | Purpose |
|-----------------|-------------------|---------|
| Conceptual Design | What-If, Checklist | Screen for major hazards |
| Detailed Design | HAZOP, FMEA, SIL determination | Identify deviations & specify protection |
| Pre-Construction | PSSR (Design Verification) | Ensure design intent is met |
| Commissioning | PSSR, Operating Procedures | Confirm safe start-up |
| Routine Operation | Mechanical Integrity, Audits, Training | Sustain performance |
| Modification | MOC, HAZOP (re-validation) | Evaluate change impact |
| Emergency | Emergency Response Plan, Permits | Limit consequences |
| Decommissioning | Incident Investigation (learning) | Knowledge capture |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — LOPA Calculation to Determine Required SIL

**Scenario:** A centrifugal pump is used to transfer benzene. A mechanical seal failure could release 500 kg of benzene, causing a vapor cloud explosion (VCE) with potential for 3 fatalities. Calculate the required SIL of an emergency isolation valve (EIV) that would close on high flow.

**Given Data:**
- Initiating event frequency (mechanical seal failure), $f_I = 0.1$ per year
- Tolerable frequency for VCE with 3 fatalities, $f_T = 10^{-5}$ per year
- Existing IPLs:
  - $IPL_1$ = Procedural inspection (monthly), $PFD_1 = 0.1$
  - $IPL_2$ = Basic Process Control System (BPCS) high-flow alarm, $PFD_2 = 0.1$
  - $IPL_3$ = Concrete dike (always effective), $PFD_3 = 0.01$

**Step 1: Write the LOPA risk equation.**

$$
f_{mitigated} = f_I \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \times PFD_{EIV}
$$

**Step 2: Equate mitigated frequency to tolerable frequency (risk acceptance criterion).**

$$
f_I \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \times PFD_{EIV} \leq f_T
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numeric values.**

$$
0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 \times PFD_{EIV} \leq 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply the constants on the left-hand side.**

$$
(0.1)(0.1) = 0.01
$$

$$
(0.01)(0.1) = 10^{-3}
$$

$$
(10^{-3})(0.01) = 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back.**

$$
10^{-5} \times PFD_{EIV} \leq 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $PFD_{EIV}$.**

$$
PFD_{EIV} \leq \dfrac{10^{-5}}{10^{-5}} = 1.0
$$

**Step 7: Interpretation.** Since $PFD_{EIV} \leq 1.0$ is trivially satisfied by *any* safety instrumented function (SIF), the existing IPLs are already sufficient. **No additional SIF is required.**

> [!NOTE]
> If the required $PFD_{EIV}$ had instead been $10^{-3}$, the EIV would need a $PFD \le 10^{-3}$, which corresponds to **SIL 2** per IEC 61511.

**SIL Classification Table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SIL\ 1 &: \quad 10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1} \\
SIL\ 2 &: \quad 10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2} \\
SIL\ 3 &: \quad 10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3} \\
SIL\ 4 &: \quad 10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Fault Tree Quantitative Analysis (OR Gate)

**Scenario:** Top event = "Loss of Containment (LOC)" from a reactor pressure relief path. Two basic events can cause LOC:
- Event A: Rupture disc fails to open, $f_A = 0.05$ / yr
- Event B: Blocked outlet piping, $f_B = 0.02$ / yr
- Event C: Operator fails to respond, $f_C = 0.03$ / yr (only relevant if A AND B both occur)

**Step 1: Draw the fault tree structure.**

```
            LOC (TOP)
               |
            AND gate
           /        \
       OR gate       C
      /   |   \
     A    B    D
```

For simplicity, consider only A and B feeding an **AND gate** that produces the TOP event.

**Step 2: Compute TOP event frequency for the AND gate.**

$$
f_{TOP} \approx f_A \times f_B \times t
$$

Where $t$ is the mission time assumed as **1 year** for annual probability estimation.

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
f_{TOP} \approx 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 1 = 10^{-3}\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to reciprocal Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).**

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{1}{f_{TOP}} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.** The combined failure (both rupture disc failure AND blocked outlet occurring in the same year) is expected once in 1000 years — which generally meets typical corporate risk tolerance for a passive dual-pathway protection system.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — PSM Implementation Tool Framework

```mermaid
graph TB
    PSM[PSM Standard] --> A[Analytical Tools]
    PSM --> B[Protective Layer Tools]
    PSM --> C[Operational Tools]

    A --> A1[HAZOP]
    A --> A2[FMEA]
    A --> A3[Fault Tree]
    A --> A4[What If]
    A --> A5[Checklist]

    B --> B1[LOPA]
    B --> B2[SIL Determination]
    B --> B3[QRA]
    B --> B4[Bowtie Analysis]

    C --> C1[Management of Change]
    C --> C2[Mechanical Integrity]
    C --> C3[Pre Startup Safety Review]
    C --> C4[Operating Procedures]
    C --> C5[Permit to Work]
    C --> C6[Incident Investigation]
    C --> C7[Compliance Audits]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — LOPA Layer-of-Protection Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    IE[Initiating Event] --> L1[IPL 1 BPCS Alarm]
    L1 --> L2[IPL 2 Operator Action]
    L2 --> L3[IPL 3 Mechanical Protection]
    L3 --> L4[IPL 4 Safety Instrumented System]
    L4 --> L5[IPL 5 Emergency Response]
    L5 --> OUT[Mitigated Consequence]
    OUT --> DEC{Tolerable Risk Met?}
    DEC -->|Yes| ACC[Accept Risk]
    DEC -->|No| RED[Add More IPLs or Reduce Initiator]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — PSM Tool Application Across Plant Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    D1[Conceptual Design] --> D2[Detailed Design]
    D2 --> D3[Construction]
    D3 --> D4[Commissioning]
    D4 --> D5[Operation]
    D5 --> D6[Modification]
    D6 --> D7[Decommissioning]

    D1 -.-> T1[What If and Checklist]
    D2 -.-> T2[HAZOP and LOPA and SIL]
    D3 -.-> T3[PSSR Design Check]
    D4 -.-> T4[PSSR Pre Startup]
    D5 -.-> T5[Mechanical Integrity and Audits]
    D6 -.-> T6[MOC and Re Validation]
    D7 -.-> T7[Incident Investigation and Lessons Learned]
```

### 4.4 Bow-Tie Diagram — Generic Loss-of-Containment Scenario

```mermaid
graph LR
    H1[Hazard 1 Overpressure] --> TOP[Top Event Loss of Containment]
    H2[Hazard 2 External Fire] --> TOP
    TOP --> C1[Consequence 1 Vapor Cloud Explosion]
    TOP --> C2[Consequence 2 Toxic Release]

    H1 --> P1[Prevention Barrier 1 Relief Valve]
    H1 --> P2[Prevention Barrier 2 BPCS]
    H2 --> P3[Prevention Barrier 3 Fire Insulation]

    C1 --> M1[Mitigation Barrier 1 Gas Detection]
    C1 --> M2[Mitigation Barrier 2 Evacuation]
    C2 --> M3[Mitigation Barrier 3 Water Curtain]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use **alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `PSM`, `L1`, `D2`) and **plain text labels** without bold or italic markdown tags, complying with Mermaid v10+ syntax requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three tools used to implement Process Safety Management standards and state their primary purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study):** A team-based, guideword-driven technique used to identify process deviations, their causes, consequences, and existing safeguards during detailed design. *(1 mark)*
2. **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis):** A semi-quantitative method that counts independent protection layers (IPLs) to determine the safety integrity level (SIL) required for an instrumented protective function. *(1 mark)*
3. **MOC (Management of Change):** A formal written procedure used to evaluate and authorize changes in process, equipment, procedures, or personnel to prevent unintended safety consequences. *(1 mark)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between HAZOP and FMEA as tools used in PSM implementation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HAZOP | FMEA |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Approach | Brainstorming, deviation-focused | Component-by-component failure-focused |
| Direction | Process-flow oriented (inductive) | Equipment-oriented (inductive) |
| Output | Causes, consequences, safeguards | Risk Priority Number (RPN) |
| Best suited for | Continuous process plants | Equipment & mechanical systems |

*(1 mark per correct distinguishing parameter; full 3 marks for three parameters.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
>
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** procedure. List any **five Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** with their typical PFD ranges. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A reactor has an overpressure scenario with initiating event frequency of $5 \times 10^{-2}$ per year. The tolerable frequency for catastrophic rupture is $10^{-5}$ per year. Three existing IPLs have PFDs of $0.1$, $0.05$, and $0.02$. Calculate the **required PFD of an additional Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** and state its **SIL**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**

LOPA is a semi-quantitative risk-assessment tool used in PSM to evaluate the adequacy of protection layers between a process deviation (scenario) and a specified consequence.

**Procedure (5 steps):**

1. **Identify the scenario** from a prior PHA (HAZOP node deviation).
2. **Determine the consequence severity category** (e.g., recordable injury, fatality, major loss).
3. **Identify the initiating event** and assign its frequency $f_I$ (events/year).
4. **List each Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** with its PFD value. Each IPL must be **independent, effective, and auditable.**
5. **Multiply the initiator frequency by all PFDs** to compute the mitigated event frequency and compare it with the corporate risk tolerance frequency.

**Five IPLs with typical PFD ranges (table):**

| IPL | Typical PFD |
|-----|-------------|
| BPCS control loop (not safety-rated) | $10^{-1}$ |
| Operator human action with $>\!30$ min response time | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Rupture Disc | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| SIL 1 Instrumented Function | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 Instrumented Function | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 Instrumented Function | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Bund / Dike (passive, always effective) | $< 10^{-3}$ |

*(1 mark for procedure description; 1 mark per IPL listed correctly with PFD — 5 IPLs for 5 marks; 1 mark for correct usage.)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Given:**
- $f_I = 5 \times 10^{-2}$ per year
- $f_T = 10^{-5}$ per year
- $PFD_1 = 0.1$, $PFD_2 = 0.05$, $PFD_3 = 0.02$

**Step 1:** Apply the LOPA equation.

$$
f_I \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \times PFD_{SIF} \le f_T
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
(5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (0.1) \times (0.05) \times (0.02) \times PFD_{SIF} \le 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply constants on the LHS.

$$
(0.1)(0.05) = 5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
(5 \times 10^{-3})(0.02) = 10^{-4}
$$

$$
(5 \times 10^{-2})(10^{-4}) = 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and solve.

$$
5 \times 10^{-6} \times PFD_{SIF} \le 10^{-5}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF} \le \dfrac{10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.0
$$

**Step 5:** Since any $PFD \le 1.0$ is acceptable, the inequality is automatically satisfied. **No additional SIF is required.**

However, if we interpret the question strictly (that some safety function *must* be added), we may report the required PFD as $10^{-1}$, which corresponds to **SIL 1**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to convert the units of $f_I$ and $f_T$ consistently (both must be in *per year*). Marks will be cut if the multiplication step is skipped or if a student writes $PFD = 2$ without interpreting its physical meaning (any value $\ge 1$ means *no* SIF needed).

---

> **Q3. Question B (Alternative — Internal Choice).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** methodology with a labelled diagram. Define the symbols for **AND gate, OR gate, basic event, and undeveloped event.** *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Construct the fault tree for "Loss of Containment" caused by either rupture disc failure (event X, $\lambda_X = 0.01$/yr) OR a combination of high pressure AND blocked outlet (events Y with $\lambda_Y = 0.02$/yr and Z with $\lambda_Z = 0.05$/yr). Compute the top-event frequency for a 1-year mission time. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**

Fault Tree Analysis is a deductive (top-down) analytical tool used in PSM to determine the combinations of equipment failures, human errors, and external events that can lead to an undesired top event.

**Symbol Table:**

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|--------|------|---------|
| Rectangle | Top / Intermediate Event | Represents an event caused by lower events via a gate |
| Circle | Basic Event | Independent failure requiring no further development |
| Diamond | Undeveloped Event | A failure with insufficient data, not further analysed |
| OR gate | Boolean OR | Output occurs if **any one** input event occurs |
| AND gate | Boolean AND | Output occurs only if **all** input events occur |

**Procedure:** Identify TOP → apply Boolean logic → identify minimal cut sets → quantify using failure data.

*(2 marks diagram; 1 mark per symbol definition × 4 = 4 marks; 1 mark for procedure.)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Fault tree structure:**
- TOP = LOC
- OR gate with two inputs:
  - Branch 1: Basic event X (rupture disc fails)
  - Branch 2: AND gate with Y (high pressure) and Z (blocked outlet)

**Step 1:** Compute the OR branch contribution (event X alone).

$$
f_{X} = 0.01\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the AND branch contribution (Y AND Z must occur together in the same year).

$$
f_{YZ} \approx \lambda_Y \times \lambda_Z \times t = 0.02 \times 0.05 \times 1 = 10^{-3}\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the TOP event frequency (OR gate: sum of contributions, since events are mutually exclusive at this scale).

$$
f_{TOP} = f_X + f_{YZ} = 0.01 + 0.001 = 0.011\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute MTBF.

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{1}{f_{TOP}} = \dfrac{1}{0.011} \approx 90.9\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation. Loss of containment is expected roughly once every 91 years, dominated by rupture-disc failure. A redundant relief path or rupture-disc upgrade is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** Students frequently add failure rates for an OR gate (correct) but **multiply** them for an AND gate (also correct here, but with proper justification). A common error is forgetting the mission-time factor $t$ when computing AND-gate outputs in per-year units. Always state assumptions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three functional tiers** of PSM tools exist: **Analytical** (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, What-If, Checklist), **Protective-Layer** (LOPA, SIL, QRA, Bow-Tie), and **Operational** (MOC, MI, PSSR, Permits, Audits, Incident Investigation).
- **HAZOP** uses **guidewords** on process nodes; **FMEA** uses **RPN = S × O × D**.
- **FTA** uses **OR gates** (sum failures) and **AND gates** (product of failures × mission time).
- **LOPA** equation: $f_{mitigated} = f_I \times \prod PFD_j$.
- **SIL** is the **negative logarithm (base 10)** of the **PFD** of a SIF.
- **QRA** outputs: **Individual Risk (IR)**, **Potential Loss of Life (PLL)**, **F-N curves**.
- **MOC** is *not* optional — every change, even temporary, must pass MOC.
- **PSSR** must occur before introducing hazardous chemicals into a new or modified facility.
- **Mechanical Integrity** covers vessels, piping, relief devices, and instrumentation — never just one category.
- **Permits** (Hot Work, Confined Space, Line Breaking) are **administrative IPLs** with $PFD \approx 0.1$ to $10^{-1}$.
- Always convert all frequencies to the **same time unit** (typically per year) before performing LOPA or QRA calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applicable Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applicable Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)**, **Applicable Industries** refers to the specific sectors of the chemical, petrochemical, petroleum refining, oil and gas, and allied manufacturing domains that handle, store, process, or transport **highly hazardous chemicals (HHCs)** above threshold quantities stipulated by regulatory frameworks such as the **OSHA PSM Standard (29 CFR 1910.119)** and the **U.S. EPA Risk Management Program (RMP) Rule (40 CFR Part 68)**.

These industries are legally mandated to implement a comprehensive PSM program that includes:

- **Process Safety Information (PSI)**
- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
- **Operating Procedures (OP)**
- **Training and Education**
- **Contractor Management**
- **Mechanical Integrity (MI)**
- **Hot Work Permits**
- **Management of Change (MOC)**
- **Incident Investigation**
- **Emergency Planning and Response (EPR)**
- **Compliance Audits**
- **Employee Participation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The term "Applicable Industries" is *not* limited to chemical plants alone. KTU expects students to recognize that PSM principles extend to any process industry where catastrophic risk (fire, explosion, toxic release) exists, irrespective of the end product.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital's **infection control protocol**. Not every ward follows the same strict sterilization — only the **ICU, operation theatres, and isolation wards** (where dangerous pathogens exist above a critical threshold) require the highest safety regime.

Similarly, **Applicable Industries** in PSM are those "ICU wards" of the industrial world — facilities where the inventory of hazardous materials crosses a *threshold quantity (TQ)* above which the consequences of a failure (BLEVE, VCE, toxic cloud) can be catastrophic to workers, the public, and the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The applicability of PSM is governed not just by *what* you make, but by *what* and *how much* of a hazardous substance you handle. Even a chocolate factory becomes a "PSM-applicable industry" if it stores **1,500,000 lbs of anhydrous ammonia** in its refrigeration system!

## 1.3 Standard Reference Thresholds

| Reference Body | Standard / Rule | Threshold Trigger |
|----------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| OSHA (USA) | 29 CFR 1910.119 | List of **137 HHCs** with TQs |
| EPA (USA) | 40 CFR Part 68 (RMP) | **140 regulated substances** |
| EU | **Seveso III Directive (2012/18/EU)** | Lower & Upper-tier TQs |
| India | **MSIHC Rules, 1989** (amended 2000) | Manufacture/storage thresholds |

---

## 1.4 Why "Applicable Industries" is a Foundational Topic

The student must internalize the **scope boundary** of PSM. Without this understanding, a chemical engineer might incorrectly assume that PSM is only for "oil refineries" — missing critical applications in pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, food processing (ammonia refrigeration), and even wastewater treatment.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid:** Think of the **"5 Ps"** of PSM-applicable industry classification:
> 1. **P**etrochemicals
> 2. **P**harmaceuticals
> 3. **P**ower generation (thermal)
> 4. **P**ulp & Paper
> 5. **P**roduction of explosives/fertilizers

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Industry Applicability

The applicability of PSM is determined by **three concentric decision layers**:

### Layer 1: Industry Type (Sectoral Test)
- Does the facility fall under **NAICS Codes 324, 325, 326** (Petroleum, Chemical, Plastics & Rubber) or other identified sectors?
- Is the facility engaged in manufacturing, processing, refining, or storage of regulated substances?

### Layer 2: Substance Test (Chemical Inventory Test)
- Is the facility handling any of the **OSHA 137 / EPA 140 listed chemicals**?
- Examples: Chlorine, Ammonia, Hydrocarbons (C1–C4), Hydrogen fluoride, etc.

### Layer 3: Threshold Test (Quantity Trigger)
- Does the quantity **at any time** exceed the Threshold Quantity (TQ) specified in the rule?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** PSM is triggered if **ALL THREE layers** are satisfied. A mismatch in *any* layer exempts the facility (partially or fully) from PSM compliance.

## 2.2 Major Applicable Industries — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Petroleum Refining & Petrochemical Industry
- **Largest PSM-regulated sector globally.**
- Handles hydrocarbons (propane, butane, naphtha) under high pressure & temperature.
- Major hazards: **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)**, **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)**, pool fires.
- Operating temperatures often **> 350 °C** and pressures **> 30 bar**.

### 2.2.2 Oil & Gas (Upstream, Midstream, Downstream)
- Upstream: Drilling & production (H₂S, CH₄).
- Midstream: Pipeline transport, LNG terminals.
- Downstream: Distribution terminals, bulk storage.

### 2.2.3 Chemical Manufacturing Industry
- Production of acids (H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HCl), alkalis, polymers.
- Highly exothermic reactions, toxic intermediates.

### 2.2.4 Pharmaceutical & Bulk Drug Industry
- Solvent handling (methanol, acetone, toluene) in large quantities.
- Reactors with **pressure-rated vessels** and reactive chemistries.
- Cross-reference: **cGMP** compliance interfaces with PSM at the **solvent-recovery** stage.

### 2.2.5 Power Generation (Thermal & Nuclear)
- Thermal: **Anhydrous ammonia** for NOₓ reduction (Selective Catalytic Reduction).
- Nuclear: **PSM principles** overlap with **nuclear safety protocols** (INPO, NRC).

### 2.2.6 Fertilizer Industry
- Ammonia, urea, ammonium nitrate production.
- **Catastrophic historical precedent:** Texas City Disaster (1947) — ammonium nitrate.

### 2.2.7 Pulp & Paper Industry
- Uses **chlorine dioxide (ClO₂)**, **hydrogen peroxide**, and **sodium hydroxide**.
- Major hazards: Toxic gas release, kraft recovery boiler explosions.

### 2.2.8 Food Processing & Cold Storage
- **Anhydrous ammonia refrigeration** systems (TQ: 10,000 lbs under RMP).
- Bleveable liquefied gas inventories.

### 2.2.9 Semiconductor & Electronics Manufacturing
- Uses HF, H₂SO₄, isopropyl alcohol, silane (SiH₄) — pyrophoric.
- Ultra-high purity gases (AsH₃, PH₃) — toxic.

### 2.2.10 Wastewater Treatment
- Chlorine, sulfur dioxide, or hypochlorite usage.
- Anaerobic digesters with **methane & H₂S** generation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Decision Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol / Reference | Numerical Value | Unit |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|------|
| OSHA PSM TQ for Anhydrous Ammonia (anhydrous) | $TQ_{NH_3}$ | **10,000** | lbs |
| OSHA PSM TQ for Chlorine | $TQ_{Cl_2}$ | **1,500** | lbs |
| OSHA PSM TQ for Hydrocarbon Liquids (e.g., propane) | $TQ_{HC}$ | **10,000** | lbs |
| Seveso III Lower-Tier TQ (general) | $TQ_{LT}$ | **5,000** | tonnes |
| Seveso III Upper-Tier TQ (general) | $TQ_{UT}$ | **50,000** | tonnes |
| RMP Program Level (Toxic) | $L$ | 1, 2, or 3 | — |
| Catastrophic Incident Threshold (N = fatality) | $N$ | $\geq 1$ | person |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The TQ values serve as the **regulatory on-off switch** for PSM. Engineers designing storage facilities must stay **below TQ** (with safety margin) to avoid triggering the full PSM compliance burden — a legitimate engineering optimization tool.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Plant Design Phase:** Engineers use TQ data to size bulk storage, decide on decentralized vs. centralized storage.
2. **Insurance & Risk Assessment:** Insurers (e.g., **Lloyd's of London**) require PSM-applicability audit before underwriting.
3. **Corporate Liability:** Determines whether a CEO/CFO can face criminal liability under the **"Responsible Corporate Officer" (RCO)** doctrine post-Bhopal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Determining Industry Applicability

> Since this topic is **regulatory-classification based** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" is replaced by an **exhaustive logical decision matrix** — the analytical engine KTU examiners expect.

## 3.1 The Applicability Decision Algorithm

We define the **applicability function** $\mathcal{A}(i, s, q)$ where:

$$
\mathcal{A}(i, s, q) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } i \in \mathcal{I}_{\text{PSM}} \;\land\; s \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{reg}} \;\land\; q \geq TQ(s) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \text{Industry identifier (NAICS code)} \\
s &= \text{Substance being handled} \\
q &= \text{Maximum inventory at any time (lbs or tonnes)} \\
\mathcal{I}_{\text{PSM}} &= \text{Set of PSM-applicable industries} \\
\mathcal{S}_{\text{reg}} &= \text{Set of regulated substances} \\
TQ(s) &= \text{Threshold Quantity for substance } s
\end{aligned}
$$

> The facility is **PSM-applicable** if and only if $\mathcal{A} = 1$.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Classification Logic

**Step 1 — Sectoral Screening**
- Identify NAICS code. If code $\in \{311, 324, 325, 326, 327, 331, 332\}$ (manufacturing-heavy), proceed to Step 2.
- If not, the facility is generally **excluded** from OSHA PSM (with limited exceptions for **hydrocarbon facilities** outside this set).

**Step 2 — Substance Screening**
- Inventory the **complete chemical register** of the facility.
- Cross-reference with **OSHA 137 / EPA 140 list**.
- If **at least one match** exists, proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Threshold Evaluation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } q_{\max}(s) \;\geq\; TQ(s) \;\;\forall s \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{match}} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Trigger PSM full compliance}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Severity Classification (EPA RMP Tiers)**

$$
L(s) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } TQ_{LT} \leq q < TQ_{UT} \\
2 & \text{if } TQ_{UT} \leq q < 5 \cdot TQ_{UT} \\
3 & \text{if } q \geq 5 \cdot TQ_{UT} \text{ or in } \mathcal{S}_{\text{extra hazardous}}
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Cold Storage Facility

> A cold storage warehouse uses **anhydrous ammonia** as refrigerant.

| Step | Check | Value | Outcome |
|------|-------|-------|---------|
| 1 | NAICS code? | 493120 (Cold Storage) | $\notin \{324, 325, 326\}$ — proceed cautiously |
| 2 | Is NH₃ on OSHA list? | **YES** | Proceed |
| 3 | Inventory $q$? | **12,000 lbs** | $q \geq TQ_{NH_3} = 10{,}000$ lbs |
| 4 | $\mathcal{A} = ?$ | $1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 1$ | **PSM-APPLICABLE** |

**Conclusion:** Despite NAICS code being outside the primary list, the threshold test makes this facility **PSM-applicable** (RMP Program Level 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the **three-layer test** explicitly in your answer. Partial screening is the #1 reason students lose marks.

## 3.4 Comparative Applicability Matrix Across Sectors

| Industry | Primary HHCs | TQ Status | Major Historical Incident | KTU Frequency |
|----------|--------------|------------|----------------------------|----------------|
| Petroleum Refining | LPG, naphtha | Always PSM | Texas City (2005) | Very High |
| Petrochemical | Ethylene, propylene | Always PSM | Bhopal (1984) | Very High |
| Pharmaceutical | Methanol, toluene | Often PSM | Multiple | High |
| Fertilizer | NH₃, NH₄NO₃ | Always PSM | West, TX (2013) | High |
| Power (Thermal) | Anhydrous NH₃ | Often PSM | Few | Moderate |
| Pulp & Paper | ClO₂, H₂O₂ | Conditional | Multiple | Moderate |
| Food Processing | NH₃ (refrig.) | Conditional | Multiple | Moderate |
| Semiconductor | HF, SiH₄, AsH₃ | Always PSM | Taiwan (2010) | Moderate |
| Wastewater | Cl₂, SO₂ | Conditional | Low | Low |
| Mining (chemical) | H₂SO₄, CN⁻ | Conditional | Multiple | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PSM Applicability Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Facility Under Review"] --> B{"NAICS Code in PSM Sector?"}
    B -- "No" --> C{"Handles Regulated HHC?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Substance on OSHA/EPA List?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z1["Exempt from PSM"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Inventory Check"]
    D -- "No" --> Z1
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Compare Quantity with TQ"]
    E --> F{"Quantity >= Threshold?"}
    F -- "No" --> Z2["Conditional / RMP Level 1"]
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Full PSM Compliance Required"]
    G --> H{"Quantity >= 5x TQ?"}
    H -- "No" --> I["RMP Program Level 2"]
    H -- "Yes" --> J["RMP Program Level 3 - Highest Risk"]
    I --> K["Implement 14 PSM Elements"]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of PSM-Applicable Industry Categories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE["Heavy Process Industries - Always PSM"]
        A1["Petroleum Refining"]
        A2["Petrochemicals"]
        A3["Fertilizer Manufacturing"]
        A4["Bulk Chemical Production"]
    end
    subgraph COND["Conditional Applicability"]
        B1["Pharmaceuticals"]
        B2["Pulp and Paper"]
        B3["Food Cold Storage"]
        B4["Semiconductor Fab"]
        B5["Wastewater Treatment"]
    end
    subgraph UTIL["Utility & Service"]
        C1["Thermal Power Plants"]
        C2["Oil and Gas Pipelines"]
        C3["LNG Terminals"]
        C4["Bulk Hazmat Terminals"]
    end
    CORE --> D["PSM Regulated Inventory"]
    COND --> D
    UTIL --> D
    D --> E["Threshold Quantity Cross-Check"]
    E --> F["Full / Partial / Exempt Classification"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Why Different Industries Need PSM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Process Safety Management"] --> Q["Common Risk: Loss of Containment"]
    Q --> R1["Fire"]
    Q --> R2["Explosion - VCE, BLEVE, Confined"]
    Q --> R3["Toxic Gas Release"]
    R1 --> S1["Hydrocarbon Industries"]
    R2 --> S1
    R2 --> S2["Ammonia Industries"]
    R3 --> S3["Chlorine / HF Industries"]
    R3 --> S4["Pharma and Semiconductor"]
    S1 --> T["All PSM Applicable Industries"]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    S4 --> T
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each industry block is mapped to a *specific catastrophic mechanism*. Examiners often ask students to **map industry → dominant hazard** — a 3-mark direct question every KTU cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define 'Applicable Industries' in the context of PSM. Mention any four sectors covered under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Applicable Industries in PSM refer to the industrial sectors that are mandated to implement Process Safety Management due to their handling of highly hazardous chemicals (HHCs) above threshold quantities (TQs) listed in regulatory frameworks.

The four major sectors covered under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 are:
1. **Petroleum Refining** (NAICS 324)
2. **Chemical Manufacturing** (NAICS 325)
3. **Plastics and Rubber Manufacturing** (NAICS 326)
4. **Pulp and Paper Mills** using strong acid/alkali processes

> *(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for industries = 3 marks total)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**"A cold storage plant uses 15,000 lbs of anhydrous ammonia. Is it PSM-applicable? Justify with the three-layer test."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Yes, the facility is **PSM-applicable**. Applying the three-layer test:
1. **Sectoral Test:** Cold storage (NAICS 493120) is outside the primary list — borderline case.
2. **Substance Test:** Anhydrous NH₃ is on the **OSHA 137 list** (TQ = 10,000 lbs). ✔
3. **Threshold Test:** Inventory (15,000 lbs) $\geq$ TQ (10,000 lbs). ✔

Since layers 2 and 3 are satisfied, the facility falls under **EPA RMP Program Level 2** and must implement the 14 PSM elements.

> *(1 mark for conclusion + 1 mark for layers + 1 mark for RMP classification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various categories of industries where Process Safety Management (PSM) is applicable. Discuss the rationale behind including each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 framework. How does it determine the applicability of PSM to a given facility? Use the **three-layer test** to elaborate. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Categories of PSM-Applicable Industries (7 Marks):**

The industries where PSM is applicable can be classified into **three broad categories**:

**1. Core Heavy Process Industries (Always Applicable):**
- **Petroleum Refining** — handles LPG, naphtha, gasoline under high T/P. Hazard: BLEVE, VCE.
- **Petrochemicals** — olefin/aromatic plants with reactive hydrocarbons. Hazard: Runaway reactions.
- **Bulk Chemicals** — production of acids, alkalis, intermediates.
- **Fertilizer Industry** — NH₃, ammonium nitrate (post-Oppau, 1921; West, TX, 2013).

**2. Conditional Applicability Industries:**
- **Pharmaceuticals** — solvent handling, batch reactors.
- **Pulp and Paper** — ClO₂, H₂O₂.
- **Food Processing** — NH₃ refrigeration.
- **Semiconductor** — HF, silane, arsine.

**3. Utility and Service Sector:**
- **Thermal Power Plants** — NH₃ for SCR.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines** — high-pressure hydrocarbon transport.
- **LNG Terminals** — cryogenic methane storage.

**Rationale:** Inclusion is based on **catastrophic potential** (life loss, environmental damage, economic loss) when a **loss-of-containment** event occurs. The **"Catastrophic Incident"** is defined as a major incident causing fatality, multiple injuries, or exceeding $1M property damage.

> *(Distribution: 1.5 marks for each of 4 industry types discussed + 1 mark for rationale = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) OSHA PSM Framework & Three-Layer Test (7 Marks):**

The **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** standard, published in **1992**, mandates PSM for facilities handling any of the **137 listed highly hazardous chemicals (HHCs)** at or above the specified threshold quantities.

**Three-Layer Applicability Test:**

$$
\mathcal{A}(i, s, q) = 1 \iff i \in \mathcal{I}_{\text{PSM}} \land s \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{reg}} \land q \geq TQ(s)
$$

1. **Industry Layer (i):** Must fall under a covered NAICS code (e.g., 324, 325, 326).
2. **Substance Layer (s):** Must involve a chemical from OSHA's 137-list **or** a flammable liquid/gas above 10,000 lbs.
3. **Quantity Layer (q):** Maximum inventory must equal or exceed $TQ(s)$.

If all three are satisfied, the facility must implement **all 14 PSM elements**, including PHA, MI, MOC, and EPR.

> *(Distribution: 2 marks for OSHA framework + 3 marks for the three-layer test + 2 marks for the 14-element requirement = 7 marks)*

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** List and briefly explain **any eight PSM-applicable industries**, with at least one example of a hazardous chemical handled in each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Threshold Quantities (TQs)** in determining PSM applicability. How do the **Seveso III Directive** and **EPA RMP Rule** differ in their TQ approach? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Eight PSM-Applicable Industries (7 Marks):**

| # | Industry | HHC Example | Hazard Type |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Petroleum Refining | Propane (C₃H₈) | BLEVE / VCE |
| 2 | Petrochemical | Ethylene (C₂H₄) | Vapour Cloud Explosion |
| 3 | Fertilizer | Anhydrous Ammonia (NH₃) | Toxic Release |
| 4 | Pharmaceutical | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Fire / Toxic |
| 5 | Semiconductor | Hydrogen Fluoride (HF) | Toxic / Corrosive |
| 6 | Pulp & Paper | Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂) | Toxic Gas |
| 7 | Food Cold Storage | Anhydrous NH₃ | Toxic Release |
| 8 | Thermal Power | Anhydrous NH₃ (SCR) | Toxic Release |

> *(1 mark per industry-chemical pair × 7 = 7 marks; 1 extra industry is bonus buffer)*

---

**(b) Role of TQ and Seveso vs. EPA RMP (7 Marks):**

**Role of Threshold Quantities (TQs):**
TQs are the **regulatory trip-wires** that determine whether a facility must comply with PSM. They convert a *qualitative* judgement ("is this dangerous?") into a *quantitative* test ("is the inventory ≥ TQ?").

**Comparison of TQ Approaches:**

| Aspect | EPA RMP Rule (40 CFR 68) | Seveso III Directive (EU) |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Year enacted | **1996** (revised 2017) | **2012** (replaced Seveso II) |
| Number of substances | **140** | **~300** |
| Tiered approach | Program Levels **1, 2, 3** | Lower-tier & **Upper-tier** |
| TQ basis | Worst-case release scenario | Domino effect / Land-use planning |
| Public disclosure | **Yes** (RMP database) | Limited (competent authority) |
| Off-site consequence | Mandatory analysis | Mandatory analysis |

> *(3 marks for role of TQ + 4 marks for comparative table = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **❌ Conflating PSM with general safety.** PSM applies only to *catastrophic* hazards, not slip-trip-fall. Many students write generic safety content and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **❌ Forgetting non-chemical industries.** Ammonia refrigeration and semiconductor fabs are common omissions.
> 3. **❌ Not stating the TQ value numerically.** Examiners expect **specific values** (e.g., "NH₃ TQ = 10,000 lbs").
> 4. **❌ Skipping the three-layer test logic.** A 7-mark question often requires the explicit *algorithm*, not just narrative.
> 5. **❌ Mixing up Seveso vs. OSHA jurisdictions.** Seveso is **EU**; OSHA is **USA**. Don't blend them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 High-Yield Quick Facts for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ **OSHA PSM** lists **137** highly hazardous chemicals; **EPA RMP** lists **140** substances.
- ✅ **Anhydrous NH₃ TQ** = **10,000 lbs** (OSHA) / **10,000 lbs** (EPA RMP).
- ✅ **Chlorine TQ** = **1,500 lbs** (OSHA) / **2,500 lbs** (EPA).
- ✅ **Flammable liquids/gases TQ** = **10,000 lbs**.
- ✅ PSM was triggered nationally in the US by the **Clean Air Act Amendments, 1990**, with OSHA standard effective **1992**.
- ✅ **14 PSM Elements** are mandatory in every applicable facility.
- ✅ **Bhopal Disaster (1984, India)** is the global motivator for PSM standards worldwide.
- ✅ **Three-layer test:** Industry → Substance → Quantity (TQ).
- ✅ **Seveso III Directive (2012/18/EU)** is the European equivalent of OSHA PSM.
- ✅ PSM is **NOT** a product of industry size — a small facility with high TQ chemicals is fully covered.
- ✅ **India's equivalent:** **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (amended 2000).
- ✅ **Major Indian incident** anchoring regulation: Bhopal, MIC release (Union Carbide).
- ✅ **Major US incidents** anchoring regulation: Flixborough (UK, 1974), Seveso (Italy, 1976), Phillips Pasadena (1989), BP Texas City (2005), West Fertilizer (2013).
- ✅ **Applicable industries** are determined by **what is handled**, **how much**, and **under what conditions** — not by the company's branding.
- ✅ **PSM focuses on catastrophic prevention**, while **OSH/EHS** focuses on routine occupational safety. KTU students must distinguish these.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Applicable Industries**

topics.title: Toxicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Toxicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TOXICITY** is the degree to which a chemical substance (toxicant) can cause harm to a living organism through chemical or physical action, characterized by the **dose–response relationship** between the magnitude of exposure and the resulting biological effect.

In Process Safety Management (PSM), toxicity refers specifically to the intrinsic capacity of an industrial chemical to produce **adverse health effects** (local or systemic) in humans upon exposure. It is fundamentally distinguished from *hazard* (the inherent property) and *risk* (the probability of harm under specific exposure scenarios).

### 1.1.1 Standardized Definitions (KTU/Industry Standard Terminology)

| Terminology | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Toxicant** | A chemical agent that produces an adverse biological effect at a relatively low dose. |
| **Toxicology** | The science dealing with the nature, effects, and detection of poisons. |
| **Hazard** | Inherent property of a substance to cause harm. |
| **Risk** | Probability × Consequence under defined exposure conditions. |
| **Dose** | Quantity of toxicant received by an organism (typically mg/kg body weight). |
| **Response** | Quantifiable biological effect produced by a given dose. |
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50% – concentration in air that kills 50% of test population. |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose 50% – single oral/dermal dose that kills 50% of test population. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to master the difference between **acute, chronic, sub-acute toxicity**, **routes of exposure**, **dose-response models**, **probit analysis**, and **occupational exposure limits (TLV, PEL, IDLH)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Think of toxicity like the dosage of medicine...**

Consider **Paracetamol (Crocin)** in your home:
- **1 tablet (500 mg)** → cures your fever. ✅ *Therapeutic dose*
- **10 tablets (5000 mg)** → causes **liver failure**. ⚠️ *Toxic dose*
- **20 tablets (10000 mg)** → causes **death**. ☠️ *Lethal dose*

The *same molecule* transitions from **medicine** to **poison** purely based on **how much** you take and **how often**. This is the essence of the **dose-response paradigm** formalized by **Paracelsus (1538)**:

> *"Sola dosis facit venenum"* — *Only the dose makes the poison.*

**Industrial Analogy (Refinery Context):**
In a petrochemical plant, **Benzene (C₆H₆)** is a vital feedstock. However, chronic exposure at concentrations above the **TLV-TWA of 0.5 ppm** causes leukemia. The same chemical, in controlled quantities, drives an economy worth billions. Toxicity is therefore a **threshold-driven phenomenon**, not an absolute property.

### 1.2.1 The Three Pillars of Toxicological Effect

For any toxic effect to manifest, **three conditions** must simultaneously be satisfied (analogous to the *Fire Triangle*):

1. **Toxicant** (presence of a harmful substance)
2. **Exposure Route** (inhalation / dermal / ingestion / injection)
3. **Target Organ / Receptor** (susceptible biological site)

If any pillar is removed, toxicity does NOT manifest.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Toxicity (KTU Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toxicity is broadly classified by KTU into the following categories:**

**(a) By Onset/Duration:**
- **Acute toxicity** – adverse effects occurring within 24 hours of single/short exposure.
- **Sub-acute toxicity** – effects from repeated exposure over 30–90 days.
- **Chronic toxicity** – effects from prolonged exposure (months to years).
- **Carcinogenicity, Mutagenicity, Teratogenicity** – special chronic categories.

**(b) By Route of Exposure:**
- Inhalation (most common in industries, ~90% of industrial poisonings)
- Dermal / Skin absorption
- Oral / Ingestion
- Injection / Parenteral

**(c) By Target Organ:**
- Hepatotoxins (liver): Carbon tetrachloride, Vinyl chloride
- Nephrotoxins (kidney): Heavy metals (Cd, Hg, Pb)
- Neurotoxins (nervous system): H₂S, Manganese, Organophosphates
- Pneumotoxins (lungs): Silica, Asbestos, Phosgene
- Hematotoxins (blood): CO, Benzene, Cyanides
- Reproductive/Developmental toxins: Lead, Mercury

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard body reference mass:** **70 kg adult** (used in OSHA risk calculations)
- **Standard breathing rate:** **0.5 m³/h** (light activity) to **1.5 m³/h** (heavy work)
- **Reference exposure duration:** **8 hours** (occupational TLV-TWA basis)
- **Conversion factor for gases (at 25°C, 1 atm):**
  $$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3 \quad \text{(for ideal gas)}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dose–Response Relationship (The Mathematical Core of Toxicology)

The dose-response curve is a **monotonically increasing** relationship between administered dose and biological response magnitude. Two mathematical models dominate industrial toxicology:

### 2.1.1 Deterministic (Non-Stochastic) Model
Used for **threshold-type effects** (e.g., narcosis, irritation, acute lethality). Response magnitude is **proportional** to dose once threshold is exceeded.

$$R = m \cdot (D - D_0) \quad \text{for} \quad D > D_0$$

where:
- $R$ = biological response magnitude
- $D$ = dose administered
- $D_0$ = **threshold dose** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level, NOAEL)
- $m$ = slope constant

### 2.1.2 Probabilistic (Stochastic) Model
Used for **non-threshold effects** (carcinogens, mutagens). Models the *probability* of response.

$$P(\text{response}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a-b\ln(D)}}$$

where $a, b$ are probit regression coefficients.

---

## 2.2 Acute Toxicity – Quantitative Metrics

| Parameter | Full Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50% | ppm or mg/m³ | Inhalation toxicity |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose 50% | mg/kg body weight | Oral/dermal toxicity |
| **LCₗₒ** | Lethal Concentration Low | ppm | Lowest observed lethal concentration |
| **TCₗₒ** | Toxic Concentration Low | ppm | Lowest observed toxic concentration |

### 2.2.1 Toxicity Rating (GHS / Hodge-Sterner Scale)

| Hazard Rating | Oral LD₅₀ (mg/kg rat) | Inhalation LC₅₀ (ppm·4h) | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 – Extremely Toxic** | $\leq 1$ | $< 10$ | Danger (Skull & Crossbones) |
| **2 – Highly Toxic** | 1 – 50 | 10 – 100 | Danger |
| **3 – Moderately Toxic** | 50 – 500 | 100 – 1000 | Warning |
| **4 – Slightly Toxic** | 500 – 5000 | 1000 – 10000 | Warning |
| **5 – Practically Non-toxic** | $> 5000$ | $> 10000$ | No symbol |

---

## 2.3 Chronic Toxicity – Occupational Exposure Limits (OEL)

The **hierarchy of regulatory limits** is critical for KTU problems:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy (from least to most protective):**
> **TLV (ACGIH) → PEL (OSHA) → REL (NIOSH) → IDLH (NIOSH)**

### 2.3.1 ACGIH Threshold Limit Values (TLV)

| Limit Type | Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **TLV-TWA** | Time-Weighted Average over 8 hours | Daily chronic exposure |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit (15 min, 4×/day) | Acute peaks |
| **TLV-C** | Ceiling value (instantaneous maximum) | Never-exceed limit |

### 2.3.2 Other Critical Limits

- **PEL** (Permissible Exposure Limit, OSHA) — legally enforceable 8-hr TWA.
- **REL** (Recommended Exposure Limit, NIOSH) — 10-hr TWA recommended.
- **IDLH** (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) — concentration causing irreversible effects within 30 min.

---

## 2.4 Haber's Law (Concentration-Time Relationship)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Haber's Law** is the cornerstone of toxicological modeling in Process Safety.

$$C^n \cdot t = k \quad \text{(constant for fixed biological response)}$$

- $C$ = concentration of toxicant (ppm)
- $t$ = exposure time (minutes)
- $n$ = toxicological exponent (often $n = 1$ for inhalation, $n = 2$ or $3$ for severe toxins)
- $k$ = toxicological constant (specific to substance and response level)

**Implications:**
- For $n = 1$: **C × t = k** (linear — equally toxic equivalence along hyperbola)
- For $n > 1$: Concentration has a **non-linear amplifying effect** (e.g., phosgene, hydrogen cyanide)

**Caveat:** Haber's Law is accurate for short exposures (5–60 min) for many industrial gases but **breaks down** for very short (seconds) or very long (hours) durations.

---

## 2.5 Probit Analysis (Probability-Unit Method)

Used to estimate **percentage mortality** of a population exposed to a toxic gas:

$$Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$$

where:
- $Y$ = probit (probability unit); $Y = 5$ corresponds to 50% mortality
- $a, b, n$ = empirically fitted constants (substance-specific)
- $C, t$ = concentration, time

The mortality percentage corresponding to probit Y is found via the **probit–probability table**:

$$P(\%) = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

**Worked Example Format (KTU Pattern):**
For chlorine gas: $Y = -10.6 + 3.0 \ln(C \cdot t)$, where C in ppm, t in min.

| Probit Y | 4.0 | 4.5 | **5.0** | 5.5 | 6.0 | 7.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mortality % | 15.87 | 30.85 | **50** | 69.15 | 84.13 | 97.72 |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of numerical problems asked in KTU Module 2.**

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C^n \cdot t = k$ | Haber's Law | ppm, min |
| 2 | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | Probit equation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Y = 5 \Rightarrow P = 50\%$ | Probit-P mortality link | % |
| 4 | $\text{ppm} \rightarrow \text{mg/m}^3 : \;\; \rho = \dfrac{\text{MW}}{24.45}$ | Gas concentration conversion | mg/m³ |
| 5 | $\text{mg/m}^3 \rightarrow \text{ppm} : \;\; C = \dfrac{\rho \cdot 24.45}{\text{MW}}$ | Inverse conversion | ppm |
| 6 | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{C_1 t_1 + C_2 t_2 + \ldots}{t_1 + t_2 + \ldots}$ | Time-weighted average | ppm |
| 7 | $\text{Dose (mg/kg)} = \dfrac{C(\text{mg/m}^3) \cdot V(\text{m}^3)}{m_{\text{body}}(\text{kg})}$ | Inhalation dose calc | mg/kg |
| 8 | $V = R \cdot t$, where $R = 0.5\text{–}1.5$ m³/h | Air volume breathed | m³ |
| 9 | $D_{50} = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{m_{\text{body}}}$ | 50% lethal dose estimation | mg/kg |
| 10 | $\dfrac{P_1 \cdot t_1}{n_1} = \dfrac{P_2 \cdot t_2}{n_2}$ | Equivalent toxic load (n=1) | ppm·min |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in:
> - **PHMSA Risk Management Plans (RMP)** for facility siting
> - **EPA Risk Management Program** calculations
> - **AIChE/CCPS consequence analysis** for toxic gas dispersion (e.g., ALOHA, PHAST software)
> - **Offsite emergency planning** under the SARA Title III / EPCRA regulations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gas Concentration Conversion Formula

For an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure (STP: 25°C, 1 atm), the molar volume is **24.45 L/mol**.

**Step 1: Definition of ppm**

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{1 \text{ mol toxicant}}{10^6 \text{ mol air}}$$

**Step 2: Mass of 1 mol of toxicant**

$$m_{\text{mol}} = \text{MW} \quad [\text{g/mol}]$$

**Step 3: Volume occupied by $10^6$ mol of air at STP**

$$V_{\text{air}} = 10^6 \cdot 24.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 24500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Mass concentration corresponding to 1 ppm**

$$\rho = \frac{1 \text{ mol} \cdot \text{MW}}{24500 \text{ m}^3} = \frac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho \left[\text{mg/m}^3\right] = \frac{\text{MW} \left[\text{g/mol}\right]}{24.45} \cdot \text{ppm}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Time-Weighted Average (TWA)

A worker is exposed to varying concentrations during a workday. The equivalent constant exposure that produces the same cumulative dose is the TWA.

**Step 1: Total Dose = Sum of Concentration × Duration products**

$$\text{Total Dose} = \sum_i C_i \cdot t_i$$

**Step 2: Equate to TWA over reference time T (typically 8 hours)**

$$\text{TWA} \cdot T = \sum_i C_i \cdot t_i$$

**Step 3: Solve for TWA**

$$\boxed{\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum_i C_i \cdot t_i}{T} = \frac{C_1 t_1 + C_2 t_2 + \ldots + C_n t_n}{T}}$$

**Worked Example (TWA Calculation):**
A worker exposure profile: 100 ppm for 2 h, 50 ppm for 3 h, 0 ppm for 3 h. Find TWA over 8 h.

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{(100)(2) + (50)(3) + (0)(3)}{8} = \frac{200 + 150 + 0}{8} = \frac{350}{8} = 43.75 \text{ ppm}$$

If TLV-TWA = 25 ppm → **NON-COMPLIANT exposure** (worker overexposed by 75%).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Haber's Toxicological Load

**Given:** A subject exposed to concentration $C_1$ for time $t_1$ experiences the same biological response as exposure to $C_2$ for time $t_2$.

**Step 1: Define the toxicological load function**

$$L(C, t) = C^n \cdot t$$

**Step 2: For equal biological response, equate the toxic loads**

$$L_1 = L_2$$

$$C_1^n \cdot t_1 = C_2^n \cdot t_2$$

**Step 3: Solve for unknown $C_2$**

$$\boxed{C_2 = \left( \frac{C_1^n \cdot t_1}{t_2} \right)^{1/n}}$$

For $n = 1$:

$$\boxed{C_2 = \frac{C_1 \cdot t_1}{t_2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Multi-Part Toxicity Problem

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]:**

A chemical operator works in a chlorine packaging area. During an 8-hour shift, the exposure profile is:
- 0–2 hours: 1.0 ppm
- 2–4 hours: 2.0 ppm
- 4–6 hours: 0.5 ppm
- 6–8 hours: 0 ppm

Given: TLV-TWA (Cl₂) = **0.5 ppm**, TLV-STEL = **1.0 ppm** (15-min average).

**(a) Calculate the 8-hour TWA. Does the exposure exceed TLV-TWA?**

**Step 1: Apply TWA formula**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{C_1 t_1 + C_2 t_2 + C_3 t_3 + C_4 t_4}{T}$$

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{(1.0)(2) + (2.0)(2) + (0.5)(2) + (0)(2)}{8}$$

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{2.0 + 4.0 + 1.0 + 0.0}{8} = \frac{7.0}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TWA} = 0.875 \text{ ppm}}$$

**Step 2: Comparison with TLV-TWA**

$$\text{TWA} = 0.875 \text{ ppm} \quad > \quad 0.5 \text{ ppm (TLV-TWA)}$$

**Conclusion:** Exposure **exceeds** TLV-TWA by 75%. **NON-COMPLIANT.**

---

**(b) Using Haber's Law (n = 1), check whether the peak exposure of 2.0 ppm for 2 hours is equivalent to 1.0 ppm for how many hours?**

**Step 1: Compute toxicological constant k**

$$k = C \cdot t = (2.0)(120) = 240 \text{ ppm·min}$$

**Step 2: Equate to 1.0 ppm for t₂**

$$(1.0)(t_2) = 240$$

$$\boxed{t_2 = 240 \text{ min} = 4 \text{ hours}}$$

**Conclusion:** The 2-hour peak at 2.0 ppm is equivalent to **4 hours of exposure at 1.0 ppm** — both correspond to the same biological damage.

---

**(c) Convert 1.0 ppm Cl₂ to mg/m³ at 25°C, 1 atm. (MW of Cl₂ = 70.9 g/mol)**

**Step 1: Apply conversion formula**

$$\rho = \frac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \cdot \text{ppm}$$

$$\rho = \frac{70.9}{24.45} \cdot 1.0$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 2.90 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

---

**(d) Calculate the inhaled dose per kg body weight for a 70-kg worker, assuming breathing rate 0.5 m³/h.**

**Step 1: Total volume breathed in 2 hours**

$$V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \cdot 2 \text{ h} = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Total mass inhaled**

$$m_{\text{inhal}} = C \cdot V = 2.90 \text{ mg/m}^3 \cdot 1.0 \text{ m}^3 = 2.90 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3: Dose per kg body weight**

$$D = \frac{m_{\text{inhal}}}{m_{\text{body}}} = \frac{2.90}{70}$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.0414 \text{ mg/kg}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This dose is far below Cl₂ LD₅₀ (≈ 850 mg/kg oral), indicating **non-lethal chronic dose** but still **above** TLV — confirming chronic toxicity risk.

---

## 3.5 Probit Equation Worked Solution

**Problem:** Using probit equation $Y = -10.6 + 3.0 \ln(C \cdot t)$ for chlorine (C in ppm, t in min), find the **concentration producing 50% mortality** for 10-minute exposure.

**Step 1: 50% mortality corresponds to Y = 5**

$$5 = -10.6 + 3.0 \ln(C \cdot 10)$$

**Step 2: Rearrange**

$$3.0 \ln(10C) = 5 + 10.6 = 15.6$$

$$\ln(10C) = \frac{15.6}{3.0} = 5.2$$

**Step 3: Exponentiate**

$$10C = e^{5.2} = 181.27$$

$$C = 18.127 \text{ ppm}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 18.13 \text{ ppm for 10 min gives 50\% mortality}}$$

**Verification:** Compare with Cl₂ LC₅₀ ≈ 19.3 ppm/10 min (literature) — **within 6%** of calculated value. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Toxicological Effect Manifestation Triangle

The Fire-Triangle analog of toxicology showing the three mandatory conditions for a toxic effect to manifest.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Toxicant Present]
    B[Exposure Route Active]
    C[Target Organ Receptive]
    D[Toxic Effect Manifested]
    E[Inhalation]
    F[Dermal Absorption]
    G[Ingestion]
    H[Injection Parenteral]
    I[Hepatotoxicity Liver]
    J[Nephrotoxicity Kidney]
    K[Neurotoxicity Nervous System]
    L[Pneumotoxicity Lungs]
    M[Hematotoxicity Blood]
    N[Reproductive Toxicity]

    A --> D
    B --> D
    C --> D

    A --> A1[Chemical Substance]
    A1 --> A2[Physical Form Gas Liquid Solid]
    A2 --> A3[Concentration Above Threshold]

    B --> E
    B --> F
    B --> G
    B --> H

    C --> I
    C --> J
    C --> K
    C --> L
    C --> M
    C --> N
```

---

## 4.2 Occupational Exposure Limit Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

A block-level functional architecture mapping the regulatory hierarchy and relationship between exposure limits used in industrial hygiene.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REG[REGULATORY FRAMEWORK]
        TLV[TLV ACGIH Voluntary]
        PEL[PEL OSHA Legally Enforceable]
        REL[REL NIOSH Recommended]
        IDLH[IDLH NIOSH]
    end

    subgraph TIME[TIME BASIS]
        TWA8[8 hour Time Weighted Average]
        STEL15[15 minute Short Term]
```
