```mermaid
        CEIL[Instantaneous Ceiling]
        IDLH30[30 minute Escape Window]
    end

    subgraph HEALTH[HEALTH EFFECT SEVERITY]
        CHR[Chronic Long Term]
        ACU[Acute Short Term]
        IRR[Reversible Irritation]
        REV[Reversible Narcosis]
        IRR2[Irreversible Damage]
        FAT[Fatality]
    end

    TLV --> TWA8
    TLV --> STEL15
    TLV --> CEIL
    PEL --> TWA8
    REL --> TWA8
    IDLH --> IDLH30

    TWA8 --> CHR
    STEL15 --> ACU
    CEIL --> IRR2
    IDLH30 --> FAT

    CHR --> IRR
    CHR --> REV
    ACU --> IRR2
    ACU --> FAT
```

---

## 4.3 Probit Analysis Computational Pipeline

End-to-end functional block diagram showing how experimental mortality data is processed through the probit model to derive risk assessment outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Experimental Data C t Percent Mortality]
    P2[Step 1 Convert Percent to Probit Y]
    P3[Step 2 Compute ln of C to the n times t]
    P4[Step 3 Linear Regression Y vs ln Cn t]
    P5[Step 4 Extract Constants a b n]
    P6[Step 5 Build Probit Equation Y equals a plus b ln Cn t]
    P7[Step 6 For Given Scenario Substitute C and t]
    P8[Step 7 Solve for Y]
    P9[Step 8 Convert Y to Percent Mortality]
    P10[Step 9 Output Consequence Estimate]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10

    P6 -.Reference.-> P11[Substance Specific Probit Library Cl2 NH3 H2S CO HCN Phosgene]
    P10 --> P12[Risk Management Decision Evacuation Shelter PPE Level]
```

---

## 4.4 Acute vs Chronic Toxicity Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Exposure Duration}
    Q1 -->|Single or less than 24 hr| Q2{Pathway of Exposure}
    Q1 -->|Repeated 30 to 90 days| SC[Sub Chronic Toxicity]
    Q1 -->|More than 90 days| CH[Chronic Toxicity]

    Q2 -->|Inhalation| A1[LC50 Test 4 hour Exposure]
    Q2 -->|Oral| A2[LD50 Test Single Dose]
    Q2 -->|Dermal| A3[LD50 Skin Application]

    A1 --> AR[Acute Response Rapid Onset]
    A2 --> AR
    A3 --> AR

    SC --> SR[Sub Chronic Response Cumulative]
    CH --> CR[Chronic Response Long Latency Carcinogen Mutagen Teratogen]

    AR --> AC[Outcome Reversible Irreversible Lethal]
    SR --> AC
    CR --> AC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A is a *compulsory* short-answer section. Answers should be **40–80 words**, formula-based where applicable.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q1.** Distinguish between **LC₅₀** and **LD₅₀** as used in acute toxicity assessment. State the units for each.

**Model Answer:**
- **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%)** is the concentration of a toxicant in air (or water) that causes death of 50% of the exposed test population within a specified time (usually 4 hours). Units: **ppm** or **mg/m³**.
- **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%)** is the single oral or dermal dose that kills 50% of test animals, expressed per unit body weight. Units: **mg/kg body weight**.
- LC₅₀ relates to *inhalation* exposure; LD₅₀ relates to *ingestion* or *dermal* exposure.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q2.** State **Haber's Law** and explain the significance of the toxicological exponent *n*.

**Model Answer:**
Haber's Law is given by:
$$C^n \cdot t = k$$
where $C$ is the toxicant concentration, $t$ is exposure duration, and $k$ is a constant for a fixed biological response. The exponent **n** quantifies the **non-linearity** of concentration effect:
- $n = 1$: linear (e.g., chlorine, ammonia)
- $n = 2$ or $3$: concentration dominates (e.g., phosgene, hydrogen cyanide)
A higher *n* indicates that **small increases in concentration cause disproportionately larger toxic effects**, justifying stricter exposure controls.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B requires *ONE* question out of two choices (Module Internal Choice). Each question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Show all calculations clearly with appropriate units.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Attempt EITHER A OR B

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the various routes of toxic exposure in industrial settings with one example for each. Discuss the factors that influence the severity of toxic response.**

**Model Solution:**

**Routes of Exposure:**

**(1) Inhalation** — most common industrial route (~90% of poisonings). Toxic gases, vapors, dusts, fumes, and aerosols enter the body through the lungs. 
*Example:* **Chlorine gas** in a leaking storage tank → respiratory irritation, pulmonary edema at high concentrations.

**(2) Dermal / Skin Absorption** — penetration through intact or damaged skin. Depends on substance's lipophilicity, molecular weight, and skin condition.
*Example:* **Benzene** spilled on skin → systemic toxicity including leukemia on chronic exposure.

**(3) Ingestion / Oral** — contaminated food, water, or hand-to-mouth transfer. Common in poor hygiene conditions.
*Example:* **Lead dust** on workers' hands → chronic neurological damage.

**(4) Injection / Parenteral** — accidental puncture (needles, sharp objects under pressure).
*Example:* **Hydrazine** injection through high-pressure leak → severe tissue necrosis.

**Factors Influencing Toxic Response:**

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Dose / Concentration** | Higher dose → more severe response |
| **Duration & Frequency** | Chronic > acute at same dose |
| **Route** | IV > inhalation > oral > dermal (typical potency order) |
| **Age, sex, genetics** | Children/elderly more susceptible; genetic polymorphism |
| **Health status** | Pre-existing liver/kidney disease reduces detoxification |
| **Co-exposure / synergism** | Smoking + asbestos → 50× lung cancer risk |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A process vessel releases ammonia (NH₃) for 30 minutes at an average concentration of 200 ppm. Using Haber's Law with n = 1, calculate:**

**(i) The equivalent exposure time at the TLV-TWA concentration of 25 ppm.**
**(ii) The equivalent concentration for a 2-hour exposure.**
**(iii) Comment on whether the leak is acceptable.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute toxicological constant k**

$$k = C^n \cdot t = (200)^1 \cdot 30 = 6000 \text{ ppm·min}$$

**(i) Equivalent time at 25 ppm:**

$$t_2 = \frac{k}{C_2} = \frac{6000}{25} = 240 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_2 = 240 \text{ min} = 4 \text{ hours}}$$

**(ii) Equivalent concentration for 2-hour (120 min) exposure:**

$$C_2 = \frac{k}{t_2} = \frac{6000}{120} = 50 \text{ ppm}$$

$$\boxed{C_2 = 50 \text{ ppm}}$$

**(iii) Acceptability:**

The leak generates an equivalent exposure of **50 ppm for 2 hours** (or **25 ppm for 4 hours**). 
- **25 ppm for 4 hours = 25 ppm × (4/8) × 8h basis TWA contribution = 12.5 ppm TWA** — well below TLV-TWA of 25 ppm. 
- However, for the 2-hour, 50-ppm segment: **50 ppm exceeds the TLV-STEL of 35 ppm (15-min)** by 43% → **NOT ACCEPTABLE** for that period.
- **Verdict: Engineering controls (ventilation, isolation) are mandatory** to reduce peak concentrations below STEL.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Discuss in detail the dose–response relationship. Explain the terms: NOAEL, LOAEL, ED₅₀, TD₅₀, with neat sketches of a typical dose-response curve.**

**Model Solution:**

The **dose-response relationship** is the quantitative correlation between the magnitude of exposure to a toxicant and the severity/incidence of the resulting biological effect. It is the foundational concept in toxicology.

**Types of Dose-Response Curves:**

**(A) Graded Dose-Response:** Continuous response in a single individual as dose increases (e.g., degree of drowsiness from alcohol).

**(B) Quantal Dose-Response:** All-or-none response in a population — fraction showing effect vs dose (e.g., % mortality vs dose). This produces the classic **sigmoidal curve**.

**Key Parameters on a Quantal Dose-Response Curve:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **NOAEL** | No Observed Adverse Effect Level — highest dose showing no adverse effect. |
| **LOAEL** | Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level — lowest dose producing detectable adverse effect. |
| **ED₅₀** | Effective Dose 50% — dose producing therapeutic/biological effect in 50% of population. |
| **TD₅₀** | Toxic Dose 50% — dose producing toxic effect in 50% of population. |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose 50% — dose killing 50% of population. |
| **Slope** | Steepness of curve; indicates population variability. Steep slope → uniform response; shallow slope → wide variability. |
| **Threshold** | Dose below which no response occurs (for non-carcinogens). |

**Safety / Reference Values Derived:**
- **ADI** (Acceptable Daily Intake) = NOAEL / Safety Factor (typically 100)
- **RfD** (Reference Dose) = NOAEL / Uncertainty Factors

```
Response (%)
   100|                     ***********
      |                  ***
    50|              **
      |           **
      |        **
      |      *
      |    *
      |  *
      |*
    0 *________________________________
      0   LD50  TD50  ED50  NOAEL   Dose →
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**The probit equation for phosgene (COCl₂) toxicity is given by:**
$$Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot t)$$
**where C is in mg/m³ and t is in minutes. Estimate the concentration of phosgene for an exposure of 30 minutes that would result in: (i) 1% mortality, (ii) 50% mortality, (iii) 99% mortality.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify probit-to-percentage conversions**

- $Y = 2.67$ → $P = 1\%$
- $Y = 5.00$ → $P = 50\%$
- $Y = 7.33$ → $P = 99\%$

**Step 2: For each scenario, solve for $C \cdot t$**

**(i) 1% mortality, Y = 2.67:**

$$2.67 = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot 30)$$

$$3.686 \ln(30C) = 21.94$$

$$\ln(30C) = 5.952$$

$$30C = e^{5.952} = 384.8$$

$$\boxed{C = 12.83 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

**(ii) 50% mortality, Y = 5.00:**

$$5.00 = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot 30)$$

$$3.686 \ln(30C) = 24.27$$

$$\ln(30C) = 6.585$$

$$30C = e^{6.585} = 724.5$$

$$\boxed{C = 24.15 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

**(iii) 99% mortality, Y = 7.33:**

$$7.33 = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot 30)$$

$$3.686 \ln(30C) = 26.60$$

$$\ln(30C) = 7.216$$

$$30C = e^{7.216} = 1368.4$$

$$\boxed{C = 45.61 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

**Step 3: Summary Table**

| Mortality % | Probit Y | C·t (mg·min/m³) | Concentration C (mg/m³) for 30 min |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.67 | 384.8 | 12.83 |
| 50 | 5.00 | 724.5 | 24.15 |
| 99 | 7.33 | 1368.4 | 45.61 |

**Conclusion:** A concentration of only **24.15 mg/m³** for 30 min produces 50% fatality — confirming phosgene's status as a **highly toxic** industrial poison (4× more lethal than chlorine on equivalent basis).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in Module 2 Toxicity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Units confusion:** Writing ppm³/m or mg/m³ with wrong power-of-10 conversion. **Always show:** $1 \text{ ppm} = \text{MW}/24.45 \text{ mg/m}^3$.
>
> 2. **Probit misuse:** Forgetting that $Y = 5 \iff P = 50\%$. Many students incorrectly use $Y = 0$ or $Y = 1$ as the reference point.
>
> 3. **Haber's Law exponent n:** Blindly using $n = 1$ for every gas. **Examiner expects** justification of $n$ value (e.g., chlorine ≈ 1, phosgene ≈ 1, HCN ≈ 2).
>
> 4. **TWA vs STEL confusion:** Mixing the two limits. TLV-TWA is **8-hour**, TLV-STEL is **15-minute**. They are **not interchangeable**.
>
> 5. **Body weight assumption:** Failing to state the standard 70 kg adult assumption in dose calculations.
>
> 6. **Sign of probit regression coefficient:** Confusing sign convention. For $Y = a + b \ln(C \cdot t)$, both $a < 0$ and $b > 0$ are typical.
>
> 7. **Routes of exposure:** Writing only "skin, lungs" without chemical-specific examples. **Examiner deducts 1 mark** if examples are missing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the examination hall.**

### 5.4.1 Definition Block
- [ ] **Toxicity** = intrinsic capacity of a chemical to cause harm.
- [ ] **Toxicology** = the science of poisons (study of toxicity).
- [ ] **Paracelsus's Law** — "Only the dose makes the poison."

### 5.4.2 Dose-Response Parameters
- [ ] **LD₅₀** — mg/kg body weight (oral/dermal).
- [ ] **LC₅₀** — ppm or mg/m³ (inhalation, 4-hr standard).
- [ ] **NOAEL** → divided by 100 = ADI (Reference Dose).
- [ ] **Threshold dose** D₀ exists for non-carcinogens only.

### 5.4.3 Routes of Exposure (in order of industrial importance)
- [ ] **Inhalation > Dermal > Ingestion > Injection**
- [ ] Each route has distinct toxicokinetics (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion).

### 5.4.4 Acute Toxicity Scale (Hodge-Sterner / GHS)
- [ ] Grade 1: LD₅₀ ≤ 1 mg/kg (extremely toxic)
- [ ] Grade 2: 1–50 mg/kg (highly toxic)
- [ ] Grade 3: 50–500 mg/kg (moderately toxic)
- [ ] Grade 4: 500–5000 mg/kg (slightly toxic)
- [ ] Grade 5: > 5000 mg/kg (practically non-toxic)

### 5.4.5 Occupational Exposure Limits (OELs)
- [ ] **TLV-TWA** (ACGIH) — 8-hr average — chronic protection.
- [ ] **TLV-STEL** (ACGIH) — 15-min average — acute peaks.
- [ ] **TLV-C** (ACGIH) — ceiling, never exceed.
- [ ] **PEL** (OSHA) — legally enforceable 8-hr TWA.
- [ ] **REL** (NIOSH) — recommended 10-hr TWA.
- [ ] **IDLH** (NIOSH) — 30-min escape without irreversible effects.

### 5.4.6 Haber's Law
- [ ] $C^n \cdot t = k$
- [ ] $n = 1$ for most industrial gases; $n = 2$ or $3$ for potent toxins.
- [ ] Useful for **equitoxic dose** calculations.

### 5.4.7 Probit Analysis
- [ ] $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$
- [ ] **Y = 5 corresponds to 50% mortality.**
- [ ] Common probit constants for major gases (Chlorine, Ammonia, H₂S, CO, Phosgene) must be memorized.
- [ ] Use standard normal tables for probit ↔ percentage conversion.

### 5.4.8 Conversion Formulas (must remember)
- [ ] $\text{ppm} \rightarrow \text{mg/m}^3$: $\rho = \dfrac{\text{MW}}{24.45}$
- [ ] $\text{mg/m}^3 \rightarrow \text{ppm}$: $C = \dfrac{\rho \cdot 24.45}{\text{MW}}$
- [ ] $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{T}$

### 5.4.9 Target Organ Toxicants (Quick Recall)
- [ ] **Liver** — CCl₄, vinyl chloride, aflatoxin
- [ ] **Kidney** — cadmium, mercury, lead
- [ ] **Nervous system** — Mn, H₂S, organophosphates
- [ ] **Lungs** — silica, asbestos, phosgene
- [ ] **Blood** — CO, benzene, cyanides
- [ ] **Reproductive** — lead, mercury, radiation

### 5.4.10 Special Toxicity Categories
- [ ] **Carcinogen** — causes cancer (benzene, asbestos, vinyl chloride)
- [ ] **Mutagen** — causes genetic mutations (radiation, formaldehyde)
- [ ] **Teratogen** — causes fetal malformations (thalidomide, alcohol)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid — Mnemonic for OEL Hierarchy:**
> **"Tom's Pet Rat Is Dying"** = **T**LV → **P**EL → **R**EL → **I**DLH (increasing severity of regulatory limit).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permissible Exposure Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Permissible Exposure Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** is the regulatory, legally enforceable limit on the amount or concentration of a chemical substance, biological agent, or physical hazard (such as noise or radiation) that a worker may be exposed to over a specified reference time period, typically an **8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** workday and a 40-hour workweek. In the United States, PELs are promulgated by the **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** under 29 CFR 1910.1000, while in India equivalent occupational exposure limits are issued by the **Directorate General Factory Advice Service and Labour Institutes (DGFASLI)** and the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Board Definition)**:  
> *"A Permissible Exposure Limit is the maximum airborne concentration of a hazardous substance to which a worker may be exposed, averaged over a defined reference period, such that nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effects."*

### 1.2 Related Occupational Exposure Paradigms

| Acronym | Full Form | Issuing Body | Time Basis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit | OSHA (29 CFR 1910) | 8-hr TWA |
| **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value | ACGIH | 8-hr TWA |
| **REL** | Recommended Exposure Limit | NIOSH | 10-hr TWA |
| **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | NIOSH | 30-min escape |
| **OEL** | Occupational Exposure Limit | Generic (India, EU) | Variable |
| **STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | ACGIH / OSHA | 15-min TWA |
| **C** | Ceiling Limit | OSHA / NIOSH | Instantaneous |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conceptual Distinction (Examiner Favorite Question)**:  
> - **PEL** is *legally enforceable*.  
> - **TLV** is *advisory and based on best-available evidence*.  
> - **REL** is *recommended best-practice guidance* by NIOSH.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Daily Caffeine Cup"

> [!TIP]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cup of Tea Limit**:  
> Imagine you are prescribed to drink **no more than 4 cups of tea per day** to keep your heart healthy. You could drink 8 cups in 1 hour (extreme peak) but if the average over the day stays at 4, you remain "within the limit." The PEL works the same way — a worker might breathe a high concentration for 30 minutes during a reactor charging step, but the **8-hour TWA** must still remain below the PEL. The PEL is therefore an **integrated dose**, not an instantaneous concentration.  

This concept is called **Dose = Concentration × Time (Haber's Law)**, which underpins the entire mathematical framework of occupational hygiene.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Reference Pressure:** $P_0 = \mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$ (1 atm)  
- **Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = \mathbf{25^\circ C = 298.15 \text{ K}}$  
- **Molar Volume at NTP:** $V_m = \mathbf{24.45 \text{ L/mol}}$ (used to convert ppm $\leftrightarrow$ mg/m³)  
- **Standard Units:** **ppm (v/v)**, **mg/m³**, **f/cc (fibers per cubic centimeter)**  
- **Standard Reference Periods:** **8 hours (TWA)**, **15 minutes (STEL)**, **Instantaneous (Ceiling)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Exposure Limits

The discipline of industrial hygiene classifies exposure limits into four functional tiers, each serving a distinct risk-management purpose.

#### Tier 1 — Long-Term Chronic Limits
These govern the cumulative, repeated exposure over a full working day. The defining metric is the **8-hour TWA**.

#### Tier 2 — Short-Term Acute Limits
These protect against brief excursions (e.g., a sudden valve leak) that may cause narcosis, irritation, or CNS depression. The defining metric is the **15-minute STEL**, which must not exceed **4 × TWA-PEL** (and absolutely **never the IDLH**).

#### Tier 3 — Ceiling / Instantaneous Limits
These are the **non-exceedable maxima** at any moment, denoted **C**. Substances such as hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) and chlorine carry ceiling limits to prevent acute fatality.

#### Tier 4 — IDLH (Immediately Dangerous)
The atmospheric concentration above which an unprotected worker would suffer **irreversible health effects or death within 30 minutes**. The IDLH value defines the threshold at which **self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** becomes mandatory.

### 2.2 Conversion Between ppm and mg/m³

The most commonly tested conversion in KTU ESE is the translation between volumetric (ppm) and gravimetric (mg/m³) concentration. This is derived from the **Ideal Gas Law** and the definition of ppm.

The conversion is governed by:

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}
$$

The reverse conversion is:

$$
C_{ppm} = C_{mg/m^3} \times \frac{24.45}{MW}
$$

Where:
- $MW$ = Molecular weight of the substance (g/mol)  
- $24.45$ = Molar volume in litres at $25^\circ C$ and 1 atm

### 2.3 Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Calculation

When a worker's exposure varies during the shift (different tasks, different locations), the **Equivalent Exposure (E)** is calculated as:

$$
E \bigl[ \text{ppm} \bigr] = \frac{C_1 \cdot t_1 + C_2 \cdot t_2 + \cdots + C_n \cdot t_n}{8}
$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = airborne concentration during task $i$  
- $t_i$ = duration of task $i$ in hours  
- The sum $\sum t_i$ need not equal 8 if the unexposed time is treated as $C = 0$

### 2.4 Additive Mixture Formula (Synergistic/Additive Effects)

When multiple substances with **similar toxicological endpoints** are present, OSHA mandates the **additive mixture equation**:

$$
\frac{C_1}{L_1} + \frac{C_2}{L_2} + \cdots + \frac{C_n}{L_n} \leq 1
$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = measured concentration of substance $i$  
- $L_i$ = PEL of substance $i$  

If the sum exceeds 1, the exposure mixture is **non-compliant** even if every individual substance is below its PEL.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The High-Yield Formula Wall (Print and Pin)**:

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Units | Key Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | ppm $\to$ mg/m³ | $C = C_{ppm} \cdot \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ | mg/m³ | $T = 25^\circ C$, $P = 1$ atm |
| 2 | mg/m³ $\to$ ppm | $C = C_{mg/m^3} \cdot \dfrac{24.45}{MW}$ | ppm | Same STP |
| 3 | 8-hr TWA | $E = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{8}$ | ppm or mg/m³ | $E \leq PEL$ |
| 4 | STEL check | $E_{15} = \dfrac{C_{15}}{15} \cdot 15$ | ppm | $E_{15} \leq STEL$ |
| 5 | Additive Mixture | $\sum \dfrac{C_i}{L_i} \leq 1$ | dimensionless | Must be $\leq 1$ |
| 6 | Equivalent TWA for Variable Shift | $E = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | same as $C$ | Compare to PEL |
| 7 | Haber's Law (Dose) | $D = C \cdot t$ | ppm·h | Underpins TWA |
| 8 | NTP Molar Volume | $V_m = 24.45$ L/mol at NTP | L/mol | Use for STP conversions |
| 9 | Ceiling (C) | $C_{inst} \leq C_{limit}$ | ppm | Never exceed |
| 10 | IDLH Fraction | $f = C_{measured} / C_{IDLH}$ | dimensionless | $f \geq 1 \Rightarrow$ SCBA |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hazard Communication (HazCom):** Drives Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS) Section 8 (Exposure Controls / PPE).
- **Ventilation Design:** PELs define the **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** flow rate via the **ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual**.
- **Respiratory Protection Programme:** When engineering controls cannot meet the PEL, the **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)** of a respirator is selected so that $C_{measured} \leq PEL \times APF$.
- **Process Safety Management (PSM):** PELs feed into the **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** and the **Management of Change (MOC)** workflow when new chemicals enter the plant.
- **Permit-to-Work Systems:** Confined-space entry permits require atmospheric monitoring relative to PEL/STEL/IDLH before granting access.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the ppm-to-mg/m³ Conversion

This is the most-tested derivation in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.

**Step 1 — Definition of ppm (volumetric).**
By definition, a concentration of 1 ppm means **1 part by volume of contaminant per 1 million parts of air by volume**. The volume fraction is therefore:

$$
x = \frac{1}{1{,}000{,}000} = 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert volumetric fraction to partial pressure.**
For an ideal gas mixture, the volume fraction equals the mole fraction (Avogadro's Law), and the partial pressure is:

$$
p_i = x_i \cdot P_{total}
$$

At standard atmospheric pressure $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$:

$$
p_i = 10^{-6} \cdot 101{,}325 \text{ Pa} = 0.101325 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to find moles per cubic metre.**
The ideal gas law in molar density form is:

$$
n = \frac{PV}{RT}
$$

For $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$ of gas at $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$ and $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$:

$$
n_{air} = \frac{101325 \times 1}{8.314 \times 298.15} = 40.87 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of contaminant in 1 m³ of air at 1 ppm.**
If the mole fraction is $10^{-6}$, the moles of contaminant in 1 m³ is:

$$
n_{contam} = 40.87 \times 10^{-6} = 4.087 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass.**
The mass in grams equals moles times molecular weight:

$$
m = n_{contam} \times MW
$$

Substituting:

$$
m = 4.087 \times 10^{-5} \times MW
$$

Recognising the prefactor as $\dfrac{1}{24.45}$:

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{MW}{24.45} \times C_{ppm}
$$

**Step 6 — Box the final identity.**

$$
\boxed{C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{MW}{24.45} \times C_{ppm}}
$$

This is the exact equation the KTU examiner expects in a derivation question.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Single-Substance TWA

> **Problem:** A maintenance fitter is exposed to toluene (PEL = 200 ppm 8-hr TWA) as follows:
> - 0–2 h: $\quad 150$ ppm (welding in tank farm)
> - 2–4 h: $\quad 250$ ppm (line breaking)
> - 4–6 h: $\quad 100$ ppm (cleaning)
> - 6–8 h: $\quad 50$ ppm (paperwork in control room)
>
> **Determine compliance status with the OSHA PEL.**

**Step 1 — Express each exposure as a product of concentration and time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 t_1 &= 150 \times 2 = 300 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_2 t_2 &= 250 \times 2 = 500 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_3 t_3 &= 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_4 t_4 &= 50 \times 2 = 100 \text{ ppm·h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the products.**

$$
\sum C_i t_i = 300 + 500 + 200 + 100 = 1100 \text{ ppm·h}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 8 hours (the reference period).**

$$
E = \frac{1100}{8} = 137.5 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with PEL.**

$$
E = 137.5 \text{ ppm} \; < \; 200 \text{ ppm (PEL)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{COMPLIANT}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Examiner Insight:** Even though the fitter was exposed to 250 ppm (above the PEL) for 2 hours, the 8-hour TWA still falls below 200 ppm. This is the practical meaning of "time-weighted" averaging.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — STEL Validation

> **Problem:** Toluene has a 15-minute STEL of 150 ppm (ceiling value, not to be exceeded). During a sample draw, the fitter experiences 175 ppm for 12 minutes. Is the excursion compliant?

**Step 1 — STEL is a 15-minute ceiling. Any 15-min average above 150 ppm is non-compliant.**

**Step 2 — Compute the 15-min equivalent exposure.**

$$
E_{15} = \frac{175 \times 12 + 0 \times 3}{15} = \frac{2100}{15} = 140 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision.**

$$
E_{15} = 140 \text{ ppm} \leq 150 \text{ ppm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{STEL compliant}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse STEL with TWA. STEL is a **15-minute time-weighted average**, not a single instantaneous peak. Four such excursions per day are permitted, separated by ≥ 60 minutes.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Additive Mixture

> **Problem:** In a solvent-blending room, the airborne concentrations are:
> - Acetone $\quad C_1 = 500$ ppm, $\quad PEL_1 = 1000$ ppm
> - MEK $\qquad C_2 = 100$ ppm, $\quad PEL_2 = 200$ ppm
> - Toluene $\quad C_3 = 50$ ppm, $\quad PEL_3 = 200$ ppm
>
> These are CNS depressants (additive effect). Is the mixture compliant?

**Step 1 — Apply the additive mixture formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \frac{C_i}{L_i} &= \frac{500}{1000} + \frac{100}{200} + \frac{50}{200} \\
&= 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.25 \\
&= 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decision.**

$$
1.25 > 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT MIXTURE}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note on the 0.5 Rule (KTU favourite):** When $\sum C_i / L_i$ falls between 0.5 and 1.0, OSHA permits continued operations but mandates **airborne monitoring every 3 months**. Above 1.0, immediate corrective action is required.

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — ppm ⇄ mg/m³ Conversion

> **Problem:** Ammonia (NH₃) has a PEL of 50 ppm. Convert this to mg/m³. Given $MW_{NH_3} = 17.03$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Apply the conversion identity.**

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{MW}{24.45} \times C_{ppm}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{17.03}{24.45} \times 50
$$

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = 0.6965 \times 50 = 34.83 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Box the answer.**

$$
\boxed{C_{PEL,NH_3} = 34.83 \text{ mg/m}^3}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Compliance Matrix

| Scenario | Calculated Metric | Reference Limit | Status | Corrective Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single TWA < PEL | $E = 137.5$ ppm | 200 ppm | OK | Routine monitoring |
| Single TWA > PEL | $E = 215$ ppm | 200 ppm | VIOLATION | Engineering controls / PPE |
| STEL exceeded | $E_{15} = 180$ ppm | 150 ppm | VIOLATION | Procedural / process change |
| Ceiling exceeded | $C_{inst} = 30$ ppm | $C = 25$ ppm | VIOLATION | Immediate evacuation |
| Mixture $\sum C_i/L_i > 1$ | 1.25 | 1.0 | VIOLATION | Substitute / segregate |
| $C \geq 0.1 \times IDLH$ | 30 ppm | $IDLH = 300$ | Action Level | Trigger medical surveillance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Occupational Exposure Limit Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Worker Inhalation Exposure"] --> nodeB["Reference Period Selection"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Which Time Window?"}
    nodeC -->|"Full Shift"| nodeD["8 Hour TWA"]
    nodeC -->|"Short Excursion"| nodeE["15 Min STEL"]
    nodeC -->|"Any Instant"| nodeF["Ceiling Limit C"]
    nodeC -->|"Life Threat"| nodeG["IDLH Threshold"]
    nodeD --> nodeH{"TWA Less than PEL?"}
    nodeH -->|"Yes"| nodeI["Compliant Routine"]
    nodeH -->|"No"| nodeJ["Engineering Control"]
    nodeE --> nodeK{"STEL Less than Limit?"}
    nodeK -->|"Yes"| nodeI
    nodeK -->|"No"| nodeL["Procedural Change"]
    nodeF --> nodeM{"C Less than Ceiling?"}
    nodeM -->|"Yes"| nodeI
    nodeM -->|"No"| nodeN["Immediate Evacuation"]
    nodeG --> nodeO{"IDLH Exceeded?"}
    nodeO -->|"No"| nodeP["SCBA Optional"]
    nodeO -->|"Yes"| nodeQ["SCBA Mandatory Permit To Work"]
    nodeI --> nodeR["Periodic Air Monitoring"]
    nodeJ --> nodeR
    nodeL --> nodeR
    nodeN --> nodeS["Hot Work Permit Rescinded"]
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeR --> nodeT["SDS Section 8 Update"]
    nodeT --> nodeA
```

### 4.2 TWA Computation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskA["Task 1 Concentration C1 and Time t1"] --> sum1["Multiply C1 t1"]
    taskB["Task 2 Concentration C2 and Time t2"] --> sum2["Multiply C2 t2"]
    taskC["Task n Concentration Cn and Time tn"] --> sumn["Multiply Cn tn"]
    sum1 --> total["Sum All Products"]
    sum2 --> total
    sumn --> total
    total --> div["Divide by 8 Hours"]
    div --> eval["Compare to PEL"]
    eval -->|"Below"| ok["Compliant"]
    eval -->|"Above"| ng["Non Compliant Action Triggered"]
```

### 4.3 Mixture Decision Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sample["Air Sample from Zone"] --> ident["Identify Chemical Species"]
    ident --> similar{"Similar Toxic Endpoint?"}
    similar -->|"No"| indep["Independent PEL Check"]
    similar -->|"Yes"| addi["Apply Additive Mixture Formula"]
    indep --> res1{"Each Ci Less than Li?"}
    res1 -->|"Yes"| ok1["Zone Compliant"]
    res1 -->|"No"| fail1["Immediate Engineering Control"]
    addi --> calc["Sum Ci divided by Li"]
    calc --> chk{"Result Less than or Equal to 1?"}
    chk -->|"Yes"| ok2["Mixture Compliant"]
    chk -->|"No"| fail2["Substitute or Segregate Chemicals"]
```

### 4.4 Industrial Hygiene Decision Tree (PEL → PPE Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    meas["Measured Concentration C"] --> ratio{"C divided by PEL"}
    ratio -->|"Less than 0.5"| noPPE["No Respirator Required"]
    ratio -->|"0.5 to 1.0"| apf1["APF 10 Half Face Respirator"]
    ratio -->|"1.0 to 10"| apf2["APF 50 Full Face or PAPR"]
    ratio -->|"10 to 100"| apf3["APF 1000 SCBA Pressure Demand"]
    ratio -->|"Greater than 100"| apf4["APF 10000 Supplied Air SCBA"]
    apf1 --> doc1["Document in HSE Register"]
    apf2 --> doc2["Document Medical Clearance"]
    apf3 --> doc3["Hot Work Permit Rescinded"]
    apf4 --> doc3
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *decision gate* in industrial hygiene practice. KTU board questions may test the logic (not the exact APF numbers), so memorise the **threshold boundaries (0.5, 1.0, 10, 100)** and the **decision sequence** above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Define Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) and Time-Weighted Average (TWA). State the reference period used by OSHA for the PEL.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** is the legally enforceable airborne concentration of a chemical substance to which a worker may be exposed, averaged over a defined reference period, such that nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effects. OSHA defines the reference period as an **8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** within a 40-hour workweek. The TWA is calculated as the integrated exposure:

$$
E = \frac{\sum C_i \, t_i}{8 \text{ h}}
$$

This metric captures the dose (concentration × time) and is the cornerstone of occupational exposure assessment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Distinguish between STEL and Ceiling Limit (C) with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit) | Ceiling Limit (C) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | 15-minute TWA that should not be exceeded | Absolute concentration that must never be exceeded at any instant |
| Averaging | Yes — over 15 min | No — instantaneous |
| Example | Toluene STEL = 150 ppm | Hydrogen Sulfide C = 20 ppm |
| Frequency | Up to 4 excursions/day, ≥ 60 min apart | Always enforced |
| Purpose | Prevent acute effects from short peaks | Prevent instantaneous fatal effects |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the relationship between the concentration of a gaseous contaminant expressed in ppm and that expressed in mg/m³ at NTP conditions. Calculate the mg/m³ equivalent of a PEL of 25 ppm for Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂, MW = 64.07 g/mol).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the starting point.** The ideal gas equation: $PV = nRT$.

**Step 2 — Molar density at NTP.** Using $T = 298.15$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), for 1 m³ of air:

$$
n/V = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298.15} = 40.87 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of 1 ppm in 1 m³.** For 1 ppm, the mole fraction is $10^{-6}$. Moles of contaminant in 1 m³ = $40.87 \times 10^{-6}$. Mass in grams = moles × MW.

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = 40.87 \times 10^{-6} \times MW \times 1000
$$

**Step 4 — Combine constants.** The factor $40.87 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 1/24.45$, hence:

$$
\boxed{C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{MW}{24.45} \times C_{ppm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to SO₂.**

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{64.07}{24.45} \times 25 = 2.6204 \times 25 = 65.51 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Final Answer:** PEL of SO₂ = **65.51 mg/m³**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A worker in a chemical plant is exposed to a mixture of two solvents with similar toxic effects. The measured concentrations and PELs are:**  
- Solvent A: $C_A = 400$ ppm, $L_A = 500$ ppm  
- Solvent B: $C_B = 100$ ppm, $L_B = 150$ ppm  

**Using the OSHA additive mixture formula, determine whether the exposure is compliant. Also compute the percent excess over the permissible mixture index.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the additive mixture criterion.** For substances with additive effects, the mixture is compliant iff:

$$
\frac{C_A}{L_A} + \frac{C_B}{L_B} \leq 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each ratio.**

$$
\frac{C_A}{L_A} = \frac{400}{500} = 0.80
$$

$$
\frac{C_B}{L_B} = \frac{100}{150} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the ratios.**

$$
I_{mix} = 0.80 + 0.6667 = 1.4667
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with 1.0.**

$$
I_{mix} = 1.4667 > 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

**Step 5 — Percent excess.**

$$
\%\text{Excess} = (1.4667 - 1.0) \times 100 = 46.67\%
$$

**Step 6 — Corrective action (KTU expects this).** Engineering controls such as local exhaust ventilation, substitution of one solvent, or administrative controls (job rotation) must reduce at least one term by 46.67% to bring the index back to unity.

**Final Answer:** $I_{mix} = 1.4667$, **46.67% above the permissible mixture index**; **NON-COMPLIANT**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] A process operator works an 8-hour shift in a fertilizer plant with the following exposure profile to ammonia (NH₃, PEL = 50 ppm 8-hr TWA):**  
- 0–1 h: $\; 30$ ppm (routine check)  
- 1–3 h: $\; 60$ ppm (valve gland leak repair)  
- 3–5 h: $\; 45$ ppm (loading area supervision)  
- 5–6 h: $\; 20$ ppm (documentation)  
- 6–8 h: $\; 0$ ppm (lunch break outside the plant)  

**Calculate the 8-hour TWA and comment on compliance. If the PEL is exceeded, by what percentage?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute each $C_i t_i$ product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 t_1 &= 30 \times 1 = 30 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_2 t_2 &= 60 \times 2 = 120 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_3 t_3 &= 45 \times 2 = 90 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_4 t_4 &= 20 \times 1 = 20 \text{ ppm·h} \\
C_5 t_5 &= 0 \times 2 = 0 \text{ ppm·h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the products.**

$$
\sum C_i t_i = 30 + 120 + 90 + 20 + 0 = 260 \text{ ppm·h}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 8 hours.**

$$
E = \frac{260}{8} = 32.5 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with PEL.**

$$
E = 32.5 \text{ ppm} < 50 \text{ ppm (PEL)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{COMPLIANT}
$$

**Step 5 — Percent margin (some questions ask for "how close to the limit").**

$$
\%\text{of PEL} = \frac{32.5}{50} \times 100 = 65\%
$$

The operator is at 65% of the PEL — still 35% below the limit. The valve gland leak should still be repaired preventively, as the 2-hour exposure at 60 ppm may approach the STEL.

**Final Answer:** TWA = **32.5 ppm**, **COMPLIANT** (at 65% of PEL).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the terms IDLH, Action Level, and Ceiling Limit. For H₂S (Hydrogen Sulphide), the OSHA PEL is 20 ppm (Ceiling), the Action Level is 10 ppm, and the NIOSH IDLH is 100 ppm. If a worker's badge dosimeter reads a peak of 80 ppm during a 5-minute upset, classify the event and state the required emergency response actions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the three terms.**

- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** The maximum airborne concentration from which a worker could escape within 30 minutes without irreversible health effects. Above IDLH, SCBA and buddy-system are mandatory.
- **Action Level (AL):** Half the PEL (50% of the OSHA limit). At or above AL, the employer must initiate **periodic monitoring, medical surveillance, and employee training**.
- **Ceiling Limit (C):** The concentration that must never be exceeded at any instant during the workday.

**Step 2 — Classify the 80 ppm 5-minute event for H₂S.**

- $80 \text{ ppm} < 100 \text{ ppm (IDLH)} \Rightarrow$ **Below IDLH** (escape still possible).
- $80 \text{ ppm} \gg 20 \text{ ppm (Ceiling)} \Rightarrow$ **Ceiling VIOLATION**.
- $80 \text{ ppm} \gg 10 \text{ ppm (AL)} \Rightarrow$ **Action Level exceeded by 8×**.

**Step 3 — Required emergency response actions.**

1. **Immediate evacuation** of the affected zone (ceiling limit violated).
2. **Don SCBA** for any responder entering the zone (since peak is within 1× IDLH, full SCBA still recommended).
3. **Trigger Incident Command System (ICS)** and notify the plant safety officer.
4. **Issue a Hot Work / Confined Space Permit rescission** if applicable.
5. **Root-cause analysis** of the upset within 24 h (process safety event).
6. **Medical evaluation** of the exposed worker — H₂S causes olfactory fatigue, so the worker may not detect further leaks.
7. **Calibration check** of H₂S sensors and the badge dosimeter.

**Final Answer:** The event is a **ceiling-limit violation** (8× the action level, 4× the ceiling) and demands **immediate evacuation, SCBA deployment, medical surveillance, and root-cause investigation**.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Using the wrong reference period** (e.g., computing TWA over 12 h instead of 8 h) — full 7 marks lost on Part (a).
> 2. **Forgetting the units of MW** in the ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion (must be g/mol).
> 3. **Confusing STEL with Ceiling** — STEL is a 15-min *average*; Ceiling is *instantaneous*.
> 4. **Mixing up PEL and TLV** in a definition question — PEL is regulatory (OSHA); TLV is advisory (ACGIH).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention 25 °C and 1 atm** when deriving the 24.45 factor — a favourite deduction line.
> 6. **Skipping the corrective action step** in mixture-problem answers — examiners allot 1–2 marks for it.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **PEL** is *legally enforceable*; **TLV** is *advisory*; **REL** is *recommended* (NIOSH).
- The OSHA reference period is **8 h TWA in a 40-h workweek**.
- **STEL** is a 15-min time-weighted average, not a peak. Maximum 4 excursions/day, separated by ≥ 60 min.
- **Ceiling (C)** is a non-exceedable instantaneous concentration.
- **IDLH** is the 30-min escape threshold; at or above it, **SCBA is mandatory**.
- **Action Level = ½ PEL**; above AL, periodic monitoring and medical surveillance are mandatory.
- The mg/m³ ⇄ ppm conversion uses the **Ideal Gas Law** at NTP: $\frac{MW}{24.45}$ is the multiplier.
- 8-hr TWA formula: $E = \sum C_i t_i / 8$.
- **Additive mixture**: $\sum C_i / L_i \leq 1$ for substances with similar toxic endpoints.
- **Haber's Law** ($D = C \cdot t$) underpins the TWA concept — dose is the product, not the peak.
- **Respirator selection** depends on the ratio $C_{measured}/PEL$ and the **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)**.
- Always state **temperature (25 °C)** and **pressure (1 atm)** when the molar volume 24.45 L/mol is used.
- For partial-credit safety in KTU ESE, **show every arithmetic step** and box the final answer.
- **Remember the 0.5 rule**: $0.5 < C/PEL < 1.0$ triggers quarterly air monitoring; $C/PEL > 1.0$ is a violation.
- **ACGIH vs OSHA** for the same substance: ACGIH TLVs are typically **stricter** (lower) than OSHA PELs.
- For **carcinogens**, OSHA often imposes a **"no-exposure" philosophy** rather than a numerical PEL (e.g., benzene, asbestos, formaldehyde).

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Permissible Exposure Limits (PECHT864)**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Physical Data

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Physical Data** in Process Safety Management (PSM) refers to the comprehensive set of **quantitative thermophysical, chemical, flammability, reactivity, and toxicological properties** of substances that are essential for identifying, evaluating, and controlling process hazards. It forms the empirical foundation upon which all downstream safety analyses — such as HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, fire and explosion hazard evaluation, relief sizing, and consequence modeling — are constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT864 Module 2), Physical Data encompasses: (a) **Flammability data** (flash point, fire point, AIT, LFL, UFL, MOC), (b) **Thermal/Phase data** (boiling point, vapor pressure, latent heat), (c) **Reactivity data** (NFPA reactivity, incompatibility matrix), and (d) **Toxicological data** (TLV, PEL, LC₅₀, LD₅₀).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Physical Data** as the **"medical health chart of a chemical."**

When a doctor examines a patient, they first record vital signs — blood pressure, heart rate, temperature — *before* diagnosing a disease. Without this baseline data, no treatment is possible. Similarly, in process safety, you cannot evaluate whether a tank, pipe, or reactor is *safe* until you know the **"vital signs"** of the chemical inside it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Propane Cylinder at Home**
> The sticker on your domestic LPG cylinder lists: *flammability range (2.1% – 9.5% in air)*, *vapor pressure at 40 °C ≈ 17 bar*, *ignition temperature ≈ 470 °C*. These are physical data points that allow the safety engineer to size relief valves, design fire suppression, and write emergency procedures.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Physical Data

The universally referenced physical data parameters in PSM include:

- **Flash Point (FP)** — the lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air near its surface.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — minimum temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously without an external spark.
- **Lower/Upper Flammable Limits (LFL/UFL)** — the concentration range (% by volume in air) over which a gas/vapor is combustible.
- **Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC)** — minimum O₂ level required to support combustion.
- **Vapor Pressure (P<sup>vap</sup>)** — pressure exerted by a vapor in equilibrium with its liquid.
- **Heat of Combustion (ΔH<sub>c</sub>)** — energy released per unit mass (or mole) on complete combustion.
- **Toxicological Thresholds** — **TLV-TWA**, **PEL**, **LC₅₀**, **LD₅₀**.

## 1.4 Authoritative Sources of Physical Data

| Source | Full Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **NFPA** | National Fire Protection Association | Flammability hazard rating (Diamond) |
| **SDS/MSDS** | Safety Data Sheet | Comprehensive 16-section data |
| **DIPPR** | Design Institute for Physical Property Data | Pure component thermophysical data |
| **NIOSH** | National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health | Toxicological exposure limits |
| **AIHA** | American Industrial Hygiene Association | Workplace exposure guidelines (WEELs) |
| **CHRIS** | Chemical Hazard Response Information System | Emergency response data |
| **HSDB** | Hazardous Substances Data Bank | Toxicological & environmental data |
| **CRC Handbook** | Chemical Rubber Company Handbook | General physical constants |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examination answers, always cite the **NFPA 704 Hazard Identification System** when discussing physical data classification.

## 1.5 Why Physical Data is the "First Pillar" of PSM

The process safety hierarchy begins with **DATA → IDENTIFICATION → ASSESSMENT → CONTROL → MITIGATION**. Without accurate physical data:

- Relief valve sizing (API 520/521) becomes erroneous.
- Fire and explosion consequence modeling (using models like TNO Multi-Energy or TNT-equivalent) fails.
- Hazard classification of plant areas (ATEX/IECEx zoning) cannot be done.
- Inerting and purging calculations become unreliable.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Physical Data

Physical data can be systematically organized into the following engineering hierarchy:

### A. **Flammability Data (Fire & Explosion Triangle Leg-1: Fuel)**
- Flash Point (closed-cup & open-cup, e.g., Pensky-Martens)
- Fire Point
- Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)
- Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) — also called Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)
- Upper Flammable Limit (UFL) — also called Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)
- Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) — also called Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC)
- Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)
- Maximum Explosion Pressure (P<sub>max</sub>)
- Maximum Rate of Pressure Rise (dP/dt)<sub>max</sub>
- K<sub>G</sub> / K<sub>St</sub> — deflagration index for gases/dusts

### B. **Thermal & Phase-Equilibrium Data (Leg-2: Energy)**
- Normal Boiling Point (NBP)
- Melting / Freezing Point
- Vapor Pressure vs. Temperature (Antoine / Clausius–Clapeyron)
- Heat of Vaporization (ΔH<sub>vap</sub>)
- Heat of Combustion (ΔH<sub>c</sub>) — Higher & Lower Heating Values (HHV/LHV)
- Specific Heat Capacity (C<sub>p</sub>)
- Thermal Conductivity (k)
- Liquid & Vapor Densities

### C. **Reactivity / Chemical Compatibility Data (Leg-3: Oxidizer-Reactant Interaction)**
- NFPA Reactivity Rating (0–4)
- Chemical incompatibility matrix
- Shock sensitivity (for energetic materials)
- Decomposition temperature
- Polymerization tendency (e.g., for monomers like styrene, acrylates)

### D. **Toxicological Data (Health Hazards)**
- **TLV-TWA** (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average, 8-hr exposure)
- **TLV-STEL** (Short-Term Exposure Limit, 15-min)
- **TLV-C** (Ceiling limit)
- **PEL** (Permissible Exposure Limit, OSHA standard)
- **LC₅₀** (Lethal Concentration for 50% of test population)
- **LD₅₀** (Lethal Dose for 50% of test population)
- **IDLH** (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)

## 2.2 The "Fire Triangle" Connection

Physical data directly parameterizes the three legs of the classic fire triangle:

$$
\text{Fuel Concentration} \in [\text{LFL}, \text{UFL}] \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Flammability Data}
$$

$$
T_{\text{system}} \geq \text{AIT} \quad \text{or} \quad E_{\text{spark}} \geq \text{MIE} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Thermal/Ignition Data}
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 \, \% \geq \text{MOC} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Oxidizer Data}
$$

## 2.3 The NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond

The **NFPA 704 Diamond** is a universal physical-data-driven classification system:

```
        BLUE (LEFT) — HEALTH HAZARD (0–4)
                |
                |
RED (TOP) — ----+----- YELLOW (RIGHT) — REACTIVITY (0–4)
FLAMMABILITY     |
(0–4)            |
        WHITE (BOTTOM) — SPECIAL HAZARDS
                (OX = oxidizer, W = water-reactive, SA = simple asphyxiant)
```

- **4 = Deadly / Extreme**
- **3 = Severe / Serious**
- **2 = Moderate / Hazardous**
- **1 = Slight / Mild**
- **0 = No hazard**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE and MSE papers.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\log_{10} P^{vap} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | **Antoine Equation** for vapor pressure (P in mmHg, T in °C) |
| 2 | $\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | **Clausius–Clapeyron** integrated form |
| 3 | $P_{total} = \sum_i P_i^{vap} \cdot x_i$ | **Raoult's Law** (ideal solution vapor pressure) |
| 4 | $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | **Partial pressure** (Dalton's Law) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{LFL_{mix}} = \sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}$ | **Le Chatelier's Rule** for flammable mixture LFL |
| 6 | $MOC = \dfrac{LFL \cdot (\% O_2 \text{ in air})}{LFL \text{ in pure O}_2} \cdot 100$ | **Minimum Oxygen Concentration** (MOC) |
| 7 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | **Heat release rate** from combustion |
| 8 | $T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\sum \nu_i C_{p,i}}$ | **Adiabatic flame temperature** estimate |
| 9 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | **Equivalence Ratio** (φ = 1/ER) |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{M_{air}}{M_{gas}} \cdot \rho_{air}$ | **Vapor Density** (Air = 1 reference) |
| 11 | $\text{IDLH} \approx 10 \times PEL$ (approximation) | **Quick estimate** of IDLH (not always valid) |
| 12 | $\text{Habitability} = \dfrac{C \cdot t^{n}}{k}$ | **Toxic load model** (Probit form, n ≈ 1 for many chemicals) |

> [!NOTE]
> In tables, we use $\dfrac{a}{b}$ instead of vertical bars to avoid markdown conflicts. For absolute values, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Physical Data

| Application | Required Physical Data |
|---|---|
| **Relief Valve Sizing** (API 520) | ΔH<sub>vap</sub>, C<sub>p</sub>, vapor pressure curve |
| **Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI, Dow)** | ΔH<sub>c</sub>, FP, AIT, MF (Material Factor) |
| **ATEX/IECEx Zone Classification** | FP, AIT, LFL, MOC, vapor density |
| **Consequence Modeling (Pool Fire, BLEVE)** | Boiling point, ΔH<sub>c</sub>, vapor pressure |
| **Toxic Dispersion Modeling (Gaussian Plume)** | LC₅₀, vapor density, release rate |
| **Inerting Design (Nitrogen Padding)** | MOC, LFL |
| **Vent Sizing for Dust Explosions** | K<sub>St</sub>, P<sub>max</sub> |
| **Process Hazards Analysis (PHA)** | All of the above |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Derivation: Clausius–Clapeyron Equation from First Principles

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation relates vapor pressure to temperature and is *the most heavily tested physical data equation* in KTU exams.

**Starting Point:** For a phase equilibrium (liquid ⇌ vapor), the change in Gibbs free energy is zero:

$$
dG_{vap} = dG_{liq} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{vap} \, dP - S_{vap} \, dT = V_{liq} \, dP - S_{liq} \, dT
$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange:**

$$
(V_{vap} - V_{liq}) \, dP = (S_{vap} - S_{liq}) \, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that** $\Delta V = V_{vap} - V_{liq}$ and $\Delta S = \Delta H_{vap}/T$ (entropy of phase change at equilibrium):

$$
\Delta V \, dP = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T} \, dT
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by** $\Delta V$ and rearrange:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}
$$

**Step 4 — Assume** $V_{liq} \ll V_{vap}$ and apply ideal gas law $V_{vap} = RT/P$:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta H_{vap}}{R T^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables:**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate assuming ΔH<sub>vap</sub> constant:**

$$
\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

$$
\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign arises from $\int T^{-2} dT = -1/T$. This final form is the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation, which is **equation #2 in your KTU formula sheet**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Le Chatelier's Rule for Flammable Mixtures

Le Chatelier's rule predicts the **LFL of a multi-component gas mixture** from the LFLs of the pure components.

**Starting Point:** For a gas mixture to be at its lower flammable limit, the heat released by the combustion of the mixture must equal the heat required to ignite the unreacted gas.

For pure component $i$ at its LFL concentration $C_i$ (vol %), the heat released per unit volume of mixture is proportional to $C_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}$.

**Step 1 — Heat balance for mixture at LFL:**

$$
\sum_i y_i \cdot C_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i} = \text{Constant}
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical simplification (assumes similar combustion energies per mole of fuel):**

$$
\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — General form for mixture LFL:**

$$
LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Le Chatelier's rule is **empirical**, not exact. It works well for similar hydrocarbon fuels but fails for highly dissimilar species (e.g., H₂ + CH₄ mixtures). KTU may test the assumption and limitations.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Vapor Pressure via Antoine Equation

**Problem:** Acetone has Antoine constants A = 6.87987, B = 1169.7, C = 204.0 (with P in mmHg, T in °C, valid for 18 °C – 95 °C). Find the vapor pressure at **T = 56.0 °C** (its normal boiling point). Verify the result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:** Antoine equation

$$
\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\log_{10} P = 6.87987 - \dfrac{1169.7}{204.0 + 56.0}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
C + T = 204.0 + 56.0 = 260.0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the fraction:**

$$
\dfrac{1169.7}{260.0} = 4.4988
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract from A:**

$$
\log_{10} P = 6.87987 - 4.4988 = 2.3811
$$

**Step 6 — Take antilog (10^x):**

$$
P = 10^{2.3811} = 760 \, \text{mmHg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Result: P<sup>vap</sup> = **760 mmHg = 1 atm** at 56 °C, which is exactly the normal boiling point of acetone. This is a **self-consistency check** the KTU examiner loves.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: LFL of a Gas Mixture (Le Chatelier)

**Problem:** A gas mixture contains 2.0% propane (LFL = 2.1%), 1.0% n-butane (LFL = 1.8%), and 0.5% n-pentane (LFL = 1.4%) by volume in air. Determine the LFL of the mixture.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write down Le Chatelier's rule:**

$$
LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$
\dfrac{y_{C_3}}{LFL_{C_3}} = \dfrac{2.0}{2.1} = 0.952
$$

$$
\dfrac{y_{C_4}}{LFL_{C_4}} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.8} = 0.556
$$

$$
\dfrac{y_{C_5}}{LFL_{C_5}} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.4} = 0.357
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the terms:**

$$
\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} = 0.952 + 0.556 + 0.357 = 1.865
$$

**Step 4 — Take the reciprocal:**

$$
LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{1.865} = 0.536 \, \%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The mixture becomes flammable at a much lower total concentration than any individual component. This is a key insight in refinery vapor cloud safety.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU student error is to **add the LFLs arithmetically** (which gives 1.43%) instead of using the harmonic-mean Le Chatelier form. Always use the **reciprocal sum**.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC)

**Problem:** Methane has LFL in air = 5.0% and LFL in pure O₂ = 5.0% (since LFL is independent of oxygen content above MOC). Calculate the **MOC** for methane.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$
MOC = \dfrac{LFL_{air} \cdot 21\%}{LFL_{O_2}} \times 100
$$

For methane, both LFLs are essentially the same (≈5%), giving:

$$
MOC = \dfrac{5.0 \times 21}{5.0} = 12\% \, O_2
$$

**Step 2 — Refined formula (more accurate):**

$$
MOC = LFL_{air} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Volume \% O}_2 \text{ in air}}{\text{Volume \% O}_2 \text{ corresponding to LFL in pure O}_2} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For most hydrocarbons, **MOC ≈ 10–12%**. Inerting systems are designed to keep O₂ < MOC with a **safety factor of 2%**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Physical Data → Safety Decision

| Property Measured | Engineering Decision Triggered | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| FP < 37.8 °C (100 °F) | Class I flammable liquid; explosion-proof electrical | NFPA 30 / NEC 500 |
| LFL known | Determine ventilation rate (6 air changes/hr min) | NFPA 30 |
| AIT known | Set max process temperature limits; use hot-work permits | API RP 2210 |
| MOC known | Size nitrogen inerting system | NFPA 69 |
| LC₅₀ known | Plan evacuation radius; select PPE | NIOSH Pocket Guide |
| ΔH<sub>c</sub> known | Size fire protection (deluge, foam) | API 2021 |
| Vapor density > 1 | Low-area vapor accumulation; mechanical ventilation | NFPA 30 |
