# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Physical Data. List the four major categories of physical data used in process safety management. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Physical Data refers to the comprehensive set of thermophysical, flammability, reactivity, and toxicological properties of chemicals that form the empirical basis for hazard identification, risk assessment, and the design of safety systems in chemical process industries.

**Four Major Categories (2 marks):**

1. **Flammability Data** — Flash Point, Fire Point, AIT, LFL, UFL, MOC, MIE.
2. **Thermal / Phase-Equilibrium Data** — Boiling Point, Vapor Pressure, ΔH<sub>vap</sub>, ΔH<sub>c</sub>, C<sub>p</sub>.
3. **Reactivity / Compatibility Data** — NFPA reactivity rating, chemical incompatibility matrix, decomposition temperature.
4. **Toxicological Data** — TLV, PEL, LC₅₀, LD₅₀, IDLH.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Flash Point and Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT). **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flash Point (FP) | Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air near its surface, **with an external ignition source** | Minimum temperature at which the substance ignites **spontaneously** without any external flame or spark |
| **External Spark Needed?** | **Yes** | **No** |
| **Apparatus Used** | Pensky-Martens / Tag closed-cup, Cleveland open-cup | Auto-ignition apparatus (per ASTM E659) |
| **Typical Values (n-hexane)** | ≈ −22 °C | ≈ 225 °C |
| **Significance** | Indicates fire risk during storage & handling | Indicates thermal runaway & hot-surface ignition risk |
| **Relation to Process Safety** | Determines classification as Class I/II/III flammable liquid (NFPA 30) | Determines maximum allowable process temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> Flash Point is **always lower** than AIT for a given substance. This relationship is a **favourite KTU conceptual question**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive Problem on Vapor Pressure and Flammability **[14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2, Q Choice-1]**

A small storage tank contains **acetone** at **35 °C**. Acetone's Antoine constants are: **A = 6.87987, B = 1169.7, C = 204.0** (P in mmHg, T in °C). Acetone's LFL = 2.6% and UFL = 12.8% by volume in air. The tank has a vented head space of 2 m³, and the liquid volume is 5 m³.

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the vapor pressure of acetone at 35 °C and the mass of acetone vapor present in the headspace. (M = 58.08 g/mol, R = 62.36 mmHg·L/(mol·K), T = 308.15 K).

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] If the headspace is suddenly vented into a small enclosed room of dimensions 4 m × 5 m × 3 m, determine whether the room atmosphere becomes flammable. Assume ideal mixing and that air temperature = 35 °C.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Antoine equation at T = 35 °C:**

$$
\log_{10} P = 6.87987 - \dfrac{1169.7}{204.0 + 35.0}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:** $204.0 + 35.0 = 239.0$

**Step 3 — Compute fraction:** $1169.7 / 239.0 = 4.8941$

**Step 4 — Subtract from A:**

$$
\log_{10} P = 6.87987 - 4.8941 = 1.9858
$$

**Step 5 — Take antilog:**

$$
P = 10^{1.9858} = 96.7 \, \text{mmHg} \approx 96.7 / 760 = 0.1272 \, \text{atm}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate moles of vapor in headspace using ideal gas law:** $PV = nRT$

Convert P to atm: 0.1272 atm; V = 2000 L; R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K); T = 308.15 K.

$$
n = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{0.1272 \times 2000}{0.0821 \times 308.15}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{254.4}{25.30} = 10.06 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate mass:**

$$
m = n \cdot M = 10.06 \times 58.08 = 584.3 \, \text{g} \approx 0.584 \, \text{kg}
$$

> **Answer (a):** P<sup>vap</sup> = **96.7 mmHg (0.127 atm)**; m<sub>vapor</sub> = **0.584 kg**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Volume of room:** V<sub>room</sub> = 4 × 5 × 3 = 60 m³ = 60,000 L

**Step 2 — Concentration of acetone vapor in room after release:**

$$
\text{Concentration (ppm)} = \dfrac{n}{n_{total}} \times 10^6
$$

Total moles of air in room:

$$
n_{air} = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{1 \times 60000}{0.0821 \times 308.15} = 2371.5 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of acetone in room** (assuming all vapor escapes): n<sub>acetone</sub> = 10.06 mol

**Step 4 — Volume percent:**

$$
\% \, \text{acetone} = \dfrac{10.06}{2371.5 + 10.06} \times 100 \approx 0.422\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with LFL:**

$$
0.422\% < 2.6\% \, (\text{LFL})
$$

**Conclusion:** The concentration is **well below the LFL**. The room atmosphere is **NOT flammable** (under ideal mixing assumption).

> **Answer (b):** Vapor concentration = **0.42%**; **not within flammability range**; **safe**.

---

### Question B: Toxicological Data & Consequence Analysis **[14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Q Choice-2]**

A **1000 kg** tank of **chlorine** (Cl₂) ruptures catastrophically in an open plant area. The following physical data is available for Cl₂:

- **Molecular weight (M):** 70.9 g/mol
- **Boiling point:** −34 °C
- **LC₅₀ (rat, 1-hr inhalation):** 400 ppm
- **IDLH:** 10 ppm
- **TLV-TWA:** 0.5 ppm
- **Vapor density relative to air:** 2.5

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the total volume of Cl₂ gas released at 25 °C and 1 atm, and the maximum airborne concentration (in ppm) at the rupture point assuming instantaneous evaporation and dispersion in a hemisphere of radius 50 m.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] Using the Pasquill-Gifford dispersion model assumption, determine the **toxic load (Haber's Law)** for a worker standing 100 m downwind for 10 minutes if the average concentration at that distance is estimated to be **1/100th** of the source concentration. Comment on the health hazard.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Moles of Cl₂ released:**

$$
n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \, \text{g}}{70.9 \, \text{g/mol}} = 14{,}104 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of Cl₂ gas at 25 °C, 1 atm:**

$$
V = \dfrac{nRT}{P} = \dfrac{14104 \times 0.0821 \times 298.15}{1}
$$

$$
V = 345{,}000 \, \text{L} = 345 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Hemispherical dispersion volume at radius 50 m:**

$$
V_{hemi} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3 = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi (50)^3 = 261{,}799 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Average concentration (vol/vol):**

$$
C = \dfrac{V_{Cl_2}}{V_{hemi}} = \dfrac{345}{261799} = 1.318 \times 10^{-3} = 1318 \, \text{ppm}
$$

> **Answer (a):** Released gas volume = **345 m³**; Source concentration = **~1318 ppm**, which is **> 3× LC₅₀** and **> 130× IDLH**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Concentration at 100 m downwind (1/100 of source):**

$$
C_{100m} = \dfrac{1318}{100} = 13.18 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Haber's Law (Toxic Load):**

$$
TL = C^n \cdot t
$$

For Cl₂, empirical exponent n ≈ 1.5 to 2.0 (use n = 1.5 for a moderate estimate).

$$
TL = (13.18)^{1.5} \times 10 = 47.85 \times 10 = 478.5 \, \text{ppm}^{1.5} \cdot \text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with toxicological thresholds:**

- TLV-TWA = 0.5 ppm → exposure is **26×** the safe limit.
- IDLH = 10 ppm → exposure is **1.3×** the IDLH.
- LC₅₀ = 400 ppm for 60 min = 400¹·⁵ × 60 = ~153,000 ppm¹·⁵·min (much higher than our TL).

**Step 4 — Health Hazard Assessment:**

- **Concentration > IDLH** → worker must don SCBA immediately.
- **Concentration > TLV by 26×** → acute respiratory damage likely.
- **Lethal risk**: Low compared to LC₅₀, but irreversible lung injury probable.

> **Answer (b):** Toxic load ≈ **478.5 ppm¹·⁵·min**; Concentration exceeds IDLH and TLV by orders of magnitude → **Severe toxic hazard; SCBA mandatory; immediate evacuation recommended within 200 m radius**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Errors:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** (mmHg vs. atm; ppm vs. mg/m³). Always state units at every step.
> 2. **Forgetting the "n" exponent in Haber's Law.** Many students incorrectly use $C \times t$ only, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing TLV with PEL.** TLV is ACGIH advisory; PEL is OSHA regulatory.
> 4. **Not using R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) consistently** with V in litres and P in atm.
> 5. **In Le Chatelier problems, dividing instead of taking reciprocal of the sum.**
> 6. **Forgetting the hemispherical dispersion assumption** in open-air release calculations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Physical Data" (Module 2)**

### 🔥 Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Physical Data** = Flammability + Thermal + Reactivity + Toxicological data.
- **Flash Point (FP)** < **Fire Point** < **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** (always for any substance).
- **LFL < UFL**; **MOC < 21%** (ambient oxygen).
- **LFL** is *fuel-lean* limit; **UFL** is *fuel-rich* limit.
- **Vapor density > 1** → vapor tends to settle in low areas (hazard for sewers/pits).
- **Vapor density < 1** → vapor rises (hazard for elevated ignition sources).

### 📊 Key Formulas (must-know for ESE)
- **Antoine Equation:** $\log_{10} P = A - B/(C+T)$
- **Clausius–Clapeyron:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$
- **Le Chatelier's Rule:** $LFL_{mix} = 1 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$
- **MOC** derived from LFL in air vs. pure O₂.
- **Haber's Law:** $TL = C^n \cdot t$, with $n \approx 1$ for many chemicals.
- **Heat release:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$.

### 📚 Authoritative Sources (cite in answers)
- **NFPA 704** (Hazard Diamond)
- **NFPA 30** (Flammable Liquids Code)
- **API 521** (Relief Devices)
- **NIOSH Pocket Guide** (Toxicology)
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (PSM Standard)
- **DIPPR Database** (Physical Properties)

### ⚠️ Engineering Judgement Triggers
| If you see... | The safety implication is... |
|---|---|
| FP < 37.8 °C | Class IA/IB flammable liquid → explosion-proof enclosure |
| AIT < 200 °C | Hot surfaces above AIT must be avoided or guarded |
| Vapor density > 1.5 | Low-level ventilation and vapor detectors required |
| IDLH < 100 ppm | Full-face SCBA mandatory; Level A suit |
| ΔH<sub>c</sub> > 30 MJ/kg | Severe fire hazard; deluge systems required |
| LFL < 1% | Extremely flammable; inerting and leak detection critical |

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Frequently-Tested Topics
1. **Antoine equation numerical problems** (very high weightage).
2. **Le Chatelier's rule for gas mixtures** (almost every year).
3. **NFPA 704 diamond** interpretation (conceptual).
4. **Difference between FP, Fire Point, AIT** (3-mark definition).
5. **MOC calculation** and inerting system design.
6. **Haber's Law and Probit analysis** for toxic gas dispersion.
7. **Sources of physical data** (DIPPR, NFPA, NIOSH).

### 🧠 Memory Trick — **"FAT-T"**
The four categories of physical data:
- **F** = Flammability
- **A** = (Thermophysical) **A**nd thermal
- **T** = Toxicological
- **T** = (Reactivity / **T**hermodynamic instability)

> **Final Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always box the final answer with units**. KTU examiners award 0.5–1 bonus mark for clarity in the final answer block.

topics.title: Reactivity Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reactivity Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Reactivity Data** refers to the systematically collected, thermodynamically and kinetically characterised quantitative information that describes how a chemical substance (or a mixture of substances) will undergo chemical transformation under defined process conditions. It encompasses thermal stability, exothermic potential, gas evolution, incompatibility profiles, and kinetic parameters that quantify the rate and severity of unintended or runaway reactions.

In the context of KTU's **Process Safety Management (PECHT864 – Module 2)** framework, reactivity data is a foundational input for:
- Hazard Identification (HAZID) and HAZOP studies
- Inherent Safety Design (ISD) evaluations
- Relief system sizing (API 521 / DIERS methodology)
- Determination of **SADT** (Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature)
- Estimation of **TMR<sub>ad</sub>** (Time to Maximum Rate under adiabatic conditions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):** Reactivity data is the quantified thermochemical and thermokinetic information required to evaluate the potential of a chemical system to undergo uncontrolled, exothermic, or gas-generating reactions that may compromise process integrity, personnel safety, and asset protection.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Angry Beehive" Model

Imagine a **closed beehive** filled with honey bees (the reactive chemicals) and a **smouldering ember** (a small heat input from a pump, friction, or local hotspot).

- **At rest** — the hive is calm, the bees are dormant, and the ember glows gently.
- **A small disturbance** (a poke, a vibration) — the ember heats a few bees. They start moving (low reaction rate).
- **As more bees move**, they generate *frictional heat* (reaction heat), which excites *more* bees — this is a **positive feedback loop**.
- **Eventually** the entire hive erupts violently (runaway reaction, possible explosion).

**Reactivity data** is essentially the **thermal "fingerprint"** of this beehive:

| Beehive Element | Chemical / Process Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Number of bees | Moles of reactive species |
| Bee irritability | Activation Energy (E<sub>a</sub>) |
| Heat from movement | Enthalpy of Reaction (ΔH) |
| Hive ventilation | Heat removal rate (cooling duty) |
| Time to total eruption | Time to Maximum Rate (TMR) |
| Critical ignition temp | Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Pillars of Reactivity Data:**
> 1. **Thermodynamic data** — tells you *IF* and *HOW MUCH* energy will be released (ΔH, ΔU, ΔG).
> 2. **Kinetic data** — tells you *HOW FAST* the energy will be released (rate constants, activation energy, TMR).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics recur throughout reactivity calculations and are **mandatory recall items** for KTU examinations:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Heat of Formation Reference:** ΔH°<sub>f</sub> at **298.15 K** and **1 atm**
- **Adiabatic Temperature Rise Boundary:** ΔT<sub>ad</sub> measured in **K** (dimensionally equivalent to °C difference)
- **TMR<sub>ad</sub> Safety Threshold:** **24 hours** at the process temperature is widely used as the "safe operating limit" for runaway avoidance
- **Decomposition Onset (DSC onset):** Typically determined at a **2 K/min** or **5 K/min** heating rate by **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)**
- **NFPA Reactivity Rating Scale:** **0 (Stable) → 4 (Capable of Detonation)**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** Examiners love asking for the **NFPA instability rating** of common chemicals. Memorise that **Nitroglycerin = 4**, **Acetone = 0**, **Hydrogen Peroxide (91%) = 4**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Reactivity Hazards

Reactivity data is conventionally classified into the following functional families:

1. **Pure Substance Thermal Stability** — Decomposition, polymerisation, condensation reactions
2. **Chemical Incompatibility** — Two or more substances reacting in unintended ways (e.g., acid + base, oxidiser + reducer)
3. **Self-Reactive Substances** — Substances that react with themselves (organic peroxides, nitro compounds, azo compounds)
4. **Water-Reactive Substances** — Substances that liberate heat/flammable gas on contact with water (alkali metals, acid chlorides, calcium carbide)
5. **Air-Reactive Substances** — Pyrophoric and oxidising materials

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic-Kinetic Duality

Every reactive hazard must be evaluated on **two independent axes**:

- **Thermodynamic Axis (Energy Release):** Determines the *maximum potential damage*. Even a slow reaction that releases enormous energy (e.g., nitroglycerin decomposition) is catastrophic if containment is breached.
- **Kinetic Axis (Rate of Release):** Determines the *time available for intervention*. A fast reaction can overwhelm emergency systems even if its total energy release is modest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Rule:** "A reaction is dangerous only when BOTH the energy release is significant AND the kinetics are fast enough to defeat the engineered safeguards." A 500 kJ/mol reaction that takes 1000 years is *less* hazardous than a 50 kJ/mol reaction that takes 0.1 seconds.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Quantities of Reactivity Data

| Symbol | Quantity | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| ΔH°<sub>rxn</sub> | Standard enthalpy of reaction | Net heat released (–) or absorbed (+) at 298 K | kJ/mol |
| ΔH°<sub>f</sub> | Standard enthalpy of formation | Heat absorbed in forming 1 mole from elements | kJ/mol |
| ΔG° | Gibbs free energy change | Maximum non-PV work obtainable; spontaneity indicator | kJ/mol |
| ΔS° | Standard entropy change | Disorder change in the reaction | J/(mol·K) |
| ΔC<sub>p</sub> | Heat capacity change | Sensible heat reservoir in non-isothermal reactor | J/(mol·K) |
| ΔT<sub>ad</sub> | Adiabatic temperature rise | Temperature rise if all ΔH heats the reacting mass | K |

---

## 2.3 Kinetic & Stability Quantities of Reactivity Data

| Symbol | Quantity | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| E<sub>a</sub> | Activation energy | Energy barrier that must be overcome | kJ/mol |
| A (or Z) | Pre-exponential factor | Frequency of effective molecular collisions | s⁻¹ (first order) |
| k(T) | Rate constant | Probability of reaction per unit time | s⁻¹ or L/(mol·s) |
| n | Reaction order | Molecularity of the rate-determining step | dimensionless |
| SADT | Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature | Lowest ambient temp at which self-heating exceeds heat loss | °C |
| TMR<sub>ad</sub> | Time to Maximum Rate (adiabatic) | Time from temperature onset to runaway peak | hours, days |
| T<sub>NR</sub> | Temperature of No Return | Temperature above which TMR < 24 h | °C |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following equations are tested repeatedly in KTU 2024 scheme ESE papers. Memorise the form, the variables, and the units precisely.

| # | Equation | Domain / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ΔH°<sub>rxn</sub> = Σ ΔH°<sub>f</sub>(products) − Σ ΔH°<sub>f</sub>(reactants) | Hess's Law for total heat release |
| 2 | ΔG° = ΔH° − T·ΔS° | Spontaneity criterion; ΔG° < 0 → spontaneous |
| 3 | ΔT<sub>ad</sub> = −ΔH°<sub>rxn</sub> / (C<sub>p,mix</sub>) | Adiabatic temperature rise in closed system |
| 4 | k(T) = A · exp(−E<sub>a</sub> / (R·T)) | Arrhenius rate law |
| 5 | TMR<sub>ad</sub> = (C<sub>p</sub> · R · T²) / (q<sub>0</sub> · E<sub>a</sub>) | Townsend & Tou approach for adiabatic runaway |
| 6 | ln(TMR) = a + b / T | Linearised TMR (T in Kelvin) — used for extrapolation |
| 7 | SADT ≈ T<sub>NR</sub> − 5 K (rule of thumb) | Quick engineering estimate |
| 8 | Pressure rise: ΔP ≈ (n<sub>gas</sub> · R · T<sub>final</sub>) / V − P<sub>initial</sub> | Closed vessel gas evolution |
| 9 | ΔP<sub>max</sub> (bar) ≈ 0.76 · ΔT<sub>ad</sub> (K) | Yoshida's correlation for closed vessel |
| 10 | Heat generation: q = −ΔH · r = −ΔH · k · C<sup>n</sup> | Power per unit volume (W/L) |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula table, **ΔT<sub>ad</sub> uses a negative sign convention** because exothermic reactions have ΔH < 0, but ΔT<sub>ad</sub> is *defined positive* (a temperature rise). Always include the negative sign in the formula and confirm that ΔT<sub>ad</sub> emerges as a positive number for exothermic reactions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reactivity data drives tangible engineering decisions in:

1. **Reactor Design** — Selection of cooling duty, jacket area, reflux condensers, and emergency venting
2. **Storage & Warehousing** — Segregation distances, temperature-controlled warehouses, oxidiser-organic segregation
3. **Transportation Safety** — UN Dangerous Goods classification, "Forbidden" passenger-aircraft cargo lists
4. **Emergency Relief Sizing** — DIERS two-phase flow venting, API 521 fire-case sizing
5. **Process Modification & Inherent Safety** — Active/passive inventory reduction, solvent substitution
6. **Insurance & Regulatory Compliance** — OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119), SEVESO III Directive, Indian Factories Act (MSIHC Rules 1989)

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Reference Pearl:** KTU often pairs this topic with **HAZOP** (Module 1) and **Relief Sizing** (Module 4). When answering questions, explicitly link the reactivity data parameter to its engineering consequence.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat of Reaction by Hess's Law (Hess Cycle)

**Problem Context:** Calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction for the combustion of methane:

$$
\text{CH}_{4(g)} + 2\text{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \text{CO}_{2(g)} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

Given standard heats of formation:

$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{CH}_{4}) = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$
$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{O}_{2}) = 0 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{element in standard state})
$$
$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{CO}_{2}) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$
$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{H}_{2}\text{O}, l) = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

### Step 1: Identify the Hess's Law working equation

The standard enthalpy of reaction equals the sum of formation enthalpies of products minus those of reactants, each weighted by stoichiometric coefficient ν:

$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum_{p} \nu_{p} \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{product}) - \sum_{r} \nu_{r} \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{reactant})
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the stoichiometric weights

There is one mole of CH₄, two moles of O₂, one mole of CO₂, and two moles of H₂O(l):

$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \left[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{CO}_{2}) + 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{H}_{2}\text{O}, l)\right] - \left[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{CH}_{4}) + 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{O}_{2})\right]
$$

### Step 3: Insert the numerical values

$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \left[1 \cdot (-393.5) + 2 \cdot (-285.8)\right] - \left[1 \cdot (-74.8) + 2 \cdot (0)\right]
$$

### Step 4: Compute the products term

$$
1 \cdot (-393.5) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$
$$
2 \cdot (-285.8) = -571.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$
$$
\text{Sum}_{\text{products}} = -393.5 + (-571.6) = -965.1 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the reactants term

$$
1 \cdot (-74.8) = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$
$$
2 \cdot (0) = 0 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$
$$
\text{Sum}_{\text{reactants}} = -74.8 + 0 = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

### Step 6: Subtract reactants from products

$$
\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (-965.1) - (-74.8) = -965.1 + 74.8 = -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

The negative sign confirms the reaction is **exothermic**, releasing 890.3 kJ per mole of methane combusted. This is the canonical textbook value and validates the methodology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative ΔH = Exothermic = Potentially Hazardous.** KTU examiners will deduct marks if the sign convention is not explicitly stated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔT<sub>ad</sub>)

**Problem Context:** For the exothermic reaction above, calculate the adiabatic temperature rise when 1 mole of methane reacts in a system with an effective heat capacity of 100 J/K (approximately 1 litre of water equivalent for simplicity).

### Step 1: Define the working equation

For an adiabatic closed system, all released heat is retained within the reacting mass and raises its temperature:

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{C_{p,\text{mix}}}
$$

where C<sub>p,mix</sub> is the effective (mass-weighted) heat capacity of the reacting mixture.

### Step 2: Convert units consistently

The ΔH<sub>rxn</sub> is in kJ/mol; convert to J/mol:

$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol} = -890{,}300 \text{ J/mol}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and compute

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{-(-890{,}300) \text{ J/mol}}{100 \text{ J/K}} = \frac{890{,}300}{100} = 8903 \text{ K}
$$

### Step 4: Sanity check

This enormous rise (~8900 K) confirms why adiabatic combustion of methane is thermodynamically capable of generating temperatures far exceeding any material's melting point. In reality, the vessel would fail long before reaching this temperature, hence the engineering focus on **relief venting** and **containment strength**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Realisation:** Real ΔT<sub>ad</sub> values for industrial reactions typically range from 50 K to 500 K. The 8903 K value here represents the *ideal* adiabatic ceiling; actual systems have partial heat losses that reduce this figure.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time to Maximum Rate (TMR<sub>ad</sub>) by Townsend-Tou Method

**Problem Context:** A runaway polymerisation has the following kinetic data:
- E<sub>a</sub> = 80 kJ/mol
- A = 1.0 × 10<sup>12</sup> s⁻¹ (first-order)
- C<sub>p</sub> = 2.0 kJ/(kg·K) = 2000 J/(kg·K)
- Mass of reacting mixture, m = 1000 kg
- Heat of reaction ΔH = −50 kJ/mol
- Initial concentration C<sub>0</sub> = 2 mol/L
- Operating temperature T<sub>0</sub> = 350 K

Calculate TMR<sub>ad</sub> at the operating temperature.

### Step 1: Recall the Townsend-Tou equation

For a zero-order or pseudo-zero-order adiabatic runaway, the time to maximum rate is:

$$
TMR_{ad} = \frac{C_{p,\text{eff}} \cdot R \cdot T^{2}}{q_{0} \cdot E_{a}}
$$

where q<sub>0</sub> is the initial volumetric heat generation rate (W/L).

### Step 2: Compute the initial heat generation rate q<sub>0</sub>

For a first-order reaction, the rate r = k·C, and heat generation per unit volume is:

$$
q_{0} = -\Delta H \cdot r_{0} = -\Delta H \cdot A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_{a}}{R T_{0}}\right) \cdot C_{0}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Arrhenius factor

$$
\frac{E_{a}}{R T_{0}} = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \times 350} = \frac{80{,}000}{2909.9} = 27.49
$$

$$
\exp(-27.49) = 1.13 \times 10^{-12}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the rate constant

$$
k = A \cdot \exp(-27.49) = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times 1.13 \times 10^{-12} = 1.13 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the initial reaction rate r<sub>0</sub>

$$
r_{0} = k \cdot C_{0} = 1.13 \times 2 = 2.26 \text{ mol/(L·s)}
$$

### Step 6: Compute the heat generation rate q<sub>0</sub>

$$
q_{0} = -\Delta H \cdot r_{0} = -(-50{,}000) \cdot 2.26 = 50{,}000 \times 2.26 = 113{,}000 \text{ W/L} = 113 \text{ kW/L}
$$

### Step 7: Compute the effective C<sub>p</sub> per unit volume

The mass of 1000 kg of mixture is given. Assume density ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³, so volume V = 1 m³ = 1000 L.

$$
C_{p,\text{eff}} = C_{p} \cdot \rho = 2000 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ J/(m}^{3}\text{·K)}
$$

Convert to J/(L·K): divide by 1000

$$
C_{p,\text{eff}} = 2000 \text{ J/(L·K)}
$$

### Step 8: Apply the Townsend-Tou equation

$$
TMR_{ad} = \frac{C_{p,\text{eff}} \cdot R \cdot T_{0}^{2}}{q_{0} \cdot E_{a}} = \frac{2000 \times 8.314 \times 350^{2}}{113{,}000 \times 80{,}000}
$$

### Step 9: Compute numerator

$$
2000 \times 8.314 = 16{,}628
$$
$$
350^{2} = 122{,}500
$$
$$
\text{Numerator} = 16{,}628 \times 122{,}500 = 2.037 \times 10^{9}
$$

### Step 10: Compute denominator

$$
113{,}000 \times 80{,}000 = 9.04 \times 10^{9}
$$

### Step 11: Final TMR<sub>ad</sub> value

$$
TMR_{ad} = \frac{2.037 \times 10^{9}}{9.04 \times 10^{9}} = 0.2254 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 12: Interpretation

A TMR<sub>ad</sub> of **0.225 seconds** is catastrophically short. There is no time for human intervention or even automated shutdown. This system is at extreme risk of thermal runaway and would be **prohibited** under any KTU-aligned process safety standard.

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** TMR<sub>ad</sub> < 24 hours is the universally accepted "no-return" threshold. Our 0.225 s value is **6 orders of magnitude** below this — the process must be redesigned with dilution, cooling, or substitution.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Determination of Temperature of No Return (T<sub>NR</sub>)

**Problem Context:** Using linearised TMR data, determine the temperature at which TMR<sub>ad</sub> = 24 hours for a system with the following experimentally fitted parameters:

$$
\ln(TMR) = a + \frac{b}{T} \quad \text{with} \quad a = -33.5, \quad b = 12{,}500 \text{ K}
$$

### Step 1: Set the target TMR

For 24 hours:

$$
TMR = 24 \text{ hours} = 86{,}400 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2: Take the natural log of both sides

$$
\ln(TMR) = \ln(86{,}400) = 11.369
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the linearised model

$$
11.369 = -33.5 + \frac{12{,}500}{T}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for T

$$
\frac{12{,}500}{T} = 11.369 + 33.5 = 44.869
$$

$$
T = \frac{12{,}500}{44.869} = 278.5 \text{ K} = 5.35 \text{ °C}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

The Temperature of No Return is **5.35 °C**, which is physically unreasonable for a chemical process — this indicates that either the coefficients are mis-specified, or the reaction is extremely sensitive and requires very low operating temperatures. In practice, this would signal the system needs inherent safety redesign (e.g., dilution, alternative chemistry).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** If your T<sub>NR</sub> falls below ambient, the chemistry itself is the problem — no engineering controls will suffice. This is a classic KTU exam question setter's trick to test your **inherent safety** awareness.

---

## 3.5 KTU 14-Mark Analytical Matrix — Cross-Method Comparison

| Method | Input Required | Output | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) | Sample mass, scan rate | Onset temp, ΔH | Direct measurement | Small sample, no pressure data |
| ARC (Accelerating Rate Calorimetry) | Sample in adiabatic calorimeter | T vs P vs time | True adiabatic data | Expensive, time-consuming |
| TMR<sub>ad</sub> (Townsend-Tou) | E<sub>a</sub>, A, C<sub>p</sub>, ΔH | Runaway time | Quick screening | Assumes zero-order |
| CHETAH (ASTM) | Group contribution, structure | ΔH, flammability limit | Predictive, no test | Less accurate for complex molecules |
| Incompatibility Matrix | Pairwise chemical testing | Heat / gas release on mixing | Practical for storage | Empirical, binary only |
| Reaction Calorimetry (RC1) | Reaction mixture | Heat flow vs conversion | Real-time, scalable | Requires actual materials |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **SADT**. Explain its significance in the safe storage of self-reactive substances. State the typical threshold criterion.

**Model Answer:**

> **SADT (Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature)** is the lowest ambient temperature at which a self-reactive substance undergoes exothermic decomposition at a rate that exceeds the rate of heat dissipation to the surroundings, leading to self-acceleration. It is **significant** because it defines the maximum permissible storage temperature, typically the SADT minus a safety margin of **5 to 10 °C**, beyond which bulk storage becomes unsafe. The standard criterion (UN Test Series H) defines SADT as the temperature at which the time to self-acceleration is **7 days or less**. SADT is determined experimentally by accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) or by heat accumulation storage tests (UN H.1 to H.4).

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between **thermodynamic** and **kinetic** reactivity data with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

> **Thermodynamic reactivity data** quantifies the *total energy change* associated with a reaction, irrespective of how fast it occurs. The key parameter is the **enthalpy of reaction (ΔH<sub>rxn</sub>)**. Example: The standard heat of combustion of methane is −890.3 kJ/mol, indicating high thermodynamic potential for energy release.
>
> **Kinetic reactivity data** quantifies the *rate* at which the reaction proceeds. The key parameter is the **rate constant k(T)**, governed by the **Arrhenius equation** k = A·exp(−E<sub>a</sub>/RT). Example: A first-order decomposition with E<sub>a</sub> = 80 kJ/mol and A = 10<sup>12</sup> s⁻¹ at 350 K has k = 1.13 s⁻¹.
>
> A reaction with high thermodynamic potential but slow kinetics (e.g., diamond → graphite, ΔG < 0) is practically non-hazardous, while a reaction with moderate ΔH but rapid kinetics (e.g., decomposition of benzoyl peroxide) is highly hazardous.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** An exothermic liquid-phase reaction in a 2000 L batch reactor has the following data:

- Reaction: A + B → C
- ΔH<sub>rxn</sub> = −120 kJ/mol
- E<sub>a</sub> = 75 kJ/mol
- A = 2.5 × 10<sup>11</sup> s⁻¹
- C<sub>p</sub> of mixture = 2.5 kJ/(kg·K)
- Density of mixture ρ = 1100 kg/m³
- Initial concentration of limiting reagent C<sub>0</sub> = 3.0 mol/L
- Operating temperature T<sub>0</sub> = 330 K

#### Part (a) (7 Marks) — Calculate the **adiabatic temperature rise (ΔT<sub>ad</sub>)** and the **maximum adiabatic pressure rise** in a closed vessel using Yoshida's correlation.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total moles in the reactor**

$$
n_{\text{total}} = C_{0} \cdot V = 3.0 \text{ mol/L} \times 2000 \text{ L} = 6000 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2: Total heat released**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = -\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot n_{\text{total}} = -(-120{,}000) \times 6000 = 720{,}000{,}000 \text{ J} = 720 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 3: Effective total heat capacity of the reacting mass**

Mass of mixture: m = ρ · V = 1100 kg/m³ × 2 m³ = 2200 kg

$$
C_{p,\text{total}} = C_{p} \cdot m = 2500 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 2200 \text{ kg} = 5{,}500{,}000 \text{ J/K}
$$

**Step 4: Adiabatic temperature rise**

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{C_{p,\text{total}}} = \frac{720 \times 10^{6}}{5.5 \times 10^{6}} = 130.9 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Yoshida's correlation for closed-vessel pressure rise**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{max}} \text{ (bar)} \approx 0.76 \times \Delta T_{ad} \text{ (K)} = 0.76 \times 130.9 = 99.5 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the sign of ΔH when computing ΔT<sub>ad</sub>. Use the **magnitude** of ΔH in the numerator (or include the explicit negative sign as shown). Marks are deducted for inconsistent sign conventions.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks) — Calculate the **TMR<sub>ad</sub>** at the operating temperature. Comment on the safety of the process.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Arrhenius factor**

$$
\frac{E_{a}}{R T_{0}} = \frac{75{,}000}{8.314 \times 330} = \frac{75{,}000}{2743.6} = 27.34
$$
$$
\exp(-27.34) = 1.41 \times 10^{-12}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the rate constant**

$$
k = A \cdot \exp(-27.34) = 2.5 \times 10^{11} \times 1.41 \times 10^{-12} = 0.3525 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the initial heat generation rate per unit volume**

$$
q_{0} = -\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot k \cdot C_{0} = -(-120{,}000) \times 0.3525 \times 3.0 = 126{,}900 \text{ W/L} \approx 127 \text{ kW/L}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the volumetric heat capacity**

$$
C_{p,\text{vol}} = C_{p} \cdot \rho = 2500 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 1100 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 2{,}750{,}000 \text{ J/(m}^{3}\text{·K)} = 2750 \text{ J/(L·K)}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Townsend-Tou equation**

$$
TMR_{ad} = \frac{C_{p,\text{vol}} \cdot R \cdot T_{0}^{2}}{q_{0} \cdot E_{a}} = \frac{2750 \times 8.314 \times 330^{2}}{126{,}900 \times 75{,}000}
$$

**Step 6: Numerator**

$$
2750 \times 8.314 = 22{,}864
$$
$$
330^{2} = 108{,}900
$$
$$
\text{Numerator} = 22{,}864 \times 108{,}900 = 2.490 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 7: Denominator**

$$
126{,}900 \times 75{,}000 = 9.518 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 8: Final TMR<sub>ad</sub>**

$$
TMR_{ad} = \frac{2.490 \times 10^{9}}{9.518 \times 10^{9}} = 0.262 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 9: Safety commentary**

A TMR<sub>ad</sub> of **0.262 s** is orders of magnitude below the **24-hour** safety threshold. The process is **highly unsafe** in this configuration. Recommended inherent safety measures include:
1. **Dilution** with inert solvent to reduce C<sub>0</sub>
2. **Intensified cooling** to suppress temperature rise
3. **Semi-batch** operation with controlled addition of B to A
4. **Online inhibitor injection** as a passive safeguard
5. **Re-evaluation of chemistry** for a less exothermic alternative route

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — students frequently mix J and kJ. Always state the unit at each step.
> 2. **Sign convention** — exothermic reactions must yield a positive ΔT<sub>ad</sub> and a positive TMR<sub>ad</sub>.
> 3. **Forgetting to comment on safety** — KTU values a written engineering conclusion at the end of every numerical answer (1–2 marks reserved).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** A 500 L storage tank contains **acrylonitrile (CH<sub>2</sub>=CH−CN)**, a self-polymerising monomer stabilised with MEHQ (monomethyl ether of hydroquinone). Discuss:

#### Part (a) (7 Marks) — The **reactivity hazards** of acrylonitrile, including polymerisation, decomposition, and incompatibility data, citing at least **four** specific incompatibilities.

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Polymerisation Hazard**
   - Acrylonitrile is a vinyl monomer that polymerises exothermically in the presence of heat, light, peroxides, or a depleted inhibitor.
   - The polymerisation enthalpy is approximately **−78 kJ/mol** of monomer.
   - The auto-acceleration temperature (similar to SADT) for uninhibited acrylonitrile is ~**160 °C**.

2. **Decomposition Hazard**
   - Above 250 °C, acrylonitrile decomposes to release HCN, CO, NO<sub>x</sub>, and other toxic gases.
   - Combustion ΔH ≈ **−1760 kJ/mol**.

3. **Incompatibility Hazards (Four Key Pairs)**
   - **Acrylonitrile + Strong oxidisers (e.g., H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, perchlorates):** Risk of violent polymerisation or explosion.
   - **Acrylonitrile + Strong acids (e.g., H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub>):** Acid-catalysed exothermic polymerisation, runaway risk.
   - **Acrylonitrile + Strong bases (e.g., NaOH):** Hydrolysis with release of ammonia and polymerisation catalysis.
   - **Acrylonitrile + Amines (e.g., aniline, ethylenediamine):** Base-catalysed polymerisation and potential for diazotisation-like side reactions.
   - **Acrylonitrile + Copper / Copper alloys:** Forms explosive copper acetylide-like species; storage vessels must avoid copper fittings.

4. **Stabilisation**
   - MEHQ at **~35–45 ppm** is the standard inhibitor; levels must be monitored and replenished every **6–12 months**.

5. **NFPA Ratings (for context):**
   - Health = **3**, Flammability = **3**, Reactivity = **2**

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks) — Outline a **reactive hazard management plan** for the tank, including storage temperature control, inhibitor monitoring, oxygen exclusion, and emergency response provisions.

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Storage Temperature Control**
   - Maintain tank at **≤ 25 °C**, well below the polymerisation onset.
   - Install redundant temperature transmitters (high-high alarm at **30 °C**, interlock at **35 °C**).

2. **Inhibitor Monitoring**
   - Quarterly sampling for **MEHQ concentration** by HPLC.
   - Re-inhibit when concentration falls below **20 ppm**.

3. **Oxygen Exclusion**
   - Maintain a **nitrogen blanket** of 0.5–1.0 kPa positive pressure on the vapour space to prevent free-radical polymerisation (oxygen acts as a stabiliser at low concentrations but accelerates at high concentrations).

4. **Inert Material of Construction**
   - Carbon steel with internal phenolic-epoxy lining; **no copper or brass** fittings anywhere in the system.

5. **Segregation**
   - Store **away from oxidisers, acids, bases, and amines** per the incompability matrix.
   - Bund wall capacity = **110%** of the largest tank volume (Indian MSIHC / international EPCRA).

6. **Emergency Response**
   - **Spray water deluge** system with flow rate ≥ **10.2 L/min·m²**.
   - **Foam blanket** for spill containment (AFFF alcohol-resistant foam).
   - **Emergency inhibitor injection** (e.g., 1000 ppm MEHQ slug dose) for incipient polymerisation.
   - **Vapour scrubber** for HCN releases (alkaline NaOH scrubber).
   - **Pressure relief** to safe location (away from personnel).

7. **Inspection & Testing**
   - Annual hydrostatic test, ultrasonic thickness testing, RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) every 5 years.

8. **Documentation**
   - **PSM compliance** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119; Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) every 5 years.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often list mitigations but **fail to quantitatively justify** them. Always tie recommendations to a **temperature threshold**, a **concentration threshold**, or a **time interval**. This is where marks are lost in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam — verify you can confidently state and use the following:**

- [ ] **Reactivity data = Thermodynamic (ΔH, ΔG, ΔS) + Kinetic (k, E<sub>a</sub>, A, TMR)** information about a chemical system.
- [ ] **Hess's Law:** ΔH<sub>rxn</sub> = ΣΔH<sub>f</sub>(products) − ΣΔH<sub>f</sub>(reactants), with negative sign indicating exothermic.
- [ ] **Adiabatic temperature rise:** ΔT<sub>ad</sub> = −ΔH / C<sub>p,mix</sub>; gives the worst-case temperature excursion.
- [ ] **Yoshida's correlation:** ΔP<sub>max</sub> (bar) ≈ **0.76 × ΔT<sub>ad</sub> (K)** for closed-vessel runaway.
- [ ] **Arrhenius:** k = A·exp(−E<sub>a</sub>/RT); doubling T increases k dramatically (rule of thumb: 10 °C → k doubles for many reactions).
- [ ] **TMR<sub>ad</sub> (Townsend-Tou):** TMR = (C<sub>p</sub> · R · T²) / (q<sub>0</sub> · E<sub>a</sub>).
- [ ] **SADT = Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature** — practical storage ceiling; safe limit = SADT − 5 to 10 °C.
- [ ] **TMR<sub>ad</sub> = 24 hours** is the **Temperature of No Return (T<sub>NR</sub>)** threshold; below this, runaway is unavoidable.
- [ ] **DSC and ARC** are the two principal experimental techniques; **CHETAH** is the principal predictive method (group contribution).
- [ ] **NFPA instability scale: 0 (stable) → 4 (detonation-capable)**; used in KTU question stems.
- [ ] **Incompatibility matrix** is a binary screening tool for storage and process compatibility.
- [ ] **Inherent safety** principles: minimise, substitute, moderate, simplify — applied when TMR<sub>ad</sub> < 24 h.
- [ ] **Sources of reactivity data:** SDS sheets, NIST WebBook, CHETAH (ASTM), Bretherick's Handbook of Reactive Chemical Hazards, CHEMSAFE.
- [ ] **Differentiate:** SADT (storage temperature) vs TMR<sub>ad</sub> (time to runaway) — common confusion point in KTU questions.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** relief sizing (DIERS), reactor cooling design, storage segregation, HAZOP nodes, transportation classification (UN/DOT).

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic — "HEAT-SICK":**
> - **H**ess's Law → ΔH
> - **E**nergy released (ΔH magnitude)
> - **A**rrhenius (rate constant k)
> - **T**MR<sub>ad</sub> (runaway time)
> - **S**ADT (storage limit)
> - **I**ncompatibility (matrix)
> - **C**alorimetry (DSC, ARC)
> - **K**inetics (E<sub>a</sub>, A)

---

**END OF NOTES — REACTIVITY DATA (MODULE 2, PECHT864)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosivity Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosivity Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Corrosivity Data** refers to the systematically compiled quantitative and qualitative information that describes the rate, type, and severity with which a given material (typically metals and alloys) degrades when exposed to a specific corrosive environment (chemical, thermal, mechanical, or a combination thereof). In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)**, corrosivity data forms the empirical backbone of **Materials of Construction (MOC) selection**, **Remaining Life Assessment (RLA)**, **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** planning, and **Mechanical Integrity (MI)** programs mandated under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 and EPA RMP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *Corrosivity data is the experimentally or historically determined rate at which a material loses its thickness, mass, or mechanical integrity when exposed to a process fluid, gas, or environment under defined conditions of temperature, pressure, velocity, and concentration.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you bought a brand-new steel bicycle and left it out in the salty sea breeze of Kochi during the monsoon. After six months, ugly reddish-brown patches appear, the chain weakens, and the frame becomes brittle. The **salt + moisture + oxygen** combination has chemically "eaten away" the metal.

Now, process engineers face the same problem — but on a far more dangerous scale. They handle **sulfuric acid at 200 °C**, **chlorides at high pressure**, or **hydrogen sulfide in a refinery hydrotreater**. They cannot rely on guesswork. They need a **data table** (analogous to a doctor's medical chart) that says:
*"If you expose Carbon Steel (A106 Grade B) to 98% Sulfuric Acid at 60 °C, it will lose 20 mils of thickness per year (mpy)."*

That numerical "patient chart" is **corrosivity data**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics Used in Corrosivity Data

- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** — typically expressed in **mpy (mils per year)** or **mm/year**.
- **Penetration Rate** — depth of metal loss per unit time.
- **Weight Loss (W)** — measured in **mg** or **g**.
- **Mils** — **1 mil = 0.001 inch = 25.4 µm** (micrometers).
- **pitting factor** = (depth of deepest pit) / (average penetration) — dimensionless.

> [!NOTE]
> The internationally accepted unit for corrosion rate is **mm/year (mmpy)**, but in US-based standards (NACE, API), **mpy (mils per year)** is still dominant. Always state units clearly in KTU answers.

## 1.4 Why Corrosivity Data is a PSM Concern

In chemical process plants, **~30–40% of unplanned shutdowns and major accidents** (ref: NACE International IMPACT reports) originate from corrosion-related failures:

- **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK)** — cyclohexane release; corrosion-fatigue of a bypass line.
- **Texas City Refinery (2005)** — Naphtha splitter corrosion.
- **Chevron Richmond (2012)** — Corrosion of a carbon steel line carrying high-sulfur crude.

Hence, OSHA's MI element specifically requires *"inspection and testing of process equipment … using appropriate methods … based on the corrosivity of the process fluids."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion — Structured Logical Breakdown

Corrosion is broadly classified into **8 industry-recognized forms** (per NACE / Fontana's *Corrosion Engineering*). Each form dictates a different inspection strategy.

### A. Uniform (General) Corrosion
- **Why:** Uniform chemical/electrochemical attack across the entire exposed surface.
- **How:** Predictable; rate measured by weight loss per ASTM G1.
- **Where in PSM:** Wall thinning of storage tanks, atmospheric exposure of carbon steel.

### B. Pitting Corrosion
- **Why:** Localized breakdown of passive film (e.g., chlorides on stainless steel).
- **How:** Small, deep holes; penetration rate at the pit can be **5–10×** the uniform rate.
- **Where in PSM:** Stainless steel heat exchangers handling brine.

### C. Crevice Corrosion
- **Why:** Stagnant solution in gaskets, lap joints, under deposits.
- **How:** Differential aeration / concentration cell formation.
- **Where in PSM:** Tube-tubesheet joints, flanged connections.

### D. Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion
- **Why:** Two dissimilar metals in electrical contact in an electrolyte.
- **How:** The **anode (more active)** corrodes preferentially (Galvanic Series).
- **Where in PSM:** Copper alloy tubes in carbon steel shell.

### E. Intergranular Corrosion
- **Why:** Precipitation of Cr-carbides at grain boundaries in austenitic SS (sensitization at 425–870 °C).
- **How:** Loss of Cr at grain boundaries ⇒ Cr-depleted zones attacked.
- **Where in PSM:** Weld heat-affected zones (HAZ) of 304 SS.

### F. Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Why:** Tensile stress + specific corrosive ion (Cl⁻, OH⁻, H₂S, NH₃) + susceptible material.
- **How:** Brittle cracking at stresses well below yield.
- **Where in PSM:** Caustic embrittlement of boilers, chloride SCC of SS.

### G. Erosion–Corrosion
- **Why:** Mechanical removal of protective film by high-velocity fluid or particulates.
- **How:** Localized attack at elbows, tees, pump impellers.
- **Where in PSM:** Slurry pipelines, two-phase flow lines.

### H. Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)
- **Why:** Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB) producing H₂S.
- **How:** Tubercle formation, deep localized pitting.
- **Where in PSM:** Cooling water systems, dead legs in piping.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 4 equations. They cover ~80% of KTU numerical questions on Corrosivity Data.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate (US units) | **mpy** |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{87600 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate (metric) | **mm/year** |
| 3 | $P = CR \cdot t$ | Total penetration over time $t$ | mils or mm |
| 4 | $P_{allow} = \dfrac{NT - MA}{CR}$ | Remaining life (years) | years |
| 5 | $PF = \dfrac{d_{pit}}{d_{uniform}}$ | Pitting factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $MPY = mmpy \times 39.4$ | Unit conversion | — |
| 7 | $\rho_{metal}$ | Density of metal | g/cm³ |

**Where:**

- $W$ = weight loss (mg for US; g for metric)
- $D$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = exposed area (in² for US; cm² for metric)
- $T$ = exposure time (hours)
- $t$ = service time
- $NT$ = Nominal Thickness (mils/mm)
- $MA$ = Minimum Allowable thickness (mils/mm)
- $d_{pit}$ = deepest pit depth
- $d_{uniform}$ = average uniform penetration

## 2.3 The Galvanic Series (Mini Summary for KTU)

**Anodic (Active — corrode first):** Mg → Zn → Al → Carbon Steel → Cast Iron → 304 SS → Pb → Sn → Cu → Brass → Ni → Inconel → Ti → Graphite → Au

> Cathode (Noble) is protected; **Anode corrodes**. Larger area cathode + small area anode ⇒ catastrophic attack (KTU favourite question).

## 2.4 Sources of Corrosivity Data (Mandatory for KTU)

1. **NACE / AMPP** (formerly NACE International) — Corrosion Data Survey, NACE MR0175 (Sulfide stress cracking).
2. **ASM Handbook Vol. 13 (Corrosion)** — comprehensive metal-environment data.
3. **API 571** — Mechanism-specific damage factors used in RBI.
4. **Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook** — Compatibility tables.
5. **NIST / WRC** bulletins.
6. **Manufacturer data sheets** (e.g., Haynes, Carpenter, Special Metals).
7. **In-house plant data** — Coupon retrieval, UT thickness monitoring, ER probes.

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Corrosion Rate (Engineering Reality)

| Factor | Effect on CR |
|--------|---------------|
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ CR (often doubles per 10 °C, Arrhenius-like) |
| ↑ Concentration of corrodent | ↑ CR (up to a limit, then may passivate) |
| ↑ Velocity | ↑ erosion-corrosion (but may reduce deposits) |
| ↓ pH | ↑ CR (for acids) |
| ↑ Dissolved O₂ | ↑ CR (for neutral aerated systems) |
| Impurities / chlorides | ↑ pitting / SCC risk |
| Inhibitors | ↓ CR (e.g., phosphate, molybdate) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Material Selection:** Choosing between carbon steel, SS 304, SS 316, Monel, Inconel, Hastelloy, FRP.
- **Corrosion Allowance:** Extra thickness added at design stage (e.g., +3 mm for CS in sour service).
- **Inspection Intervals:** RBI programs use CR to schedule NDE.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** ER (Electrical Resistance) probes and LPR (Linear Polarization Resistance) probes feed live CR data.
- **PHA / HAZOP:** Corrosion deviation = causes loss of containment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Corrosion Rate Equation

We want an expression that gives **CR in mils per year (mpy)** from basic measurable quantities: weight loss, density, area, and time.

### Step 1 — Convert weight loss into volume lost

$$V = \frac{W}{D}$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of metal lost (cm³)
- $W$ = weight loss (g)
- $D$ = density (g/cm³)

### Step 2 — Convert volume lost into an equivalent average thickness

Average thickness loss is volume per unit exposed area:

$$d = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $d$ = average thickness loss (cm)
- $A$ = exposed area (cm²)

### Step 3 — Convert cm to mils

Since **1 mil = 0.001 inch**, and **1 inch = 2.54 cm**:

$$1 \; \text{cm} = \frac{1}{2.54} \; \text{in} = \frac{1000}{2.54} \; \text{mils} = 393.7 \; \text{mils}$$

So,

$$d_{\text{mils}} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A} \times 393.7$$

### Step 4 — Normalize to a yearly basis

If $T$ is exposure time in **hours**, then the time per year is $24 \times 365 = 8760$ hours.

$$CR = d_{\text{mils}} \times \frac{8760}{T} = \frac{393.7 \times W \times 8760}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the constant

$$393.7 \times 8760 = 3{,}448{,}812 \approx 3.45 \times 10^{6}$$

But because $W$ in this engineering formulation is taken in **milligrams (mg)** rather than grams, the equation is rewritten as:

$$CR = \frac{534 \cdot W_{mg}}{D \cdot A_{in^2} \cdot T_{hr}} \quad \text{(mpy)}$$

Because $1\,g = 1000\,mg$:

$$\frac{393.7 \times 8760 \times 10^{-3}}{1} \approx 534$$

This is the **standard ASTM G1 / NACE formula** taught universally.

> [!NOTE]
> The metric version is: $CR = \dfrac{87600 \cdot W_g}{D \cdot A_{cm^2} \cdot T_{hr}}$ mm/year, where the constant 87600 absorbs all the unit conversions.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A carbon steel coupon of exposed area 10 in², weighing initially 25.000 g, is retrieved from a hydrocarbon service line after 720 hours. Final weight = 24.750 g. Density of carbon steel = 7.86 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/year**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute weight loss**

$$W = 25.000 - 24.750 = 0.250 \; g = 250 \; mg$$

**Step 2 — Apply the US formula**

$$CR = \frac{534 \times 250}{7.86 \times 10 \times 720}$$

Compute numerator:

$$534 \times 250 = 133{,}500$$

Compute denominator:

$$7.86 \times 10 \times 720 = 56{,}592$$

Therefore:

$$CR = \frac{133{,}500}{56{,}592} = 2.36 \; \text{mpy}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mm/year**

$$mmpy = \frac{mpy}{39.4} = \frac{2.36}{39.4} = 0.0599 \; \text{mm/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to NACE, CR ≤ 1 mpy is "**good**", 1–5 mpy is "**fair**", 5–20 mpy is "**poor**", and > 20 mpy is "**severe**". Here, 2.36 mpy is "fair" — material is acceptable for non-critical service.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Remaining Life Calculation

**Problem:** A pipe has **nominal thickness 8.0 mm**, **minimum allowable thickness 5.0 mm**, operating in a service where CR = 0.4 mm/year. Find the **remaining life** and the **next inspection date** if last inspection was on 1 Jan 2024.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply remaining-life equation**

$$P_{allow} = NT - MA = 8.0 - 5.0 = 3.0 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute remaining life**

$$t_{life} = \frac{P_{allow}}{CR} = \frac{3.0}{0.4} = 7.5 \; \text{years}$$

**Step 3 — Next inspection date**

$$1 \; \text{Jan} \; 2024 + 7.5 \; \text{years} = 1 \; \text{July} \; 2031$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Pitting Factor (Analytical Matrix)

| Service | Avg. CR (mpy) | Deepest pit (mils) | Pitting Factor |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Line A — overhead condenser | 1.2 | 7.2 | 6.0 |
| Line B — reboiler tube | 0.8 | 1.6 | 2.0 |
| Line C — sour water line | 3.0 | 18.0 | 6.0 |

**Inference:** Lines A and C require **closer RBI scrutiny** (PF > 5 = aggressive localized attack). Line B with PF = 2 is acceptable for general monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"If pitting factor is greater than 5, what does it indicate?"* — Answer: **Localized attack, more severe than uniform corrosion; require detailed inspection (e.g., profile radiography, phased-array UT, or intelligent pigging).*

## 3.5 Unit Conversion Reference Matrix

| From | To | Multiply by |
|------|----|----|
| mpy | mm/year | 0.0254 |
| mm/year | mpy | 39.37 |
| inch | mil | 1000 |
| g/cm³ | lb/in³ | 0.0361 |
| in² | cm² | 6.4516 |
| g | mg | 1000 |

## 3.6 Compatibility Decision Flow (Analytical Reasoning)

1. **Identify process fluid** (acid, base, hydrocarbon, water, etc.).
2. **Identify operating conditions** (T, P, concentration, velocity).
3. **Look up corrosivity data** (NACE Corrosion Data Survey, ASM Vol. 13, manufacturer charts).
4. **Compute CR** for candidate materials.
5. **Apply acceptance criteria:** CR ≤ 5 mpy (general), CR ≤ 1 mpy (high-purity/USP water).
6. **Add corrosion allowance** at design stage.
7. **Schedule RBI** based on CR × risk.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Types of Corrosion (Classification Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Corrosion in Process Safety] --> B1[Uniform General]
    A0 --> B2[Localized]
    A0 --> B3[Mechanically Assisted]
    A0 --> B4[Environment Assisted Cracking]
    A0 --> B5[Microbial]

    B1 --> C1A[Atmospheric Oxidation]
    B1 --> C1B[General Acid Attack]
    B1 --> C1C[General Caustic Attack]

    B2 --> C2A[Pitting]
    B2 --> B2B[Crevice]
    B2 --> C2C[Galvanic]
    B2 --> C2D[Filiform]
    B2 --> C2E[Intergranular]

    B3 --> C3A[Erosion Corrosion]
    B3 --> C3B[Cavitation]
    B3 --> C3C[Fretting]
    B3 --> C3D[Impingement]

    B4 --> C4A[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    B4 --> C4B[Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    B4 --> C4C[Liquid Metal Embrittlement]
    B4 --> C4D[Corrosion Fatigue]

    B5 --> C5A[MIC by SRB Sulfate Reducing Bacteria]
    B5 --> C5B[MIC by Iron Oxidizing Bacteria]
    B5 --> C5C[Algal Fouling]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Material Selection Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start: Define Service] --> S1[Identify Process Fluid Composition]
    S1 --> S2[Quantify Operating Conditions T P V Concentration]
    S2 --> S3[Search Corrosivity Data Sources]
    S3 --> S31[NACE Corrosion Data Survey]
    S3 --> S32[ASM Handbook Vol 13]
    S3 --> S33[Manufacturer Data Sheets]
    S3 --> S34[Plant Historical Records]

    S31 --> S4
    S32 --> S4
    S33 --> S4
    S34 --> S4

    S4[Compute CR for Candidate Alloys] --> S5{CR less than 5 mpy}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Select Material and Add Corrosion Allowance]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Upgrade to Higher Alloy or FRP Lining]
    S7 --> S4
    S6 --> S8[Finalize MOC and Issue Isometric]
    S8 --> S9[Schedule RBI Program]
    S9 --> S10[End of Selection Cycle]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Corrosion Monitoring Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Process Pipe or Vessel] --> P2[Metal Loss Sensor Layer]
    P2 --> Q1[Weight Loss Coupons]
    P2 --> Q2[Electrical Resistance ER Probes]
    P2 --> Q3[Linear Polarization Resistance LPR Probes]
    P2 --> Q4[Ultrasonic Thickness UT]
    P2 --> Q5[Hydrogen Probes]
    P2 --> Q6[Intelligent Pig ILI]

    Q1 --> R1[Data Acquisition System]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q5 --> R1
    Q6 --> R1

    R1 --> R2[Corrosion Rate Calculator]
    R2 --> R3{Rate exceeds Alarm Limit}
    R3 -- Yes --> R4[Trigger Inspection / Shutdown]
    R3 -- No --> R5[Continue Monitoring Loop]
    R5 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Galvanic Series Hierarchy (Anode to Cathode)

```mermaid
graph LR
    G1[Magnesium] --> G2[Zinc]
    G2 --> G3[Aluminum Alloys]
    G3 --> G4[Carbon Steel]
    G4 --> G5[Cast Iron]
    G5 --> G6[Stainless Steel 304 Active]
    G6 --> G7[Lead]
    G7 --> G8[Tin]
    G8 --> G9[Copper Brass]
    G9 --> G10[Nickel]
    G10 --> G11[Inconel Monel]
    G11 --> G12[Titanium]
    G12 --> G13[Graphite]
    G13 --> G14[Gold Platinum]

    G1 -.->|Anode Sacrificial| NOTE1[Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection]
    G14 -.->|Cathode Noble| NOTE2[Most Noble: No Corrosion]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Define corrosivity data. Mention any two standard sources from which it is obtained.**

**Model Answer:**

Corrosivity data is the quantitative and qualitative information describing the rate and form of degradation of engineering materials when exposed to a specific process environment. It includes corrosion rate, pitting factor, weight loss, and penetration depth under defined conditions of temperature, pressure, velocity, and chemical concentration.

Two standard sources:
1. **NACE / AMPP Corrosion Data Survey** (industry standard for metals-and-chemicals interaction).
2. **ASM Handbook Volume 13 — Corrosion** (comprehensive, peer-reviewed corrosion data).

> [!NOTE]
> Alternate accepted answers: API 571 Mechanism Atlas; Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook; manufacturer data sheets (Haynes, Special Metals); plant historical coupon data.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**A carbon steel coupon loses 200 mg in 500 hours from an exposed area of 8 in². Density = 7.85 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in mpy and comment on its severity.**

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- $W = 200$ mg
- $A = 8$ in²
- $T = 500$ hr
- $D = 7.85$ g/cm³

Applying:

$$CR = \frac{534 \times 200}{7.85 \times 8 \times 500} = \frac{106{,}800}{31{,}400} = 3.40 \; \text{mpy}$$

**Severity Comment (NACE classification):**
- ≤ 1 mpy — Good
- 1–5 mpy — Fair ✔ (our value: 3.40 mpy)
- 5–20 mpy — Poor
- \> 20 mpy — Severe

The corrosion rate of **3.40 mpy** falls in the **"Fair"** category — material is acceptable for non-critical service, but periodic monitoring is recommended.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the corrosion rate formula in mpy from first principles. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

We derive the **ASTM G1 / NACE** standard equation from basic units.

**Step 1:** Volume of metal dissolved = mass lost ÷ density

$$V = \frac{W}{D}$$

**Step 2:** Average thickness loss (in cm) = volume ÷ area

$$d_{cm} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}$$

**Step 3:** Convert cm to mils using 1 cm = 393.7 mils

$$d_{mils} = \frac{393.7 \cdot W}{D \cdot A}$$

**Step 4:** Extrapolate to per-year basis. Hours per year = 8760

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{393.7 \cdot W \cdot 8760}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

**Step 5:** Combine constants and express $W$ in mg

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \cdot W_{mg}}{D \cdot A_{in^2} \cdot T_{hr}}$$

This is the **standard US corrosion-rate equation**.

**(b) An SS-316 pipe in a chloride service has nominal thickness 10 mm, minimum allowable thickness 6.5 mm, and current measured CR = 0.30 mm/year. Compute the remaining life and the next inspection date. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Corrosion allowance available**

$$P_{allow} = NT - MA = 10 - 6.5 = 3.5 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Remaining life**

$$t_{life} = \frac{3.5}{0.30} = 11.67 \; \text{years} \approx 11 \; \text{years and 8 months}$$

**Step 3 — Inspection scheduling**

If the most recent inspection was **1 June 2024**, the next inspection should be scheduled around **1 February 2036** (i.e., 11.67 years later).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert from **mm/year to mm/11.67 years** or omit the **unit in the final answer**. Always state: "Remaining life = 11.67 years" with **both** the numerical value and units clearly written. Also, do not forget to subtract MA from NT before dividing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain any five types of corrosion with a process-industry example for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (5 of 8 types required):**

1. **Uniform Corrosion** — Even wall-thinning of a CS storage tank roof exposed to acid fumes. CR predictable; mitigated by coating or thicker walls.

2. **Pitting Corrosion** — SS 304 heat-exchanger tubes in chloride-bearing cooling water. Deep, narrow pits; mitigated by upgrading to SS 316 or using lower-Cl⁻ water.

3. **Galvanic Corrosion** — Copper-alloy tubes rolled into a carbon-steel tubesheet of a heat exchanger. The CS tubesheet corrodes where copper tube ends contact it. Mitigated by isolating metals with non-conductive gaskets or using a sacrificial anode.

4. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — Caustic embrittlement of boiler tubes carrying NaOH. Stress from residual welding + conc. NaOH + temperature ⇒ intergranular cracking. Mitigated by stress-relief annealing.

5. **Erosion-Corrosion** — Elbow of a slurry pipeline carrying catalyst fines. Mitigated by using thicker elbows, sweep fittings, or switching to a more erosion-resistant alloy (e.g., high-chromium white iron).

> Any 5 of 8 are acceptable. Each carries ~1.4 marks.

**(b) A coupon retrieval program in a refinery gives the following data for three streams. Identify which stream needs immediate RBI intervention and why. (7 marks)**

| Stream | Material | CR (mpy) | Pitting Factor | Service |
|--------|----------|----------|----------------|---------|
| S-101 | CS A106 | 8.5 | 1.5 | Sour water |
| S-102 | SS 304 | 2.0 | 7.0 | Overhead condenser |
| S-103 | Monel 400 | 0.4 | 1.2 | HF alkylation |

**Model Solution:**

- **S-101 (CS A106):** CR = 8.5 mpy is in the "Poor" range. Pitting factor is low (1.5) ⇒ uniform attack. A high uniform CR means rapid wall-thinning, requiring close monitoring.
- **S-102 (SS 304):** CR = 2.0 mpy appears "Fair", BUT pitting factor = 7.0 indicates **severe localized attack** — pits are 7× deeper than the average. This is the most dangerous scenario: **the bulk metal looks healthy, but isolated pits can perforate the wall abruptly.**
- **S-103 (Monel 400):** CR = 0.4 mpy and PF = 1.2 ⇒ excellent resistance; no concern.

**Conclusion:** **Stream S-102 (SS 304 overhead condenser) needs immediate RBI intervention** because:
- Pitting factor 7.0 indicates aggressive localized corrosion.
- The service is chloride-bearing (overhead condensers in crude units).
- Visible uniform thinning will not be detected by standard UT grid; **phased-array UT, intelligent pigging, or coupon retrieval in critical locations is required.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students pick S-101 (highest CR) — that is a *distractor*. In RBI, **localized attack with high pitting factor is more dangerous than uniform thinning** because it can cause sudden leak-before-break. Marks are lost if you don't compute and compare pitting factors.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Corrosivity data** = rate + type + severity of material degradation; backbone of MI and RBI programs.
- **Two governing equations** to memorize:
  - $CR = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ mpy (US)
  - $CR = \dfrac{87600 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ mm/year (metric)
- **Unit conversions:** 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year; 1 mil = 25.4 µm; 1 inch = 2.54 cm.
- **NACE severity bands:** ≤ 1 = Good; 1–5 = Fair; 5–20 = Poor; > 20 = Severe.
- **8 corrosion types:** Uniform, Pitting, Crevice, Galvanic, Intergranular, SCC, Erosion, MIC.
- **Galvanic rule:** Small anode + large cathode = catastrophic; always insulate dissimilar metals.
- **Pitting factor > 5** ⇒ localized attack; *more dangerous than uniform* even at low CR.
- **Remaining life:** $t = (NT - MA) / CR$.
- **Sources of data:** NACE/AMPP, ASM Vol. 13, API 571, manufacturer datasheets, in-plant coupon/ER/LPR probes.
- **Inspection methods:** Coupon retrieval, UT thickness, ER probes, LPR probes, intelligent pigging, hydrogen probes, radiography.
- **In KTU answers:** Always *state the units*, *show the formula substitution step*, and *interpret the result* (good/fair/poor/severe) for full marks.

> **End of Notes — Corrosivity Data (Module 2)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal and Chemical Stability Data
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal and Chemical Stability Data

# Thermal and Chemical Stability Data — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thermal Stability** of a substance is defined as the resistance of a material to decomposition, degradation, or exothermic reaction when subjected to thermal energy. In the context of process safety engineering, it quantifies the maximum temperature at which a chemical can be stored, handled, or processed without undergoing an uncontrolled runaway reaction, decomposition, or self-ignition.

**Chemical Stability** is defined as the ability of a substance to maintain its chemical identity and resist unwanted chemical reactions (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis, polymerization, corrosion, or incompatibility reactions) when in contact with other chemicals, air, moisture, light, or process materials under specified conditions of temperature and pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (COD – Course Outcome Driver):** A chemical that does **not** undergo an exothermic decomposition of more than **100 cal/g** (≈ **418.4 J/g**) before reaching its boiling point is classified as **thermally stable**. Beyond this threshold, runaway reaction hazard assessment becomes mandatory under KTU-aligned process safety curricula (reference: NFPA 704 / OSHA 1910.1200).

> [!NOTE]
> Both thermal and chemical stability data are foundational inputs to **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**, **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** studies, **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, and **Emergency Relief System (ERS)** design as mandated by **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a **glass bottle of honey** stored in your kitchen cabinet.

- **Thermal Stability Analogy:** The honey bottle can sit on the shelf at room temperature for months without issue. However, if you place it directly on a **stove burner** or in a **microwave** (excessive thermal energy), the sugars inside will caramelize, scorch, or even ignite. The "maximum safe shelf temperature" of honey is its **thermal stability limit**. Similarly, organic peroxides, nitro-compounds, and hydrazine derivatives have very narrow thermal stability windows.

- **Chemical Stability Analogy:** Now imagine pouring that honey into a **rusty iron container** or mixing it with **bleach**. The honey's color changes, it may ferment uncontrollably, or it may release toxic fumes. Honey is **chemically incompatible** with certain metals or strong oxidizers. In a chemical plant, mixing an organic solvent with an incompatible acid/base or oxidizer is a major root cause of incidents — **Bhopal 1984 (MIC + water)** is the canonical industrial example.

Just as a chef knows which ingredients can safely be mixed and at what cooking temperatures, a process safety engineer must know the **thermal** and **chemical stability data** of every material in the plant.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum

In the NEP 2020 aligned outcome-based framework, this topic directly maps to:

| KTU Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** — Analyze thermal/chemical hazards | Analyze (L4) | Interpreting DSC, TGA, ARC curves |
| **CO3** — Apply safety data in PHA | Apply (L3) | Using SADT in storage design |
| **CO5** — Evaluate runaway reaction risks | Evaluate (L5) | Predicting TMR, onset temperature |

---

## 1.4 Standardized Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination):**
> - **Onset Temperature (T_on):** The lowest temperature at which an exotherm is detected by thermal analysis.
> - **Heat of Decomposition (ΔH_decomp):** Energy released per unit mass (J/g or cal/g) during decomposition.
> - **Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT):** Lowest ambient temperature at which self-accelerating decomposition occurs in a commercial package.
> - **Time to Maximum Rate (TMR_ad):** Time interval for an adiabatic runaway to reach the maximum rate.
> - **Activation Energy (E_a):** Minimum energy required to initiate a chemical reaction (kJ/mol).
> - **Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔT_ad):** Temperature increase under adiabatic decomposition conditions.

All numerical values and constants used in this module will employ **SI units**, with US customary equivalents provided where KTU valuation permits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stability Testing Methods

Stability data acquisition follows a hierarchical, **tier-based testing strategy** (aligned with the **CCPS Reactive Chemicals Testing Protocol** and **ASTM E537** standards).

### Tier 1 — Screening Tests (Low sample mass, fast screening)
- **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** — ASTM E537, ISO 11357
- **Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)**
- **Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)**
- **Reaction Screening Device (RSD)**
- **Dewar flask exotherm test**

### Tier 2 — Intermediate Tests
- **Accelerating Rate Calorimetry (ARC)** — ASTM E1981
- **Vent Sizing Package (VSP2)** — Fauske & Associates
- **Phi-Tec II (Sikabend) adiabatic calorimetry**

### Tier 3 — Large-Scale / Realistic Simulation
- **Deflagration/Detonation tests**
- **Large-scale runaway reaction simulation**

---

## 2.2 Core Theory of Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)

In a DSC experiment, a small sample (typically **5–15 mg**) and a reference (inert material) are subjected to a controlled temperature ramp (heating rate **β**, typically **1–20 °C/min**).

The instrument measures the **heat flow (dQ/dt)** required to maintain both the sample and reference at the same temperature.

**Output:** A plot of **dQ/dt (W/g)** versus **T (°C)**, yielding:
1. **Onset temperature** of exotherm
2. **Peak temperature**
3. **Heat of reaction** (area under curve)

The heat of reaction is calculated as:

$$\Delta H = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dQ}{dt} \cdot \frac{dt}{dT} \, dT = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{1}{\beta} \left( \frac{dQ}{dt} \right) dT$$

where:
- **β** = heating rate (°C/min or K/min)
- **dQ/dt** = measured heat flow (W/g)
- **T₁, T₂** = start and end temperatures of the exothermic peak

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery Table — Must memorize for ESE. No external references are permitted during KTU 2024 examinations.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | k in s⁻¹ | Rate constant for decomposition |
| 2 | **Heat of Decomposition** | $\Delta H_{decomp} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (1/\beta)(dQ/dt) \, dT$ | J/g | From DSC integration |
| 3 | **Adiabatic Temperature Rise** | $\Delta T_{ad} = \Delta H_{decomp} / C_p$ | K | Adiabatic runaway severity |
| 4 | **Time to Maximum Rate (TMR) — Townsend & Tou model** | $TMR = \dfrac{C_p \cdot R \cdot T^2}{q_0 \cdot E_a}$ | s or min | Adiabatic induction time |
| 5 | **SADT (Semenov)** | $q_0 = \Delta H \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_{SADT}}$ | W/kg | Self-accelerating decomposition |
| 6 | **SADT (simplified, NFPA)** | $SADT \approx T_{onset} - (\Delta T_{safety})$ | °C | Storage temperature limit |
| 7 | **Frank-Kamenetskii Hot-Spot** | $\delta_{crit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \lambda \cdot R \cdot T_0^2}{\rho \cdot q \cdot E_a}}$ | m | Critical pile size for thermal runaway |
| 8 | **Activation Energy (Kissinger method)** | $\ln(\beta / T_p^2) = \ln(AR/E_a) - E_a / (R T_p)$ | — | Multi-heating-rate DSC |
| 9 | **CHETAH Criterion** | $\Delta H_{decomp} / T_{onset} > $ threshold | J/(g·K) | ASTM CHETAH screening |
| 10 | **TGA Mass Loss** | $\alpha = (m_0 - m_t) / (m_0 - m_\infty)$ | dimensionless | Conversion fraction |

> **Note on table syntax:** Vertical bars `|` in absolute values are avoided (use `\vert` notation if encountered in derivation).

---

## 2.4 Time-to-Maximum-Rate (TMR) — Physical Interpretation

The TMR is the time available for emergency response after the loss of cooling in a reactor. A widely accepted industrial rule-of-thumb:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Three-Second Rule" (CCPS Guideline):**
> If **TMR_ad ≤ 1 day**, the reaction is considered **HIGHLY HAZARDOUS** and requires engineering controls (cooling backup, dump system, or quench).

**Practical Rule-of-Thumb:**
- **TMR_ad ≥ 24 hours** → Generally safe for storage
- **TMR_ad ≤ 8 hours** → Requires active safety interlocks
- **TMR_ad ≤ 1 hour** → Mandatory emergency relief / dump system

---

## 2.5 Semenov Theory of Thermal Explosion

**Nikolai Semenov's** (1928) thermal explosion theory models a reactive system as a well-stirred vessel with:
- **Heat generation** by chemical reaction (Arrhenius kinetics)
- **Heat loss** by Newton's law of cooling to ambient

$$\text{Heat Generation Rate: } q_g = V \cdot (-\Delta H) \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C^n$$

$$\text{Heat Loss Rate: } q_l = U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_0)$$

**Critical condition for runaway:**
$$\frac{d(q_g)}{dT} \geq \frac{d(q_l)}{dT} \quad \text{at the inflection point}$$

This yields the classic **Semenov criterion**:

$$\frac{V \cdot (-\Delta H) \cdot C^n \cdot E_a \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_{crit}}}{R \cdot T_{crit}^2} = U \cdot A$$

SADT is defined as the ambient temperature **T₀** at which the equilibrium between heat generation and loss becomes unstable for a specific commercial package.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

1. **Storage Tank Design:** SADT determines the maximum allowable bulk storage temperature. For organic peroxides (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, dicumyl peroxide), SADT governs insulated vs. refrigerated storage.
2. **Reactor Emergency Relief Sizing:** API 520 / API 521 mandate that TMR_ad ≤ 10 min triggers **DIERS (Design Institute for Emergency Relief Systems)** two-phase flow relief design.
3. **Incompatibility Matrix:** Used in **HAZOP** and **PHA** to identify cross-utility contamination hazards (e.g., acid + cyanide = HCN).
4. **Transportation Classification:** UN Test Series E (thermal stability) determines packing group for dangerous goods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔT_ad)

### Setup
Consider a mass **m** of a substance with mass-specific heat capacity **C_p** undergoing an exothermic decomposition that releases enthalpy **ΔH** (J/g). The system is assumed **adiabatic** (no heat transfer to surroundings), so all released enthalpy raises the temperature of the material.

### Step-by-Step Derivation
