| Typical SIL | 1 | 2–3 | 1 | 2–3 |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium | High |
| Use case | Non-critical interlocks | High hazard, low false-trip cost | High availability needed (e.g., turbine trip) | High-SIL critical SIFs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"1oo2 = Safety First, 2oo2 = Availability First, 2oo3 = Best of Both."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Unit] --> S1[Sensor A]
    P --> S2[Sensor B]
    P --> S3[Sensor C]
    S1 --> VOTE[Voting Logic 2oo3]
    S2 --> VOTE
    S3 --> VOTE
    VOTE --> PLC[Safety PLC TMR]
    PLC --> FE1[Solenoid Valve 1]
    PLC --> FE2[Solenoid Valve 2]
    FE1 --> SV[Shutdown Valve Closes]
    FE2 --> SV
    SV --> SAFE[Safe State Achieved]
    PLC -.Alarm.-> OP[Operator HMI]
    PLC -.Event Log.-> HIST[Historian]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- The **Process Unit** (e.g., a hydrocarbon separator) is monitored by **three redundant sensors** (A, B, C).
- The **Voting Logic (2oo3)** ensures that the trip is initiated only if at least two sensors detect a high level — this prevents spurious trips while maintaining SIL 3.
- The **Safety PLC** (TMR = Triple Modular Redundant) acts as the **Logic Solver** with two-out-of-three processor agreement.
- The PLC de-energizes the **Solenoid Valves** (fail-safe design: loss of air/electrical signal closes the shutdown valve).
- The **Shutdown Valve** isolates the process, achieving the **Safe State**.
- Operator HMI receives the alarm; the Historian records the event for post-incident review.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow – IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard and Risk Assessment HAZOP / LOPA] --> B[Determine Safety Instrumented Function SIF Requirements]
    B --> C[SIL Target Allocation per SIF]
    C --> D[SIS Design and Engineering]
    D --> E[Installation and Commissioning]
    E --> F[Validation and Pre-Startup Safety Review]
    F --> G[Operation Maintenance Proof Testing]
    G --> H{Performance Acceptable?}
    H -- No --> I[Modify Design or Increase Proof Test Frequency]
    I --> D
    H -- Yes --> J[Decommissioning and Modification]
    J --> K[End of Lifecycle]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- The IEC 61511 **Safety Lifecycle** begins with HAZOP and LOPA, which identify hazards and assign SIL targets to protective layers.
- After engineering and commissioning, **proof testing** verifies the SIF functionality at prescribed intervals.
- Continuous monitoring and performance analysis feed back into design modifications, closing the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** loop required for functional safety management.

### 4.3 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) – Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Initiating Cause e.g., Pump Failure] --> L1[Layer 1 BPCS Regulatory Control]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Operator Alarm and Intervention]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Safety Instrumented Function SIS]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Mechanical Relief Device PSV]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Plant Emergency Response]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Community Emergency Response]
    L6 --> RESULT{Mitigated Event Frequency Acceptable?}
    RESULT -- No --> M[Add IPL or Increase RRF]
    M --> L3
    RESULT -- Yes --> END[Acceptable Risk]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- LOPA uses **IPL (Independent Protection Layer)** concepts. Each layer must be **independent, effective, and auditable**.
- The **SIS is Layer 3**, sandwiched between human intervention (Layer 2) and passive mechanical protection (Layer 4).
- The **event frequency** is reduced multiplicatively through each layer; if the residual risk is still unacceptable, additional layers or higher SIL targets are mandated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The "1-in-10,000 rule" (tolerable individual risk) and "1-in-100,000 rule" (tolerable societal risk) are the boundaries used in LOPA to determine the required number of IPLs and SIL targets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define Safety Instrumented System (SIS). How is it different from a Basic Process Control System (BPCS)?*

**Model Answer:**

A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is an independent control system that takes a process to a safe state when pre-set process conditions are violated. It consists of sensors, logic solvers, and final elements configured as one or more Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs).

**Key Differences from BPCS:**

| Aspect | BPCS | SIS |
|:------|:-----|:----|
| Primary purpose | Maintain process within operating window | Bring process to safe state on demand |
| Independence | – | Must be physically and logically separate from BPCS |
| Standards | General instrumentation standards | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 |
| Operating mode | Continuous control | Dormant until demand |
| Failure philosophy | "Fail-safe" is desirable but not strictly enforced | "Fail-safe" is mandatory |
| Testing | Continuous, self-checking | Periodic proof testing required |

[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the difference table]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is the significance of Safety Integrity Level (SIL)? State the PFD range for SIL 2.*

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of SIL:**
- SIL is a measure of the risk reduction provided by a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) operating in a low-demand mode of operation.
- It quantifies the **probability that the SIF will perform its safety function when demanded**.
- SIL is determined during the LOPA / risk assessment stage and dictates the engineering, redundancy, and proof-testing requirements of the loop.

**PFD Range for SIL 2:**

$$ 10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2} $$

[3 Marks: 2 for significance, 1 for the PFD range]

---

### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Explain the function of each layer.
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of a pressure transmitter, a Safety PLC, and a shutdown valve. The following data are provided:
- Pressure transmitter: $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Safety PLC: $PFD_{Logic} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$
- Shutdown valve: $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Proof test interval: $T_i = 4380$ hr (6 months)

Calculate the SIL achieved by the loop. State one improvement to upgrade the loop to SIL 2 if it does not already meet it.
*(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) SIS Architecture (7 Marks):**

The SIS has three principal layers, often termed the "**Sense → Decide → Actuate**" architecture:

1. **Sensing Layer (Field Instruments):**
   - Detects the unsafe process condition.
   - Examples: pressure, temperature, flow, level transmitters; flame and gas detectors.
   - **Redundancy schemes** (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3) balance safety vs. availability.

2. **Logic Solver Layer (Safety PLC):**
   - Receives sensor signals, applies voting logic, and decides to trip or not.
   - Must be **TÜV-certified** to a specific SIL.
   - Architectures: Single channel, dual channel (1oo2D), or Triple Modular Redundant (TMR).
   - Provides self-diagnostics, watchdog timers, and secure I/O.

3. **Final Element Layer (Actuators and Valves):**
   - Implements the physical protective action: closes block valves, stops pumps, de-energizes heaters.
   - **Fail-safe design:** Loss of air/electrical signal = protective action.
   - Includes bypass valves for maintenance, with administrative controls to prevent accidental bypass.

The SIF also includes a **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** for alarm management and an **Event Sequence Recorder (ESR)** for post-trip analysis.

[7 Marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for sensing, 2 for logic and actuation, 1 for HMI/ESR]

---

**(b) SIL Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 – PFD of the pressure transmitter (1oo1):**

$$ PFD_{PT} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-6})(4380)}{2} $$

$$ PFD_{PT} = 6.57 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 2 – PFD of the shutdown valve (1oo1):**

$$ PFD_{Valve} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(4380)}{2} $$

$$ PFD_{Valve} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 3 – Sum the PFDs:**

$$ PFD_{SYS} = PFD_{PT} + PFD_{Logic} + PFD_{Valve} $$

$$ PFD_{SYS} = 6.57 \times 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-4} + 4.38 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ PFD_{SYS} = 1.145 \times 10^{-2} $$

**Step 4 – Map to SIL:**

Since $1.145 \times 10^{-2}$ lies between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$, the loop achieves **SIL 1**.

**Step 5 – Improvement to upgrade to SIL 2:**

To upgrade to SIL 2, the loop $PFD_{SYS}$ must be reduced below $10^{-2}$. Two practical options:

- **Reduce the proof test interval** $T_i$. For example, halving $T_i$ to 2,190 hr (3 months) reduces each PFD by half, yielding $PFD_{SYS} \approx 5.7 \times 10^{-3}$, which is within SIL 2.
- **Upgrade to a 1oo2 (or 2oo3) voting architecture** for the sensor and the final element, dramatically lowering individual PFDs via the squared term.

[7 Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for transmitter PFD, 1 for valve PFD, 1 for summation and SIL, 2 for improvement]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a flowchart the **IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle** as applied to process industries. Why is proof testing critical in this lifecycle?
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Two pressure transmitters are installed in a 1oo2 voting configuration to protect a reactor. Each transmitter has $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-7}$ /hr. The proof test interval is $T_i = 17,520$ hr (2 years). The Safety PLC and shutdown valve together contribute $PFD = 2 \times 10^{-3}$. Calculate the PFDavg for the sensor subsystem and the total loop. Identify the SIL achieved and recommend whether proof testing should be made more frequent.
*(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle (7 Marks):**

The IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle consists of the following phases (presented as a flow):

1. **Process Hazards & Risk Assessment (Phase 1–4):** HAZOP, FMEA, and LOPA identify hazards and assign SIL targets.
2. **Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers (Phase 5):** Decide which hazards are mitigated by the SIS, alarms, or relief devices.
3. **SIS Design & Engineering (Phase 6–9):** Specify sensors, logic solver, final elements; develop cause-and-effect matrix; design redundancy.
4. **Installation, Commissioning, and Validation (Phase 10–13):** Verify SIF performance through pre-startup safety review (PSSR) and independent SIL verification.
5. **Operation, Maintenance, and Proof Testing (Phase 14–16):** Conduct periodic proof tests to detect dangerous undetected failures; the proof test interval $T_i$ is critical because it appears directly in the PFD formula.
6. **Modification, Decommissioning, and End-of-Life (Phase 17–18):** Ensure that modifications do not degrade the original SIL target.

**Why Proof Testing is Critical:**
Proof testing is the only practical method to **reveal dangerous undetected failures** that have accumulated since the last test. The PFDavg formulas all contain $T_i$ — if the proof test interval is too long, PFDavg exceeds the SIL target, compromising the safety function.

[7 Marks: 4 for lifecycle phases, 2 for proof testing explanation, 1 for diagram or closure]

---

**(b) 1oo2 Sensor Subsystem and Total Loop (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 – Compute sensor PFDavg for 1oo2 voted system:**

$$ PFD_{Sensor}^{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i)^2}{3} $$

$$ \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i = (4 \times 10^{-7})(17{,}520) = 7.008 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ PFD_{Sensor}^{1oo2} = \dfrac{(7.008 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \dfrac{4.911 \times 10^{-5}}{3} $$

$$ PFD_{Sensor}^{1oo2} = 1.637 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Step 2 – Total loop PFDavg:**

$$ PFD_{SYS} = PFD_{Sensor}^{1oo2} + PFD_{PLC+Valve} $$

$$ PFD_{SYS} = 1.637 \times 10^{-5} + 2 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ PFD_{SYS} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 3 – Identify the SIL:**

Since $2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$, the loop achieves **SIL 2**.

**Step 4 – Proof Testing Recommendation:**

The current $T_i = 2$ years yields a SIL 2 loop with comfortable margin ($PFD$ is well below the $10^{-2}$ upper limit). However, if safety margin needs to be increased (e.g., for regulatory compliance or upcoming modifications), proof testing could be performed **annually (8,760 hr)**, which would halve the $PFD_{PLC+Valve}$ contribution to $1 \times 10^{-3}$, making the loop operate closer to the SIL 2 / SIL 3 boundary.

[7 Marks: 2 for sensor PFD, 1 for total PFD, 1 for SIL identification, 3 for proof test recommendation with justification]

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in "Safety Systems" questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T_i$ from years to hours.** 1 year = 8,760 hours, NOT 365 × 24 = 8,760. Yes, that is 8,760 — but students often use 8,000 by mistake. (Often 1 mark lost.)
> 2. **Confusing 1oo2 with 2oo2 voting.** Many students write 1oo2 and 2oo2 PFD formulas interchangeably. (Often 2–3 marks lost in 14-mark problems.)
> 3. **Not summing all three PFD contributions** (sensor + logic + valve) in a complete loop problem. (Often 2 marks lost.)
> 4. **Writing the SIL range incorrectly** — writing it as $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ (reversed). The range for SIL 2 is $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$.
> 5. **Not explaining the difference between BPCS and SIS** even when the question asks for it. (3-mark questions often penalize 1 mark for this.)
> 6. **Confusing Proof Test Interval with Mission Time.** $T_i$ is the gap between tests, NOT the total equipment lifetime.
> 7. **Failing to provide the cause-and-effect matrix concept** in 14-mark questions on SIS architecture.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Safety Systems"**

- ✅ **SIS = Sense → Decide → Actuate.** Always draw the block diagram with three layers.
- ✅ **BPCS and SIS must be independent** — separate hardware, software, power, and communication.
- ✅ **SIL 1:** $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ ; **SIL 2:** $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ ; **SIL 3:** $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$.
- ✅ **PFD for 1oo1 = $\lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$** — memorize this first; all others are derived from it.
- ✅ **PFD for 1oo2 = $(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2 / 3$** — squared term makes the SIF much safer.
- ✅ **PFD for 2oo2 = $\lambda_{DU} T_i$** — same as 1oo1, but higher availability.
- ✅ **Total PFD = Sum of individual PFDs** of sensor, logic solver, and final element.
- ✅ **1oo2 favors safety, 2oo2 favors availability, 2oo3 balances both** — standard exam comparison.
- ✅ **Proof Test Interval $T_i$ is a first-order variable** in PFD — halving it halves the PFD (for 1oo1).
- ✅ **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** governs hardware fault tolerance (HFT) per IEC 61508.
- ✅ **SIF = Safety Instrumented Function**, not the same as SIS (which is the *system*).
- ✅ **LOPA = Layer of Protection Analysis** — used to allocate SIL targets to SIFs.
- ✅ **ESD = Emergency Shutdown System** — a subsystem of SIS handling plant-wide trips.
- ✅ **HIPPS = High-Integrity Pressure Protection System** — for overpressure in case of block valve leakage.
- ✅ **Relief valves (PSV) are passive IPLs** — they are *independent* of the SIS in LOPA.
- ✅ **IEC 61511** is the process industry standard; **IEC 61508** is the generic functional safety standard.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**P**roof **T**esting **r**educes **PFD**" — PTRP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagrams: Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diagrams: Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Process Safety Management (PSM)**, a **Block Diagram** is a standardized graphical representation that uses labeled rectangular blocks connected by directed signal/process flow lines to depict the functional architecture of a process unit, a control loop, or a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Each block represents a single physical device, a mathematical operation, or a system function, while the connecting lines represent the direction of material, energy, or signal flow.

According to **ISA-5.1** (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification) and **ISA-84 / IEC 61511** standards, block diagrams in process safety specifically refer to the schematic abstraction of:
- **Basic Process Control Systems (BPCS)**
- **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF)** within a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)
- **Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD)** for determining system availability
- **Cause–Consequence mapping** at a modular level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Block Diagrams form the foundational visual language of process safety documentation. They precede the development of Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&ID) and are mandatory deliverables in the *Process Safety Information (PSI)* package required under the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (d) regulation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Block Diagram as a "Recipe Card for a Chemical Factory"**

Imagine you walk into a kitchen and want to understand how a chef prepares a dish. Instead of watching them cook, you are given a **recipe card** with simple boxes:
- Box 1: "Boil water"
- Box 2: "Add pasta"
- Box 3: "Drain water"
- Box 4: "Add sauce"
- Arrows between boxes show the **flow of action**.

A **Block Diagram** in process safety is exactly this — but instead of cooking steps, it shows how **raw materials, instruments, valves, controllers, and safety trips** interact to produce a safe product. Each rectangular block is a *function* (a sensor, a controller, a valve), and the arrows represent the **flow of material or information signals**.

**Why this matters in Process Safety:**
- It lets a safety engineer "see" the entire plant logic on a single page.
- It exposes **single points of failure** (critical when designing for SIL-1, SIL-2, or SIL-3).
- It becomes the input document for **HAZOP, LOPA, and Fault Tree Analysis**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used in Block Diagrams

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | **hours** | Reliability block analysis |
| **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | dimensionless (0 to 1) | SIL verification |
| **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | dimensionless | Safety layer quantification |
| **Process Safety Time** | **seconds** | SIS response design |
| **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** | frequency / year | Block-level event mapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The international standard governing block diagrams in safety contexts is **IEC 61511** (Functional Safety — Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector), which defines the required architecture for SIL-1 through SIL-3 systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Block Diagram — Building Blocks

A process safety block diagram is composed of the following structural elements:

- **Block (Function Box):** A rectangle containing the name of the function (e.g., "Temperature Sensor," "Pressure Controller," "Emergency Shutdown Valve").
- **Summing Junction (Comparator):** A circle with input arrows and a signed output (used in feedback control loops).
- **Take-off Point:** A branch point where a signal is duplicated without modification.
- **Signal Flow Line:** A directed arrow showing the direction of energy, material, or information transfer.
- **Annotation Block:** A small text box for ISA tag numbers (e.g., `TT-101`, `FV-203`, `XV-1001`).

> [!NOTE]
> **ISA Tag Format (KTU must-know):**
> `FT-101` → **F**unction (Flow) + **T**ype (Transmitter) + Loop **101**
> `XV-1001` → **X** = unspecified / **V** = Valve, Loop 1001

---

### 2.2 Classification of Block Diagrams in Process Safety

Block diagrams are not a single entity; the KTU 2024 syllabus classifies them as follows:

1. **Functional Block Diagram (FBD):** Shows the logical relationship between safety functions without specifying hardware.
2. **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD):** Shows success/failure paths to determine system availability.
3. **Control Block Diagram:** Shows BPCS feedback loops with transfer functions.
4. **Safety Block Diagram (SIS Block):** Shows the sensor–logic solver–final element chain of a Safety Instrumented Function.
5. **Cause–Consequence Block Diagram:** Used in HAZOP to trace deviations from cause to consequence.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU UNIVERSITY EXAMS** — Memorize these for Part A and Part B.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{series} = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot G_3 \dots G_n$ | Series block multiplication | Sequential control stages |
| 2 | $G_{parallel} = G_1 + G_2 + G_3 \dots + G_n$ | Parallel block addition | Redundant safety paths |
| 3 | $G_{feedback} = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$ | Negative feedback closed-loop | BPCS controller tuning |
| 4 | $G_{feedback} = \dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$ | Positive feedback closed-loop | Unstable / runaway systems |
| 5 | $R_{series} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \dots R_n$ | Series reliability multiplication | SIS sensor chain reliability |
| 6 | $R_{parallel} = 1 - [(1-R_1)(1-R_2)\dots(1-R_n)]$ | Parallel (redundant) reliability | 1oo2, 2oo3 voting systems |
| 7 | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | SIL-1/SIL-2 verification |
| 8 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Safety layer quantification |
| 9 | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Component reliability input |
| 10 | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \dots + \lambda_n$ | Failure rate addition (independent) | RBD failure modeling |

> **Notation Reminder for KTU Valuation:**
> - $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate (per hour)
> - $T_{proof}$ = Proof test interval (hours)
> - $H$ = Feedback transfer function (always dimensionless)

---

### 2.4 Block Diagram Algebra — The 'Why' and 'How'

#### **Why do we need Block Diagram Reduction?**

In real process plants, control loops have **multiple overlapping feedback paths**, **feed-forward branches**, and **cross-coupled controllers**. Direct analysis is impossible without simplification. Block diagram reduction provides a **mathematical pathway** to convert a complex interconnected network into a single equivalent transfer function.

#### **How does Reduction Work?**

You systematically replace blocks using the rules below until only one input and one output remain:

1. **Combine cascaded (series) blocks** by multiplying their transfer functions.
2. **Combine parallel blocks** by adding their transfer functions.
3. **Eliminate feedback loops** using the closed-loop transfer function formula.
4. **Move summing junctions and take-off points** to create non-touching loops.
5. **Repeat** until a single equivalent block remains.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick (Examiner loves this):** Always check for *non-touching loops* — two feedback loops that do not share any block. These are reduced using the formula:
>
> $$\Delta_{combined} = \Delta_1 \cdot \Delta_2$$
>
> where $\Delta$ is the **loop gain determinant** for each isolated loop.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Block diagrams in process safety are used by:

- **Plant Design Engineers:** To specify the BPCS architecture during Front-End Engineering Design (FEED).
- **Safety Engineers:** To perform SIL verification per IEC 61511.
- **HAZOP Teams:** To identify which blocks need protection layers.
- **Operations Teams:** As a training aid to understand trip initiation paths.
- **Auditors (OSHA / KTU external examiners):** To verify compliance with PSM elements.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

**Starting Setup:**
- Forward path transfer function: $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function: $H(s)$
- Input: $R(s)$
- Output: $C(s)$
- Error signal: $E(s)$

**Step 1 — Define the error signal at the summing junction:**
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 2 — Express the output through the forward block:**
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation 2 into Equation 1:**
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Collect $E(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:**
$$E(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)] = R(s)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E(s)$:**
$$E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into Equation 2 to get the closed-loop transfer function:**
$$C(s) = \dfrac{G(s) \cdot R(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

**Step 7 — Final closed-loop transfer function:**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}}$$

This is the **gold standard** result every KTU examiner expects in Part B answers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Reliability of Series Block Configuration

**Scenario:** A Safety Instrumented Function has three independent blocks in series — a Sensor, a Logic Solver, and a Final Element. Each must succeed for the SIF to function.

**Step 1 — Define reliability of each block:**
$$R_1, R_2, R_3 = \text{individual reliabilities}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events:**
$$R_{system} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3$$

**Step 3 — Generalize for n blocks:**
$$\boxed{R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration for KTU:**
Let $R_1 = 0.99$, $R_2 = 0.98$, $R_3 = 0.97$.

$$R_{series} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9410$$

> **Conclusion:** Series reliability **decreases** as more blocks are added — this is why redundant parallel paths are essential for high-SIL systems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reliability of Parallel (Redundant) Block Configuration

**Scenario:** Two identical sensors are installed in parallel with a 1oo2 voting logic. The system succeeds if **at least one** sensor works.

**Step 1 — Probability that Block 1 fails:**
$$P(F_1) = 1 - R_1$$

**Step 2 — Probability that Block 2 fails:**
$$P(F_2) = 1 - R_2$$

**Step 3 — Probability that both fail (independent events):**
$$P(F_{both}) = (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$$

**Step 4 — Probability that system fails:**
$$P(F_{system}) = (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$$

**Step 5 — System reliability:**
$$R_{parallel} = 1 - P(F_{system}) = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$$

**Step 6 — Generalize for n parallel blocks:**
$$\boxed{R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1-R_i)}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example:**
Let $R_1 = R_2 = 0.90$ (two redundant transmitters).

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-0.90)(1-0.90) = 1 - 0.10 \times 0.10 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

> **Conclusion:** Parallel redundancy **increases** system reliability dramatically — the foundation of fault-tolerant SIS design.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: PFD Average for a Single Channel

**Step 1 — Define the dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$** (per hour).

**Step 2 — Assume failures are detected only during periodic proof testing** at interval $T_{proof}$.

**Step 3 — Average probability of failure on demand over one proof test cycle:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_0^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \left[\dfrac{t^2}{2}\right]_0^{T_{proof}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Risk Reduction Factor:**
$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical KTU example:**
Given $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour, $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (1 year):

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{-5})(8760)}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{4.38 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 22.83$$

> **Conclusion:** This places the SIF in **SIL-1 territory** (RRF between 10 and 100).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — SIL Classification vs PFD Range

| SIL Level | PFD Range | RRF Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL-0** | Not safety-related | < 10 | BPCS only |
| **SIL-1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | Fire & gas detection |
| **SIL-2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | Burner management systems |
| **SIL-3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) |
| **SIL-4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 | Rarely used in process industry |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Relationship of Process Safety Diagrams

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[BFD - Block Flow Diagram] --> A2[PFD - Process Flow Diagram]
    A2 --> A3[P and ID - Piping and Instrumentation Diagram]
    A3 --> A4[SIS Block Diagram]
    A3 --> A5[BPCS Block Diagram]
    A4 --> A6[SIL Verification Block]
    A5 --> A7[HAZOP Node Block]
    A6 --> A8[Sensor Logic Solver Final Element]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[SIL-1 or SIL-2 or SIL-3 Classification]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> The hierarchy starts from a high-level **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** (mass balance only), descends to **PFD** (process flows + major equipment), then to **P&ID** (every valve, instrument, and pipe), and finally branches into specialized safety block diagrams for **SIS** and **BPCS** analysis.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[Process Variable e.g. Pressure] --> B2[Sensor Block PT - Pressure Transmitter]
    B2 --> B3[Logic Solver Block SIS PLC]
    B3 --> B4[Final Element Block XV - Shutoff Valve]
    B4 --> B5[Hazardous Event Mitigated]
    B2 -.1002 Voting.-> B6[Redundant Sensor]
    B6 --> B3
    B3 -.Diagnostics.-> B7[Proof Test Interval TP]
    B7 --> B8[PFD Average Calculation]
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> This block diagram represents a **high-integrity pressure safety system** with redundant sensors (1oo2 voting), a logic solver (Safety PLC), and a final element (shutdown valve). The dashed lines indicate the **diagnostic and reliability calculation layers** used during SIL verification.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Control Loop Block Diagram with Feedback

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Setpoint SP] --> C2[Summing Junction Plus Minus]
    C3[Process Variable PV Feedback] --> C2
    C2 --> C4[Controller Block Gc s]
    C4 --> C5[Final Control Element Valve Gv s]
    C5 --> C6[Process Block Gp s]
    C6 --> C7[Process Output]
    C6 --> C8[Measurement Transmitter H s]
    C8 --> C3
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> This is a classic **negative feedback control loop**. The error signal (SP − PV) drives the controller, which adjusts the valve to bring the process variable back to the setpoint. Block diagram reduction yields the closed-loop transfer function derived in Section 3.1.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) — Series vs Parallel

```mermaid
graph TD
    D1[Input Demand Event] --> D2[Sensor R1]
    D2 --> D3[Logic Solver R2]
    D3 --> D4[Valve R3]
    D4 --> D5[Safe State Achieved]
    D2 -.Redundancy.-> D6[Backup Sensor R1B]
    D6 --> D3
    D4 -.Redundancy.-> D7[Backup Valve R3B]
    D7 --> D5
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> Solid arrows form the **primary safety path (series RBD)**. Dashed arrows represent **parallel redundancy**. The combined reliability is calculated using the series-parallel formulas from Section 3.2 and 3.3.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Cause–Consequence Block for HAZOP Study

```mermaid
graph TD
    E1[Cause - Pump Failure] --> E2[Deviation - No Flow]
    E2 --> E3[Consequence - Overheating]
    E3 --> E4[SIF Activates - High Temp Trip]
    E4 --> E5[Mitigation - ESD Valve Closes]
    E5 --> E6[Safe State - Reactor Isolated]
    E3 -.Worst Case.-> E7[Rupture Disk Activation]
    E7 --> E6
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> This cause–consequence block diagram is a **HAZOP deliverable**, showing how an initiating cause propagates through deviations to consequences and is finally mitigated by independent protection layers (IPL).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define a **Block Diagram** as used in Process Safety Management. List any **four standard components** of a block diagram.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A Block Diagram is a graphical representation of a process system using rectangular blocks to denote physical devices or functions and directed lines to represent the flow of material, energy, or signal between them.
>
> The four standard components are:
> 1. **Block (Function Box)** — represents a device or function.
> 2. **Signal Flow Line** — directed arrow showing information/material flow.
> 3. **Summing Junction** — circular node that combines signals algebraically.
> 4. **Take-off Point** — branch point for signal duplication.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark per component, total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
State the **reliability formula** for two independent blocks connected in:
(a) Series
(b) Parallel

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **(a) Series Connection:** $R_{series} = R_1 \cdot R_2$
>
> **(b) Parallel Connection:** $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$
>
> Series reliability **decreases** with added blocks; parallel reliability **increases** with added redundant blocks. *(1.5 marks each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE Module 2):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Draw a labeled **Block Diagram of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** with at least one redundant sensor path. Identify and label the **three primary functional blocks** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a process with three components in series having reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, and $R_3 = 0.90$, calculate the **overall system reliability**. If a fourth identical component ($R_4 = 0.90$) is added in **parallel** with $R_3$, what is the **new system reliability**? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram of SIS (7 Marks):**

A well-labeled SIS block diagram must contain:

1. **Sensor Block** (e.g., Pressure Transmitter `PT-101`) — detects process deviation.
2. **Logic Solver Block** (e.g., Safety PLC) — executes trip logic.
3. **Final Element Block** (e.g., Emergency Shutdown Valve `XV-1001`) — isolates hazard.
4. **Redundancy Indication** — dashed arrow showing backup sensor (1oo2 voting).

```
Process → [Sensor PT-101] → [Logic Solver PLC] → [Valve XV-1001] → Safe State
                    ↑
            [Backup Sensor PT-101B]  (parallel)
```

*(Marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for identifying 3 blocks, 2 for redundancy logic, 1 for labels = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Series reliability of original system:**
$$R_{series} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3$$
$$R_{series} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 = 0.7866$$

**Step 2 — Reliability of $R_3$ combined with $R_4$ in parallel:**
$$R_{3,4} = 1 - (1-R_3)(1-R_4)$$
$$R_{3,4} = 1 - (1-0.90)(1-0.90) = 1 - 0.10 \times 0.10 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Step 3 — New overall system reliability:**
$$R_{new} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_{3,4}$$
$$R_{new} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.99 = 0.8653$$

**Conclusion:** Adding parallel redundancy raised system reliability from **0.7866** to **0.8653**, demonstrating the engineering value of redundant SIS channels.

*(Marks: 2 for Step 1, 3 for Step 2, 2 for Step 3 = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **block diagram reduction rules** for the following configurations with neat sketches:
(i) Two blocks in series
(ii) Two blocks in parallel
(iii) A feedback loop **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Safety Instrumented Function has a dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and a proof test interval $T_{proof} = 4380$ hours (6 months). Calculate the **average PFD** and the corresponding **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**. Identify the **SIL level** of this SIF. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Reduction Rules (7 Marks):**

**Configuration (i) — Series:**
$$G_{series} = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s)$$

```
R → [G1] → [G2] → C        R → [G1·G2] → C
```

**Configuration (ii) — Parallel:**
$$G_{parallel} = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$$

```
R → [G1] → + → C            R → [G1 + G2] → C
R → [G2] → + →
```

**Configuration (iii) — Negative Feedback Loop:**
$$\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

```
R → (+) → [G] → C
     (-) ← [H] ←
```

*(Marks: 2 per configuration = 6 marks, 1 for neat sketches = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) PFD and SIL Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate PFD average:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(4380)}{2}$$
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{0.0219}{2} = 0.01095$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Risk Reduction Factor:**
$$RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{0.01095} \approx 91.32$$

**Step 3 — SIL Classification:**
Since $10 \leq RRF \leq 100$, the SIF belongs to **SIL-1**.

**Conclusion:** The SIF provides a risk reduction of approximately 91-fold, qualifying it for **SIL-1** safety integrity level per IEC 61511.

*(Marks: 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 2 for Step 3, 1 for units/standards reference = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "1+" denominator in feedback loops:** Students frequently write $\dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$ for negative feedback. The correct form for negative feedback is $\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Mixing up reliability formulas:** Writing $R_{parallel} = R_1 + R_2$ instead of $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ is a **classic losing move**. *Loss: 3 marks.*
> 3. **Forgetting units in PFD calculation:** The answer $PFD_{avg}$ is **dimensionless**. Omitting the proof test interval units causes mark deduction.
> 4. **Not labeling ISA tags on block diagrams:** Examiners expect tag numbers like `PT-101`, `XV-1001`. A diagram without tags is incomplete. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 5. **Missing the voting notation (1oo2, 2oo3):** When you draw redundant blocks, always state the voting logic explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Diagrams (Module 2)**

- [x] A **Block Diagram** = rectangular block + directed line = function + signal flow.
- [x] Block diagrams are governed by **ISA-5.1** (symbols) and **IEC 61511** (safety systems).
- [x] The three pillars of a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** = **Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element**.
- [x] **Series reliability** → $R_{series} = \prod R_i$ (decreases with more blocks).
- [x] **Parallel reliability** → $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ (increases with redundancy).
- [x] **Negative feedback closed-loop** → $\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$.
- [x] **Positive feedback closed-loop** → $\dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$ (unstable systems).
- [x] **PFD average** for a single channel → $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$.
- [x] **Risk Reduction Factor** → $\dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$.
- [x] **SIL-1:** RRF 10–100 | **SIL-2:** RRF 100–1000 | **SIL-3:** RRF 1000–10000.
- [x] Block diagrams feed into **HAZOP, LOPA, Fault Tree Analysis, and SIL verification**.
- [x] Always include **ISA tag numbers**, **voting logic**, and **redundancy notation** on diagrams.
- [x] The hierarchy of process diagrams: **BFD → PFD → P&ID → SIS/BPCS Block Diagram**.
- [x] Block diagrams are a **mandatory PSM deliverable** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (d).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a schematic representation of the major equipment, process flow streams, operating conditions, and material/energy balances of an entire chemical process plant. It is a *qualitative-quantitative* document that answers the question: **"What is the process doing and how much is flowing through it?"**

The PFD is the **first line-drawing deliverable** generated after process simulation (e.g., Aspen Plus, HYSYS, PRO/II) is completed. It is created during the **Conceptual / Basic Engineering** phase of a project.

**Key Distinguishing Feature:**
The PFD **does NOT show** minor piping, every valve, every instrument, or construction details. It focuses on:
- Major process units (reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, vessels)
- Main process and utility stream lines
- Stream numbers and material balance data tables
- Major control loops (only the most critical)
- Operating conditions (T, P, flow rate, composition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** PFDs are governed by **ISA-5.1 (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification)** and **ISA-101.01 (HMI Systems)** for the limited instrumentation shown, while the overall drawing layout follows **ISO 14617** and company-specific engineering standards.

---

## 1.2 Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed engineering schematic that depicts the *complete* piping, equipment, valves, fittings, instrumentation, control loops, and interlocks of a process unit. It is created during the **Detailed Engineering / Front-End Engineering Design (FEED)** phase and serves as the master document for construction, fabrication, and safety analysis.

The P&ID answers the engineering question: **"How exactly will this process be built, controlled, operated, and maintained safely?"**

**Key Distinguishing Feature:**
The P&ID **DOES show**:
- Every pipe (with line number, size, material, insulation class, and service)
- All valves (manual, automatic, safety, control)
- All instruments (with ISA tag numbers)
- All control loops and interlock logic (using ISA-5.1 bubble symbols)
- Equipment internals where relevant (trays, packing, baffles)
- Safety devices (PSV, RSD, ESD, fire protection)
- Vents, drains, and sampling points

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students frequently confuse PFD and P&ID. Remember: PFD = *what flows where and how much*; P&ID = *how every pipe, valve, and instrument is installed*. In a 14-mark question, this distinction alone can fetch 2 marks.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe vs. Blueprints" Analogy

Imagine you are baking a complex cake in a commercial bakery:

| Document Type | Bakery Analogy | What it Contains |
|---|---|---|
| **PFD** | The **Recipe Card** hung on the wall | Ingredients (streams), quantities (mass balance), oven temperature (T, P), major steps (unit operations) |
| **P&ID** | The **Full Engineering Blueprints** of the bakery | Every pipe carrying water/milk, every gas valve, every thermostat sensor, every safety shutoff, every electrical interlock |

The **recipe** tells the head chef what to do. The **blueprints** tell the plumber, electrician, and safety inspector how to build it. **Both are needed** — neither replaces the other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Safety Implication:** A PFD is sufficient to *understand* the process. A P&ID is **mandatory** to *safely design, operate, and audit* the process. P&IDs are the foundational documents used in **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) studies, LOPA, and PSM audits** — the heart of KTU's Process Safety Management course.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Drawing Sheet Size:** ISO A1 (594 × 841 mm) or ANSI D (22" × 34") — industry standard for P&IDs.
- **Scale:** PFDs and P&IDs are **not drawn to scale**. Sizes are relative for readability.
- **Standard Reference Elevation:** Ground level (Grade) and operating level (EL) are indicated.
- **Units:** SI units are mandatory under ISO; US Customary may be used in North American projects.
- **Grid System:** A reference grid (A1, A2, B1...) is used to locate equipment across multiple sheets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 PFD — Detailed Structural Anatomy

A PFD is composed of **five functional zones** drawn on a single (or multiple) sheet:

### Zone 1: Title Block (Lower-Right Corner)
- Project name, client, drawing number, revision, scale, date, drawn by, checked by, approved by.
- Legend block (custom symbols if any).

### Zone 2: Equipment Layout (Central Area)
Major equipment is shown as **simplified icons**:
- **Horizontal vessel** = rectangle with rounded ends
- **Vertical column** = tall rectangle
- **Shell-and-tube heat exchanger** = rectangle with internal zigzag or two-circle symbol
- **Pump** = circle with impeller line
- **Compressor** = circle with diagonal hatching
- **Reactor** = rectangle with internal mixer symbol (CSTR) or coil lines (PFR)

Each equipment is tagged as **V-101, E-201, R-301, T-401, P-501** (where letter = service, number = unit).

### Zone 3: Process Stream Lines
- **Main process flow:** Thick solid line with arrows
- **Utility streams:** Thin solid line
- **Recycle and bypass:** Dashed lines (when shown on PFD)
- **Future connections:** Dotted lines (for tie-ins)

Each stream has a **stream number (S-101)** and is referenced to a **Material Balance Table**.

### Zone 4: Material Balance Table (Top or Right)
A table listing for **every stream**:
- Stream number
- Phase (V/L/VL)
- Temperature (°C)
- Pressure (kPa or bar)
- Mass flow (kg/h) or molar flow (kmol/h)
- Composition (mol% or mass%)

### Zone 5: Major Control Loops (Selective)
Only the **primary regulatory control loops** (e.g., FC, TC, LC, PC on the main process units) are shown. Detailed control architecture is reserved for the P&ID.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A PFD typically contains **8 to 12 control loops maximum**. If a drawing shows 50+ control loops, it has crossed over into P&ID territory.

---

## 2.2 P&ID — Detailed Structural Anatomy

The P&ID expands the PFD by adding **all engineering detail** required for fabrication, construction, commissioning, and safety review.

### Component Categories Shown in a P&ID:

| Category | Examples | Information Conveyed |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment** | Vessels, columns, exchangers, pumps, reactors | Tag, size, internals, design T/P, material of construction |
| **Piping** | All process, utility, drain, vent, sample lines | Line number, size, material, insulation, service |
| **Valves** | Gate, globe, ball, butterfly, check, control, safety, relief | Type, size, actuator, fail-safe mode |
| **Instruments** | Pressure, temperature, level, flow transmitters and indicators | ISA tag, function, range, signal type |
| **Control Loops** | Feedback, cascade, ratio, override, split-range | Bubble symbols, interlock logic, DCS/PLC I/O |
| **Safety Devices** | PSV, rupture disc, ESDV, flame arrestor, deluge valve | Set pressure, sizing basis, code reference |
| **Auxiliary Systems** | Sample coolers, steam traps, filters, steam traps, drains | Tag and service |

---

## 2.3 ISA-5.1 Instrumentation Identification — The Core Tagging Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-5.1** is the **single most important standard** for any P&ID. It defines a **letter-code tagging system** for every instrument, with the first letter denoting the **measured variable** and subsequent letters denoting **function, modifier, or readout**.

### 2.3.1 ISA Tag Format

$$
\text{ISA Tag} = \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Variable}} \underbrace{\text{Y}}_{\text{Modifier}} \underbrace{\text{ZZ}}_{\text{Function}} \quad \text{(e.g., TT-101, FIC-201, PSHH-301)}
$$

### 2.3.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — ISA-5.1 Letter Codes

| First Letter | Measured Variable | Subsequent Letters | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Analysis | **A** | Alarm |
| **B** | Burner / Flame | **B** | — |
| **C** | Conductivity | **C** | Controller |
| **D** | Density / Differential | **D** | — |
| **E** | Voltage / Electric | **E** | Element (Sensor) |
| **F** | Flow | **F** | — |
| **G** | Gauging / Glass | **G** | Glass / Gauge |
| **H** | Hand / High | **H** | High |
| **I** | Current / Indicate | **I** | Indicate |
| **J** | Power | **J** | — |
| **K** | Time / Clock | **K** | — |
| **L** | Level | **L** | Light / Low |
| **M** | Moisture / Motor | **M** | — |
| **N** | — | **N** | — |
| **O** | — | **O** | Orifice |
| **P** | Pressure / Point | **P** | Point |
| **Q** | Quantity | **Q** | — |
| **R** | Record / Radiation | **R** | Record / Run |
| **S** | Speed / Switch | **S** | Switch |
| **T** | Temperature / Transmit | **T** | Transmit / Test |
| **U** | Multivariable | **U** | — |
| **V** | Vibration / Valve | **V** | Valve |
| **W** | Weight / Well | **W** | Well |
| **X** | Unclassified | **X** | Unclassified |
| **Y** | Event / State | **Y** | Relay / Compute |
| **Z** | Position / Drive | **Z** | Drive |

### 2.3.3 Critical ISA-5.1 Suffix Modifiers

| Modifier | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **HH** | High-High (2nd stage alarm / trip) | TAHHL — Temperature Alarm High-High Low |
| **LL** | Low-Low (2nd stage alarm / trip) | PASLL — Pressure Alarm Switch Low-Low |
| **T** | Transmitter | FT-101 (Flow Transmitter) |
| **I** | Indicator | PI-201 (Pressure Indicator) |
| **C** | Controller | FIC-301 (Flow Indicating Controller) |
| **V** | Valve | FV-301 (Flow Control Valve) |
| **S** | Switch | LSH-401 (Level Switch High) |

---

## 2.4 Line Numbering System (Process Piping Class)

Every pipe on a P&ID carries a **Line Number** in the format:

$$
\text{Line Tag} = \underbrace{\text{S}}_{\text{Service Code}} - \underbrace{\text{NN}}_{\text{Piping Class}} - \underbrace{\text{NNN}}_{\text{Sequential No.}}
$$

**Example:** `4"-P-101A` → 4-inch line, Process service, Piping Class 101, Service A.

**Common Service Codes (KTU High-Yield):**

| Code | Service | Typical Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Process | Hydrocarbons, chemicals |
| **U** | Utility | Cooling water, air, N₂ |
| **S** | Steam | HP/MP/LP steam |
| **D** | Drain | Condensate, liquid waste |
| **V** | Vent | Relief, vapour |
| **SA** | Sample | Process samples |
| **CW** | Cooling Water | Treated cooling water |
| **IA** | Instrument Air | Dry, oil-free air |

---

## 2.5 Control Loop Architecture

A **control loop** is the integration of:
1. **Sensor/Transmitter** (measures PV)
2. **Controller** (computes error, sends MV)
3. **Final Control Element** (valve, VFD) — manipulates the process
4. **Transmission lines** (pneumatic 3-15 psi, electronic 4-20 mA, digital HART/Fieldbus)

**Loop Types (KTU High-Yield):**

| Loop Type | Description | P&ID Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback (Simple)** | Single PV → single MV | One bubble (e.g., FIC) |
| **Cascade** | Primary controller's SP = Secondary controller's PV | Two linked bubbles |
| **Ratio** | Maintains Q_A / Q_B = constant | Ratio station + controller |
| **Override (Constraint)** | Selects min/max of multiple controllers | Low/High signal selector |
| **Split-Range** | One controller drives two or more valves in segments | Single output, multiple valves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern plant operators use P&IDs to trace **every alarm, interlock, and ESD (Emergency Shutdown)** path. A single missing or mis-tagged instrument in a P&ID can lead to catastrophic incidents — this is why P&IDs are **legally enforceable documents** under OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119) and equivalent international regulations.

---

## 2.6 PFD vs. P&ID — KTU Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | PFD | P&ID |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Show *what* the process is | Show *how* to build/operate it |
| **Engineering Phase** | Conceptual / Basic | Detailed / FEED |
| **Information Level** | Qualitative + Key Quantities | Fully Quantitative + Constructive |
| **Equipment** | Major units only | Every unit + internals |
| **Piping** | Main process lines only | Every pipe + branches + drains |
| **Valves** | Few major valves | Every valve (manual, auto, safety) |
| **Instruments** | Primary control loops only | Every instrument with ISA tag |
| **Safety Devices** | May show PSVs only | All PSVs, RSDs, ESDs, interlocks |
| **Interlocks / Logic** | Not shown | Shown in detail |
| **Operability / Construction** | Process operability | Construction, fabrication, O&M |
| **Audience** | Process engineers, management | Designers, constructors, operators, safety engineers |
| **Used in HAZOP?** | Preliminary only | **Yes — primary document** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Problem-Solving Framework

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: How to Interpret a P&ID for Safety Analysis

Since the KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **Process Safety Management (PSM)**, the highest-value skill is reading a P&ID from a **safety-lens perspective**. Below is a step-by-step analytical procedure.

---

### **Procedure: P&ID Safety Walkthrough (8 Steps)**

**Step 1 — Identify the Process Boundary**
Trace the **battery limits** of the diagram. Identify inlet streams (raw materials, feeds) and outlet streams (products, by-products, wastes). Confirm all utilities entering the boundary (steam, CW, IA, N₂, fuel).

**Step 2 — Identify All Major Equipment**
List every tagged equipment. For each, record:
- Equipment tag (e.g., V-101, E-201, R-301)
- Design pressure and temperature
- Material of construction (MOC)
- Inventory (volume in m³) — critical for PSM coverage

**Step 3 — Identify All Process Lines and Services**
Categorize every line as:
- **Process** (P) — hydrocarbon, chemical, reactive
- **Utility** (U) — CW, steam, air
- **Drain/Vent** (D/V) — closed or open
- **Sample** (SA) — manual or online analyzer

**Step 4 — Identify All Control Loops**
For each loop, identify:
- The **measured variable** (PV) and its transmitter
- The **manipulated variable** (MV) and its final control element
- The **controller** location (DCS/PLC)
- The **control action** (direct/reverse)
- The **fail-safe mode** of the valve (air-to-open, air-to-close)

**Step 5 — Identify All Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)**
Look for **safety instrumented symbols**:
- **PSV / PRV** — Pressure Safety/Relief Valve
- **ESD** — Emergency Shutdown
- **SIS** — Safety Instrumented System (per IEC 61511)
- **Interlocks** (e.g., low flow → close fuel valve)
- **Trip signals** (T, H, HH, LL)

**Step 6 — Identify All Alarms**
Locate every **first-letter + A** instrument (e.g., PAH, PAL, LAH, FAL, TAH). Distinguish:
- **First-stage alarms** (operator attention)
- **High-High / Low-Low** (automatic corrective action)

**Step 7 — Identify Potential Hazard Scenarios**
For each deviation (high T, high P, low flow, loss of utility), trace the **automatic response** built into the P&ID. This forms the basis of a **HAZOP node-level review**.

**Step 8 — Cross-Reference with Cause & Effect Matrix**
Verify that the P&ID is consistent with the **Cause & Effect (C&E) matrix** and **SIF logic diagrams**. Any missing interlock is a safety gap.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: ISA Tag Decoding (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** Decode the following ISA tags and state the **function, location convention, and fail-safe action** of each:

1. `TT-101`
2. `FIC-202`
3. `LAHHL-303`
4. `PSV-401`
5. `FT-501A`
6. `ESD-601`

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

| Tag | Decoding |
|---|---|
| **TT-101** | **T** = Temperature, **T** = Transmitter. → **Temperature Transmitter #101** (sends 4–20 mA signal representing measured temperature to the DCS). |
| **FIC-202** | **F** = Flow, **I** = Indicate, **C** = Controller. → **Flow Indicating Controller #202** (measures flow, displays locally, computes MV, drives FV-202). |
| **LAHHL-303** | **L** = Level, **A** = Alarm, **HH** = High-High, **L** = Low (the trailing L is the type modifier indicating alarm at Low; in this case the sequence LAHH+Low is unusual — corrected form is LAH-303 + LSL-303). For exam purposes, this tag implies a **Level High-High Alarm with Low Trip**. |
| **PSV-401** | **P** = Pressure, **S** = Safety, **V** = Valve. → **Pressure Safety Valve #401** (spring-loaded, set pressure defined, sized per API 520/521). |
| **FT-501A** | **F** = Flow, **T** = Transmitter, **501A** = sequence. → **Flow Transmitter #501A** (the "A" suffix indicates primary/redundant — common in SIS design per IEC 61511). |
| **ESD-601** | **ESD** = Emergency Shutdown, **601** = sequence. → **Emergency Shutdown Device #601** (manually activated push-button or trip signal that initiates a defined shutdown sequence). |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Control Loop Component Mapping

| Loop Component | ISA Symbol Type | Typical Location on P&ID | Failure Mode (Fail-Safe) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Field Transmitter (TT, PT, LT, FT)** | Circle with horizontal line through it | On/near the equipment | Output goes to 4 mA or 20 mA based on application |
| **DCS/PLC Controller (FIC, TIC, LIC, PIC)** | Circle with diagonal line | In control system (logical location) | Bumpless transfer on sensor failure |
| **Control Valve (FV, TV, LV, PV)** | Bow-tie symbol + actuator | On the process line | Air-To-Open (ATO) or Air-To-Close (ATC) |
| **Indicator (PI, TI, LI, FI)** | Circle | On or near the tap point | Read-only, no failure mode |
| **Switch (PSHH, LSHL, FSL)** | Diamond (per ISA-5.1 newer revisions) | On the process line | Discrete output; fails to safe state |
| **Safety Valve (PSV, PRV)** | Spring-loaded disc symbol | On vessel or line | Opens at set pressure; recloses on cool-down |

---

## 3.4 Design Verification Calculations for Safety Valves (Cross-Reference)

While P&ID itself is non-mathematical, a KTU question may ask to **size or verify a PSV** referenced on the P&ID. The simplified API-520 orifice equation is:

$$
A = \frac{W_{\text{required}}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_1}{V} \cdot \dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T}}}
$$

Where:
- $A$ = required effective discharge area (mm² or in²)
- $W_{\text{required}}$ = required relief rate (kg/h or lb/h)
- $C$ = coefficient based on gas/liquid
- $K_d$ = coefficient of discharge
- $K_b$ = capacity correction factor
- $K_c$ = combination correction
- $P_1$ = upstream relieving pressure (kPa absolute)
- $V$ = specific volume
- $M$ = molecular weight
- $T$ = relieving temperature (K)
- $Z$ = compressibility factor

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** The PSV orifice size selected from API 526 is then **tagged** on the P&ID (e.g., `PSV-101 4D1`). The connection line, inlet/outlet piping, and rupture disc (if any) **must** be shown on the P&ID — this is a common exam-pitfall.

---

## 3.5 Pitfall Matrix — Why Students Lose Marks

| Common Mistake | Marks Lost | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Drawing **all valves and instruments on a PFD** | 2–3 | PFD shows only major loops |
| Using **wrong ISA tag format** (e.g., FCT-101) | 1 | First letter = variable; later letters = functions |
| **Omitting PSV** on a heated vessel | 2 | Every heat-source vessel needs a PSV per ASME BPVC |
| **Forgetting fail-safe mode** of control valve | 1 | State ATO or ATC explicitly |
| Not distinguishing **indicator vs. transmitter vs. controller** | 1 | Bubble type per ISA-5.1 |
| Missing **drain/vent** connections on vessel | 1 | Always show low-point drain and high-point vent |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) in terms of purpose, information content, and use in safety studies.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 50–70 words):**

A **PFD** is a conceptual diagram showing *major equipment*, *main process streams*, and *key operating conditions* (T, P, flow) developed during basic engineering to communicate the process concept. A **P&ID** is a detailed engineering document showing *every pipe, valve, instrument, control loop, and safety device* developed during detailed engineering for construction and operation. The P&ID is the **primary document used in HAZOP and LOPA studies**, whereas the PFD is used for preliminary process understanding. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Decode the following ISA-5.1 instrument tags and state the function of each: (a) PT-205, (b) FT-310, (c) LAHH-415.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Tag | Decoding | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PT-205** | P = Pressure, T = Transmitter | Sends 4–20 mA signal proportional to measured pressure to the DCS; no local indication. |
| **FT-310** | F = Flow, T = Transmitter | Converts measured flow (via orifice, vortex, magmeter, etc.) to a standard 4–20 mA signal for control/indication. |
| **LAHH-415** | L = Level, A = Alarm, HH = High-High | Activates a **second-stage high-level alarm** (audible/visual), typically initiating automatic corrective action (e.g., close inlet valve). |

*(1 mark each — 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **key features and components of a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**. List at least **six distinct categories** of information conveyed by a P&ID. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure vessel V-101 operates normally at **5 barg** and **120 °C**, and is heated by a 10 barg saturated steam utility. Steam control is via a pneumatic control valve FV-101. The vessel is protected by a spring-loaded Pressure Safety Valve (PSV-101) sized for blocked-outlet case with required relief rate of **8500 kg/h**. Steam supply line is 4-inch Sch 40, carbon steel.

**Compute the following with clear assumptions and show all steps:**
1. The **maximum allowable accumulation** per ASME BPVC Section VIII (set PSV set pressure = MAWP × 1.10 for fire case). Assume MAWP = 7 barg.
2. The **set pressure** of PSV-101.
3. State **three design features** that must be shown on the P&ID for PSV-101 to ensure it operates reliably. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — Key Features of a P&ID (7 marks):**

A P&ID is a comprehensive schematic that depicts the complete mechanical and instrumentation design of a process unit. The **six key categories** of information conveyed are:

1. **Equipment Details** — Tag number, type, size, design T/P, MOC, internals (e.g., V-101, E-201).
2. **Piping Details** — Every line with line number, size, material, service, insulation, and tracing.
3. **Valve Details** — All valves (gate, globe, check, control, safety, relief) with type, size, and actuator.
4. **Instrumentation** — All field instruments with ISA-5.1 tag numbers (TT, PT, FT, LT, etc.).
5. **Control & Interlock Loops** — All feedback, cascade, ratio, override loops; ESD and interlock logic.
6. **Safety Devices & Tie-ins** — PSV, rupture disc, ESDV, flame arrestor, drains, vents, sampling points.

> *(1 mark for neat explanation; 1 mark for sketch representation; 5 × 1 mark for six categories — 7 marks total)*

---

**Part (b) — PSV Sizing & P&ID Features (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)**

Given: MAWP = **7 barg** = $7 \times 10^5$ Pa gauge.

**Step 2 — Compute Set Pressure (Fire Case Accumulation)**

For a **fire case** scenario (per ASME BPVC Section VIII, UG-125), the permissible accumulation is **+10% of MAWP** (for unfired vessels, normally +10% overpressure; for fire case, +20% is used in some interpretations; we apply 10% per most rigorous school-board exam style).

$$
P_{\text{set}} = \text{MAWP} \times (1 + \text{accumulation fraction})
$$

$$
P_{\text{set}} = 7.0 \text{ barg} \times 1.10 = 7.70 \text{ barg}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Allowable Accumulation Pressure (MAAP)**

$$
P_{\text{MAAP}} = P_{\text{set}} \times \text{some standard — for fire case, overpressure} = 7.70 \text{ barg}
$$

The **accumulation** (the pressure rise above MAWP) is:

$$
\Delta P = 7.70 - 7.00 = 0.70 \text{ barg} = 70 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Note the Relief Rate**

The required relief rate of **W = 8500 kg/h** must be discharged at the set pressure of **7.70 barg** for the **fire case**. The orifice area (using API 520 for steam) would then be selected; for this exam, recognizing the rate and pressure is sufficient.

**Step 5 — Three P&ID Design Features for PSV-101 Reliability (3 marks):**

1. **No block valve between PSV and vessel** — direct inlet piping with no restriction.
2. **Inlet piping sized for negligible pressure drop** (max 3% of set pressure) and outlet piped to safe vent location (atmospheric vent, elevated, away from sources of ignition).
3. **Rupture disc upstream** (if process fluid is fouling/coking) with a free-draining gap, **drip-leg** for condensate removal on steam service, and **isolation valve with car-seal open** (CSO) lock for maintenance.

> *(1 mark for set pressure calculation; 1 mark for accumulation; 1 mark for rate recognition; 3 × 1 mark for design features; + 1 mark for assumptions — 7 marks total)*

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the **ISA-5.1 instrumentation symbol system** for a typical **temperature control loop** in a chemical reactor. Identify and explain the role of **five distinct symbols** (transmitter, controller, indicator, control valve, alarm) used in this loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distillation column has the following **cascade temperature–flow control** structure for the reflux:
- Column temperature at tray 8 is measured by TT-301.
- The temperature controller TIC-301 sets the remote setpoint of FIC-302.
- The flow controller FIC-302 receives feedback from FT-302 and drives FV-302 on the reflux line.
- Column is protected by a low-low temperature alarm (TALL-301) and a high-pressure alarm (PAH-302).

**Answer the following:**
1. Draw the **signal-flow block diagram** of the cascade loop, clearly indicating primary (master) and secondary (slave) controllers.
2. State **two advantages** of cascade control over simple feedback control.
3. Identify the **fail-safe action** of FV-302 (the reflux control valve) considering that loss of instrument air should NOT cause flooding of the column. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — ISA-5.1 Symbols in a Temperature Control Loop (7 marks):**

| Symbol | ISA Tag | Role in Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **TT-301** | Temperature Transmitter | Senses reactor temperature via RTD/thermocouple; outputs 4–20 mA to DCS. |
| **TI-301** | Temperature Indicator | Local or DCS display of measured temperature (read-only). |
| **TIC-301** | Temperature Indicating Controller | Compares PV to SP; outputs 4–20 mA (or 3–15 psi) to the control valve. |
| **TV-301** | Temperature Control Valve | Final control element; throttles heating/cooling medium flow. |
| **TAH-301** | Temperature Alarm High | Activates audio/visual alarm when T exceeds high setpoint. |
| **TT-301 + TV-301 Signal Line** | Dashed line (4–20 mA electronic) | Signal transmission path (vs. solid line for process fluid). |

> *(1.5 marks for loop sketch; 5 × 1 mark for symbol identification + role; 0.5 mark for noting signal vs. process line distinction — 7 marks total)*

---

**Part (b) — Cascade Loop Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Signal Flow Block Diagram (3 marks)**

```
Tray 8 Temperature → TT-301 → TIC-301 (Master/Primary)
                                       ↓ (Remote Setpoint)
FT-302 → FIC-302 (Slave/Secondary) → FV-302 → Reflux Flow → Column → Tray 8 T
```

* **Primary (Master) loop:** Tray 8 T → TT-301 → TIC-301 → sets FIC-302 setpoint.
* **Secondary (Slave) loop:** Reflux flow → FT-302 → FIC-302 → FV-302 → reflux.

**Step 2 — Two Advantages of Cascade Control (2 marks):**

1. **Disturbance rejection:** The slave loop (flow) corrects for disturbances in the reflux line (e.g., pump pressure fluctuations) **before** they affect the column temperature. The master loop only handles slow, large-scale thermal disturbances.
2. **Improved dynamic performance:** The slave loop is much faster than the master loop, so the overall closed-loop response is faster and more stable than a single feedback loop.

**Step 3 — Fail-Safe Action of FV-302 (2 marks):**

- **Process Requirement:** Loss of instrument air must **NOT cause flooding** of the column (i.e., reflux flow should be **minimized** or **fully shut off** on air failure).
- **Conclusion:** FV-302 must be **Air-To-Close (ATC)** — i.e., on loss of air signal, the valve closes and stops reflux flow. (Equivalent: ATO with reverse-acting controller; but the **valve fail-safe action** is ATC.)
- **P&ID Annotation:** Mark on the P&ID as `FV-302 ATC, fail closed`.

> *(3 marks for diagram; 2 marks for advantages; 2 marks for fail-safe — 7 marks total)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in P&ID Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Fail Open" vs. "Air-To-Open"** — these are NOT the same. A valve is "fail-open" based on **loss of actuator energy** (air or electric), not the controller signal. State explicitly: **ATO (Air-To-Open) or ATC (Air-To-Close)**.
> 2. **Wrong ISA first letter** — e.g., writing "FT" for a flow **switch** is incorrect; an **FSL** is a flow **switch low**, while an **FT** is a **transmitter** (continuous). A single-letter change = -1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the PSV** — any vessel that can be over-pressurized (heated, pumped in, blocked outlet) **must** have a relief device. Marks awarded for "PSV shown" are routinely 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No signal line vs. process line distinction** — P&IDs use **dashed lines for instrumentation signals (4–20 mA, 3–15 psi)** and **solid lines for process/utility piping**. Confusing these = -1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting drain and vent** — vessels must show low-point drain (D) and high-point vent (V). Omission = -1 mark per occurrence.
> 6. **Not stating units on operating conditions** — write T = 120 °C, P = 5 barg explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **PFD** = "What flows where" — major equipment + main streams + key T, P, flow.
- [ ] **P&ID** = "How to build and control" — every pipe, valve, instrument, interlock.
- [ ] P&ID is the **primary HAZOP and LOPA document** — legally significant under OSHA PSM and IEC 61511.
- [ ] **ISA-5.1** is the universal instrument tagging standard (first letter = variable).
- [ ] Common tags: `TT`/`PT`/`FT`/`LT` = transmitters; `TIC`/`FIC` = controllers; `FV`/`PV` = control valves; `PSV` = pressure safety valve; `ESD` = emergency shutdown.
- [ ] **HH/LL** = High-High / Low-Low (second-stage trips); **H/L** = first-stage alarms.
- [ ] **Line numbering format:** `Size-Service Code-Sequence` (e.g., 4"-P-101A).
- [ ] **Service codes:** P (Process), U (Utility), S (Steam), D (Drain), V (Vent), CW (Cooling Water), IA (Instrument Air), SA (Sample).
- [ ] **Signal lines are dashed**; process lines are **solid** with flow arrows.
- [ ] **Cascade control** uses two controllers: **master (slow) + slave (fast)** for improved disturbance rejection.
- [ ] **Fail-safe mode of valve** is dictated by **process safety** — e.g., reflux valve on a column fails closed to prevent flooding.
- [ ] **PSV set pressure** = MAWP × (1 + accumulation), where accumulation = 10% (single-event) or 20% (fire case) per ASME BPVC.
- [ ] **P&ID symbols:** Vessels (rectangle), Columns (tall rectangle), Exchangers (rectangle with coil), Pumps (circle with impeller), Compressors (circle with diagonal), Valves (bow-tie), Instruments (circle/hexagon/square per ISA).
- [ ] **Drain and vent connections** are mandatory on every pressure vessel.
- [ ] **Rupture disc + PSV** combinations require a free-draining gap; otherwise pressure can build up between them.
- [ ] P&IDs are **never drawn to scale** — relative positioning only, but **signal/piping logic must be exact**.
- [ ] Final deliverable progression: **Simulation → PFD → P&ID → HAZOP → As-Built P&ID**.
- [ ] **KEEP P&IDs UPDATED** — any field modification must be reflected via Management of Change (MOC) process.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Topic: Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)*
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Course: PECHT864 Process Safety Management*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to PHA Techniques: Checklist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to PHA Techniques: Checklist

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a systematic, structured, and disciplined methodology employed to identify, evaluate, and control hazards associated with chemical and process industries. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus for PECHT864, PHA techniques form the cornerstone of Process Safety Management (PSM) as mandated by regulatory frameworks such as **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** and **EPA's Risk Management Program (RMP)**.

Among the family of PHA techniques, the **Checklist Method** is recognized as the most elementary yet highly effective qualitative safety review tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Checklist** is a *systematic, ordered enumeration of items, conditions, or parameters* that is used as an aid in performing a Process Hazard Analysis. The technique relies on the experience and prior knowledge of the design/operating team, comparing the existing system against a standardized list of known hazards, engineering standards, regulatory requirements, and industry best practices to identify potential deviations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Think of a PHA Checklist exactly like a Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist.** ✈️

Imagine a pilot preparing for takeoff. Before every flight, the pilot does not "reinvent the wheel" by figuring out what to check. Instead, they run through a pre-printed list: *"Fuel level?" "Flaps?" "Landing gear?" "Altimeter?"* This list was created over decades of aviation experience, capturing every critical thing that *could* go wrong.

In the **same way**, a chemical engineer performing a Checklist PHA uses a pre-made list of questions specifically designed for their industry (e.g., petroleum refining, pharmaceutical manufacturing, ammonia plants). They go through each item, comparing the facility's design and operations against the checklist. Anything that fails the check or raises a flag becomes a **potential hazard** that needs corrective action.

| Parameter | Aviation Pre-Flight | PHA Checklist |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool Used** | Printed checklist | Hazard review checklist |
| **Knowledge Source** | Decades of accident data | Decades of incident reports |
| **Purpose** | Catch errors before flight | Catch hazards before operation |
| **Format** | Yes/No, quantitative values | Yes/No, qualitative ratings |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **PHA Team Size**: Typically **3–7 experienced members** (KTU standard reference)
- **Checklist Length**: Industry-specific, ranging from **50 to 500+ items**
- **Review Duration**: **2–8 hours** for a simple checklist review
- **Document Standard**: Compliance with **OSHA PSM** and **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines
- **RAGAGEP**: **Recognized and Generally Accepted Good Engineering Practice** — the standard against which checklists are calibrated

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The Checklist method is *qualitative* (descriptive), not *quantitative* (numerical). It answers "What can go wrong?" but not "How likely is it?" or "How severe is it?" Those questions require techniques like **HAZOP** or **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** covered in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The Checklist PHA technique operates on the **principle of precedent-based hazard identification**. Its theoretical foundation rests on three pillars:

### Pillar 1: Heuristic Knowledge Aggregation
The checklist is essentially a **compiled database of historical failures**. It distills the collective wisdom of:
- Past incidents (e.g., Bhopal 1984, Flixborough 1974, Texas City 2005)
- Regulatory codes (ASME, API, NFPA, IEC)
- Vendor recommendations
- Corporate engineering standards

### Pillar 2: Comparative Conformity Assessment
The technique forces a structured comparison:

$$\text{System Compliance} = f(\text{As-Designed vs. Checklist Standard})$$

If the gap is positive (deviation exists), a hazard is flagged.

### Pillar 3: Binary Decision Logic
Each checklist item typically requires a **Yes / No / Not Applicable (NA)** response:

$$R_i = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \rightarrow \text{Compliant, no hazard} \\ \text{No} & \rightarrow \text{Non-compliant, hazard identified} \\ \text{NA} & \rightarrow \text{Item not relevant to system} \end{cases}$$

## 2.2 Hierarchical Classification of Checklists

Checklists are categorized into two primary tiers based on scope and applicability:

### Tier 1: Generic / General Checklists
- **Scope**: Broad, applicable across multiple industries (e.g., any chemical plant)
- **Content**: General items like "Is ventilation adequate?", "Are relief valves installed?", "Is PPE available?"
- **Use Case**: Initial screening, pre-audits, quick safety reviews
- **Advantage**: Requires minimal site-specific knowledge

### Tier 2: Specific / Customized Checklists
- **Scope**: Tailored to a **specific process, unit operation, or piece of equipment**
- **Examples**:
   - *Storage Tank Checklist*: Bonding, grounding, dike capacity, vent sizing, fire protection
   - *Reactor Checklist*: Cooling system redundancy, runaway reaction controls, agitator failure modes
   - *Compressor Checklist*: Vibration monitoring, lube oil systems, surge control
- **Use Case**: Detailed hazard analysis of critical equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners often ask the difference between *Generic* and *Specific* checklists. Memorize that **Generic checklists are developed first, then customized into Specific checklists** by adding/deleting items relevant to the unit under review.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Methodology

The Checklist PHA follows a structured, sequential workflow:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Define scope and boundaries of the analysis | PHA Charter |
