| **2** | Assemble multidisciplinary team (Process, Mechanical, Safety, Operations) | Team Roster |
| **3** | Select or develop appropriate checklist | Approved Checklist |
| **4** | Conduct facility walkdown & document review | Field Data |
| **5** | Compare system against each checklist item | Compliance Matrix |
| **6** | Identify deviations, hazards, and recommendations | Findings Log |
| **7** | Prioritize findings (typically High/Medium/Low) | Risk Ranking |
| **8** | Prepare PHA report and management response | Final Report |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Hazard Identification Rate | $H_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{hazards}}{T_{review}}$ | Items flagged per hour |
| Compliance Ratio | $C_{ratio} = \dfrac{N_{compliant}}{N_{total}}$ | Percentage of checklist items passed |
| Coverage Index | $K_c = \dfrac{N_{applicable}}{N_{total}}$ | Measures checklist relevance |
| Team Effectiveness Score | $E_t = \alpha N_{chem} + \beta N_{mech} + \gamma N_{safety}$ | Weighted team expertise |
| Risk Priority (Simplified) | $R_{priority} = S \times L$ | Severity × Likelihood (ordinal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Checklist technique is widely used during **HAZOP pre-screening**, **Management of Change (MOC) reviews**, **pre-startup safety reviews (PSSR)**, and **periodic compliance audits**. It is the *go-to tool when time, expertise, or resources are limited*.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OSHA PSM Audits**: Used as a primary tool during compliance audits
- **Insurance Risk Assessments**: Insurers use checklists to evaluate insured facilities
- **Greenfield Project Reviews**: Engineers use checklists during design phase to ensure standards compliance
- **Operating Plant Routine Reviews**: Annual safety reviews of operating units
- **Hazardous Area Classification**: Verifying zoning of electrical equipment in explosive atmospheres
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Compliance Score for a Facility

**Scenario:** A chemical storage facility is being reviewed using a 50-item checklist. During the review, the team identifies the following results:

- Items marked **Yes** (Compliant): $N_{yes} = 38$
- Items marked **No** (Non-compliant / Hazard): $N_{no} = 8$
- Items marked **NA** (Not Applicable): $N_{NA} = 4$

**Required:** Calculate the Compliance Ratio ($C_{ratio}$) and the Hazard Density Index ($H_d$).

---

### Step 1: Verify Total Item Count

The total items in the checklist must equal the sum of all responses:

$$N_{total} = N_{yes} + N_{no} + N_{NA}$$

$$N_{total} = 38 + 8 + 4 = 50 \text{ items}$$

✅ *Verification passes — checklist is complete.*

---

### Step 2: Calculate the Compliance Ratio

The compliance ratio is defined as the proportion of items that passed the safety check:

$$C_{ratio} = \dfrac{N_{compliant}}{N_{applicable}}$$

where the number of *applicable* items excludes NA responses:

$$N_{applicable} = N_{total} - N_{NA}$$

$$N_{applicable} = 50 - 4 = 46 \text{ items}$$

Substituting into the compliance ratio:

$$C_{ratio} = \dfrac{38}{46}$$

$$\boxed{C_{ratio} \approx 0.826 \text{ or } 82.6\%}$$

---

### Step 3: Calculate the Hazard Density Index

The Hazard Density Index expresses the number of identified hazards per applicable checklist item:

$$H_d = \dfrac{N_{hazards}}{N_{applicable}}$$

$$H_d = \dfrac{8}{46}$$

$$\boxed{H_d \approx 0.174 \text{ or } 17.4\%}$$

---

### Step 4: KTU Interpretation Framework

| Metric | Value | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{ratio}$ | 82.6% | Facility is mostly compliant; 17.4% gap exists |
| $H_d$ | 17.4% | Approximately 1 in every 6 checks reveals a hazard |
| Priority | High | Triggers immediate corrective action protocol |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Checklist vs. Other PHA Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to understand the **relative positioning** of the Checklist method within the broader PHA framework. Below is the analytical comparison matrix:

| Parameter | Checklist | HAZOP | FMEA | What-If | Fault Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Comparison-based | Deviation-based | Failure-mode-based | Brainstorming-based | Top-down logic |
| **Data Required** | Historical standards | P&IDs, process data | Equipment specs | Process knowledge | Failure data |
| **Qualitative/Quantitative** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| **Team Size** | 2–4 | 5–8 | 3–6 | 2–5 | 2–4 |
| **Time Required** | Low (hours) | High (days–weeks) | Medium | Low | Medium–High |
| **Best For** | Routine, standard processes | Complex continuous processes | Mechanical equipment | Novel processes | Critical high-consequence systems |
| **Output** | Yes/No findings | Cause–Consequence table | RPN score | Hazard scenarios | Minimal cut sets |
| **Drawback** | Misses novel hazards | Time-intensive, expert-heavy | Component-focused only | Highly subjective | Requires failure rate data |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A 14-mark question frequently asks: *"Compare the Checklist method with HAZOP and justify when each is preferred."* The matrix above provides a structured comparative answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Check Item Risk Priority

For each failed checklist item, a risk priority number is computed:

$$R_{priority,i} = S_i \times L_i \times D_i$$

where:
- $S_i$ = Severity score (1–5)
- $L_i$ = Likelihood score (1–5)
- $D_i$ = Detectability score (1–5)

**Example Calculation:**
A failed item on emergency isolation valve has:
- $S = 5$ (catastrophic if failure occurs)
- $L = 3$ (occasionally occurs)
- $D = 2$ (highly detectable)

$$R_{priority} = 5 \times 3 \times 2 = 30$$

This score (30) indicates a **high-priority recommendation** requiring immediate corrective action.

---

## 3.4 Advantages and Limitations — Detailed Analysis

### ✅ Advantages (Why Use Checklists)

1. **Simplicity**: Minimal training required; can be used by junior engineers
2. **Speed**: Rapid identification of well-known hazards
3. **Documentation**: Generates structured, auditable records
4. **Compliance**: Direct verification against regulatory standards
5. **Cost-Effective**: Lower resource demand compared to HAZOP
6. **Knowledge Preservation**: Encodes institutional and industry experience

### ❌ Limitations (Why Checklists Fail)

1. **Novelty Blindness**: Cannot identify hazards *never seen before* (e.g., novel chemistries, new process routes)
2. **Complacency**: Teams may give "Yes" answers without genuine verification
3. **Rigid Format**: Discourages creative or out-of-box thinking
4. **Outdated Lists**: Checklists not updated after major incidents lose value
5. **No Cause-Consequence Link**: Identifies *what* is wrong, not *why* or *what next*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** Students often write "Checklist is the best PHA method." This is **incorrect**. The Checklist is *one of the simplest* methods, suited for routine and standardized reviews. For complex or novel processes, **HAZOP or What-If/Checklist hybrid methods** are mandated by KTU 2024 syllabus recommendations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Checklist PHA Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> B[Step 2: Assemble Multidisciplinary Team]
    B --> C[Step 3: Select or Develop Checklist]
    C --> D[Step 4: Conduct Facility Walkdown and Document Review]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply Checklist Item by Item]
    E --> F{Item Compliant?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Mark Compliant and Continue]
    F -- No --> H[Document Hazard and Recommendation]
    F -- NA --> I[Mark Not Applicable]
    G --> J{All Items Reviewed?}
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Step 6: Prioritize Findings H M L]
    K --> L[Step 7: Prepare PHA Report]
    L --> M[Step 8: Management Review and Action Plan]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Information Flow in Checklist PHA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Historical Incident Database] --> D[Checklist Repository]
    B[Regulatory Standards] --> D
    C[Vendor Manuals] --> D
    E[Corporate Safety Standards] --> D
    D --> F[Selected Checklist]
    F --> G[Site Facility Data]
    G --> H[Comparison Engine]
    H --> I[Deviation Register]
    I --> J[Risk Ranking Module]
    J --> K[PHA Final Report]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHA Checklists] --> B[Generic Checklist]
    A --> C[Specific Checklist]
    B --> B1[Plant-wide Safety Items]
    B --> B2[Regulatory Compliance Items]
    B --> B3[General Process Items]
    C --> C1[Equipment Specific]
    C --> C2[Process Specific]
    C --> C3[Material Specific]
    C1 --> D1[Compressor Checklist]
    C1 --> D2[Tank Checklist]
    C1 --> D3[Reactor Checklist]
    C2 --> E1[Distillation Column]
    C2 --> E2[Heat Exchanger]
    C3 --> F1[Flammable Materials]
    C3 --> F2[Toxic Materials]
    C3 --> F3[Reactive Chemicals]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Matrix Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose PHA Technique] --> B{Is process novel?}
    B -- No --> C{Is it a routine review?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Checklist Method]
    C -- No --> E[Use What-If Method]
    B -- Yes --> F{Is detailed analysis needed?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use HAZOP Method]
    F -- No --> H[Use FMEA Method]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define PHA Checklist. Mention any two advantages of using the Checklist method.**

**Model Answer:**
A **PHA Checklist** is a systematic enumeration of items, conditions, and parameters used to identify hazards in a process facility by comparing the existing system against established standards, historical incident data, and engineering best practices. It operates on binary Yes/No/NA logic.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Simplicity**: The method is easy to use and requires minimal specialized training, making it suitable for routine reviews.
2. **Time and Cost Efficient**: It enables rapid hazard identification within hours, consuming fewer resources than complex techniques like HAZOP.

*(Examiner Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between a Generic Checklist and a Specific Checklist.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Generic Checklist | Specific Checklist |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broad, multi-industry applicability | Narrow, single equipment/process focus |
| **Development** | Based on general industry standards | Derived from generic checklist by customization |
| **Examples** | "Is fire protection available?" | "Is the reactor's cooling water backup pump operational?" |

*(Examiner Note: 3 distinct points are required for full marks.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain in detail the Checklist method of Process Hazard Analysis. Discuss its procedural steps, advantages, limitations, and applications.**

#### Part (a) — Procedural Steps and Methodology (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Checklist method follows a **structured eight-step procedure**:

1. **Define Scope and Boundaries**: Clearly outline the unit operations, equipment, and process conditions under review.

2. **Assemble the PHA Team**: Form a multidisciplinary team consisting of:
   - Process Engineer
   - Mechanical/Maintenance Engineer
   - Safety Officer
   - Operations Supervisor
   - Instrument/Control Engineer

3. **Select or Develop the Checklist**: Choose from existing industry checklists or develop a customized one based on:
   - Previous incident reports
   - RAGAGEP standards
   - OEM manuals

4. **Conduct Walkdown and Document Review**: Physically inspect the facility and review P&IDs, PFDs, operating procedures, and maintenance logs.

5. **Apply the Checklist Item-by-Item**: For each item, record a response (Yes/No/NA) and provide justification.

6. **Identify Deviations and Hazards**: Any "No" response becomes a candidate hazard requiring documentation.

7. **Prioritize Findings**: Classify each hazard as **High, Medium, or Low** using a risk matrix.

8. **Prepare PHA Report**: Document findings, recommendations, and corrective action plans with target completion dates.

#### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Applications (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Easy to understand and implement
2. Requires minimal time and expertise
3. Provides auditable documentation
4. Excellent for verifying regulatory compliance
5. Preserves institutional knowledge

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot identify novel or unprecedented hazards
2. May lead to complacency if not updated
3. Lacks cause-consequence analysis
4. Overly rigid format restricts creative thinking

**Applications:**
- Pre-startup Safety Reviews (PSSR)
- Routine plant safety audits
- Management of Change (MOC) evaluations
- Compliance audits for OSHA PSM
- Insurance risk assessments

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Compare the Checklist method with the HAZOP method of PHA. Under what circumstances would you recommend each technique?**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Checklist | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Comparative, based on standards | Deviation-based using guide words |
| **Complexity** | Low | High |
| **Team Required** | 2–4 members | 5–8 specialized members |
| **Time Required** | 2–8 hours | Several days to weeks |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Output Format** | Yes/No compliance matrix | Cause–Consequence table |
| **Best Application** | Standard, routine processes | Complex, novel processes |
| **Identification of Novel Hazards** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Documentation Detail** | Moderate | Extensive |
| **Training Required** | Minimal | Extensive (1–2 weeks) |

#### Part (b) — Recommendation Scenarios (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Use the Checklist Method When:**
1. The process is **standard and well-understood** (e.g., a refinery atmospheric distillation unit).
2. A **rapid screening** is required before a more detailed review.
3. **Limited expertise or time** is available.
4. The review is for **routine compliance verification**.
5. **Auditing** an existing facility against established codes.

**Use the HAZOP Method When:**
1. The process involves **novel chemistry** or new technology.
2. The facility handles **highly hazardous chemicals**.
3. A detailed **cause–consequence analysis** is required.
4. The process has **complex interconnections** between multiple unit operations.
5. Regulatory mandates **explicitly require** HAZOP (e.g., OSHA PSM for highly toxic chemicals).

**Hybrid Approach:** In practice, the **What-If/Checklist** method is often used as a preliminary analysis, followed by a full **HAZOP study** for critical nodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students confuse *Checklist* with *HAZOP* — remember Checklist uses **standards comparison**, HAZOP uses **deviation analysis**.
> 2. Many students forget to mention the **NA (Not Applicable)** option — this is a critical concept.
> 3. Failing to distinguish **Generic vs. Specific** checklists costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. Not citing **OSHA PSM** or **RAGAGEP** as the basis reduces industrial context marks.
> 5. Students often miss the **practical limitations** of checklists — examiners reward students who acknowledge the *novelty blindness* issue.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Checklist PHA** is the *simplest and most accessible* PHA technique
- ✅ It uses a **Yes / No / NA** binary decision format
- ✅ Two main types: **Generic Checklists** and **Specific Checklists**
- ✅ Based on **RAGAGEP** (Recognized and Generally Accepted Good Engineering Practice)
- ✅ Suitable for **routine, standard, and well-understood processes**
- ✅ Inadequate for **novel processes** or **complex cause-consequence analysis**
- ✅ Eight-step methodology: *Scope → Team → Checklist → Walkdown → Apply → Identify → Prioritize → Report*
- ✅ Compliance Ratio formula: $C_{ratio} = \dfrac{N_{compliant}}{N_{applicable}}$
- ✅ Risk Priority: $R = S \times L \times D$
- ✅ Often combined with **What-If analysis** as a hybrid pre-HAZOP tool
- ✅ Documented under regulatory frameworks like **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)**
- ✅ Not a substitute for **HAZOP** in high-hazard or novel chemical processes

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: What-If Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** What-If Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What-If Analysis (WIA)** is a **qualitative, team-based, brainstorming hazard evaluation technique** used in Process Safety Management (PSM) to identify potential hazards, operability problems, and unintended consequences by systematically posing hypothetical "What-If" questions about a process, system, or operation.

According to **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** standards, What-If Analysis is recognized as an acceptable hazard analysis methodology for covering process hazards, engineering and administrative controls, and consequences of failure.

The technique involves a multidisciplinary team reviewing:
- Process flow diagrams (PFDs)
- Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)
- Plot plans
- Operating procedures

…and asking structured questions beginning with "**What if...?**" for every conceivable deviation from the design intent.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of What-If Analysis like a Doctor's Diagnostic Checklist.**

When a doctor examines a patient, they don't just check one symptom — they go through a structured mental checklist:
- *"What if the patient has high BP?"*
- *"What if there's a family history of diabetes?"*
- *"What if this medication reacts with another?"*

Each **"What if"** question triggers:
1. An identification of a **potential problem**
2. A determination of its **consequences**
3. A decision on **existing safeguards**
4. A recommendation for **further action**

In the same way, an engineering team walks through a chemical plant on paper and asks *"What if the cooling water pump fails?"* or *"What if the operator opens the wrong valve?"* — systematically probing for weak spots before an incident exposes them.

---

### 1.3 Position in the Process Safety Toolkit

| Technique | Type | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **What-If Analysis** | Qualitative, Brainstorming | Early-stage design, simple systems |
| **HAZOP** | Qualitative, Keyword-based | Detailed design of complex chemical processes |
| **FMEA** | Qualitative/Quantitative | Equipment failure analysis |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Quantitative, Deductive | Specific accident pathways |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** What-If Analysis is often used as a **screening tool** before applying more resource-intensive techniques like HAZOP or when the process is not complex enough to warrant a full HAZOP study.

---

### 1.4 Core Metrics and Standards

- **Team Size:** Typically **4–8 members** (KTU-recommended range based on CCPS Guidelines)
- **Study Duration:** Few days to several weeks depending on complexity
- **Standard Reference:** **AIChE/CCPS "Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures"** (3rd Edition)
- **Regulatory Backing:** Listed under OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119) and EPA RMP (40 CFR Part 68)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of What-If Analysis

The technique is designed to achieve the following engineered outcomes:

- 🔍 **Hazard Identification:** Uncover potential chemical, mechanical, electrical, or human-factor hazards.
- ⚠️ **Consequence Assessment:** Evaluate the severity of consequences if the hazard is realized.
- 🛡️ **Safeguard Evaluation:** Identify existing engineering, administrative, and PPE controls.
- 📋 **Recommendation Generation:** Propose additional measures to mitigate unacceptable risks.
- 📚 **Documentation:** Create a structured record for regulatory compliance and future reference.

---

### 2.2 Structured Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The What-If Analysis follows a **systematic 7-stage workflow** as endorsed by CCPS.

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Objectives**
- Establish system boundaries (entire plant, specific unit, or single operation).
- Identify the goals: compliance, design review, incident investigation, or modification review.

**Step 2 — Assemble the Team**
A multidisciplinary team ensures diverse perspectives:

| Role | Expertise Contribution |
|---|---|
| Team Leader / Facilitator | Methodology expert, guides discussion |
| Process Engineer | P&ID, chemistry, process flow knowledge |
| Operations / Plant Operator | Practical field experience, "what really happens" |
| Maintenance Engineer | Equipment reliability, failure modes |
| Safety Engineer / EHS | Hazard recognition, regulatory standards |
| Instrument/Control Engineer | DCS, interlocks, alarm systems |
| Project Engineer (if design stage) | Design intent, specifications |

**Step 3 — Gather Documentation**
- Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)
- Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)
- Operating Procedures & SOPs
- Equipment datasheets
- Plot plans and layout drawings
- Previous incident reports
- Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS/SDS)

**Step 4 — Generate "What-If" Questions**
The facilitator prompts the team with questions structured as:

$$ \text{Question Template} = \text{"What if} \; + \; \text{[Initiating Event]} \; + \; \text{?"} $$

Examples:
- *What if the feed pump fails?*
- *What if the temperature controller malfunctions?*
- *What if valve HV-101 is left open during startup?*
- *What if there is a loss of instrument air?*
- *What if the operator misreads the gauge?*
- *What if a lightning strike hits the storage tank?*

**Step 5 — Identify Consequences and Causes**
For each question, the team lists:
- **Causes:** Why might this happen?
- **Consequences:** What would happen if it did?
- **Severity:** How bad would it be? (Typically Low / Medium / High)
- **Likelihood:** How probable? (Rare / Possible / Frequent)

**Step 6 — Identify Existing Safeguards**
- **Engineering Controls:** Relief valves, interlocks, alarms, trip systems, containment dikes
- **Administrative Controls:** SOPs, training, permits, supervision
- **PPE:** Personal protective equipment as last line of defense

**Step 7 — Develop Recommendations**
For risks deemed unacceptable, the team recommends:
- Additional safeguards
- Procedural changes
- Further analysis (e.g., LOPA, QRA)
- Design modifications

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> What-If Analysis is largely **qualitative**, but it uses **risk-ranking logic** borrowed from quantitative methods.

| Parameter | Symbol / Notation | Formula / Logic | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Priority** | $R$ | $R = S \times L$ | Dimensionless Index |
| **Severity** | $S$ | Categorical: 1 (Minor) → 4 (Catastrophic) | Qualitative Scale |
| **Likelihood** | $L$ | Categorical: 1 (Rare) → 4 (Frequent) | Qualitative Scale |
| **Risk Acceptability** | — | If $R \geq 8$: Action Required ; If $R \leq 4$: Acceptable | Boolean |
| **Coverage Ratio** | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{N_{\text{hazards identified}}}{N_{\text{questions posed}}} \times 100\%$ | **%** |
| **Team Effectiveness Index** | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{N_{\text{unique hazards}}}{N_{\text{team members}}}$ | Hazards / Member |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** In KTU examinations, the qualitative logic ($R = S \times L$) is the most commonly tested numerical formulation related to What-If Analysis.

---

### 2.4 Application Domains in Engineering

What-If Analysis is widely applied in:

- 🛢️ **Oil & Gas:** Refinery units, offshore platforms, pipeline operations
- 🧪 **Chemical Plants:** Reactors, distillation columns, storage facilities
- 💊 **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch reactors, fermentation, drying operations
- ⚙️ **Manufacturing:** Assembly lines, robotics, hydraulic presses
- 🏗️ **Construction:** Crane operations, excavation, hot work
- 🛡️ **Military / Defense:** Munitions handling, propellant storage

The technique is particularly valuable during:
- **Early design stages** (when changes are inexpensive)
- **Management of Change (MOC)** reviews
- **Pre-startup Safety Reviews (PSSR)**
- **Periodic Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) revalidations**

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Risk Score Derivation (KTU High-Yield)

Let us derive the standard **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** used in ranking What-If findings:

**Given:**
- Severity $S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ corresponding to Minor, Moderate, Major, Catastrophic
- Likelihood $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ corresponding to Rare, Unlikely, Possible, Frequent

**Step 1:** Define the **Risk Function** $R$ as the product of severity and likelihood:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= f(S, L) \\[6pt]
R &= S \times L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the minimum values to determine the **minimum risk score**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\min} &= S_{\min} \times L_{\min} \\[6pt]
R_{\min} &= 1 \times 1 \\[6pt]
R_{\min} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the maximum values to determine the **maximum risk score**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= S_{\max} \times L_{\max} \\[6pt]
R_{\max} &= 4 \times 4 \\[6pt]
R_{\max} &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Define the **Acceptability Threshold** using industry-standard banding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Band} &= 
\begin{cases}
\text{Low (Acceptable)} & \text{if } R \leq 4 \\[4pt]
\text{Medium (Caution)} & \text{if } 5 \leq R \leq 9 \\[4pt]
\text{High (Action Required)} & \text{if } R \geq 10
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** This 4×4 matrix is the **standard scoring framework** referenced in KTU Process Safety modules. Always show your banding logic explicitly for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Propane Storage Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A 10,000-gallon propane bullet tank with a pressure relief valve, level transmitter, and remote shut-off valve. Apply What-If Analysis for selected scenarios.

**Question 1:** *"What if the level transmitter fails and shows a false low reading?"*

| Parameter | Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Cause** | Instrument failure, calibration drift, electrical fault |
| **Consequence** | Operator continues filling → tank overfills → liquid expansion → relief valve lifts → propane release → fire/explosion risk |
| **Severity ($S$)** | 4 (Catastrophic) |
| **Likelihood ($L$)** | 2 (Unlikely) |
| **Risk Score ($R$)** | $R = 4 \times 2 = 8$ → **Medium (Action Required)** |
| **Existing Safeguards** | Independent high-level switch, relief valve, operator rounds |
| **Recommendation** | Install redundant level transmitter; add high-high level alarm with automatic shutoff |

**Question 2:** *"What if the operator leaves during shift change without proper handover?"*

| Parameter | Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Cause** | Procedural non-compliance, training gap |
| **Consequence** | Incoming operator unaware of abnormal condition → delayed response → escalation |
| **Severity ($S$)** | 3 (Major) |
| **Likelihood ($L$)** | 3 (Possible) |
| **Risk Score ($R$)** | $R = 3 \times 3 = 9$ → **Medium (Action Required)** |
| **Existing Safeguards** | Shift handover SOP, supervisor presence |
| **Recommendation** | Mandatory written handover log; checklist verification; competency-based training |

**Question 3:** *"What if ambient temperature rises above 45 °C during summer?"*

| Parameter | Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Cause** | Environmental factor |
| **Consequence** | Propane vapor pressure increases → relief valve may lift → vapor cloud formation |
| **Severity ($S$)** | 3 (Major) |
| **Likelihood ($L$)** | 2 (Unlikely — design margin exists) |
| **Risk Score ($R$)** | $R = 3 \times 2 = 6$ → **Medium (Monitor)** |
| **Existing Safeguards** | Pressure relief valve sized for 50 °C, water spray cooling, shading |
| **Recommendation** | Re-evaluate relief valve sizing under climate change projections |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — What-If vs. Other Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 questions comparing hazard analysis methods.

| Feature | What-If Analysis | HAZOP | FMEA | Checklist |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Brainstorming | Keyword-guided | Component-based | Pre-defined list |
| **Best Stage** | Early design / screening | Detailed engineering | Equipment design | Compliance audit |
| **Team Required** | 4–8 members | 5–8 members | 3–5 members | 1–2 members |
| **Time Required** | Days | Weeks | Weeks | Hours |
| **Quantitative?** | No (qualitative) | No (qualitative) | Yes (RPN) | No |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | Very Low |
| **Coverage** | Broad, scenario-based | Exhaustive, deviation-based | Equipment-focused | Limited |
| **Documentation** | Structured table | Detailed worksheet | Equipment register | Pass/Fail list |
| **Regulatory Acceptance** | ✅ Yes (OSHA PSM) | ✅ Yes (preferred) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (limited) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 What-If Analysis Workflow (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Define Scope and Objectives"] --> B["Assemble Multidisciplinary Team"]
    B --> C["Gather Process Documentation"]
    C --> D["Divide System into Nodes or Sections"]
    D --> E["Brainstorm What-If Questions"]
    E --> F["Identify Causes and Consequences"]
    F --> G["Evaluate Existing Safeguards"]
    G --> H["Calculate Risk Score R equals S times L"]
    H --> I{"Is Risk Acceptable?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Document and Close"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Develop Recommendations"]
    K --> L["Assign Action Items with Owners"]
    L --> M["Track to Closure"]
    M --> J
    J --> N["Final PHA Report"]
```

---

### 4.2 Team Composition Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoreTeam["Core What-If Analysis Team"]
        TL["Team Leader Facilitator"]
        PE["Process Engineer"]
        OP["Operations Specialist"]
        ME["Maintenance Engineer"]
        SE["Safety and EHS Expert"]
        IC["Instrumentation Engineer"]
    end
    
    subgraph Support["Optional Support"]
        MG["Plant Management"]
        RE["Regulatory Liaison"]
        EX["External Subject Matter Expert"]
    end
    
    TL --- PE
    TL --- OP
    TL --- ME
    TL --- SE
    TL --- IC
    PE --- Support
    SE --- Support
```

---

### 4.3 Risk Decision Matrix (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Initiating Event What If Question"] --> Cause["Identify Causes"]
    Cause --> Conseq["Determine Consequences"]
    Conseq --> Sev["Assign Severity S 1 to 4"]
    Sev --> Like["Assign Likelihood L 1 to 4"]
    Like --> Calc["Calculate Risk R equals S times L"]
    Calc --> Band{"Risk Band"}
    Band -- "R equals 1 to 4" --> Accept["Acceptable Document Only"]
    Band -- "R equals 5 to 9" --> Caution["Caution Review Safeguards"]
    Band -- "R equals 10 to 16" --> Action["Action Required New Recommendations"]
    Caution --> Verify["Verify Adequacy of Controls"]
    Verify --> Adequate{"Adequate?"}
    Adequate -- "Yes" --> Accept
    Adequate -- "No" --> Action
    Action --> Recommend["Develop Mitigation Recommendations"]
    Recommend --> Close["Assign Owner and Target Date"]
```

---

### 4.4 What-If Analysis Information Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["Input Documents"]
        PFD["Process Flow Diagram"]
        PID["P and ID Drawings"]
        SOP["Operating Procedures"]
        MSDS["Material Safety Data Sheets"]
    end
    
    subgraph Process["What-If Analysis Process"]
        Q1["Generate What-If Questions"]
        Q2["Brainstorm with Team"]
        Q3["Rank Risks"]
    end
    
    subgraph Outputs["Output Deliverables"]
        WL["What-If Worksheet"]
        RR["Risk Register"]
        REC["Recommendations Tracker"]
        RPT["Final PHA Report"]
    end
    
    Inputs --> Process
    Process --> Outputs
    REC --> Follow["Follow-up Audits"]
    REC --> MOC["Management of Change"]
    RPT --> Compliance["Regulatory Compliance"]
```

---

### 4.5 Advantages vs. Limitations Matrix

| ✅ Advantages | ❌ Limitations |
|---|---|
| Simple, requires no specialized software | Highly dependent on team experience |
| Low cost and rapid execution | May miss subtle or complex interactions |
| Flexible — applicable at any project stage | No systematic coverage guarantee |
| Excellent team-building and knowledge-sharing | Qualitative only — no numerical risk values |
| Effective for "what-if" thinking beyond normal scenarios | Cannot easily handle multi-failure events |
| Accepted by OSHA, EPA, and most regulators | Documentation can be inconsistent across teams |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define What-If Analysis. List any four situations where it is most appropriately applied.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
What-If Analysis is a qualitative, team-based hazard identification technique in which a multidisciplinary team systematically generates hypothetical "What-If" questions about a process to identify potential deviations from design intent, assess consequences, and recommend safeguards.

**Four Appropriate Applications:**
1. Early-stage process design reviews (when changes are low-cost)
2. **Management of Change (MOC)** evaluations for plant modifications
3. Periodic **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** revalidations
4. Pre-startup Safety Reviews (PSSR) for new or modified facilities
5. Simple, low-complexity processes where HAZOP is not warranted

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the typical team composition for conducting a What-If Analysis and the role of the Team Leader.*

**Model Answer:**

**Team Composition (4–8 members):**
- Team Leader / Facilitator
- Process Engineer
- Operations / Plant Operator
- Maintenance Engineer
- Safety Engineer
- Instrumentation / Control Engineer

**Role of Team Leader:**
1. Guides the brainstorming session using the What-If methodology
2. Ensures the team stays focused on scope and objectives
3. Maintains documentation discipline (worksheet, recommendations)
4. Resolves disagreements and ensures consensus on risk rankings
5. Verifies completeness of coverage before closing the study

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module exams allow students to choose **ONE out of TWO** questions from each module. Both options are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain in detail the step-by-step methodology for conducting a What-If Analysis as recommended by CCPS. State the importance of multidisciplinary teams in the process.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A reactor operates at 5 bar pressure and 180 °C with the following What-If scenarios identified:

| Scenario | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling water pump failure | 4 | 3 |
| Operator opens bypass valve | 3 | 4 |
| Power failure during exothermic reaction | 4 | 2 |

Calculate the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for each scenario using $R = S \times L$ and classify them into Low / Medium / High risk bands.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Methodology:**

1. **Define Scope:** Clearly delineate the system boundaries, study objectives, and deliverables.
2. **Assemble Team:** Form a 4–8 member multidisciplinary team covering process, operations, maintenance, safety, and instrumentation.
3. **Gather Documentation:** Collect PFDs, P&IDs, SOPs, MSDS, and prior incident reports.
4. **System Partitioning:** Break the process into logical nodes or sections (e.g., feed system, reactor, heat exchanger, product storage).
5. **Generate Questions:** Brainstorm "What-If" questions covering equipment failure, human error, utility loss, and external events.
6. **Identify Causes and Consequences:** For each question, document *why* it could happen and *what* would result.
7. **Evaluate Safeguards:** List existing engineering and administrative controls.
8. **Rank Risks:** Apply $R = S \times L$ logic and categorize acceptability.
9. **Develop Recommendations:** Propose additional safeguards for unacceptable risks.
10. **Document and Report:** Produce a What-If worksheet, risk register, and final PHA report.

**Importance of Multidisciplinary Teams:**

- **Diverse Expertise:** Each member brings unique perspective — engineers see design intent, operators see real-world failures, safety experts see regulatory gaps.
- **Reduced Bias:** Single-discipline teams tend to overlook blind spots.
- **Improved Buy-in:** Team members who participate in the analysis become champions for implementing recommendations.
- **Knowledge Transfer:** Cross-functional learning occurs during the workshop.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** OSHA PSM explicitly requires multi-disciplinary participation.

---

**(b) Risk Score Calculations:**

**Scenario 1 — Cooling Water Pump Failure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= S_1 \times L_1 \\[4pt]
R_1 &= 4 \times 3 \\[4pt]
R_1 &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

Classification: $R = 12 \geq 10$ → **HIGH RISK (Action Required)**

---

**Scenario 2 — Operator Opens Bypass Valve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= S_2 \times L_2 \\[4pt]
R_2 &= 3 \times 4 \\[4pt]
R_2 &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

Classification: $R = 12 \geq 10$ → **HIGH RISK (Action Required)**

---

**Scenario 3 — Power Failure During Exothermic Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3 &= S_3 \times L_3 \\[4pt]
R_3 &= 4 \times 2 \\[4pt]
R_3 &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Classification: $5 \leq R = 8 \leq 9$ → **MEDIUM RISK (Review Safeguards)**

---

**Summary Table:**

| Scenario | S | L | R = S × L | Risk Band |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling water pump failure | 4 | 3 | 12 | High |
| Operator opens bypass valve | 3 | 4 | 12 | High |
| Power failure during exothermic reaction | 4 | 2 | 8 | Medium |

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Compare and contrast What-If Analysis with HAZOP and FMEA in terms of methodology, applicability, advantages, and limitations.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the structure of a typical What-If Analysis worksheet. For a solvent storage facility, generate **five valid What-If questions** and provide their corresponding causes, consequences, and recommended safeguards.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis:**

| Feature | What-If | HAZOP | FMEA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Open-ended brainstorming | Deviation-based (guide words) | Equipment-component based |
| **Best Stage** | Early design / screening | Detailed engineering design | Equipment design & reliability |
| **Coverage** | Broad, scenario-driven | Exhaustive node-by-node | Component-by-component |
| **Quantitative Output** | No | No | Yes (RPN calculation) |
| **Cost & Duration** | Low cost, days | High cost, weeks | Medium cost, weeks |
| **Team Size** | 4–8 | 5–8 | 3–5 |
| **Strength** | Flexibility, simplicity | Thoroughness, regulatory gold-standard | Quantitative ranking |
| **Limitation** | Dependent on team skill | Time-intensive | Limited to single-component failures |
| **Output Document** | What-If worksheet + risk table | HAZOP nodes + deviations | FMEA register |
| **Best For** | Simple processes, MOC, PSSR | Complex continuous chemical processes | Mechanical/electrical systems |

---

**(b) What-If Worksheet Structure + Example Questions:**

**Standard Worksheet Columns:**

1. Question Number
2. What-If Question
3. Causes
4. Consequences
4. Severity (1–4)
5. Likelihood (1–4)
6. Risk Score (R = S × L)
7. Existing Safeguards
8. Recommendations
9. Action Owner / Due Date

**Example Questions for Solvent Storage Facility:**

| # | What-If Question | Causes | Consequences | Recommendations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | *What if the level gauge on Tank T-101 fails and shows a false low reading?* | Instrument drift, calibration error, electrical fault | Operator continues filling; tank overfills; solvent spill; vapor cloud formation | Install redundant level transmitter + high-high level alarm + automatic shutoff |
| 2 | *What if the bund wall around the tank develops cracks?* | Ground settlement, corrosion, impact damage | Loss of secondary containment; spill spreads to drainage; environmental contamination | Routine bund integrity inspection; crack repair protocol; corrosion-resistant coating |
| 3 | *What if the vent flame arrestor is blocked?* | Dust accumulation, polymer fouling, ice formation | Pressure builds during filling; vacuum during withdrawal; tank damage | Regular inspection and cleaning; differential pressure alarm; spare arrestor |
| 4 | *What if there is a lightning strike during a thunderstorm?* | Natural event | Ignition of flammable vapors if released; fire/explosion | Verify lightning protection system; bonding and grounding checks |
| 5 | *What if the firewater pump fails during an emergency?* | Mechanical failure, power loss, valve closed | Inability to cool adjacent tanks; fire escalation; potential domino effect | Diesel-driven backup firewater pump; weekly test runs; redundant jockey pump |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Resulting in Mark Deduction):**
>
> 1. **Confusing What-If with HAZOP:** Writing "no flow," "more flow," "less pressure" guide words in What-If answers. **Guide words are HAZOP, not What-If.** What-If questions must begin with *"What if...?"*
>
> 2. **Omitting the Risk Formula:** Simply listing hazards without calculating $R = S \times L$ costs 2–3 marks. Always show your **scoring logic explicitly**.
>
> 3. **Single-Discipline Team:** Writing "team of engineers" without specifying **multidisciplinary** composition loses marks. Always mention the **specific roles**.
>
> 4. **No Recommendations:** Listing hazards and consequences without proposing **engineering / administrative controls** is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Skipping Documentation:** Failing to mention the **worksheet, risk register, and final PHA report** shows lack of completeness.
>
> 6. **Mixing Severity and Likelihood Definitions:** Students often swap S and L scales. **Severity = how bad; Likelihood = how often.**

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every Exam:**

- ✅ **What-If Analysis = Qualitative + Team-based + Brainstorming** technique.
- ✅ Questions **MUST begin with "What if...?"** — never use HAZOP guide words here.
- ✅ Team composition: **4–8 members**, multi-disciplinary (process, operations, maintenance, safety, instrumentation).
- ✅ Core formula: **$R = S \times L$** where both range from 1 to 4 → $R$ ranges from 1 to 16.
- ✅ Risk Banding: $R \leq 4$ → Low (Acceptable); $5 \leq R \leq 9$ → Medium (Caution); $R \geq 10$ → High (Action Required).
- ✅ Standard reference: **CCPS Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures (3rd Edition)**.
- ✅ Regulatory acceptance: **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** and **EPA 40 CFR Part 68 (RMP)**.
- ✅ Best used for: **early design, simple processes, MOC, PSSR, PHA revalidation**.
- ✅ Key inputs: **PFDs, P&IDs, SOPs, MSDS, incident reports**.
- ✅ Outputs: **What-If worksheet, Risk Register, Recommendations tracker, Final PHA Report**.
- ✅ Limitations: **Team-dependent, qualitative only, no systematic coverage guarantee**.
- ✅ Comparison anchor: **HAZOP is more systematic; What-If is more flexible; FMEA gives RPN.**
- ✅ Facilitator's role is **critical** — guides discussion, maintains documentation, ensures completeness.
- ✅ Recommendations must include **both engineering and administrative controls** where applicable.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 2: What-If Analysis | PECHT864 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis)** is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive, qualitative (and sometimes quantitative) risk assessment technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a system, process, design, or service, by analyzing the **Severity (S)** of their consequences, the **Occurrence (O)** of their causes, and the **Detectability (D)** of the failure before it reaches the customer or environment.

In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** as prescribed by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 and KTU 2024 syllabus, FMEA is a **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** tool that systematically catalogs:
- The **Failure Mode** (the way a component or step can fail)
- The **Effect** of that failure (the consequence on the system/environment)
- The **Cause** of the failure (the root mechanism)

It outputs a numerical **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** which is the core decision metric for prioritizing corrective actions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of FMEA like a Pre-Flight Checklist for a Boeing 737, but for a chemical reactor.**

Imagine you are a mechanic inspecting a car before a long mountain journey. You don't just check the brakes — you list:
- **What can fail?** (Brake pads wearing out, brake fluid leak, ABS sensor malfunction)
- **What happens if it fails?** (Car won't stop, skidding, accident)
- **Why would it fail?** (Heat, low fluid, worn friction material)
- **How likely is it?** (Once a year? Once in 10 years?)
- **How soon will I notice it?** (Dashboard warning? Total silence until catastrophe?)

Now multiply this logic across **every** component of the system, assign numerical scores, and rank them. The top-ranked items get fixed first. **That is FMEA.**

In a chemical plant, FMEA is the engineer's way of asking: *"If this pump, valve, sensor, or operating step deviates from its design intent — what is the worst that can happen to people, the environment, and the asset?"*

## 1.3 Historical & Standards Context

- **1949:** U.S. military procedural MIL-P-1629 (first formal FMEA standard)
- **1960s–70s:** NASA adopted it for Apollo and Space Shuttle programs
- **1977:** Ford Motor Company adapted it for automotive (PFMEA)
- **1993:** QS-9000 mandated it for automotive suppliers
- **2008 (revised 2019):** IEC 60812 / IEC 31010 — the modern global standard
- **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019):** Current industry benchmark aligning U.S. and German automotive practice
- **KTU 2024 Reference:** Aligns with OSHA 1910.119(e), CCPS *Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures*, and IEC 60812

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Failure Mode** | The way in which a component/element could potentially fail to meet its design intent |
| **Effect** | The consequence of the failure on the system, process, environment, or customer |
| **Cause** | The mechanism that triggers the failure mode |
| **Severity (S)** | How serious is the consequence? (1–10) |
| **Occurrence (O)** | How frequently does the cause occur? (1–10) |
| **Detection (D)** | How easily can we detect the failure *before* it propagates? (1–10) |
| **RPN** | Risk Priority Number = S × O × D |
| **SOD Score** | Modern replacement for RPN (used in AIAG-VDA 2019) |
| **Action Priority (AP)** | AIAG-VDA 2019 classification (H/M/L) replacing RPN ranking |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of FMEA (KTU High-Yield — Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three principal FMEA categories are tested in KTU exams. Know the differences cold.

| Type | Abbreviation | Focus | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA** | SFMEA | Whole-system interaction, interfaces, redundancies | Aerospace, nuclear, complex plants |
| **Design FMEA** | DFMEA | Product/component design weaknesses | Mechanical/equipment design stage |
| **Process FMEA** | PFMEA | Manufacturing/processing steps and their variables | Assembly, fabrication, chemical operations |

A fourth emerging variant, **Software FMEA (SWFMEA)**, addresses firmware and PLC logic — but is not part of the KTU PECHT864 core syllabus.

## 2.2 The Seven Classical Steps of FMEA

1. **Define the Scope, System Boundary & Assumptions** — Document what is in-scope and out-of-scope.
2. **Assemble a Cross-Functional Team** — Typically 4–8 members: process engineer, operations, maintenance, quality, safety, instrumentation.
3. **Identify Functions and Failure Modes** — What must each component do? How can it fail to do it?
4. **Determine Effects and Causes** — Downstream consequences and upstream mechanisms.
5. **Score Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D)** — Using standardized 1–10 scales.
6. **Compute RPN and Prioritize** — Calculate and rank.
7. **Recommend Actions and Re-Score** — Lower RPN by reducing S, O, or D; reassign responsibility and target date.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Every numerical problem in KTU FMEA module boils down to these equations. Memorize the scales; understand the logic.

### 2.3.1 Core Risk Equations

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{RPN} = S \times O \times D}
$$

> **Range:** 1 (minimum) to 1000 (maximum)
> **Critical Threshold (KTU convention):** RPN ≥ **125** typically demands corrective action; RPN ≥ 200 is *high priority*; RPN ≥ 400 is *must-act immediately*.

**AIAG-VDA 2019 alternative (no RPN, uses Action Priority):**

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{AP} = f(S, O, D) \in \{\text{High, Medium, Low}\}}
$$

### 2.3.2 Severity Scale (S) — IEC 60812 / AIAG-VDA Reference

| Score | Effect | Process Safety Example |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | Catastrophic | Loss of life, multi-million USD loss, public exposure |
| 9 | Very high | Permanent disability, major environmental release |
| 8 | Major | Severe injury, fire, >$100k loss |
| 7 | High | Lost-time injury, significant production loss |
| 6 | Moderate | Medical treatment injury, regulatory violation |
| 5 | Low | First-aid injury, high rework |
| 4 | Very low | Minor injury noticed by customer |
| 3 | Minor | Defect noticed by most customers |
| 2 | Very minor | Defect noticed by careful inspection |
| **1** | None | No effect, theoretical only |

### 2.3.3 Occurrence Scale (O) — Failure Rate Basis

| Score | Rate per 10⁶ operations / km / hr | Process Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | ≥ 100,000 | Failure almost inevitable |
| 9 | 50,000 | Very high, persistent failures |
| 8 | 20,000 | High, frequent failures |
| 7 | 10,000 | Frequent |
| 6 | 4,000 | Moderately high |
| 5 | 2,000 | Moderate |
| 4 | 1,000 | Occasionally |
| 3 | 400 | Low |
| 2 | 100 | Very low |
| **1** | ≤ 1 | Remote, failure unlikely |

### 2.3.4 Detection Scale (D) — Note: Higher D = Worse

> [!WARNING]
> Detection in FMEA is *inverse* — a high score (10) means **hard to detect**, NOT easy. This is the single most common KTU trap.

| Score | Detection Probability | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | Cannot detect | Silent runaway reaction |
| 9 | Very remote | Alarm exists but disabled |
| 8 | Remote | Operator unlikely to notice |
| 7 | Very low | Audible alarm only |
| 6 | Low | Visual check needed |
| 5 | Moderate | DCS trend monitoring |
| 4 | Moderately high | Alarms + checks |
| 3 | High | Multiple redundant sensors |
| 2 | Very high | Real-time continuous monitoring |
| **1** | Almost certain | Automatic interlock + alarm |

### 2.3.5 Aggregated Risk Metrics

For multi-failure-mode systems:

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{Total~System~RPN} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{RPN}_i}
$$

Where *n* is the number of identified failure modes.

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{Average~RPN} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{RPN}_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{Residual~RPN} = S_{\text{after}} \times O_{\text{after}} \times D_{\text{after}}}
$$

**% RPN Reduction** (for showing effectiveness of corrective action):

$$
\boxed{\%\Delta \mathrm{RPN} = \frac{\mathrm{RPN}_{\text{initial}} - \mathrm{RPN}_{\text{final}}}{\mathrm{RPN}_{\text{initial}}} \times 100\%}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMEA is **mandatory** in:
- **Automotive:** IATF 16949 (formerly QS-9000)
- **Aerospace:** AS9100, SAE ARP5580
- **Medical devices:** FDA 21 CFR 820.30, IEC 62366
- **Process industries:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PHA requirements
- **KTU Industrial Practice:** Used in B.Tech project work, process design, and lab safety reviews
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Centrifugal Pump in a Solvent Transfer Line (KTU Numerical Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **representative KTU 14-mark numerical problem**. Practice the complete flow.

### Given
A centrifugal pump (P-101) transfers toluene from a storage tank to a reactor. After team review, three failure modes are identified with the following scoring:

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Pump impeller erosion | Flow loss → reactor undercharge | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| F2 | Mechanical seal leak | VOC release → fire/explosion risk | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| F3 | Bearing failure (overheat) | Pump seizure → plant shutdown | 7 | 3 | 5 |

**Required:** Compute RPN, rank by priority, identify the critical failure, and recommend an action.

### Step 1 — Compute Initial RPN for each failure mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{RPN}_{F1} &= S_{F1} \times O_{F1} \times D_{F1} \\
&= 8 \times 5 \times 4 \\
&= 160
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{RPN}_{F2} &= S_{F2} \times O_{F2} \times D_{F2} \\
&= 9 \times 4 \times 6 \\
&= 216
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{RPN}_{F3} &= S_{F3} \times O_{F3} \times D_{F3} \\
&= 7 \times 3 \times 5 \\
&= 105
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total and Average System RPN

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Total~RPN} &= 160 + 216 + 105 \\
&= 481
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Average~RPN} &= \frac{481}{3} \\
&= 160.33
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Rank the Failure Modes

**Rank 1 (Critical):** F2 — Mechanical seal leak (RPN = **216**)
**Rank 2:** F1 — Impeller erosion (RPN = 160)
**Rank 3:** F3 — Bearing failure (RPN = 105)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even though F1 has the highest Occurrence (5), F2 is critical because its *Severity* (9) combined with poor *Detection* (6) produces the highest RPN. **Always rank by RPN, not by individual scores.**

### Step 4 — Recommend Action for F2 (Top Priority)

| Action | Target Dimension | Expected New Score |
|---|---|---|
| Install double mechanical seal with barrier fluid | Reduces O: 4 → 2 | 9 × 2 × 6 = 108 |
| Add VOC gas detector with DCS alarm at 25% LEL | Reduces D: 6 → 2 | 9 × 2 × 2 = 36 |
| Combined action (recommended) | Reduces O and D | 9 × 2 × 2 = 36 |

### Step 5 — Compute % RPN Reduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta\mathrm{RPN}_{F2} &= \frac{216 - 36}{216} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{180}{216} \times 100\% \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Outcome:** RPN reduced from 216 to 36 — an 83.33% reduction, well below the action threshold of 125. Action is justified and effective.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — FMEA for a Reactor Temperature Control Loop (Process Safety Context)

### Given
A jacketed CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) operating at 80 °C has three potential failure modes in its temperature control loop:

| # | Component | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Temperature sensor (TT-101) | Drift (reads low) | Reactor overheats → runaway | 10 | 5 | 7 |
| T2 | Cooling water control valve (TV-101) | Fails closed | Loss of cooling → runaway | 10 | 3 | 5 |
| T3 | Interlock (SIS-001) | Fails to trip on HH | No automatic shutdown | 9 | 2 | 4 |

### Step 1 — Compute RPNs

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{RPN}_{T1} &= 10 \times 5 \times 7 = 350 \\
\mathrm{RPN}_{T2} &= 10 \times 3 \times 5 = 150 \\
\mathrm{RPN}_{T3} &= 9 \times 2 \times 4 = 72
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Critical Analysis

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{RPN}_{T1} = 350 \;\; \text{(Highest, Above Emergency Threshold)}}
$$

Failure mode T1 (sensor drift) is the **single most dangerous failure** because:
- The sensor *lies* — operators believe temperature is normal
- The DCS never triggers any alarm
- Cooling control is fooled into reducing flow

### Step 3 — Mitigation

| Action | Dimension Affected | New Score | New RPN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Install redundant sensor (voting 2oo3) | D: 7 → 2 | 10 × 5 × 2 | 100 |
| Add periodic calibration every 3 months | O: 5 → 2 | 10 × 2 × 2 | 40 |
| Add high-high trip independent of DCS | D: 7 → 1 | 10 × 2 × 1 | 20 |
| **Combined (recommended)** | O and D both ↓ | 10 × 2 × 1 | **20** |

### Step 4 — Final RPN Calculation Post-Mitigation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{RPN}_{T1,\text{final}} &= 10 \times 2 \times 1 \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta\mathrm{RPN}_{T1} &= \frac{350 - 20}{350} \times 100\% \\
&= 94.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — FMEA vs HAZOP vs Fault Tree

| Feature | **FMEA** | **HAZOP** | **Fault Tree** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Approach | Inductive (bottom-up) | Inductive (guided by guide words) | Deductive (top-down) |
| Direction | Component → System | Node → Deviation | Top event → Causes |
| Best for | Component/equipment failure | Process deviations in continuous plants | Complex multi-pathway failures |
| Quantification | RPN (semi-quantitative) | Qualitative matrix | Boolean + quantitative probability |
| Time | Moderate | High | High |
| KTU Relevance | Very High | Very High | Moderate |
| Output | Ranked list of failure modes | Causes & consequences matrix | Boolean expression of failure paths |
| Limitation | Cannot easily handle multi-failure interactions | Requires detailed P&ID knowledge | Requires clear top event |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is FMEA preferred at the design stage while HAZOP is preferred for operating plants?"* The answer above — FMEA is component-centric and does not need an existing P&ID; HAZOP requires the documented design baseline.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — FMEA Workflow Procedure (Seven Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> stepB[Step 2: Assemble Cross-Functional Team]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Identify Functions and Failure Modes]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Determine Effects and Causes]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Score S, O, D from 1 to 10]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    stepF --> stepG{RPN above 125 threshold?}
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH[Step 7a: Recommend Corrective Action]
    stepH --> stepI[Re-Score After Action]
    stepI --> stepF
    stepG -- No --> stepJ[Document and Close]
    stepF --> stepK[Update FMEA Worksheet and Log]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Risk Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp[Identified Failure Mode] --> s{Severity Score}
    s -- S is 9 or 10 --> sHigh[Must Mitigate Regardless of RPN]
    s -- S is 1 to 8 --> oa[Proceed to RPN]
    oa --> rp{RPN Value}
    rp -- RPN above 200 --> aHigh[High Priority Immediate Action]
    rp -- RPN 125 to 200 --> aMed[Medium Priority Schedule Action]
    rp -- RPN below 125 --> aLow[Acceptable Monitor Only]
    sHigh --> aHigh
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — FMEA Component Decomposition (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sysA[Process System] --> sysB[Sub-System A Pumping]
    sysA --> sysC[Sub-System B Reaction]
    sysA --> sysD[Sub-System C Cooling]
    sysB --> compA1[Centrifugal Pump P-101]
    sysB --> compA2[Mechanical Seal MS-201]
    sysB --> compA3[Bearing Assembly BA-301]
    sysC --> compB1[Reactor R-001]
    sysC --> compB2[Agitor MTR-401]
    sysC --> compB3[Level Switch LSH-501]
    sysD --> compC1[CW Valve TV-601]
    sysD --> compC2[Temperature Sensor TT-701]
    sysD --> compC3[Safety Interlock SIS-801]
    compA1 --> fmA1[Failure Mode Erosion]
    compA2 --> fmA2[Failure Mode Seal Leak]
    compA3 --> fmA3[Failure Mode Bearing Overheat]
    compB1 --> fmB1[Failure Mode Overpressure]
    compB2 --> fmB2[Failure Mode Agitator Stop]
    compB3 --> fmB3[Failure Mode Level Sensor Stuck]
    compC1 --> fmC1[Failure Mode Valve Fails Closed]
    compC2 --> fmC2[Failure Mode Sensor Drift]
    compC3 --> fmC3[Failure Mode Interlock Fails to Trip]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — RPN Calculation Logic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[FMEA Row Input] --> idFail[Identify Failure Mode]
    idFail --> idCause[Identify Root Cause]
    idCause --> idEff[Identify Downstream Effect]
    idEff --> sScore[Assign Severity 1 to 10]
    sScore --> oScore[Assign Occurrence 1 to 10]
    oScore --> dScore[Assign Detection 1 to 10]
    dScore --> mul[Multiply S times O times D]
    mul --> rpnOut[Output RPN 1 to 1000]
    rpnOut --> rank[Sort Descending]
    rank --> thr{RPN above 125}
    thr -- Yes --> act[Initiate Corrective Action]
    thr -- No --> doc[Document and Archive]
    act --> rescore[Re-Score After Implementation]
    rescore --> mul
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block 5 — Cause–Failure–Effect Chain (Single Row Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootCause[Root Cause e.g. Vibration] --> failMode[Failure Mode e.g. Impeller Crack]
    failMode --> localEff[Local Effect e.g. Flow Loss]
    localEff --> sysEff[System Effect e.g. Reactor Undercharge]
    sysEff --> finalEff[End Effect e.g. Off-Spec Product]
    rootCause -. S Score .-> annotS[S 8]
    failMode -. O Score .-> annotO[O 5]
    sysEff -. D Score .-> annotD[D 4]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define FMEA. What are its three scoring parameters?**

**Model Answer:**

> **FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis)** is a systematic, inductive risk assessment methodology used to identify potential failure modes of a system, process, or product, evaluate their consequences, and prioritize corrective actions based on quantified risk.
>
> The three scoring parameters are:
> 1. **Severity (S):** Magnitude of the consequence of failure (scale 1–10).
> 2. **Occurrence (O):** Likelihood/frequency of the failure cause (scale 1–10).
> 3. **Detection (D):** Probability that the failure will be detected before it reaches the customer/environment (scale 1–10).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Design FMEA (DFMEA) and Process FMEA (PFMEA).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DFMEA | PFMEA |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Design of the product/component | Manufacturing/operating process |
| **Failure concern** | Will the product fail in service? | Will the process produce defective output? |
| **Conducted at** | Design/engineering stage | Process planning/operation stage |
| **Cause types** | Design flaws, material selection | Operator error, machine fault, method variation |
| **Customer** | End user of product | Internal next process / external customer |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**An exothermic batch reactor operates at 140 °C with a cooling jacket controlled by a PID loop. As part of the PHA team, conduct an FMEA study by identifying any four potential failure modes. For each failure mode, assign S, O, D scores (1–10), compute the RPN, rank them, identify the most critical failure, and propose a corrective action for the highest-ranked mode. Compute the % RPN reduction after the action. Use the following seed data:**

| # | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | Cooling water valve sticks open | 6 | 5 | 4 |
| M2 | Temperature sensor (RTD) drifts high | 9 | 4 | 5 |
| M3 | Agitator mechanical failure (seizure) | 8 | 3 | 6 |
| M4 | Cooling pump trips (loss of CW flow) | 9 | 4 | 4 |

#### **Model Solution**

**Step 1 — Compute RPNs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{RPN}_{M1} &= 6 \times 5 \times 4 = 120 \\
\mathrm{RPN}_{M2} &= 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180 \\
\mathrm{RPN}_{M3} &= 8 \times 3 \times 6 = 144 \\
\mathrm{RPN}_{M4} &= 9 \times 4 \times 4 = 144
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rank the failure modes:**

| Rank | Mode | RPN |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **M2 — RTD drifts high** | **180** |
| 2 (tie) | M3 — Agitator seizure | 144 |
| 2 (tie) | M4 — CW pump trips | 144 |
| 4 | M1 — Valve sticks open | 120 |

> **Critical failure: M2** (highest RPN; severity is 9 because runaway reaction can cause catastrophic loss).

**Step 3 — Recommend action for M2:**

| Action | Dimension | New Score |
|---|---|---|
| Install redundant RTD with 2oo3 voting | D: 5 → 2 | 9 × 4 × 2 = 72 |
| Quarterly calibration of all RTDs | O: 4 → 2 | 9 × 2 × 2 = 36 |
| Add independent high-high trip (SIL-2) | D: 5 → 1 | 9 × 2 × 1 = 18 |
| **Combined action (recommended)** | O: 4 → 2; D: 5 → 1 | **9 × 2 × 1 = 18** |

**Step 4 — Compute % RPN reduction for M2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta\mathrm{RPN}_{M2} &= \frac{180 - 18}{180} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{162}{180} \times 100\% \\
&= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification — residual RPN must be below threshold 125:**

$$
\mathrm{RPN}_{M2,\text{residual}} = 18 \ll 125 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The combined mitigation strategy reduces M2 RPN by 90%, from 180 down to 18. The action is fully effective and below the KTU acceptance threshold.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the seven steps of conducting an FMEA in detail. State at least two limitations of the traditional RPN method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare FMEA with HAZOP and Fault Tree Analysis across the following axes: (i) Approach direction, (ii) Best application stage, (iii) Output form, (iv) Quantification capability, (v) Typical industry use, (vi) Handling of multi-failure interactions, and (vii) Data requirement.

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

**The seven steps of FMEA:**

1. **Define Scope and Boundaries:** Clearly state the system, sub-systems, and components under analysis. Document assumptions (e.g., ambient conditions, operating modes, exclusions).

2. **Assemble Cross-Functional Team:** A team of 4–8 experts — process, mechanical, instrumentation, operations, safety, quality, and a facilitator. Diversity ensures blind spots are minimized.

3. **Identify Functions and Failure Modes:** For every component, document its intended function. For each function, list *every conceivable way* it can fail (e.g., no output, partial output, erratic output, premature output, late output).

4. **Determine Effects and Causes:** For each failure mode, trace the **effect** on the immediate component, the sub-system, the system, and the end user. Identify the **cause** (root mechanism).

5. **Score S, O, D (1–10):** Use the standardized 1–10 scales (per AIAG-VDA / IEC 60812) for Severity, Occurrence, and Detection. **Detection is inverse — high D means hard to detect.**

6. **Compute RPN and Prioritize:** Calculate RPN = S × O × D. Rank from highest to lowest. Apply the KTU threshold (RPN ≥ 125 → action needed).

7. **Recommend Actions and Re-Score:** For each high-RPN item, assign a corrective action targeting reduction of S, O, or D. Re-score and recalculate RPN. Document responsibility and target date. **Close out only when RPN falls below threshold or is formally accepted as residual risk.**

**Limitations of Traditional RPN:**

1. **Same RPN, different risk:** RPN treats (S=10, O=2, D=5) = 100 and (S=5, O=5, D=4) = 100 as equal, but the first is far more dangerous (catastrophic × rare vs. moderate × common). This hides the true criticality.

2. **Non-unique scales:** Different industries (automotive vs. aerospace vs. process) use different S/O/D scales, making cross-company RPN comparison meaningless.

3. **Detection inversion is counterintuitive** and often mis-scored by inexperienced teams, inflating RPN artificially.

4. **Difficulty handling multi-failure interactions** (cascading failures, common-cause failures).

5. **Does not account for safeguards** already in place; mitigation logic must be added separately.

> *The AIAG-VDA 2019 revision replaces RPN with the **Action Priority (AP)** approach to address limitation 1 above.*

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Comparison Table**

| Axis | **FMEA** | **HAZOP** | **Fault Tree** |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Approach | Inductive (bottom-up) | Inductive (deviation-based) | Deductive (top-down) |
| (ii) Best stage | Design / early engineering | Detailed design / operating plant with P&ID | Any stage; complex systems |
| (iii) Output | Ranked RPN list + actions | Cause–consequence matrix per node | Boolean logic diagram + cut sets |
| (iv) Quantification | Semi-quantitative (RPN) | Qualitative to semi-quantitative | Fully quantitative (probabilities) |
| (v) Typical use | Automotive, aerospace, mechanical design | Chemical, petrochemical, pharma | Aerospace, nuclear, high-consequence industries |
| (vi) Multi-failure handling | Limited (single-failure focus) | Moderate (interaction noted) | Strong (AND/OR gates model interaction) |
| (vii) Data requirement | Failure rate databases (e.g., OREDA) | P&ID, process intent, deviation causes | Component failure rates, human error rates |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in PECHT864 FMEA questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Detection scoring direction** — many students write D = 10 for *easy* detection. Detection score of 10 means *impossible* to detect. (–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Adding S, O, D instead of multiplying** — S + O + D is **wrong**; the correct formula is S × O × D. (–3 marks)
>
> 3. **Ignoring the action threshold** — KTU expects you to state whether the RPN is above/below the threshold (typically 125) and justify your action. Without this, even a correct RPN loses credit.
>
> 4. **Failing to show units in scales** — Always state that the scales are 1–10 and the RPN range is 1–1000. (–1 mark)
>
> 5. **Mis-reranking after corrective action** — Re-score properly using the new S, O, D values; do not arbitrarily reduce RPN by 50%.
>
> 6. **Writing a single-action answer for a multi-part question** — When asked to (a) compute and (b) compare, students often skip the comparison table. (–3 marks)
>
> 7. **Citing AIAG-VDA as a textbook** — write "AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook, 2019 edition" or "IEC 60812:2006" if asked for reference.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density last-minute revision for KTU exam hall:**

- [ ] **FMEA =** Failure Modes and Effects Analysis = *inductive, bottom-up* risk assessment.
- [ ] **Three scoring parameters:** Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D) — *each 1 to 10*.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $\mathrm{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ — *range 1 to 1000*.
- [ ] **Action threshold (KTU convention):** RPN ≥ **125** triggers corrective action; ≥ 200 is high priority; ≥ 400 is emergency.
- [ ] **Detection is INVERSE:** D = 10 = *cannot* detect; D = 1 = *certain* detection.
- [ ] **Three types:** System FMEA (SFMEA), Design FMEA (DFMEA), Process FMEA (PFMEA).
- [ ] **Seven steps:** Scope → Team → Functions/Modes → Effects/Causes → Score → RPN/Rank → Action/Re-score.
- [ ] **AIAG-VDA 2019 update:** RPN replaced by **Action Priority (AP)** — High/Medium/Low.
- [ ] **FMEA vs HAZOP:** FMEA = component-centric, design stage; HAZOP = process-centric, operating plant with P&ID.
- [ ] **FMEA vs Fault Tree:** FMEA = inductive, single failure; FTA = deductive, multi-failure (AND/OR gates).
- [ ] **Standards to cite:** IEC 60812 (2015), AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019), OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(e), IEC 31010.
- [ ] **% RPN Reduction formula:** $\%\Delta \mathrm{RPN} = \frac{\mathrm{RPN}_{\text{initial}} - \mathrm{RPN}_{\text{final}}}{\mathrm{RPN}_{\text{initial}}} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **SOD Score (modern alternative):** Severity × Occurrence × Detection, with threshold-based decision tree.
- [ ] **RPN reduction strategies:** Lower O (preventive maintenance, training), lower D (sensors, alarms, interlocks), lower S (inherent safety design).
- [ ] **Five common failure mode keywords:** *Fails to start, fails running, fails to stop, intermittent, spurious.*
- [ ] **FMEA worksheet columns** (KTU standard): Item | Function | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D | RPN | Action | Responsibility | Target Date.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis and Event Tree Analysis
Bow-Tie Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bow-Tie Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — BOW-TIE ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bow-Tie Analysis (BTA)** is a structured, graphical risk-evaluation technique that integrates **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** on the left side and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** on the right side, joined at a single central **Top Event (TE)**. It explicitly depicts *threats* (causes), *consequences* (outcomes), and the *preventive* and *mitigative control barriers* (also called *safeguards* or *risk controls*) positioned between them.

The methodology was originally developed by **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI)** of the United Kingdom in the late **1970s** as a practical, operator-friendly communication tool for major-hazard facilities. It is now formally embedded in international standards such as **ISO 31000 (Risk Management)** and is a recommended visual artifact in **CCPS / AIChE Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures (3rd Edition)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Dam and Reservoir" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Bow-Tie Analysis as a river flowing through a series of dams.**
> - The **mountain spring** (left side) represents the *Threats* — rainfall, landslide, upstream dam failure.
> - The **village downstream** (right side) represents the *Consequences* — flooding, property loss, casualties.
> - The **central town being protected** is the *Top Event* — loss of containment of the reservoir.
> - Each **dam along the river** is a *Preventive Control* (stops the flood from reaching the town) or a *Mitigative Control* (reduces damage after the flood leaves the town).
> - If one dam cracks (**escalation factor / degraded barrier**), the next dam must hold — this is the **defence-in-depth** principle.

This analogy immediately conveys the two complementary objectives of process safety:

1. **Prevention** — Stop the unsafe event from occurring.
2. **Mitigation** — Limit the harm if the unsafe event has occurred.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Top Event (TE)** | The central undesired event (e.g., Loss of Containment — LOC) that Bow-Tie pivots around. |
| **Threat** | A *cause* or initiating event that, if uncontrolled, can lead to the Top Event. |
| **Consequence** | An *outcome* (injury, fire, environmental release) following the Top Event. |
| **Preventive Control (PC)** | A barrier placed on the left side that *prevents* a Threat from escalating into the Top Event. |
| **Mitigative Control (MC)** | A barrier placed on the right side that *limits* the damage once the Top Event has occurred. |
| **Escalation Factor (EF)** | A condition that *degrades* or *defeats* a barrier (e.g., maintenance skipped, blocked valve). |
| **Degradation Factor (DF)** | A specific, named *mechanism* by which a control loses effectiveness over time. |
| **PFD** | Probability of Failure on Demand of a Safety Instrumented Function (per **IEC 61511**). |
| **RRF** | Risk Reduction Factor — ratio of unmitigated to residual risk. |
| **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the goalpost against which Bow-Tie adequacy is judged. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state explicitly whether a control is *Preventive* (left side, before the event) or *Mitigative* (right side, after the event). Marks are routinely lost when these are confused.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bow-Tie diagrams are now mandatory or strongly recommended in:

- Offshore oil & gas (UK HSE, NOPSEMA Australia, NMA Norway).
- Refineries and petrochemical complexes.
- LNG terminals and storage depots.
- Pharmaceutical and fine-chemical plants handling Highly Hazardous Chemicals (HHCs).
- Mining and major hazard facilities under **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119**.

The output of a Bow-Tie feeds directly into the **Safety Case**, **HSE Management System**, **Management of Change (MOC) reviews**, **audit programs**, and **emergency response plans**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Bow-Tie Diagram

A complete Bow-Tie contains **seven (7) essential structural elements**:

1. **Threats** (1 to *n*) — emanating from the left, drawn as separate paths.
2. **Top Event** — the single central node, always drawn as a hexagonal or red-coloured event.
3. **Preventive Controls** (1 to *n* per threat pathway) — drawn on each threat pathway *before* the Top Event.
4. **Mitigative Controls** (1 to *n* per consequence pathway) — drawn *after* the Top Event.
5. **Consequences** (1 to *n*) — fanning out to the right.
6. **Escalation Factors** — small red flags attached to each control, indicating degradation mechanisms.
7. **Management System Elements (Procedures / Standards / Training)** — the umbrella above the entire diagram that keeps the barriers functional.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Levels of a Bow-Tie

A standard industry Bow-Tie is documented at **three levels of detail**:

| Level | Name | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Summary / Concept Bow-Tie | One TE, 2–3 threats, 2–3 consequences, 1 control per pathway. | Board-level communication, induction training. |
| **Level 2** | Detailed Bow-Tie | Multiple threats, multiple consequences, 2–3 controls per pathway. | Operational risk management, HSE audits. |
| **Level 3** | Quantitative Bow-Tie | Each control assigned a **PFD** value; each threat assigned a frequency (events/year). | SIL determination, QRA inputs. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Learning Outcome Mapping:** Bow-Tie is the **pivot** connecting *Hazard Identification* (Module 1) to *Risk Evaluation & Control* (Module 3). Mastery of Levels 1 and 2 is essential for the **ESE (End Semester Examination)**.
