### 2.3 The Logic Flow — Why Bow-Tie Works

The method rests on a chain of four logical assertions:

- **Assertion 1:** Every major accident has a finite, enumerable set of *causes* (threats).
- **Assertion 2:** Each cause can be intercepted by *barriers* before the undesired event occurs.
- **Assertion 3:** Once the undesired event occurs, there is a finite, enumerable set of *outcomes* (consequences).
- **Assertion 4:** Each outcome can be intercepted by *barriers* after the event to limit damage.

Mathematically, a Bow-Tie is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** in which:

- The left half is a **logical OR-gate** (any threat reaching the centre triggers the Top Event if all preventive controls fail).
- The right half is a **logical AND-gate** (all mitigative controls in a given pathway must fail for the consequence to materialise fully).

### 2.4 Quantitative Layer — KTU Formula Sheet

Although Bow-Tie is predominantly a **qualitative** tool, the **Level 3 (Quantitative) Bow-Tie** introduces measurable risk reduction. The following table is the **complete high-yield formula set** expected for the KTU examination.

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Pipe Rule:** Every absolute-value or conditional delimiter is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as a literal `|` — to prevent markdown-table syntax corruption.

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base Risk (Unmitigated)** | $R_{\text{base}} = f_{\text{threat}} \times C_{\text{severity}}$ | events/year × severity index |
| 2 | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $PFD = 1 - \text{Reliability}$ | dimensionless, $0 < PFD < 1$ |
| 3 | **Preventive Control Effectiveness** | $PFD_{\text{PC}}$ | lower value = better barrier |
| 4 | **Mitigative Control Effectiveness** | $PFD_{\text{MC}}$ | lower value = better barrier |
| 5 | **Combined PFD — Controls in Series** | $PFD_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - PFD_i)$ | applies when one barrier must work **after** the other (independent) |
| 6 | **Combined PFD — Controls in Parallel (Redundancy)** | $PFD_{\text{parallel}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$ | applies when **any one** of several barriers can stop the pathway (e.g., 2oo3 voting) |
| 7 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{\text{base}}}{R_{\text{residual}}} = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless, $RRF \geq 1$ |
| 8 | **Residual Risk** | $R_{\text{residual}} = f_{\text{threat}} \times C_{\text{severity}} \times PFD_{\text{PC}} \times PFD_{\text{MC}}$ | events/year |
| 9 | **ALARP Test** | $R_{\text{residual}} \leq R_{\text{tolerable}}$ | compared to company risk matrix |
| 10 | **Sil Determination (IEC 61511)** | $\sum PFD_{\text{SIF}} \leq 10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-5}$ | depends on SIL 1 / 2 / 3 target |

### 2.5 Worked Logic — Series vs Parallel Controls

**Scenario A — Two Independent Pressure Relief Valves in Series (one set, one backup):**
- They are functionally in **parallel** because opening *either* one relieves the overpressure.
- $PFD_{\text{total}} = PFD_1 \times PFD_2$

**Scenario B — Level Sensor **AND** Shutdown Valve on a Reactor:**
- Both must work to prevent overflow.
- They are in **series**.
- $PFD_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - PFD_{\text{sensor}})(1 - PFD_{\text{valve}})$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students treat *any* pair of safety barriers as being in series. Always ask: **"Must BOTH fail for the hazard to propagate, or just ONE?"** — This single question decides the formula.

### 2.6 Bow-Tie vs HAZOP vs LOPA — Where Does Bow-Tie Fit?

| Feature | HAZOP | LOPA | Bow-Tie |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Identify deviations | Quantify event frequency | Visualise the full risk picture |
| **Output** | Nodes & causes | Frequency / consequence numbers | A single graphical diagram |
| **Quantitative?** | No | Yes | Optional (Level 3) |
| **Best Use Stage** | Front-end design / MOC | SIL selection | Operations / communication / training |

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus sequence**, HAZOP and LOPA precede Bow-Tie and feed their data into it. **Bow-Tie is the integrating lens.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined PFD for Two Independent Controls in Series

Consider a threat pathway with **two independent preventive controls**: Control A (a level transmitter) and Control B (a shutdown valve). Failure of *both* is required for the Top Event to occur.

**Step 1 — Define the reliability of each control:**

$$R_A = 1 - PFD_A \quad \text{and} \quad R_B = 1 - PFD_B$$

**Step 2 — Reliability of the combined system (both must succeed):**

$$R_{\text{combined}} = R_A \times R_B$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the definitions:**

$$R_{\text{combined}} = (1 - PFD_A)(1 - PFD_B)$$

**Step 4 — Expand the product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{combined}} &= 1 - PFD_A - PFD_B + PFD_A \cdot PFD_B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express combined unreliability (PFD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{\text{combined}} &= 1 - R_{\text{combined}} \\
&= 1 - \bigl[1 - PFD_A - PFD_B + PFD_A \cdot PFD_B\bigr] \\
&= PFD_A + PFD_B - PFD_A \cdot PFD_B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final canonical form:**

$$PFD_{\text{series}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - PFD_i)$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The product term $PFD_A \cdot PFD_B$ is often tiny (e.g., $10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-4}$) and may be neglected for a first-pass estimate, but in **SIL-3 systems** it is non-negligible and **must** be retained.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the unmitigated risk** as the product of threat frequency and consequence severity:

$$R_{\text{base}} = f_{\text{threat}} \times C_{\text{severity}}$$

**Step 2 — Define residual risk** after barriers:

$$R_{\text{residual}} = f_{\text{threat}} \times C_{\text{severity}} \times PFD_{\text{PC,total}} \times PFD_{\text{MC,total}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \frac{R_{\text{base}}}{R_{\text{residual}}} \\
&= \frac{f_{\text{threat}} \times C_{\text{severity}}}{f_{\text{threat}} \times C_{\text{severity}} \times PFD_{\text{PC,total}} \times PFD_{\text{MC,total}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{PC,total}} \times PFD_{\text{MC,total}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final compact expression:**

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{total}}}$$

> **Sanity Check:** If $PFD = 0.01$ (99 % reliable), then $RRF = 100$. This means the barriers cut the risk by a factor of 100 — a value that appears often in well-engineered process plants.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (Numeric Anchoring)

A pressurized storage tank containing **toluene** has the following Bow-Tie data for the Top Event *Loss of Containment (LOC)*:

- **Threat:** Overpressure due to blocked vent — frequency $f = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ events/year.
- **Consequence:** Vapour Cloud Explosion — severity $C = 1{,}000$ (arbitrary index).
- **Preventive Control 1 (PC₁):** Pressure Safety Valve — $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-2}$.
- **Preventive Control 2 (PC₂):** Independent High-Pressure Trip — $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-1}$.
- **Mitigative Control (MC):** Foam Pourer + Water Spray Deluge — $PFD = 5 \times 10^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Compute combined preventive PFD (series):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{\text{PC,total}} &= 1 - (1 - PFD_1)(1 - PFD_2) \\
&= 1 - (0.99)(0.90) \\
&= 1 - 0.891 \\
&= 0.109
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute residual risk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{residual}} &= f \times C \times PFD_{\text{PC,total}} \times PFD_{\text{MC}} \\
&= (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times 1000 \times 0.109 \times 0.50 \\
&= 0.545 \; \text{risk units/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute RRF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{PC,total}} \times PFD_{\text{MC}}} = \frac{1}{0.109 \times 0.50} = \frac{1}{0.0545} \approx 18.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

| Quantity | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Unmitigated risk | $10 \; \text{risk units/year}$ | $10^{-2} \times 1000$ |
| Residual risk | $0.545 \; \text{risk units/year}$ | After all barriers |
| RRF | $\approx 18.3$ | Risk reduced ~18-fold |

> [!NOTE]
> If company **tolerable risk** is $1.0$ risk unit/year, then $R_{\text{residual}} = 0.545 < 1.0$ → **ALARP satisfied**. If it were $\geq 1.0$, additional barriers or lower-PFD SIL-rated controls would be required.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Threats vs Controls Coverage

A useful Bow-Tie audit tool is the **Coverage Matrix**. For each threat, list which controls (PC and MC) act on it. A cell is **1** if the control is effective against that threat, **0** otherwise.

| Threat \ Control | PSV | HP Trip | Deluge | Fire-water Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blocked Vent** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **External Fire** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Pump Seal Leak** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **Gasket Failure** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

> **Audit Conclusion:** External fire and pump-seal leak are not protected by PSV — this is **acceptable** because the PSV is designed for overpressure, not thermal loading. However, the absence of a high-pressure trip on pump-seal-leak is a **gap** worth flagging in the MOC review.

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Escalation Factor Frequency Adjustment

If a control has an *escalation factor* (e.g., routine maintenance skipped 1 in 100 times), the control's effective PFD becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{\text{effective}} &= PFD_{\text{nominal}} + (1 - PFD_{\text{nominal}}) \times P_{\text{EF}} \\
&\approx PFD_{\text{nominal}} + P_{\text{EF}} \quad \text{(for small } PFD\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $P_{\text{EF}}$ is the probability of the escalation factor occurring. This adjustment is what makes Bow-Tie a **living** risk document — it captures not just hardware failure but **human / organisational** failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standard Bow-Tie Layout

The diagram below is a Mermaid-rendered **functional architecture** showing the seven structural elements and their directional information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Threat 1: Overpressure]:::threat --> P1A[PC1: Pressure Safety Valve]:::prev
    A2[Threat 2: External Fire]:::threat --> P2A[PC2: High Pressure Trip]:::prev
    A3[Threat 3: Seal Leak]:::threat --> P3A[PC3: Routine Inspection]:::prev
    A4[Threat 4: Gasket Failure]:::threat --> P4A[PC4: Mechanical Integrity SOP]:::prev

    P1A --> TE[Top Event: Loss of Containment]:::top
    P2A --> TE
    P3A --> TE
    P4A --> TE

    TE --> M1A[MC1: Emergency Block Valve]:::mit
    TE --> M2A[MC2: Foam Deluge System]:::mit
    TE --> M3A[MC3: Emergency Venting]:::mit

    M1A --> C1[Consequence 1: Fire]:::conseq
    M2A --> C2[Consequence 2: Explosion]:::conseq
    M3A --> C3[Consequence 3: Toxic Release]:::conseq

    EF1[Escalation Factor: Skipped Maintenance]:::esc -. degrades .-> P1A
    EF2[Escalation Factor: Blocked Drain]:::esc -. degrades .-> M2A

    MS[Management System: Procedures, Training, Audit]:::mgmt -.- A1
    MS -.- A2
    MS -.- A3
    MS -.- A4
    MS -.- P1A
    MS -.- P2A
    MS -.- P3A
    MS -.- P4A
    MS -.- M1A
    MS -.- M2A
    MS -.- M3A

    classDef threat fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prev fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef top fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef mit fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef conseq fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef esc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef mgmt fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagram

| Element | Visual Cue | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Threats** (left) | Orange boxes | Causes that *could* trigger the Top Event. |
| **Preventive Controls (PC)** | Green boxes | Stop the threat before it becomes the Top Event. |
| **Top Event** | Red hexagon | The undesired central event. |
| **Mitigative Controls (MC)** | Blue boxes | Reduce severity *after* the Top Event. |
| **Consequences** (right) | Purple boxes | Possible outcomes of the Top Event. |
| **Escalation Factors** | Pink diamond (dashed) | Conditions that *degrade* a control's effectiveness. |
| **Management System** | Yellow banner (dotted) | The organisational umbrella ensuring all controls function. |

### 4.3 Construction Procedure — How to Build a Bow-Tie in Practice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical / Assignment Tip:** Many KTU practical questions ask the student to *construct* a Bow-Tie for a given process. Follow this 8-step procedure.

1. **Identify the Top Event** — one sentence, e.g., *Loss of containment of chlorine from the storage header.*
2. **Brainstorm Threats** — 3 to 8 plausible causes from HAZOP / FMEA / incident history.
3. **Identify Preventive Controls** — list all hardware, software, and human barriers.
4. **Brainstorm Consequences** — what could go wrong if the Top Event occurs (fire, toxic release, explosion).
5. **Identify Mitigative Controls** — emergency systems, evacuation, firewater, etc.
6. **Add Escalation Factors** — for each control, ask "What could make this fail?"
7. **Add Management System** — SOPs, training, inspection, audit, MOC.
8. **Validate with a multi-disciplinary team** — operations, maintenance, process safety, and instrument engineers.

### 4.4 Limitations of the Bow-Tie

A Bow-Tie is **not a substitute** for:
- A **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)** for major-hazard facilities.
- A **full fault tree** when very high reliability is required.
- A **dynamic process simulator** for transient analysis.

It **is** an excellent communication, training, and operational decision-support tool, but examiners expect the student to acknowledge its **qualitative nature** in answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define Bow-Tie Analysis. List its four essential elements.**
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Bow-Tie Analysis is a graphical risk-evaluation technique that combines Fault Tree Analysis (causes) and Event Tree Analysis (consequences) around a central Top Event. Its four essential elements are:
> 1. **Threats** (causes) on the left side.
> 2. **Top Event** in the centre.
> 3. **Consequences** (outcomes) on the right side.
> 4. **Controls** — Preventive (left) and Mitigative (right) barriers.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Preventive and Mitigative controls in a Bow-Tie.**
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Preventive Controls** are placed on the left side of the Top Event and act *before* the undesired event occurs — they prevent a Threat from escalating into the Top Event (e.g., pressure safety valve, high-temperature trip, preventive maintenance).
> - **Mitigative Controls** are placed on the right side of the Top Event and act *after* the event has occurred — they limit the severity of Consequences (e.g., emergency block valve, foam deluge, evacuation procedure).
> - The key distinction is **temporal**: preventive = *before*; mitigative = *after*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model Style]**
>
> **Q3 (A).** For an atmospheric storage tank containing **flammable naphtha**, construct a complete Bow-Tie for the Top Event **"Loss of Containment (LOC)"**.
>
> **(a)** Identify **at least four (4) credible Threats** and place **at least two (2) Preventive Controls** on each pathway. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Identify **at least three (3) Consequences** and place **at least two (2) Mitigative Controls** on each pathway. Also explain how the **Management System** keeps the controls effective. (7 marks)
>
> **Model Answer (Question A):**
>
> **(a) Threats and Preventive Controls (7 marks):**
>
> | Threat | Preventive Control 1 | Preventive Control 2 |
> |---|---|---|
> | **T1: Overfill due to level instrument failure** | Level transmitter (high-high) + automatic shutoff valve | Independent level sight glass + operator round |
> | **T2: External fire exposure** | Bund wall + fire-water monitors | Thermal insulation + deluge system on the shell |
> | **T3: Shell corrosion** | Cathodic protection + scheduled UT thickness monitoring | Internal coating + pH control of stored product |
> | **T4: Lightning strike** | Lightning arrestors + earthing system | Bonding of all metallic fittings |
>
> **Diagram Reference:** Use the Mermaid template in Section 4.1, replacing the four generic threats with T1–T4.
>
> **(b) Consequences, Mitigative Controls, and Management System (7 marks):**
>
> | Consequence | Mitigative Control 1 | Mitigative Control 2 |
> |---|---|---|
> | **C1: Pool fire** | Foam pourer on bund | Manual fire-water hose reels + trained fire team |
> | **C2: Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** | Gas detection + automatic plant shutdown | Blast wall + emergency isolation of nearby units |
> | **C3: Environmental release to soil/water** | Bund secondary containment (110% volume) | Oil-water separator + spill response team |
>
> **Management System (2 marks within the 7):**
> - Written **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** for loading/unloading.
> - **Competency-based training** and refresher courses for operators.
> - **Scheduled inspection & preventive maintenance** log.
> - **Management of Change (MOC)** protocol for any modification.
> - **Internal HSE audit** (typically annual) plus external audit every 3 years.
> - These ensure that the **hardware barriers** (green and blue boxes) continue to function as designed.

---

> **Q3 (B).** A Bow-Tie for the Top Event **"Reactor Runaway"** has the following data:
> - Threat frequency of uncontrolled exotherm: $f = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ events/year.
> - Consequence severity index: $C = 500$ units.
> - Preventive Control A (Temperature Sensor): $PFD_A = 1 \times 10^{-2}$.
> - Preventive Control B (Cooling Water Valve): $PFD_B = 5 \times 10^{-2}$.
> - Mitigative Control C (Quench System): $PFD_C = 1 \times 10^{-1}$.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **residual risk** and the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the company **tolerable risk** is $0.2$ units/year, assess whether the system is **ALARP-compliant**. If not, propose **two engineering improvements** with quantitative justification. (7 marks)
>
> **Model Answer (Question B):**
>
> **(a) Residual Risk and RRF (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Combined Preventive PFD (A and B in series):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> PFD_{AB} &= 1 - (1 - PFD_A)(1 - PFD_B) \\
> &= 1 - (0.99)(0.95) \\
> &= 1 - 0.9405 \\
> &= 0.0595
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Combined PFD along the threat-to-consequence pathway (preventive in series with mitigative):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> PFD_{\text{total}} &= 1 - (1 - PFD_{AB})(1 - PFD_C) \\
> &= 1 - (0.9405)(0.90) \\
> &= 1 - 0.84645 \\
> &= 0.15355
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Residual risk:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> R_{\text{residual}} &= f \times C \times PFD_{\text{total}} \\
> &= 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \times 0.15355 \\
> &= 0.384 \; \text{risk units/year}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Risk Reduction Factor:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> RRF &= \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{0.15355} \approx 6.51
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **(b) ALARP Assessment and Improvements (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 5 — ALARP Test:**
> $R_{\text{residual}} = 0.384$ vs $R_{\text{tolerable}} = 0.200$ → **Not ALARP-compliant** because $0.384 > 0.200$. (2 marks)
>
> **Step 6 — Engineering Improvements (Quantitative Justification):**
>
> **Improvement 1 — Add a redundant temperature sensor (PFD = $1 \times 10^{-3}$):**
> New $PFD_{AB}$ in **parallel** with the new sensor:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> PFD_{AB,\text{new}} &= PFD_A \times PFD_{\text{new}} \times (1 - PFD_B) + PFD_B \times (1 - PFD_A) \\
> &\approx 0.01 \times 0.001 + 0.05 \times 0.99 \\
> &\approx 4.96 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{(using full series formula yields similar reduction)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Recomputed residual: $R_{\text{residual,new}} \approx 0.5 \times 0.384 = 0.192 < 0.200$ → **ALARP satisfied.** (2 marks)
>
> **Improvement 2 — Upgrade quench system to SIL-2 rated (PFD_C drops from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> PFD_{\text{total,new}} &= 1 - (0.9405)(0.99) = 0.069 \\
> R_{\text{residual,new}} &= 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \times 0.069 = 0.173 < 0.200
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **ALARP satisfied.** (2 marks)
>
> **Step 7 — Cost-Benefit Note (1 mark):** Either improvement is technically adequate; the cheaper one (upgrading the quench) is generally preferred if capex for the redundant sensor exceeds ~5× the cost of the SIL-2 upgrade.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing series vs parallel control logic** — single most common error; results in off-by-orders-of-magnitude risk values. (Loss: 4–6 marks per sub-part.)
> 2. **Forgetting the $PFD \cdot PFD$ cross-term** in the series formula — acceptable only for rough estimation; for **SIL** calculations it is mandatory.
> 3. **Mixing Preventive and Mitigative controls** in the diagram — examiners award 0 marks for mislabelled arrows.
> 4. **Stating "ALARP satisfied" without numerical proof** — must always show $R_{\text{residual}} \leq R_{\text{tolerable}}$ with substituted numbers.
> 5. **Omitting the Management System** — at least 1 mark is reserved for this in any 14-mark Bow-Tie question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Bow-Tie Analysis** integrates **Fault Tree (left)** and **Event Tree (right)** around a single **Top Event**.
- **Four pillars:** Threats, Top Event, Consequences, Controls (Preventive + Mitigative).
- **Preventive Controls** act *before* the Top Event; **Mitigative Controls** act *after*.
- **Escalation Factors** degrade barriers; the **Management System** maintains them.
- Three levels of Bow-Tie: **Summary (L1)**, **Detailed (L2)**, **Quantitative (L3)**.
- **Series PFD:** $PFD = 1 - \prod (1 - PFD_i)$ — both must work.
- **Parallel PFD:** $PFD = \prod PFD_i$ — any one can stop the chain.
- **Risk Reduction Factor:** $RRF = 1 / PFD_{\text{total}}$.
- **Residual Risk:** $R_{\text{residual}} = f \times C \times PFD_{\text{PC}} \times PFD_{\text{MC}}$.
- **ALARP Test:** Residual ≤ Tolerable (must show numerical substitution).
- Bow-Tie is **qualitative-first**; quantitative values come from **LOPA** or **PFD/SIL** data.
- Bow-Tie **feeds** Safety Cases, MOC reviews, HSE audits, and emergency plans.
- Bow-Tie is **NOT** a substitute for full QRA or transient simulation.
- Always validate a Bow-Tie with a **multi-disciplinary team** (operations, maintenance, instrumentation, safety).
- Industry origin: **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI)**, late 1970s.
- Standards alignment: **ISO 31000**, **CCPS Guidelines**, **IEC 61511** (for SIL-rated controls).
- For KTU ESE: **memorise** the five formula table rows; **draw** the diagram clearly; **label** every control as P or M; **show** ALARP comparison numerically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** is a systematic, mathematically rigorous engineering methodology used to calculate numerical estimates of risk associated with the operation of chemical process plants, oil & gas installations, and other hazardous industrial facilities. According to the Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS) guidelines and the KTU 2024 Scheme process safety syllabus, QRA is defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QRA = f (Frequency of Hazardous Events × Consequences of Hazardous Events)**
> Formally: $R = \sum_i f_i \times C_i$, where $f_i$ is the initiating event frequency (per year) and $C_i$ is the consequence magnitude (in terms of fatality, injury, property damage, or environmental impact).

QRA integrates three core analytical pillars:

1. **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** – Systematic enumeration of credible accident scenarios using techniques such as HAZOP, FMEA, and Checklists.
2. **Frequency / Probability Analysis** – Quantification of how often an initiating event will occur (e.g., using Fault Tree Analysis, Event Tree Analysis, component failure databases like OREDA, IEEE Std 500).
3. **Consequence Analysis** – Modeling of the physical outcomes of the event (e.g., dispersion of toxic gas, jet fire, pool fire, BLEVE, VCE) using source models, dispersion models (Gaussian/Pasquill-Gifford), fire models (point source, pool), and explosion models (TNT equivalent, Multi-Energy).

The outputs of a QRA are typically expressed as:
- **Individual Risk (IR)** – risk to a hypothetical person at a specific location (units: per year).
- **Societal Risk (SR)** – aggregate risk to a population, often expressed via **FN curves** plotting cumulative frequency (F) vs number of fatalities (N).
- **ALARP Demonstration** – showing that residual risks are reduced to *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Weather Forecasting for Disasters" Analogy**
> Think of QRA like a long-term weather probability map for an industrial site. A meteorologist doesn't just say "a storm might come" — they calculate the probability of a Category-5 hurricane hitting your coastal town next year, the expected wind speed, the storm surge height, and the resulting damage. QRA does exactly the same thing for chemical plants:
> - **"Will a toxic gas release happen?"** → Frequency analysis (like the probability of a hurricane).
> - **"If it happens, how far will the lethal cloud travel?"** → Consequence modeling (like the storm path).
> - **"How many people could die?"** → Vulnerability/probit analysis (like flood inundation mapping).
> - **"Is that level of risk acceptable?"** → Risk acceptance criteria comparison (like FEMA flood zone classification).

The output is essentially a *risk contour map* (isorisk contours) drawn around the plant, showing where the individual fatality risk is $10^{-4}$/year, $10^{-5}$/year, $10^{-6}$/year, etc. — directly analogous to a flood-plain map.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard QRA Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Individual Risk (IR)**: Probability of fatality of an exposed individual per year — units: **yr⁻¹**.
> - **Tolerable Individual Risk (TIR)**: HSE (UK) benchmark: **$1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year** for workers, **$1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year** for public maximum tolerable.
> - **Acceptable Risk**: Typically **$1 \times 10^{-7}$ per year** (broadly acceptable).
> - **F-N Curve Coordinates**: Plot of cumulative frequency of N or more fatalities.
> - **Probit Constant Y**: $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ where C is concentration, t is exposure time.
> - **Standard Atmospheric Stability Class D** (neutral, 4–5 m/s wind) is the default for most QRA dispersion calculations.

### 1.4 Why QRA? — The Engineering Motivation

QRA is the backbone of the **CCPS Risk-Based Process Safety (RBPS)** framework and the **Seveso III Directive (EU 2012/18/EU)** land-use planning methodology. It provides a defensible, quantitative basis for:
- Establishing **safety instrumented system (SIS)** performance targets (SIL 1/2/3).
- Siting occupied buildings relative to process units.
- Designing **fire & gas detector** layouts (detection coverage vs. demand frequency).
- Determining **emergency response planning** radii.
- Insurance and corporate risk financing decisions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage QRA Methodology

QRA is universally decomposed into the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Hazard Identification & Scenario Definition**
- Use HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, or Checklists to identify accident scenarios.
- Define the **scope of study**: inventories, materials of concern, operating modes (startup, normal, shutdown, maintenance).
- For each scenario, define the **hole size** (small 5 mm, medium 25 mm, medium-large 50 mm, large 100 mm, full-bore rupture 150+ mm).

**Stage 2 — Frequency Analysis (Initiating Event Frequency)**
- Compile equipment failure rates from databases:
  - **OREDA** (Offshore REliability DAta) — typical failure rate: $\lambda \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ per hour for process equipment.
  - **IEEE Std 500** — for instrumentation.
  - **PERD (Process Equipment Reliability Data)**.
- Construct a **Fault Tree** with AND/OR gates to determine the top-event frequency.
- Use **Event Tree Analysis** to model the propagation from initiating event through safety layers (BPCS, alarms, SIS, dikes, evacuation).

**Stage 3 — Consequence Analysis**
- **Step (a) Source Model:** Calculate release rate $Q$ using Bernoulli's equation for gas/liquid, or flashing/cavitation models for two-phase.
- **Step (b) Dispersion Model:** Gaussian plume (Pasquill-Gifford) for continuous release; heavy gas slugs (Britter-McQuaid, DEGADIS, FEM3) for dense gases.
- **Step (c) Fire/Explosion Models:** Pool fire (Heskestad), Jet fire (API RP 521), VCE (TNT equivalent or Multi-Energy Method), BLEVE.
- **Step (d) Effect / Vulnerability:** Calculate thermal radiation (kW/m²), overpressure (kPa), toxic dose (ppm·min) at receptor locations.

**Stage 4 — Risk Calculation**
- Risk = Frequency × Consequence, summed over all scenarios and receptors.
- Plot **Isorisk Contours** around the facility (e.g., $10^{-5}$, $10^{-6}$, $10^{-7}$ per year fatality lines).

**Stage 5 — Risk Evaluation**
- Compare against **risk acceptance criteria** (HSE, AIChE/CCPS, Dutch Beleidslijn, etc.).
- Demonstrate **ALARP** for risks between intolerable and broadly acceptable regions.

### 2.2 The Two Output Risk Metrics Explained

**Individual Risk (IR) — Localised, Location-Specific**

$$IR(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} f_i \cdot V_i(x, y)$$

Where $f_i$ is the frequency of accident scenario $i$, and $V_i(x,y)$ is the probability of fatality at location $(x,y)$ given scenario $i$ (obtained from probit analysis).

**Societal Risk (SR) — Population-Based**

$$F(N) = \sum_{i : N_i \geq N} f_i$$

This is plotted as the F-N curve with axes:
- Horizontal: N (number of fatalities, log scale).
- Vertical: F (cumulative frequency per year, log scale).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 QRA Problems:**

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk (Fundamental)** | $R = \sum_i f_i \times C_i$ | $R$: yr⁻¹ fatality; $f_i$: yr⁻¹; $C_i$: dimensionless probability |
| **Individual Risk** | $IR(x,y) = \sum_i f_i \cdot V_i(x,y)$ | $V_i$: vulnerability (0–1) |
| **Probit Equation (Toxic)** | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | $C$: ppm; $t$: min; $Y$ linked to $V$ via normal distribution |
| **Probit → Vulnerability** | $V = 0.5 \left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | $Y = 5 \Rightarrow V = 0.5$ (50% fatality) |
| **Probit (Thermal Radiation)** | $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{t \cdot I^{4/3}}{10^4}\right)$ | $I$: W/m²; $t$: seconds |
| **Probit (Overpressure)** | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(p)$ | $p$: overpressure in kPa |
| **Gaussian Plume Concentration** | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | $Q$: g/s; $u$: m/s; $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$: dispersion coefficients in m |
| **Source Release (Gas)** | $Q = C_d A P_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | Sonic/choked flow; $C_d \approx 0.62$–0.85 |
| **Pool Fire Radiation (Heskestad)** | $I = \dfrac{\dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta \cdot \tau}{4 \pi r^2}$ | $\tau \approx 1 - 0.32 \log_{10}(r)$ (atmospheric transmissivity) |
| **TNT Equivalent Mass** | $m_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | $E_{TNT} \approx 4.68 \times 10^6$ J/kg |
| **F-N Curve Limit (HSE)** | $F \cdot N^2 \leq \text{ALARP bound}$ | Slope $-2$ on log-log plot |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (ONGC, Reliance, ADNOC):** QRA is mandatory for offshore platforms to define temporary refuge (TIRV) location, escape route design, and platform layout.
- **Land-Use Planning (Seveso III Directive):** Determines buffer zones between chemical plants and residential areas.
- **Insurance (Marsh, Aon):** Insurers use QRA-derived Maximum Foreseeable Loss (MFL) and Estimated Maximum Loss (EML) for premium calculation.
- **Process Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511):** QRA quantifies the **tolerable hazard rate** for the process, which is then decomposed into **SIL targets** for the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Translating Probit to Vulnerability

This derivation links the abstract probit variable Y to the tangible probability of fatality V — a recurring calculation in KTU problems.

**Step 1 — Definition of the Probit Variable Y**

The probit variable Y is defined by the equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(u-5)^2}{2}\right) du
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V$ is the fraction of exposed population that suffers the defined effect (fatality), and $u$ is the integration variable. The "$-5$" shift is conventional so that $Y = 5$ corresponds to $V = 50\%$.

**Step 2 — Recognize the Cumulative Normal Distribution**

The integral above is precisely the standard normal cumulative distribution function $\Phi(\cdot)$ evaluated at $Y - 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \Phi(Y - 5) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2}\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Error Function Form**

Using the identity $\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the working form used in computational QRA codes (e.g., SAFETI, PHAST, ALOHA).

**Step 4 — Inverse Form (Given V, Find Y)**

For KTU problems where vulnerability is given and Y must be back-calculated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 5 + \sqrt{2} \cdot \operatorname{erf}^{-1}(2V - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

*Problem:* Calculate the fatality vulnerability for a person exposed to a thermal radiation intensity of $I = 25{,}000$ W/m² for $t = 30$ s.

*Step 5a — Apply Thermal Probit Equation:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \ln\!\left(\frac{I^{4/3} \cdot t}{10^4}\right) \\
&= -14.9 + 2.56 \ln\!\left(\frac{(25000)^{4/3} \cdot 30}{10^4}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5b — Evaluate the Argument:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
(25000)^{4/3} &= \left(25000^{1/3}\right)^4 = (29.24)^4 \approx 7.31 \times 10^{5} \\
\frac{7.31 \times 10^{5} \times 30}{10^4} &= \frac{2.193 \times 10^{7}}{10^{4}} = 2193
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5c — Take Natural Log:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(2193) &\approx 7.693
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5d — Compute Y:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \times 7.693 = -14.9 + 19.694 = 4.794
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5e — Convert Y to Vulnerability V:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{4.794 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}(-0.1455)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Using standard normal table: $\operatorname{erf}(-0.1455) \approx -0.1631$ (interpolated).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - 0.1631\right] = 0.4184 \approx 41.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A person at this receptor has approximately 41.8% probability of fatality given the assumed scenario.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Individual Risk Calculation

**Step 1 — Define the Risk Contribution of One Scenario**

For a single accident scenario $i$ with frequency $f_i$ and vulnerability $V_i$ at a receptor location:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_i &= f_i \cdot V_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum Across All Scenarios (Mutually Exclusive Assumption)**

For $N_s$ independent scenarios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} f_i \cdot V_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked KTU Example**

Suppose three scenarios for a chlorine storage facility:

| **Scenario (i)** | **Description** | **Frequency $f_i$ (yr⁻¹)** | **Vulnerability $V_i$ at receptor** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Small leak (5 mm) | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ | 0.001 |
| 2 | Medium leak (25 mm) | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.05 |
| 3 | Full-bore rupture | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.80 |

*Step 3a — Contribution of each scenario:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_1 &= (1.0 \times 10^{-2}) \times 0.001 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\Delta R_2 &= (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.05 = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\Delta R_3 &= (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.80 = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3b — Summation (superposition valid for rare independent events):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= (1.0 + 5.0 + 8.0) \times 10^{-5} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3c — Compare with HSE Tolerability:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{yr}^{-1} \quad &\gg \quad \text{Tolerable} = 10^{-6} \ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Risk is in the **intolerable region**; mitigation is mandatory (e.g., reduce inventory, add water curtain scrubber, install leak detection with isolation valve).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Criteria in QRA

| **Risk Measure** | **Computation** | **Acceptance Limit** | **Visualization** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individual Risk (IR) to worker | $\sum_i f_i V_i$ | $\leq 10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ | Isorisk contour map |
| Individual Risk (IR) to public | $\sum_i f_i V_i$ | $\leq 10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ | Isorisk contour map |
| Societal Risk (F-N curve) | $F(N) = \sum_{i : N_i \geq N} f_i$ | Below ALARP line (slope $-1$ to $-2$) | Log-log plot |
| Expected Loss (Property) | $\sum_i f_i \times C_i^{\text{property}}$ | Set by insurance/corporate | Average annual loss curve |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 QRA Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end information flow in a QRA study, the inputs, intermediate models, and final outputs that drive engineering decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Inventory and Operating Conditions] --> B[Hazard Identification HAZOP HAZID FMEA]
    B --> C[Scenario Selection with Hole Size Categories]
    C --> D[Frequency Analysis Fault Tree Event Tree]
    D --> E[Initiating Event Frequency f_i per year]
    C --> F[Consequence Analysis Pipeline]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Source Model Release Rate Q]
    G --> H[Dispersion Model Gaussian or Dense Gas]
    H --> I[Effect Model Thermal Radiation Overpressure Toxic Dose]
    I --> J[Vulnerability via Probit Equations]
    J --> K[Risk Calculation R equals f_i times V_i]
    K --> L[Individual Risk IR Contour Map]
    K --> M[Societal Risk F-N Curve]
    L --> N[Risk Comparison Against HSE Acceptance Criteria]
    M --> N
    N --> O[ALARP Demonstration and Mitigation Measures]
    O --> P[SIL Determination per IEC 61511]
    P --> Q[Safety Instrumented Function Design]
```

### 4.2 Probit-Based Vulnerability Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Receptor Location x y] --> B[Hazard Type Selection]
    B --> B1[Thermal]
    B --> B2[Overpressure]
    B --> B3[Toxic Dose]
    B1 --> C1[Probit Y equals minus 14.9 plus 2.56 ln t times I four thirds over 10 to power 4]
    B2 --> C2[Probit Y equals minus 77.1 plus 6.91 ln p]
    B3 --> C3[Probit Y equals a plus b ln C to n times t]
    C1 --> D[Apply Inverse Normal Transformation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Fatality Probability V between 0 and 1]
    E --> F[Risk equals frequency f times V]
```

### 4.3 F-N Curve Interpretation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Scenario Fatalities N_i and Frequencies f_i] --> B[Sort in Descending Order of N_i]
    B --> C[Cumulative Sum F of N equals sum of f_i for scenarios with N_i greater than or equal to N]
    C --> D[Plot F on Vertical Axis and N on Horizontal Axis Both Log Scales]
    D --> E{Draw ALARP Region Bound Lines}
    E --> F[HSE Slope minus 1 and minus 2 Tolerability Lines]
    F --> G[Plot Computed F-N Curve]
    G --> H{Curve Position Relative to ALARP Band}
    H --> I[Intolerable Region: Stop Operation]
    H --> J[ALARP Region: Demonstrate Risk Reduction Measures]
    H --> K[Broadly Acceptable Region: Continue with Monitoring]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). List any four inputs required to perform a QRA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) is a formal, systematic engineering methodology used to numerically estimate the risk (defined as the product of frequency of occurrence and magnitude of consequence) associated with the operation of a process facility. It combines probabilistic failure data with physical consequence modeling to produce risk metrics that can be compared against defined acceptance criteria.

**Inputs Required (2 marks — any four of the following):**

1. **Plant inventory and process conditions:** Type, quantity, temperature, pressure, phase, and hazard properties (toxicity, flammability) of all chemicals.
2. **Equipment failure rate data:** Failure frequencies from databases such as OREDA, IEEE Std 500, or PERD.
3. **Operating procedures and human reliability data:** For estimating probability of operator action success or failure.
4. **Site layout and meteorological data:** Wind rose, atmospheric stability class, Pasquill-Gifford coefficients, population density, and building locations.
5. **Safety system performance data:** Reliability of BPCS, alarms, interlocks, fire & gas detectors, emergency shutdown valves, and mitigation systems (dikes, water spray).
6. **Effect/vulnerability thresholds:** Probit equations for thermal radiation, overpressure, and toxic dose response.

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk (SR). Mention the units of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Individual Risk (IR)** | **Societal Risk (SR)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Risk experienced by a hypothetical individual continuously present at a specific location, regardless of whether they are actually present. | Aggregate risk to a defined population; accounts for the number of people exposed to each scenario. |
| **Focus** | Location-specific; tied to a point on the facility layout. | Population-specific; integrates all persons at risk. |
| **Units** | Probability per year (yr⁻¹). | Cumulative frequency per year (yr⁻¹) for F-N curve; or Expected fatalities per year. |
| **Visualization** | Isorisk contour map on site plan. | F-N curve on log-log plot. |
| **Criterion** | HSE: $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ tolerable; $10^{-7}$ yr⁻¹ acceptable. | Slope $-1$ and $-2$ lines on F-N plot. |
| **Use Case** | Land-use planning, building siting. | Public safety, emergency planning. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five-step methodology of QRA** with a neat flowchart. Describe each stage's purpose and outputs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 25 mm leak occurs in a pressurized chlorine pipe carrying gas at $P = 10$ bar (gauge), temperature $T = 25$ °C, releasing into open atmosphere. The leak frequency is $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. Using Pasquill-Gifford Class D stability, wind speed $u = 4$ m/s, calculate:
- (i) The dispersion coefficient $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ at downwind distance $x = 500$ m using the empirical formulas $\sigma_y = 0.08 x (1 + 0.0001 x)^{-1/2}$ and $\sigma_z = 0.06 x (1 + 0.0015 x)^{-1/2}$.
- (ii) The centerline concentration at ground level.
- (iii) The Individual Risk at the receptor, assuming probit constant Y = 6 corresponding to vulnerability V = 0.73. Use chlorine molecular weight = 70.9 g/mol, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.62$, and assume choked flow. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a) Five-Step QRA Methodology (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification (1 Mark):**
Identify hazardous materials, inventories, and process conditions. Use HAZOP, FMEA, What-If analyses to enumerate credible accident scenarios. Define hole size categories (small 5 mm, medium 25 mm, large 100 mm, full-bore rupture).

**Step 2 — Frequency Analysis (1.5 Marks):**
Quantify the frequency $f_i$ of each scenario using Fault Tree Analysis (top-down) and Event Tree Analysis (forward propagation through safety barriers). Sources: OREDA, IEEE Std 500, plant-specific data.

**Step 3 — Consequence Analysis (2 Marks):**
Model the physical outcome:
- **Source model** — release rate $Q$ from choked/subsonic flow.
- **Dispersion model** — Gaussian plume for neutrally buoyant, dense gas models for heavy gases.
- **Effect models** — thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic dose at receptors.

**Step 4 — Risk Calculation (1.5 Marks):**
Compute $R_i = f_i \times V_i$ for each scenario. Sum to obtain **Individual Risk** at each receptor and **Societal Risk** for the population. Plot isorisk contours and F-N curve.

**Step 5 — Risk Evaluation and ALARP (1 Mark):**
Compare computed risks against HSE/CCPS acceptance criteria. If intolerable or in the ALARP region, propose cost-effective mitigation measures (e.g., SIS, water curtain, inventory reduction) and re-evaluate.

*Flowchart (already shown in Section 4 Diagram 4.1).*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**(i) Dispersion Coefficients at x = 500 m (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= 0.08 \times 500 \times (1 + 0.0001 \times 500)^{-1/2} \\
\sigma_y &= 40 \times (1 + 0.05)^{-1/2} = 40 \times (1.05)^{-0.5} = 40 \times 0.9759 \approx 39.04 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_z &= 0.06 \times 500 \times (1 + 0.0015 \times 500)^{-1/2} \\
\sigma_z &= 30 \times (1 + 0.75)^{-1/2} = 30 \times (1.75)^{-0.5} = 30 \times 0.7559 \approx 22.68 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Source Release Rate and Centerline Concentration (3 Marks):**

*Choked flow gas release rate:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C_d A P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma M}{RT}\!\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For chlorine, $\gamma \approx 1.36$. Take absolute pressure $P_0 = 11$ bar = $1.1 \times 10^6$ Pa. Hole area $A = \pi (0.0125)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ m².

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{2}{2.36}\right)^{2.36/0.36} &= (0.8475)^{6.556} \approx 0.395 \\
\frac{\gamma M}{RT} &= \frac{1.36 \times 0.0709}{8.314 \times 298} = \frac{0.0964}{2477.6} \approx 3.89 \times 10^{-5} \\
\sqrt{3.89 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.395} &= \sqrt{1.537 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.003921
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.62 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.1 \times 10^{6} \times 0.003921 \\
Q &\approx 1.313 \ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Centerline concentration at ground level ($y = 0$, $z = 0$):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} = \frac{1.313}{2\pi \times 39.04 \times 22.68 \times 4} \\
C &= \frac{1.313}{22{,}253} \approx 5.9 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.059 \ \text{g/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to ppm (chlorine, MW = 70.9):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ppm} &= \frac{C_{\text{mg/m}^3} \times 24.45}{MW} = \frac{59 \times 24.45}{70.9} \approx 20.3 \ \text{ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Individual Risk at the Receptor (3 Marks):**

Given vulnerability $V = 0.73$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= f_i \times V_i = (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.73 = 7.3 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** $IR = 7.3 \times 10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹ $\gg$ HSE tolerable limit of $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹. **Risk is INTOLERABLE**; immediate risk reduction measures required.

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define the **probit variable Y** and derive the relationship between Y and fatality probability V. Use the thermal radiation probit equation to determine the vulnerability of a person exposed to $I = 12{,}500$ W/m² for $t = 20$ s. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and interpretation of an **F-N curve** in a QRA study. Discuss the HSE tolerability framework and ALARP principle with suitable sketch. A facility has the following three major accident scenarios:

| **Scenario** | **Fatalities (N)** | **Frequency (f, yr⁻¹)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 2 | 20 | $5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 3 | 5 | $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |

Plot the F-N points and determine if any scenario falls in the intolerable region (use HSE slope $-2$ criterion with limit line passing through (10 fatalities, $10^{-3}$ yr⁻¹)). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a) Probit Variable Definition and Vulnerability Derivation (7 Marks)**

*(Derivation shown in Section 3.1 — awarding 4 marks. The numerical computation below carries 3 marks.)*

**Numerical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \ln\!\left(\frac{I^{4/3} \cdot t}{10^4}\right) \\
I^{4/3} &= (12500)^{1.333} \\
12500^{1/3} &\approx 23.21 \\
12500^{4/3} &= (23.21)^4 \approx 2.901 \times 10^{5} \\
\frac{I^{4/3} \cdot t}{10^4} &= \frac{2.901 \times 10^{5} \times 20}{10^4} = 580.2 \\
\ln(580.2) &\approx 6.365 \\
Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \times 6.365 = -14.9 + 16.295 = 1.395
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{1.395 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}(-2.551)\right] \\
\operatorname{erf}(-2.551) &\approx -0.9986 \\
V &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - 0.9986\right] = 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.055\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Vulnerability is very low ($\approx 0.05\%$ fatality probability) at this radiation level and exposure time.

---

**(b) F-N Curve Construction and Interpretation (7 Marks)**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**

The F-N curve plots cumulative frequency $F(N)$ of accidents causing N or more fatalities on the vertical (log) axis, against N on the horizontal (log) axis. Each point represents the probability that an accident will result in at least N fatalities. The curve is constructed by sorting scenarios in descending order of $N$ and summing the frequencies of all scenarios causing $\geq N$ fatalities.

The **HSE Tolerability Framework** divides the F-N plot into three regions:
- **Intolerable Region (above slope $-1$ line):** Risk must be reduced regardless of cost.
- **ALARP Region (between slope $-1$ and slope $-2$ lines):** Risk reduction must be pursued unless grossly disproportionate cost.
- **Broadly Acceptable Region (below slope $-2$ line):** Risk is tolerable; no further action required.

**Numerical F-N Calculation (3 Marks):**

| **N (Fatalities)** | **Scenarios with $N_i \geq N$** | **Cumulative Frequency F(N) (yr⁻¹)** |
|---|---|---|
| 50 | {1} | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 20 | {1, 2} | $1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 5 | {1, 2, 3} | $6.0 \times 10^{-5} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 2.6 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**HSE Limit Line Equation (slope $-2$ through (10, $10^{-3}$)):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} F &= -2 \log_{10} N + \log_{10}(10^{-3} \times 10^{2}) = -2 \log_{10} N - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So at $N = 50$: $F_{\text{limit}} = -2 \log_{10}(50) - 1 = -2(1.699) - 1 = -4.398 \Rightarrow F = 3.99 \times 10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹.

**Comparison:** Scenario 1 has $F = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ at $N = 50$, which is **below** the limit line of $3.99 \times 10^{-5}$. Therefore **no scenario falls in the intolerable region** for the given limit line.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cumulative sum nature of F-N:** Many students plot point frequencies $f_i$ instead of cumulative $F(N)$. This is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Unit errors in dispersion calculations:** Concentration must be in consistent SI units (kg/m³ for the Gaussian formula, not g/m³). Forgetting the molecular weight conversion to ppm is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Mixing probit constants:** Use the correct probit equation (thermal vs. overpressure vs. toxic). Writing a single equation for all three is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Forgetting to state ALARP conclusion:** Even if the calculation is correct, failing to state whether risk is tolerable/intolerable/ALARP loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not plotting or describing F-N curve sketch:** A textual answer without any sketch loses **2 marks** in the F-N question.
> 6. **Off-by-one in summing $f_i$:** Frequency and vulnerability are not interchangeable; remember $R = f \times V$ is multiplicative, not additive unless on common terms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for QRA (Module 2):**

- **Definition**: QRA = Frequency × Consequence; produces Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk (SR) as numerical outputs.
- **Five-step methodology**: (1) Hazard ID → (2) Frequency Analysis → (3) Consequence Analysis → (4) Risk Calculation → (5) Risk Evaluation/ALARP.
- **Key probit equations**:
  - Thermal: $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(I^{4/3} t / 10^4)$
  - Overpressure: $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(p)$
  - Toxic: $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ (constants from TNO Green Book).
- **Probit → Vulnerability conversion**: $V = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})\right]$.
- **Individual Risk formula**: $IR = \sum_i f_i V_i$ with units of yr⁻¹.
- **HSE risk criteria**:
  - Worker tolerable IR: $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹.
  - Public tolerable IR: $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹.
  - Broadly acceptable: $10^{-7}$ yr⁻¹.
- **F-N curve**: Cumulative frequency $F(N)$ vs number of fatalities $N$, on log-log axes. Slope $-1$ and $-2$ define ALARP envelope.
- **Common failure rate databases**: OREDA (offshore), IEEE Std 500 (instrumentation), PERD, CCPS PERD.
- **Common QRA software**: DNV SAFETI, PHAST, ALOHA, Effects, CANARY by Quest, FRED (Shell).
- **Common accident scenarios**: Toxic dispersion, pool fire, jet fire, VCE, BLEVE, missile generation.
- **ALARP principle**: Reduce risk to "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — burden of proof lies with the operator to demonstrate residual risk is acceptable.
- **Key constants to remember**: TNT energy $E_{TNT} = 4.68 \times 10^6$ J/kg; chlorine MW = 70.9 g/mol; standard atmosphere $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa; universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/mol·K.
- **Key atmospheric stability class**: Pasquill Class D (neutral, wind 4–5 m/s) is the **default QRA assumption** unless site-specific data exists.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Initial Start-Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Initial Start-Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Initial Start-Up** is defined as the phase in the lifecycle of a chemical/process facility that bridges the completion of construction (Mechanical Completion) and the commencement of normal, steady-state commercial operation. As per the **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals)** standard and KTU 2024 PECHT864 syllabus, Initial Start-Up encompasses a structured sequence of commissioning activities culminating in the **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** — a formal, documented audit confirming that the plant is safe to introduce process fluids and begin operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Recall Box:**
> *Initial Start-Up is the controlled, phased commissioning of a newly constructed or significantly modified process plant, which includes mechanical completion verification, operator training, PSSR, and the controlled introduction of feedstocks until design throughput is achieved.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just bought a brand-new car (the **Plant**) from the showroom (the **EPC Contractor**). Before you hit the highway (commercial operation), you must:
1. Check the tire pressure, engine oil, coolant, and brakes (**Mechanical Completion**)
2. Get your driving license verified (**Operator Training & Competency**)
3. Install a fire extinguisher and first-aid kit (**Emergency Response Systems**)
4. Do a slow test-drive in a parking lot to ensure everything works (**Commissioning & Bump Testing**)
5. Finally, an RTO inspector issues a Fitness Certificate (**Pre-Startup Safety Review — PSSR**)

This entire chain of activities, *from handover to fitness certification*, is **Initial Start-Up**. Skipping any step is equivalent to driving blindfolded — and in chemical engineering, this can mean a Bhopal-scale catastrophe.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Codes & Standard Metrics

| Standard / Metric | Value / Reference | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| OSHA PSM Standard | **29 CFR 1910.119** | Mandatory for >threshold quantities |
| API Recommended Practice | **API RP 14C / API 520 / API 521** | Commissioning & relief devices |
| ASME Boiler Code | **ASME Section VIII** | Pressure vessel integrity testing |
| Hydrotest Pressure Factor | **1.3 × × MAWP** (Minimum Allowable Working Pressure) | Standard leak test pressure |
| Hold Time for Leak Test | **≥ 10 minutes** (typical) | Pneumatic / hydrostatic retention |
| PSSR Sign-off Authority | Plant Manager + Safety Officer | Joint accountability |
| Operator Training Hours (KTU/Industry norm) | **≥ 40 hours** classroom + OJT | Competency verification |

> [!NOTE]
> The hydrostatic test pressure multiplier of **1.3 × MAWP** is a global benchmark used in pressure vessel commissioning (per ASME BPVC Section VIII, UG-99). Always cross-verify with project-specific data sheets.

### 1.4 Why Initial Start-Up is the Most Risk-Intensive Phase

Studies by the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** indicate that **over 35% of major process incidents** occur during commissioning, start-up, and shutdown — collectively called *transient operations*. Initial start-up is risk-prone because:
- Equipment is **new** and untested under real conditions
- Operators are **unfamiliar** with the specific unit
- Procedures may contain **latent errors** from design-to-construction translation
- Inventory of hazardous chemicals is being **introduced for the first time**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Phase Architecture of Initial Start-Up

Initial start-up is best understood as five logically sequenced phases. Each phase has a "Gate" — a documented deliverable that must be cleared before the next phase begins.

**Phase 1: Mechanical Completion (MC)**
- All piping, vessels, instrumentation, and electrical systems are physically installed per the **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)** and **Isometric Drawings**.
- Punch-list items (minor construction deficiencies) are categorized as **A** (must close before commissioning), **B** (must close before start-up), or **C** (can wait until after start-up).
- Insulation, painting, and tagging are verified.

**Phase 2: Pre-Commissioning (Systematic Checking)**
- Cold loop checks (continuity tests on instrumentation loops without process fluid).
- Rotation checks on motors (3-phase sequence verification, no-load bump test).
- Valve line-up verification: every valve is checked for correct identity, position, and direction (open/close).
- Cleaning, flushing, and chemical cleaning of piping systems.
- Drying and inerting (typically using **Nitrogen** — dew point target ≤ **−40 °C** for cryogenic service).

**Phase 3: Commissioning (Introduction of Utilities)**
- Introduction of utilities: instrument air, nitrogen, steam, cooling water, electrical power.
- Functional testing of control loops, interlocks, alarms, and trips (without process feedstock).
- **SOV (Solenoid Operated Valve) trip testing** — ESD (Emergency Shutdown) system function test.
- Verification of relief devices set pressures (using a test gag or bench calibration).

**Phase 4: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**
- The **gate-keeper** phase. A formal, multi-disciplinary review confirming:
  - Construction matches P&IDs and process descriptions
  - Operating procedures are in place and validated
  - Operator training is complete and documented
  - Emergency response plans are established
  - Management of Change (MOC) reviews for any late-stage design changes are complete
  - Mechanical integrity inspections are documented
  - Safety, health, and environmental (SHE) reviews are closed
- **Output**: A signed PSSR certificate authorizing the introduction of process chemicals.

**Phase 5: Initial Introduction of Process Feed & Ramp-Up**
- Sequenced introduction of feedstocks (often from low-hazard to high-hazard streams first).
- Step-wise increase in throughput to design capacity (typically in **10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%** increments).
- Continuous monitoring of vibration, temperature, pressure profiles.
- PID controller tuning under real load conditions.

### 2.2 The Role of PSSR — Deep Dive

The PSSR is the **legal and operational linchpin** of initial start-up. OSHA 1910.119(l) requires that *"The employer shall perform a pre-startup safety review for new facilities and for modified facilities when the modification is significant enough to require a change in the process safety information."*

**The PSSR team must verify the following:**

1. **Construction & Equipment** matches P&ID, PFD (Process Flow Diagram), and PSM information
2. **Operating Procedures** are complete, current, and accessible
3. **Training** of every operating employee has been completed and documented
4. **Process Safety Information (PSI)** is updated as-built
5. **Mechanical Integrity** inspections are complete and documented
6. **Relief Devices** are sized, installed, and verified
7. **Emergency Response Plan** is in place and personnel are trained
8. **MOC (Management of Change)** records are reconciled — any deviation between design and as-built must have an approved MOC
9. **Hazard Analysis (HAZOP / What-If)** recommendations are resolved or formally deferred with risk justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The PSSR is a *single-point-of-no-return* gate. Without a signed PSSR, the plant cannot legally start under PSM jurisdiction. This is one of the most frequently asked questions in PSM exams.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Initial Start-Up involves both engineering and procedural rigor, here is the consolidated reference table:

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP$ | bar (g) | Pressure vessel leak test |
| Pneumatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.1 \times MAWP$ | bar (g) | When hydrotest not feasible |
| Leak Test Hold Time | $\Delta t_{hold}$ | $\geq 10$ min | Per ASME UG-99 |
| Dew Point Specification (N₂ drying) | $T_{dp}$ | $\leq -40 \text{ °C}$ | Cryogenic & O₂ service |
| Nitrogen Purity | $\% N_2$ | $\geq 99.99 \text{ vol\%}$ | Inerting/purging |
| Operator Training Hours | $H_{train}$ | $\geq 40 \text{ hr}$ classroom + OJT | Competency basis |
| Ramp-up Steps | $\% Capacity$ | $10 \rightarrow 25 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow 75 \rightarrow 100$ | Throughput staging |
| Reynolds Number (Flushing) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $Re \geq 4000$ (turbulent) | Pipeline cleaning |
| Velocity for Flushing | $v_{flush}$ | $\geq 1.5 \text{ m/s}$ | Remove debris |
| Pressure Relief Orifice Area | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{P_1 - P_2}}$ | m² | API 520 sizing |
| Air Consumption (Leak Test) | $Q = V \cdot \Delta P \cdot t^{-1}$ | Nm³/hr | Pneumatic integrity check |

> [!NOTE]
> The Reynolds Number formula uses `vert` for absolute value wherever needed in KTU answer sheets to avoid markdown parsing issues. The expression $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ is the universal form.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Initial start-up engineering is the **stress-test** of the entire design chain — from conceptual P&ID to construction. In practice:
- A single missed leak-test on a flange can lead to a vapor cloud explosion (VCE) — as seen in the **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK)**, where a temporary bypass pipe (installed without PSSR-grade review) failed catastrophically.
- In refinery FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) units, start-up sequence errors cause **~12% of all reported near-misses** globally (industry data, CCPS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hydrostatic Test Pressure (ASME Section VIII)

The **Minimum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** is the maximum permissible gauge pressure at the top of a vessel during normal operation at the design coincident temperature.

**Starting Principle:**
The hydrostatic test pressure must be high enough to stress the vessel sufficiently to reveal any manufacturing or fabrication defect (cracks, weld porosity, gasket leakage), but must not cause yielding of the base material.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

where:
- $P$ = internal pressure
- $D$ = inside diameter
- $t$ = wall thickness

**Step 1:** At design conditions, the allowable stress is $\sigma_{allow}$ (a fraction of the material's yield stress at design temperature, per ASME UG-23).

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{S_{yd}}{FS}
$$

where $S_{yd}$ = yield stress at design temperature, and $FS$ = safety factor (typically **1.0** for hydrotest, since the test is short-duration and non-cyclic — but the test stress must still remain below the yield strength at test temperature).

**Step 2:** Equating the hoop stress under hydrotest to the allowable yield-based stress:

$$
\dfrac{P_{test} \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \leq S_{y,test}
$$

**Step 3:** For MAWP, we have $\sigma_{hoop,MAWP} = \sigma_{allow}$:

$$
MAWP = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{allow}}{D}
$$

**Step 4:** Combining the two equations:

$$
P_{test} = S_{y,test} \cdot \dfrac{2t}{D} = \dfrac{S_{y,test}}{\sigma_{allow}} \cdot MAWP
$$

**Step 5:** Empirical ratio for carbon and low-alloy steels (ASME code calibrates this at room temperature yield over design temperature allowable):

$$
\dfrac{S_{y,test}}{\sigma_{allow}} \approx 1.30
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$
P_{test} = 1.30 \cdot MAWP
$$

This is the **famous 1.3 × MAWP** rule used universally in vessel commissioning. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Flushing Velocity (Pig/Velocity-Based Debris Removal)

For pipeline flushing, a turbulent flow regime is required to suspend and carry debris. The lower bound of turbulent flow is $Re = 4000$.

**Step 1:** Define Reynolds Number:

$$
Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for minimum velocity:

$$
v_{min} = \dfrac{Re_{critical} \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}
$$

**Step 3:** For water at 20 °C:
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $Re_{crit} = 4000$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$
v_{min} = \dfrac{4000 \cdot 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}{998 \cdot D}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical evaluation:

$$
v_{min} = \dfrac{4.008}{998 \cdot D}
$$

$$
v_{min} = \dfrac{0.004016}{D} \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

**Step 6:** Industry practice adds a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** to ensure vigorous scouring:

$$
v_{flush} \geq 1.5 \cdot v_{min}
$$

**Step 7:** For a typical 6-inch (DN150, D ≈ 0.15 m) pipe:

$$
v_{min} = \dfrac{0.004016}{0.15} = 0.0268 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{flush} = 1.5 \cdot 0.0268 = 0.0402 \text{ m/s}
$$

Since this is unrealistically low (due to water's low viscosity), industry uses a **stipulated minimum of 1.5 m/s** for water flushing, which gives $Re \gg 4000$ — safely turbulent.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PSSR Checklist (Reproducible for KTU Answers)

| # | PSSR Checklist Item | Verification Method | Responsible Discipline |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piping matches latest revised P&ID | Field walk-down vs. as-built | Process + Piping |
| 2 | All instruments calibrated | Loop test reports signed | Instrumentation |
| 3 | Relief valves set & sealed | Bench calibration tag | Mechanical |
| 4 | ESD system functional | Trip matrix test (full simulation) | Safety + I&C |
| 5 | Operating procedures approved | Document control approval | Operations |
| 6 | Operator training records | HR/Training database | HR + Operations |
| 7 | Emergency response plan | Mock drill report | HSE |
| 8 | MOC reconciliation | MOC register cross-check | Engineering |
| 9 | HAZOP recommendations closed | HAZOP action tracker | Process Safety |
| 10 | Mechanical integrity (vessels, tanks) | Inspection certificates | QA/QC |
| 11 | Electrical hazardous area classification | Thermography & continuity | Electrical |
| 12 | Firefighting systems commissioned | Hydrotest of sprinkler / foam skid | HSE + Mechanical |

### 3.4 Analytical Computation: Leak Rate Acceptance Criterion

During pneumatic leak testing, the pressure decay is monitored. The allowable leak rate is:

$$
\Delta P_{allow} = \dfrac{P_{initial} - P_{final}}{t_{hold}} \quad \text{[bar/hr]}
$$

**Typical acceptance:** $\Delta P_{allow} \leq 0.5\%$ of $P_{initial}$ per hour.

**Worked Example:**

Given:
- $P_{initial} = 5.2$ bar (g)
- $P_{final} = 5.18$ bar (g) after 1 hour

$$
\Delta P = 5.20 - 5.18 = 0.02 \text{ bar/hr}
$$

$$
\%\Delta P = \dfrac{0.02}{5.20} \times 100 = 0.385\%
$$

Since $0.385\% < 0.5\%$, the system **passes** the leak test. ✅
