## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define the term **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** as per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119. List any four items verified during a PSSR.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
PSSR is a formal, documented safety review conducted **before** the introduction of hazardous process fluids into a newly constructed or significantly modified facility. It confirms that all PSM elements — equipment, procedures, training, and emergency preparedness — are in place and consistent with the final design.

Four verification items:
1. Construction matches the latest P&IDs and Process Safety Information (**1 mark**)
2. Operating procedures are approved, current, and accessible (**0.5 marks**)
3. Operator training records are complete (**0.5 marks**)
4. Relief devices are sized, set, and sealed (**0.5 marks**)

---

**Q2.** What is the significance of the **1.3 × MAWP** rule in the context of initial start-up?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The 1.3 × MAWP rule defines the hydrostatic test pressure for pressure vessels during commissioning (**1 mark**). It ensures that the vessel is stressed sufficiently to expose fabrication defects such as weld cracks, porosity, and gasket leaks (**1 mark**), while keeping the stress below the material's yield strength at test temperature (**1 mark**). This is mandated by **ASME Section VIII, UG-99** as a global benchmark for integrity verification before process fluids are introduced.

---

### 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Coverage

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five sequential phases** of initial start-up of a process plant. Highlight the deliverables (gates) at each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a step-by-step derivation, justify the **hydrostatic test pressure = 1.3 × MAWP** recommendation. Mention the standard that mandates this. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Phases of Initial Start-Up (7 Marks):**

| Phase | Name | Key Activities | Gate / Deliverable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mechanical Completion** | Equipment installation, piping, electrical, insulation, painting | MC Certificate + Punch-list |
| 2 | **Pre-Commissioning** | Loop checks, motor rotation, valve line-up, flushing, drying | Pre-Commissioning Dossier |
| 3 | **Commissioning** | Utility introduction, instrument calibration, ESD trip test, relief valve setting | Commissioning Report |
| 4 | **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | Multi-disciplinary audit of PSM elements, MOC reconciliation, training records, emergency plans | **Signed PSSR Certificate** |
| 5 | **Initial Feed & Ramp-Up** | Sequenced feed introduction, stepwise throughput increase (10-25-50-75-100%) | Handover to Operations |

Each phase forms a **logical gate** — the next phase cannot begin until the current gate is formally signed off. The PSSR is the most critical gate, as it legally authorizes the introduction of hazardous process fluids (**2 marks** for explaining PSSR role; **5 marks** for the rest).

**(b) Derivation of 1.3 × MAWP (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Hoop stress in a thin-walled pressure vessel:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2:** At MAWP, the hoop stress equals the allowable design stress:

$$
MAWP = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{allow}}{D}
$$

**Step 3:** During hydrotest, the vessel must be stressed but not yielded:

$$
P_{test} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2t} \leq S_{y,test}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
P_{test} \leq S_{y,test} \cdot \dfrac{2t}{D}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $MAWP$:

$$
P_{test} \leq \dfrac{S_{y,test}}{\sigma_{allow}} \cdot MAWP
$$

**Step 6:** For carbon/low-alloy steels, the empirical ratio $\dfrac{S_{y,test}}{\sigma_{allow}} \approx 1.30$, giving:

$$
\boxed{P_{test} = 1.3 \cdot MAWP}
$$

**Standard:** **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII, UG-99 (Hydrostatic Test Pressure)** (**1 mark for citing standard**).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **role and responsibilities** of the PSSR team. List **eight** items that must be verified during a PSSR for a newly commissioned ammonia refrigeration plant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A newly installed pressure vessel has a **MAWP = 12 bar (g)** and a design temperature of **150 °C**. Determine the **hydrostatic test pressure** to be applied during commissioning. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel ($S_{y,test} = 262 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_{allow} = 200 \text{ MPa}$). Verify the answer against the standard rule. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PSSR Team Role and Verification (7 Marks):**

The PSSR team is a **multi-disciplinary audit body** comprising representatives from Process Engineering, Operations, Maintenance, Safety/Health/Environment, QA/QC, and Project Engineering. Their collective role is to independently confirm that the plant, as built, is **safe** and **fit-for-purpose** before hazardous fluids are introduced (**2 marks for role description**).

**Eight verification items for an ammonia refrigeration plant:**

1. Ammonia vessels and piping match the latest P&IDs (**0.5 marks**)
2. Pressure relief devices (PSVs) on ammonia receivers are sized and set per ASME/API (**0.5 marks**)
3. Ammonia detection systems (calibrated sensors, alarms) are functional (**0.5 marks**)
4. Ventilation systems in machinery room are operational (**0.5 marks**)
5. Emergency eyewash and safety showers are installed and tested (**0.5 marks**)
6. Operator training on ammonia handling and leak response is documented (**0.5 marks**)
7. Material compatibility (carbon steel vs. ammonia) is verified (**0.5 marks**)
8. MOC register is reconciled — any field deviations are approved (**0.5 marks**)
9. Emergency response plan for ammonia release is in place (**0.5 marks**)
10. HAZOP recommendations are closed (**0.5 marks**)

(Any eight fetch the remaining **5 marks**.)

**(b) Hydrostatic Test Pressure Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $MAWP = 12$ bar (g)
- Design temperature = 150 °C
- $S_{y,test} = 262$ MPa (yield strength at hydrotest temperature ~ ambient)
- $\sigma_{allow} = 200$ MPa (allowable stress at design temperature)

**Step 1:** Apply the rigorous derivation:

$$
P_{test} = \dfrac{S_{y,test}}{\sigma_{allow}} \cdot MAWP
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
P_{test} = \dfrac{262}{200} \cdot 12
$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$
P_{test} = 1.31 \cdot 12 = 15.72 \text{ bar (g)}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the standard rule (1.3 × MAWP):

$$
P_{test,standard} = 1.30 \cdot 12 = 15.60 \text{ bar (g)}
$$

**Step 5:** Verification: The rigorous value (15.72 bar) exceeds the standard rule (15.60 bar) by 0.77%. Since the standard rule is the **minimum mandatory** pressure, both values are acceptable, but the project specification dictates which one to use (**2 marks for comparison & comment**).

**Final Answer:** $P_{test} \approx 15.72$ bar (g) by rigorous derivation; **15.60 bar (g)** by ASME standard (**1 mark for final answer**).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PSSR with commissioning** — PSSR is a *review*, not a test. It happens *after* commissioning, *before* process fluids. Examiners deduct marks if students describe PSSR as "testing equipment."
>
> 2. **Writing "hydrotest pressure = MAWP"** — This is the most common numerical error. Always show the **1.3 factor** explicitly.
>
> 3. **Omitting the PSSR deliverable** — The signed certificate is the *output* of PSSR. Forgetting it loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing pneumatic (1.1 × MAWP) with hydrostatic (1.3 × MAWP)** test pressures. KTU examiners love this trap.
>
> 5. **Skipping MOC reconciliation** in PSSR list. MOC is a *standalone* PSM element (29 CFR 1910.119(l)(4)) and its reconciliation is mandatory.
>
> 6. **In numerical problems, not stating units.** Marks are reserved explicitly for the unit "bar(g)" or "MPa."

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Initial Start-Up:**

- ✅ Initial Start-Up = bridge between **construction** and **commercial operation**.
- ✅ Five phases: **MC → Pre-Commissioning → Commissioning → PSSR → Feed Introduction & Ramp-Up**.
- ✅ **PSSR** is the legal gate per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(l)**.
- ✅ PSSR team must verify **construction, procedures, training, PSI, MI, MOC, HAZOP, ERP, relief devices**.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic test pressure** = $1.3 \times MAWP$ (ASME Section VIII, UG-99).
- ✅ **Pneumatic test pressure** = $1.1 \times MAWP$ (used when hydrotest is unsafe, e.g., lined vessels).
- ✅ Leak test acceptance: pressure decay $\leq 0.5\%$ per hour.
- ✅ Flushing velocity $\geq 1.5$ m/s for water; Reynolds Number $Re \geq 4000$ for turbulent cleaning.
- ✅ Operator training: **≥ 40 hours** classroom + on-the-job training (OJT).
- ✅ Ramp-up sequence: **10 → 25 → 50 → 75 → 100%** of design capacity.
- ✅ Punch-list classes: **A (close before commissioning), B (close before start-up), C (post-start-up closure)**.
- ✅ ESD and interlock trip testing is **mandatory** before PSSR.
- ✅ **MOC reconciliation** is non-negotiable — any field deviation must have an approved MOC.
- ✅ Historical context: **Flixborough 1974** disaster was caused by skipping proper PSSR-grade review.
- ✅ KTU frequently asks: "List PSSR items," "Justify 1.3 × MAWP," "Differentiate hydrostatic vs. pneumatic tests."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Operations** in Process Safety Management (PSM) are defined as the **steady-state, planned, and intended operating conditions** of a process plant during which all process variables (temperature, pressure, flow rate, level, composition) remain within their established **Safe Operating Limits (SOL)** and **Operating Limits**, while all equipment functions as per its design intent and all administrative controls (procedures, permits, training, communication) are followed as per the **Process Safety Information (PSI)** and **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.

In the framework of **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (the global benchmark adopted in KTU curriculum), Normal Operations represent the baseline condition from which all other process states (Startup, Shutdown, Emergency, Maintenance) deviate. It is the **"designed-for" state** of the plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Normal Operations is NOT "any operation that is not an emergency." It is a precisely defined state where:
> - Process variables are within **Design Limits** and **Safe Operating Limits**
> - Operating procedures are being followed strictly
> - All safeguards (alarms, interlocks, relief devices) are in **standby/armed** mode
> - Personnel are operating under their **trained competency levels**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Driving a Car on a Highway**
>
> Think of a chemical plant like a car cruising on a highway at **80 km/h** in 5th gear:
> - **Normal Operations** = Cruising smoothly. The engine temperature is normal, oil pressure is optimal, fuel level is steady, and you are following all traffic rules (lane discipline, speed limits).
> - **Operating Limits** = The green zone on your dashboard gauges (temperature needle in the middle).
> - **Safe Operating Limits** = The wider yellow zone (warning before damage).
> - **Emergency Operations** = Engine overheating or brake failure — you are outside the safe envelope.
> - **Operator Responsibility** = The driver's continuous attention to road, mirrors, and dashboard readings.
>
> Just as the highway has a **speed limit (regulatory limit)** and the **car has a mechanical limit (red zone on tachometer)**, a process plant has **procedural limits** and **equipment/mechanical limits**. Both must be respected simultaneously.

## 1.3 Critical Metrics & Standard Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard PSM Metrics for Normal Operations:**
> - **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)** = minimum concentration of vapor in air that can ignite — process must operate **below 25% of LFL** (per OSHA/NFPA) in confined areas
> - **TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** = **8 hours** per OSHA standard for occupational exposure
> - **STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)** = **15 minutes** short-term exposure
> - **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** = absolute maximum permissible concentration
> - **Normal Set Point (SP)** = target value of a process variable
> - **Process Variable (PV)** = current measured value
> - **Deviation** = $\vert PV - SP \vert$
> - **Allowable Deviation** = $\pm$ instrument error + engineering tolerance (typically **±2% to ±5%** of span)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar Elements of Normal Operations (Structural Breakdown)

Normal Operations in a PSM-managed facility rests on **Five Pillars**. Each pillar is mandated under OSHA PSM and is a direct KTU exam focus area.

### Pillar 1 — Process Safety Information (PSI)
- Accurate, up-to-date written information about:
  - **Chemical hazards** (toxicity, flammability, reactivity, corrosivity)
  - **Process technology** (block flow diagrams, process flow diagrams, P&IDs)
  - **Process equipment** (design specifications, materials of construction, relief system design basis)
- PSI is the **knowledge backbone** for all operating procedures.

### Pillar 2 — Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)
- Written, step-by-step instructions for **each phase** of normal operations.
- Must cover: initial startup, normal startup, normal operations, normal shutdown, emergency shutdown, and startup after emergency.
- Must be **readily accessible** to operators and **updated whenever MOC (Management of Change) occurs**.

### Pillar 3 — Training
- Every operator must be **trained, certified, and re-certified** (typically annually) on:
  - The specific process they operate
  - Operating procedures
  - Safety and health hazards
  - Safe work practices
- Documentation of training is mandatory.

### Pillar 4 — Safe Work Practices & Permits
- **Hot Work Permit** (welding, cutting, grinding in flammable areas)
- **Cold Work Permit**
- **Confined Space Entry Permit**
- **Line Breaking Permit**
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)**
- These are **administrative controls** active during normal operations to manage non-routine tasks.

### Pillar 5 — Communication & Documentation
- **Shift Handover Logs**
- **Daily Operating Logs**
- **Communication of process status** between shifts, supervisors, and contractors
- Documentation forms the **audit trail** for incident investigation if deviations occur.

## 2.2 Safe Operating Limits vs. Operating Limits vs. Design Limits

This is the **most frequently tested hierarchy** in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Tier Limit Hierarchy (outermost to innermost):**
>
> **Design Limits** (Equipment Failure Limits)
> → The mechanical/physical limits beyond which the equipment physically fails or ruptures.
> → Example: Vessel MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) = **10 bar**
>
> **Safe Operating Limits (SOL)**
> → The operational envelope beyond which a process upset can lead to loss of containment or hazard.
> → Set between design limits and operating limits as a **safety margin**.
> → Example: SOL for pressure = **8 bar** (with 20% margin below MAWP)
>
> **Operating Limits**
> → The narrow band of normal, desired operation.
> → Example: Normal operating pressure = **6 ± 0.5 bar**
>
> **Hierarchy:** Operating Limits ⊂ Safe Operating Limits ⊂ Design Limits

## 2.3 Deviation Management During Normal Operations

A **deviation** is any departure from the operating limits but still within SOL. Deviations are the **early warning signals** for impending upsets.

| Deviation Level | Description | Required Action | Time Frame |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Level 1 — Minor** | Within alarm range, auto-corrected by control loop | Operator logs and monitors | Continuous |
| **Level 2 — Moderate** | Triggers process alarm, requires operator intervention | Operator takes corrective action as per SOP | Minutes |
| **Level 3 — Major** | Exceeds SOL, triggers interlock/shutdown logic | Emergency response per SOP | Seconds to minutes |
| **Level 4 — Critical** | Exceeds design limit | Catastrophic — incident investigation mandatory | Immediate |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Math in Process Safety:** While Normal Operations is largely procedural, certain quantitative calculations are central to KTU problem-solving.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Deviation** | $\Delta X = \vert PV - SP \vert$ | Engineering units (°C, bar, m³/h) | Measure of process variability |
| **Relative Deviation** | $\delta = \dfrac{\Delta X}{SP} \times 100\%$ | % | Compare variability across scales |
| **Safety Margin (Pressure)** | $SM_P = \dfrac{MAWP - OP}{MAWP} \times 100\%$ | % | Confirm process is within safe envelope |
| **Safety Margin (Temperature)** | $SM_T = \dfrac{T_{max} - T_{op}}{T_{max}} \times 100\%$ | % | Same for thermal envelope |
| **25% LFL Rule (NFPA)** | $C_{actual} \leq 0.25 \times LFL$ | % vol in air | Confined space entry criterion |
| **Set Point Error (Instrument)** | $\%Error = \dfrac{PV_{true} - PV_{indicated}}{Span} \times 100$ | % | Calibration verification |
| **IDLH Time Limit** | $t_{max} = \dfrac{IDLH}{C_{actual}}$ | hours | Worker exposure estimation |
| **Permit Validity** | $t_{validity} \leq$ Shift end (typically 8 h) | hours | Hot/cold work permit duration |
| **Operator Response Time** | $t_{response} \leq \dfrac{\tau_{process}}{3}$ | seconds | For first-order process with time constant $\tau$ |
| **Documentation Frequency** | Daily logs = **1 per shift** (3 shifts/day) | logs/day | Operating log compliance |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for KTU Answers:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the bare pipe character `|`) when writing absolute value in markdown tables — the pipe breaks table formatting.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Normal Operations management is the **backbone** of:
- **Refineries** (Crude distillation, catalytic cracking units)
- **Petrochemical plants** (Ethylene crackers, polymer plants)
- **Pharmaceutical API manufacturing** (batch reactors with strict temperature control)
- **LNG terminals** (cryogenic normal operations at **−162°C**)
- **Ammonia plants** (high-pressure synthesis at **150–300 bar**)

Without robust Normal Operations management, every other PSM element (Mechanical Integrity, MOC, Incident Investigation) loses its effectiveness because the **baseline of safe operation is undefined**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Safety Margin Calculation (KSEB-Style)

> **Problem:** A reactor has a **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP) = 14 bar**. During normal operations, the controller maintains the operating pressure at **SP = 10.5 bar** with an allowable deviation of **±0.7 bar**. Calculate:
> (a) The maximum operating pressure observed during normal operation.
> (b) The safety margin with respect to MAWP.
> (c) The relative deviation at maximum operating pressure.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given Data (Step 1 — Always extract given data first):**
- MAWP $= 14 \text{ bar}$
- Set Point $SP = 10.5 \text{ bar}$
- Allowable Deviation $\Delta P_{allow} = \pm 0.7 \text{ bar}$

**Step 2 — Part (a): Maximum Operating Pressure**

The maximum operating pressure is the upper bound of the normal operating window:

$$
P_{max,op} = SP + \Delta P_{allow}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
P_{max,op} = 10.5 \text{ bar} + 0.7 \text{ bar} = 11.2 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Part (b): Safety Margin Calculation**

The safety margin is the percentage gap between the maximum operating pressure and the design limit (MAWP):

$$
SM_P = \dfrac{MAWP - P_{max,op}}{MAWP} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
SM_P = \dfrac{14 - 11.2}{14} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SM_P = \dfrac{2.8}{14} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SM_P = 0.20 \times 100\% = \mathbf{20\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Part (c): Relative Deviation at Maximum**

The relative deviation compares the actual deviation to the set point as a percentage:

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{P_{max,op} - SP}{SP} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{11.2 - 10.5}{10.5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{0.7}{10.5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = 6.67\%
$$

### Interpretation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Result Summary:} \\
&P_{max,op} = 11.2 \text{ bar} \\
&SM_P = 20\% \quad \text{(acceptable — typical PSM industry standard is } \geq 10\% \text{)} \\
&\delta_{max} = 6.67\% \quad \text{(within acceptable instrument tolerance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** The 20% safety margin is the **minimum acceptable** under ASME and OSHA PSM guidelines. Anything below 10% would trigger an MOC (Management of Change) review.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — 25% LFL Confined Space Verification

> **Problem:** A maintenance crew must enter a storage tank containing residual benzene vapors. The tank volume is **50 m³**, the LFL of benzene is **1.2% vol in air**, and the measured vapor concentration in the tank is **0.25% vol**. The benzene's **IDLH = 500 ppm** and **TWA = 1 ppm** (OSHA PEL). Determine:
> (a) Whether entry is permitted under the 25% LFL rule.
> (b) The maximum permissible worker exposure duration per shift (using TWA).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Part (a): 25% LFL Test**

Per NFPA and OSHA PSM, entry is permitted only if the actual concentration is **less than or equal to 25% of LFL**:

$$
C_{permissible} = 0.25 \times LFL
$$

Substituting LFL of benzene:

$$
C_{permissible} = 0.25 \times 1.2\% = 0.30\% \text{ vol}
$$

Comparing with the measured concentration:

$$
C_{actual} = 0.25\% \text{ vol} < C_{permissible} = 0.30\% \text{ vol}
$$

Since $C_{actual} < C_{permissible}$, **entry is permitted** subject to all other permit requirements.

**Step 2 — Part (b): Maximum Exposure Duration**

The Time-Weighted Average (TWA) of 1 ppm applies over an 8-hour shift. For a measured concentration equal to the actual (0.25% vol = 2500 ppm), exposure duration is calculated as:

$$
t_{max} = \dfrac{TWA}{C_{actual}} \times 8 \text{ hours}
$$

Substituting:

$$
t_{max} = \dfrac{1 \text{ ppm}}{2500 \text{ ppm}} \times 8 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
t_{max} = \dfrac{8}{2500} \text{ hours}
$$

$$
t_{max} = 0.0032 \text{ hours} = 11.52 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Safety Insight:** Even though the **25% LFL rule** allows entry, the **TWA calculation** shows the worker would exceed OSHA's benzene exposure limit in less than **12 seconds**! Therefore, **entry is denied** and continuous air-supplied respiratory protection (SCBA) is mandatory. The 25% LFL rule alone is **NOT sufficient** for toxic atmospheres.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Shift Handover & Documentation Frequency

> **Problem:** A plant operates 365 days/year with 3 shifts/day. Each shift requires: (i) one daily operating log, (ii) one shift handover note, and (iii) one process parameter checklist (15 parameters × 8 readings/shift). Calculate the annual documentation volume.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Daily Logs**

$$
\text{Logs/day} = 3 \text{ shifts/day} \times 1 \text{ log/shift} = 3 \text{ logs/day}
$$

$$
\text{Annual logs} = 3 \times 365 = \mathbf{1095 \text{ logs/year}}
$$

**Step 2 — Shift Handover Notes**

Same as shifts/year: $\mathbf{1095 \text{ notes/year}}$

**Step 3 — Parameter Readings**

$$
\text{Readings/year} = 15 \text{ parameters} \times 8 \text{ readings/shift} \times 3 \text{ shifts/day} \times 365 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Readings/year} = 15 \times 8 \times 3 \times 365 = \mathbf{131{,}400 \text{ readings/year}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **electronic logbooks and DCS-based Historian systems** (e.g., Honeywell PHD, OSIsoft PI) are mandatory in modern PSM plants. Manual documentation of 131,400 readings/year is impractical and error-prone.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five Pillars of Normal Operations (Hierarchical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Normal Operations Baseline"] --> B["Process Safety Information PSI"]
    A --> C["Standard Operating Procedures SOPs"]
    A --> D["Operator Training and Competency"]
    A --> E["Safe Work Practices and Permits"]
    A --> F["Communication and Documentation"]

    B --> B1["Chemical Hazards"]
    B --> B2["Process Technology"]
    B --> B3["Process Equipment Design"]

    C --> C1["Normal Startup Procedures"]
    C --> C2["Steady State Operating Procedures"]
    C --> C3["Normal Shutdown Procedures"]

    D --> D1["Initial Training"]
    D --> D2["Annual Refresher"]
    D --> D3["Certification Records"]

    E --> E1["Hot Work Permit"]
    E --> E2["Cold Work Permit"]
    E --> E3["Confined Space Permit"]
    E --> E4["Lockout Tagout LOTO"]

    F --> F1["Shift Handover Logs"]
    F --> F2["Daily Operating Logs"]
    F --> F3["Management of Change MOC Records"]

    B1 --> G["Safe Operating Envelope"]
    C2 --> G
    D2 --> G
    E4 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["Sustained Normal Operations"]
```

## 4.2 The Three-Tier Limit Hierarchy (Concentric Safety Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["DESIGN LIMITS - Equipment Failure Threshold - e.g. MAWP 14 bar"]
    L2["SAFE OPERATING LIMITS SOL - Upset Prevention - e.g. 11.2 bar"]
    L3["OPERATING LIMITS - Normal Steady State - e.g. 10.5 plus minus 0.7 bar"]

    L1 --- L2
    L2 --- L3

    L1 --> A1["Beyond this: Mechanical Failure or Rupture"]
    L2 --> A2["Beyond this: Process Upset, Alarm, Interlock Activation"]
    L3 --> A3["Beyond this: Deviation - Operator Action Required"]
```

## 4.3 Process Variable Deviation and Response Protocol (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Set Point SP target value"] --> CTRL["PID Controller"]

    CTRL --> ACT["Final Control Element - Valve or Heater"]

    ACT --> PROC["Process Unit - Reactor or Column"]

    PROC --> SENS["Sensor Transmitter"]

    SENS --> PV["Process Variable PV measured value"]

    PV --> COMP["Comparator - Calculate Deviation"]

    COMP --> DEV["Deviation Delta X equals PV minus SP absolute"]

    DEV --> D1{"Within Operating Limits plus minus 2 percent"}
    DEV --> D2{"Exceeds SOL"}
    DEV --> D3{"Exceeds Design Limit"}

    D1 --> R1["Operator Logs and Monitors - Level 1"]
    D2 --> R2["Process Alarm - Operator Corrective Action - Level 2 or 3"]
    D3 --> R3["Emergency Shutdown - Incident Investigation - Level 4"]

    R1 --> DOC["Daily Operating Log Entry"]
    R2 --> DOC
    R3 --> DOC
    DOC --> AUD["Audit Trail for Compliance Audit"]
```

## 4.4 Permit-to-Work System in Normal Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Maintenance Task Identified During Normal Operations"] --> B{"Type of Work"}

    B -->|"Welding, Grinding, Cutting"| C1["HOT WORK PERMIT"]
    B -->|"Cleaning, Painting, Inspection"| C2["COLD WORK PERMIT"]
    B -->|"Tank/Vessel Entry"| C3["CONFINED SPACE PERMIT"]
    B -->|"Equipment Isolation"| C4["LOCKOUT TAGOUT PERMIT"]

    C1 --> D1["Check Flammable Atmosphere - LFL below 1 percent"]
    C2 --> D2["Check Area Hazards - No Ignition Sources"]
    C3 --> D3["Test Atmosphere - O2 19.5 to 23.5 percent - Toxic below PEL"]
    C4 --> D4["Verify Zero Energy State - Apply Locks and Tags"]

    D1 --> E["Permit Issued - Valid for 1 Shift Max"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F["Work Executed Under Permit"]
    F --> G["Permit Closed - Area Restored - Documentation Updated"]
    G --> H["Daily Operating Log Updated"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]**

**Q1.** Define **Normal Operations** in the context of Process Safety Management. List any **four operating parameters** that must be maintained within Safe Operating Limits.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Normal Operations refer to the steady-state, planned operating conditions of a process plant during which all process variables remain within their established Safe Operating Limits (SOL) and Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are followed.

**Four operating parameters:**
1. **Temperature** — e.g., reactor temperature $T \pm 2°C$
2. **Pressure** — e.g., operating pressure within ±5% of set point
3. **Flow rate** — e.g., feed flow $F \pm 1\%$
4. **Level** — e.g., vessel level maintained between 30%–70%

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1.b]**

**Q2.** What is the **25% LFL rule**? Why is it critical during normal operations and confined space entry?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **25% LFL (Lower Flammable Limit) rule** is a safety standard that mandates any flammable vapor concentration in a confined work area must remain **at or below 25% of its LFL** before hot work or entry is permitted.

**Criticality:**
- Provides a **safety factor of 4** below the flammable threshold, accounting for atmospheric variability.
- Prevents flash-back and explosion during normal maintenance work.
- Per **NFPA 350** and **OSHA 1910.146**, this is a non-negotiable permit precondition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice, Q11]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **three-tier limit hierarchy** (Design Limits, Safe Operating Limits, Operating Limits) in PSM with a suitable diagram. Use a numerical example of a pressure vessel to illustrate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** in sustaining Normal Operations. What are the mandatory components of an SOP as per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q11(a) — 7 Marks:

**Introduction (1 mark):**

The three-tier limit hierarchy is a foundational concept in Process Safety Management that defines the **operating envelope** of any process plant. Each tier represents a progressively narrower band of safe operation.

**Tier 1 — Design Limits (2 marks):**
- These are the **mechanical/physical** limits beyond which equipment fails.
- For a pressure vessel: **MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)** as stamped on the vessel nameplate, calculated per **ASME Section VIII**.
- **Example:** MAWP $= 14 \text{ bar}$

**Tier 2 — Safe Operating Limits (SOL) (2 marks):**
- Set with a **safety margin (typically 10–25%)** below design limits.
- Exceeding SOL triggers alarms and operator intervention.
- **Example:** SOL $= 11.2 \text{ bar}$ (20% margin)

**Tier 3 — Operating Limits (2 marks):**
- The narrowest band — the desired steady-state operation.
- **Example:** Operating pressure $= 10.5 \pm 0.7 \text{ bar}$ (i.e., 9.8 to 11.2 bar)

**Hierarchy Diagram (text representation — for answer sheet):**

```
[ Design Limit: 14 bar ]
        ↓ 20% margin
[ Safe Operating Limit: 11.2 bar ]
        ↓ ±0.7 bar window
[ Operating Limit: 10.5 ± 0.7 bar ]
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q11(b) — 7 Marks:

**Definition and Purpose (2 marks):**
SOPs are written, step-by-step instructions that define how to operate a process safely during normal operations. They serve as the **bridge between Process Safety Information and the operator's hands**.

**Mandatory Components as per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(f) (5 marks):**

1. **Initial Startup** — steps for first-time commissioning
2. **Normal Startup** — routine startup after maintenance/turnaround
3. **Normal Operations** — steady-state operating instructions
4. **Normal Shutdown** — orderly shutdown procedure
5. **Emergency Shutdown** — and conditions triggering it
6. **Emergency Operations** — response to deviations
7. **Startup after Emergency Shutdown** — recovery procedure

**Additional Required Elements:**
- Operating limits and consequences of deviation
- Safety and health considerations
- Safe work practices (permits, LOTO)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "steps to operate" without mentioning **operating limits, consequences of deviation, and emergency operations**. These three are mandatory per OSHA and carry **3 marks** alone. Missing them = guaranteed 50% marks lost on this question.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the **five pillars of Normal Operations** in PSM. How does each pillar contribute to sustaining normal operations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distillation column operates at a normal pressure of **2.5 bar (gauge)** with an allowable deviation of **±0.2 bar**. The design pressure (MAWP) is **5 bar (gauge)**.
- (i) Calculate the **maximum operating pressure**.
- (ii) Calculate the **safety margin** with respect to MAWP.
- (iii) If a deviation of **+0.3 bar** is observed, classify the deviation level and state the required operator action as per PSM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q11(a) Alternative — 7 Marks:

**The Five Pillars (1 mark each, total 5 marks):**

| # | Pillar | Contribution to Normal Operations |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Process Safety Information (PSI)** | Provides the knowledge base — chemical hazards, equipment specs, P&IDs |
| 2 | **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** | Defines exact steps for safe operation |
| 3 | **Operator Training** | Ensures competency to execute SOPs and respond to deviations |
| 4 | **Safe Work Practices & Permits** | Manages non-routine tasks (hot work, confined space) safely |
| 5 | **Communication & Documentation** | Maintains shift continuity and audit trail |

**Synthesis (2 marks):**
The pillars are **interdependent**. Without PSI, SOPs cannot be written. Without SOPs, training has no content. Without training, permits are issued to incompetent personnel. Without documentation, incidents cannot be investigated. Together they form a **closed-loop safety system** that sustains normal operations.

---

#### Model Solution for Q11(b) Alternative — 7 Marks:

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $P_{op} = 2.5 \text{ bar (g)}$
- $\Delta P_{allow} = \pm 0.2 \text{ bar}$
- $MAWP = 5 \text{ bar (g)}$

**(i) Maximum Operating Pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{max,op} = P_{op} + \Delta P_{allow} = 2.5 + 0.2 = \mathbf{2.7 \text{ bar (g)}}
$$

**(ii) Safety Margin (2 marks):**

$$
SM_P = \dfrac{MAWP - P_{max,op}}{MAWP} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5 - 2.7}{5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SM_P = \dfrac{2.3}{5} \times 100\% = \mathbf{46\%}
$$

**(iii) Deviation Classification (2 marks):**

Observed deviation $= +0.3 \text{ bar}$. Since $+0.3 \text{ bar} > \Delta P_{allow} = \pm 0.2 \text{ bar}$, this is a **Level 2 — Moderate Deviation**.

$$
P_{actual} = 2.5 + 0.3 = 2.8 \text{ bar (g)}
$$

Check against SOL ($= 11.2$ equivalent = assumed SOL at 80% of MAWP $= 4.0$ bar):

Since $2.8 \text{ bar} < 4.0 \text{ bar (SOL)}$, the deviation is **within SOL but exceeds operating limits**.

**Required Action:**
- Process alarm is triggered.
- Operator must take **immediate corrective action** per SOP (e.g., reduce heat input, increase reflux, vent if necessary).
- Log the deviation in the daily operating log.
- If uncorrected within 5 minutes, escalate to supervisor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall (Numerical Part):** Students often write "the plant is unsafe" just because deviation exceeds operating limits. The correct PSM answer requires checking **three things in order**: (1) Operating limit exceeded? → (2) SOL exceeded? → (3) Design limit exceeded? Only the **deepest exceeded tier** determines the response. Always classify into **Level 1 / 2 / 3 / 4**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Normal Operations (Module 3)**
>
> - **Definition:** Steady-state planned operation within Safe Operating Limits following SOPs.
> - **OSHA Reference:** 29 CFR 1910.119 — Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals.
> - **Five Pillars:** PSI, SOPs, Training, Permits, Communication & Documentation.
> - **Three-Tier Limit Hierarchy:** Operating Limits ⊂ Safe Operating Limits ⊂ Design Limits.
> - **Set Point (SP)** vs **Process Variable (PV)** — Deviation = $\vert PV - SP \vert$.
> - **25% LFL Rule:** Confined space / hot work only if actual concentration ≤ 25% of LFL.
> - **TWA = 8 hours; STEL = 15 minutes; IDLH = absolute danger threshold.**
> - **Safety Margin Formula:** $SM = \dfrac{Limit_{design} - Operating_{max}}{Limit_{design}} \times 100\%$ (must be ≥ 10%).
> - **Hot Work Permit Validity:** Maximum 1 shift (8 hours); must be reissued each shift.
> - **Confined Space:** O₂ between **19.5% and 23.5%** mandatory.
> - **Operator Response Time:** Must be ≤ $\dfrac{\tau_{process}}{3}$ for first-order dynamics.
> - **Documentation:** Daily logs + shift handover + parameter readings; modern plants use DCS Historians.
> - **MOC Trigger:** Any change to process, equipment, or procedures requires Management of Change review.
> - **Audit Frequency:** PSM Compliance Audit at least **every 3 years** (OSHA mandate).
> - **Most-tested KTU topics:** Three-tier hierarchy, SOP components, 25% LFL rule, safety margin calculation, deviation classification.
> - **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing "Normal Operations" with "all non-emergency states." Normal Operations is **specific** — it excludes startups, shutdowns, and emergencies.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporary Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Temporary Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Temporary Operations** are defined as any non-routine, short-duration, or transient activities within a process plant that deviate from the original design intent, approved operating procedures, or permanently installed equipment configuration. These operations are typically required to facilitate maintenance, repair, commissioning, modification, or emergency response activities while the plant (or a portion of it) remains operational. According to **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals)** and **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines, temporary operations include but are not limited to: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Temporary Operations Encompass:** > - Bypassing or overriding safety instrumented systems (SIS), interlocks, and alarms > - Use of **jumper hoses, temporary piping, and portable equipment** > - **Hot tapping** on live process lines > - Open venting, draining, or sampling of hazardous materials > - Operating equipment beyond its design limits > - **Start-up, shut-down, and emergency operations** > - Maintenance overrides and line-breaking activities > - Use of **temporary electrical connections** in hazardous areas **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** > "Temporary Operations refer to all activities conducted outside the normal operating envelope of a process facility, which require explicit authorization, hazard analysis, time-limited approval, controlled execution, and formal restoration procedures to ensure that the integrity of the original process safety management system is not compromised." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine driving a car on a highway at 100 km/h. Now imagine you suddenly need to **remove the steering wheel** and **disable the brakes** because you're performing emergency repairs while the car is still moving on a public road. This is essentially what a **temporary operation** is in a process plant. **Real-World Analogy — The "Open Heart Surgery While the Patient Runs a Marathon" Metaphor:** | Normal Operation | Temporary Operation | |---|---| | Patient lying on operating table | Patient running while surgeon operates | | All safety systems intact | Some safety systems bypassed | | Stable heart rate | Variable heart rate with stress | | Doctors work at leisure | Doctors must work fast | | Recovery is straightforward | Recovery is critical | In a chemical plant, the "patient" is the process (containing flammable, toxic, or reactive chemicals at high temperature/pressure), and the "surgeon" is the maintenance team or operator. Just as **open-heart surgery on a running marathoner** is extremely dangerous, performing maintenance while the plant operates requires extraordinary precautions. **Another Analogy — The Dam with a Removed Spillway Gate:** Think of a hydroelectric dam with carefully designed spillway gates that prevent overflow. If you remove one spillway gate temporarily for repair, the dam's flood-control capacity is reduced. Without compensating measures, a sudden flood could breach the dam. Similarly, **temporarily bypassing a pressure relief valve** removes a critical layer of protection — the process must be operated at lower pressure, monitored more carefully, and the protection must be restored as soon as possible. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight for Students:** > Temporary operations are the **"Achilles' Heel"** of process safety. History shows that catastrophic incidents like **Flixborough (1974, 28 fatalities)** and **BP Texas City Refinery (2005, 15 fatalities)** were directly linked to inadequately managed temporary operations. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Maximum Allowable Temporary Operation Duration:** Typically **30 to 90 days** (varies by company policy; OSHA PSM does not specify but industry best practice suggests ≤30 days for high-hazard situations) - **Risk Reduction Target:** Temporary operations should ideally achieve a **risk reduction factor ≥ 10** compared to unprotected baseline (per LOPA methodology) - **Approval Authority Hierarchy Levels:** **3 to 5 levels** depending on risk severity (Operator → Supervisor → Plant Manager → HSE Director → Senior Management) - **Hazard Identification Method Coverage:** Must cover **all process equipment in the affected area** — typically ≥ 95% of nodes per HAZOP study # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Temporary Operations Management Temporary operations management rests on **five foundational pillars** that align with KTU 2024 scheme Course Outcomes: ### Pillar 1: Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA) Before any temporary operation begins, the team must identify **all new hazards** introduced by the deviation from normal operation. This includes: - **Mechanical hazards:** Pressure surges, thermal stress, vibration - **Chemical hazards:** Exposure to toxic/corrosive substances - **Process hazards:** Loss of containment, runaway reactions - **Human factors:** Operator fatigue, confusion, training gaps - **Environmental hazards:** Atmospheric release, soil contamination ### Pillar 2: Authorization Hierarchy A **multi-tier approval system** ensures that the higher the risk, the more senior the approver. Common structure: | Risk Level | Approval Authority | Documentation | |---|---|---| | **Low Risk** (Slight/Low) | Area Supervisor | Field Permit | | **Medium Risk** (Moderate) | Plant Manager + HSE | Permit-to-Work (PTW) | | **High Risk** (High) | HSE Director + Operations Head | Hot Work Permit + Isolation Certificate | | **Very High Risk** (Extreme) | Site Director + Corporate HSE | MOC (Management of Change) Documentation | ### Pillar 3: Time Limitation & Expiry Control Every temporary operation must have a **defined expiry date**. Common practice: - Maximum duration: **72 hours** for critical safety system bypasses - Maximum duration: **30 days** for non-critical modifications - Renewable only after re-assessment ### Pillar 4: Continuous Monitoring & Communication - Posting of **temporary operating instructions** at the control panel - **Communication log** with shift handover - **Real-time monitoring** of affected parameters (pressure, temperature, flow) - **Standby rescue/emergency response** teams for high-risk activities ### Pillar 5: Formal Restoration & Verification Upon completion of the temporary operation: - **Removal of bypasses** in reverse order of installation - **Pre-startup safety review (PSSR)** to verify integrity - **Functional testing** of restored safety systems - **Closure documentation** and lessons-learned review ## 2.2 Categories of Temporary Operations (Detailed Classification) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification — Memorize This:** ### Category A: Equipment-Related Temporary Operations 1. **Bypass of Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs)** — extremely high risk; PRV is the last line of defense against overpressure 2. **Jumper hoses** around blocked equipment or instrumentation 3. **Temporary pumps, compressors, or heat exchangers** 4. **Portable/mobile equipment** (vacuum trucks, generators) 5. **Blind insertion/removal** during line breaking ### Category B: Procedural Temporary Operations 1. **Manual override** of automatic control loops 2. **Bypass of safety instrumented functions (SIF)** and interlocks 3. **Operation outside design pressure/temperature limits** 4. **Mixed-phase operations** (e.g., liquid in vapor service line) 5. **Depressurizing / venting for maintenance** ### Category C: Site-Based Temporary Operations 1. **Hot work** (welding, cutting, grinding) in operating areas 2. **Confined space entry** during live operation 3. **Excavation** near buried process lines 4. **Scaffolding erection** in hazardous zones 5. **Electrical isolation/live work** ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > For temporary operations, the mathematical framework primarily revolves around **risk quantification**. The following table summarizes all essential formulas: | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Application** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | **Risk (R)** | $R = P \times C$ | Base risk calculation | dimensionless / hr | | **Residual Risk (R_res)** | $R_{res} = P \times C \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - PFD_i)$ | Risk after protection layers | dimensionless / hr | | **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** | $PFD = \lambda \cdot T/2$ (for proof-tested single channel) | Reliability of protection layer | dimensionless | | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = 1 / PFD$ | Effectiveness of safeguard | dimensionless | | **SIL Availability Equation** | $Availability = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System uptime | fraction | | **Frequency of Event (F)** | $F = f_{init} \times \prod_{i} PFD_i$ | LOPA frequency calculation | events/year | | **Hazard Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = 1/MTBF$ | Equipment failure rate | failures/hr | | **Severity Classification** | $S = f(C, P, T, E)$ | Where C=Consequence, P=Probability, T=Time, E=Exposure | categorical | | **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** | $Mitigated\ Risk = Initiating\ Event \times \prod PFD_i$ | Independent protection layers | events/year | | **Time-Limited Risk Acceptance** | $R_{acceptable} = \frac{R_{target} \times T_{permanent}}{T_{temporary}}$ | Proportionate risk reduction | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Formula for KTU Exam — The LOPA Equation:** > > $$Mitigated\ Frequency = f_{initiating} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$ > > **Where:** > - $f_{initiating}$ = frequency of the initiating event (events/year) > - $PFD_i$ = probability of failure on demand for each independent protection layer (IPL) > - $n$ = total number of active IPLs during temporary operation > > For a temporary operation with **fewer IPLs** (since one or more are bypassed), the mitigated frequency **increases** — this is why temporary operations carry inherently higher risk. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice In real chemical, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical plants: 1. **Refineries** may need to bypass a furnace tube temperature interlock to clean a fouled catalyst bed — this requires depressurizing, cooling, and inerting the furnace while adjacent units operate. 2. **Petrochemical plants** may insert a temporary heat exchanger to maintain production while cleaning the primary exchanger — risk of leak increases 10× to 100×. 3. **Pharmaceutical plants** may use temporary pumps for solvent transfer during CIP (Clean-In-Place) validation — risk of cross-contamination requires extensive procedural controls. **Real Industrial Significance:** > A study by the **UK Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** indicated that approximately **30% of major process safety incidents** involved some form of temporary operation that was inadequately managed. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Risk Assessment Matrix ## 3.1 Comprehensive Risk Assessment Matrix for Temporary Operations Since temporary operations involve **procedural and management-driven analysis** rather than pure mathematical derivation, this section provides an exhaustive **analytical framework** for risk evaluation, including detailed step-by-step logic with quantitative equations. ### Step 1: Identify the Deviation from Normal Operation **Logic Flow:** 1. Document the **baseline** process state (P, T, flow, composition) 2. Identify the **proposed temporary change** (bypass, override, new equipment) 3. List all **safety functions affected** 4. Quantify the **deviation magnitude** using the equation: $$Deviation\ Magnitude (\Delta) = \frac{\vert Operating\ Parameter_{temporary} - Operating\ Parameter_{design} \vert}{Operating\ Parameter_{design}} \times 100\%$$ **Worked Example:** Consider a reactor with design operating pressure **P_design = 10 bar**. During maintenance, the operator proposes to operate at **P_temp = 8 bar** because the primary pressure controller is being calibrated. **Calculation:** $$\Delta P = \frac{\vert 8 - 10 \vert}{10} \times 100\% = 20\%$$ **Interpretation:** - A 20% deviation falls into the **"Significant"** category (>15%) per typical industry classification - This requires **formal MOC procedure** and possibly HAZOP revalidation ### Step 2: Identify New Hazards Introduced **Logic Flow:** 1. Apply **What-If Analysis** for each deviation 2. Use **HAZOP keywords** (NO/NONE, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) 3. Cross-reference with **process hazard analysis (PHA)** database 4. Identify **previously unrecognized** failure modes **HAZOP Application Example:** | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Risk | |---|---|---|---|---| | NO flow in cooling water | Pump failure + bypassed low-flow trip | Reactor overheat → runaway reaction | Manual monitoring + backup pump | HIGH | | MORE pressure | Blocked outlet + bypassed PRV | Vessel rupture → BLEVE/explosion | Reduced throughput + operator presence | EXTREME | | AS WELL AS toxic gas | Temporary hose leak | Worker exposure → fatality | SCBA + gas detection | HIGH | ### Step 3: Quantify Risk Using LOPA Methodology **The LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) Equation:** $$Mitigated\ Event\ Frequency = f_{initiating\ event} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$ **Step-by-Step Worked Example:** **Scenario:** A reactor normally has 3 IPLs (Independent Protection Layers): 1. Basic Process Control System (BPCS) — PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$ 2. Safety Instrumented Function (SIF/SIL-2) — PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$ 3. Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) — PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$ **Initiating Event:** Loss of cooling water (frequency = $1 \times 10^{-1}$ events/year) **Normal Mitigated Frequency:** $$F_{normal} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1})$$ $$F_{normal} = 1 \times 10^{-5}\ events/year$$ **Temporary Operation Scenario:** PRV is bypassed for maintenance. **Temporary Mitigated Frequency:** $$F_{temporary} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1)$$ $$F_{temporary} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ events/year$$ **Risk Comparison:** $$\frac{F_{temporary}}{F_{normal}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 10\times\ increase\ in\ risk$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This 10× risk increase is a KTU Exam Favorite Question** — be prepared to calculate and interpret this. ### Step 4: Determine Required Compensatory Measures To bring the risk back to acceptable levels, **additional temporary IPLs** must be added. Using the risk target: $$f_{target} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ events/year\ (typical\ corporate\ target)$$ **Required additional PFD:** $$PFD_{additional} = \frac{f_{target}}{f_{temporary} \times PFD_{existing}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 \times 10^{-1} \times 1 \times 10^{-2})}$$ $$PFD_{additional} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-7}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$$ This means an additional safeguard with **PFD ≤ 0.1** (10% reliability) is required — achievable through measures like: - Continuous operator monitoring - Portable gas detectors with audible alarms - Frequent (every 2 hours) manual inspections ### Step 5: Define Time Limits The maximum permissible duration of a temporary operation is calculated using the principle of **cumulative acceptable risk**: $$T_{max} = \frac{R_{lifetime\ acceptable}}{R_{temporary}}$$ **Example Calculation:** $$T_{max} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-5}\ events/year}{1 \times 10^{-4}\ events/year} = 0.1\ year = 36.5\ days$$ **Interpretation:** The temporary operation can be maintained for a maximum of **36.5 days** before the cumulative risk exceeds lifetime acceptable levels. ### Step 6: Approve, Execute, Monitor, and Restore This is the **operational phase** where: 1. All approvals are documented 2. Field execution follows approved procedures 3. Monitoring logs are maintained 4. Emergency response is staged 5. Restoration checklist is executed (removing bypasses, testing safety systems) ## 3.2 The Authorization Matrix — Detailed Decision Logic | **Step** | **Action** | **Responsible Party** | **Documentation** | **Validation** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Request raised | Operations/Maintenance | Temporary Operation Request Form | Initial screening | | 2 | Risk classification | HSE Engineer | Risk Assessment Record | Hazard analysis complete | | 3 | LOPA calculation | Process Safety Engineer | LOPA Worksheet | Quantitative analysis | | 4 | Compensatory measures | Operations + HSE | Safeguard Verification List | Each measure tested | | 5 | Approval — Low Risk | Area Supervisor | Permit-to-Work | Risk = LOW | | 6 | Approval — Medium Risk | Plant Manager | Permit + MOC Form | Risk = MEDIUM | | 7 | Approval — High Risk | HSE Director | Full MOC + Corporate Notification | Risk = HIGH | | 8 | Approval — Extreme Risk | Site Director + Corporate | Emergency MOC + Board Notification | Risk = EXTREME | | 9 | Execution | Field Personnel + Supervisor | Daily Permit Log | Continuous monitoring | | 10 | Renewal/Reassessment | Same as step 2-7 | Updated Risk Assessment | Every 7 days max | | 11 | Restoration | Maintenance + Operations | Removal Checklist | Functional testing | | 12 | PSSR | Multi-disciplinary Team | Pre-Startup Safety Review | All systems verified | | 13 | Closure | HSE | Lessons Learned Report | Knowledge capture | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q: Define temporary operations in process safety management. List any four examples of temporary operations commonly encountered in chemical process plants.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Temporary operations are non-routine, short-duration activities that deviate from the original design intent, approved operating procedures, or permanently installed equipment configuration in a process plant. They are typically required to facilitate maintenance, repair, commissioning, modification, or emergency response activities while the plant (or portion of it) remains operational. **Examples (Any 4 × 0.5 Mark Each = 2 Marks):** 1. **Bypass of pressure relief valves** (PRVs) for maintenance 2. Use of **jumper hoses** around blocked equipment or instrumentation 3. **Manual override** of automatic control loops or safety instrumented functions (SIFs) 4. **Hot work** (welding, cutting, grinding) in operating hazardous areas 5. **Open venting or draining** of process fluids for line breaking 6. **Confined space entry** during live plant operations 7. **Temporary pumps, compressors, or heat exchangers** 8. **Hot tapping** on live process lines --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q: What is the role of Management of Change (MOC) procedure in temporary operations? State any two hazards associated with bypassing a pressure relief valve.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Role of MOC (1.5 Marks):** The Management of Change (MOC) procedure ensures that all temporary operations involving modifications to equipment, procedures, or safety systems are: - Properly reviewed for safety implications - Authorized at appropriate management levels based on risk - Documented with a clear scope, duration, and responsible parties - Communicated to all affected personnel through training and posting - Subject to a pre-startup safety review (PSSR) upon completion The MOC prevents uncontrolled changes that could introduce unrecognized hazards, ensuring **risk is not increased beyond acceptable levels** during the temporary period. **Hazards of PRV Bypass (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Loss of Last Line of Defense Against Overpressure:** Without the PRV functioning, if pressure exceeds design limits (due to blocked outlet, thermal expansion, or runaway reaction), the vessel may rupture catastrophically, causing BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) or unconfined vapor cloud explosion. 2. **Increased Cumulative Risk Exposure:** The longer the PRV is bypassed, the higher the cumulative probability of an overpressure event occurring. Industry data suggests that even a 24-hour bypass can increase risk by a factor of **10 to 100** depending on the process. 3. (Optional) **No Automatic Protection:** Operator intervention is required; if the operator is absent or distracted, no automated protection exists. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions feature an internal choice. Both Question A and Question B below are complete 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students answer either Question A OR Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Question] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain in detail the procedural framework for management of temporary operations in a process plant. Discuss the roles and responsibilities of key personnel involved.** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Temporary operations, by their nature, deviate from normal operating conditions and introduce additional risks to the process, personnel, and environment. A robust procedural framework is essential to ensure these operations are conducted safely and efficiently. The framework typically follows the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle aligned with international standards like OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, CCPS Guidelines, and ISO 31000. **Procedural Framework (4 Marks):** **Stage 1 — Initiation and Planning:** - Identification of the need for temporary operation (maintenance, modification, emergency repair) - Submission of a **Temporary Operation Request Form** with proposed scope, duration, and justification - Preliminary hazard identification using checklists and What-If analysis **Stage 2 — Risk Assessment and Classification:** - Detailed risk assessment using HAZOP, LOPA, or FMEA methods - Classification into **Low, Medium, High, or Extreme** risk categories - Identification of additional safeguards required to mitigate risk **Stage 3 — Approval and Authorization:** - **Low Risk:** Approval by Area Supervisor only - **Medium Risk:** Approval by Plant Manager + HSE Engineer with documented MOC - **High Risk:** Approval by HSE Director + Operations Head with full MOC documentation - **Extreme Risk:** Approval by Site Director + Corporate HSE with board notification **Stage 4 — Documentation and Communication:** - Issuance of **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** clearly displayed at work location - Posting of **temporary operating instructions** at control panel - **Toolbox talk** and **shift handover briefings** for all affected personnel - Update of P&IDs (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams) with temporary changes highlighted **Stage 5 — Execution and Monitoring:** - Continuous monitoring by field personnel with **defined check intervals** (typically 1-2 hours) - Daily reporting to shift supervisor - Emergency response resources on standby - Strict adherence to permit conditions **Stage 6 — Periodic Review and Renewal:** - Re-assessment every **7 days** (or as defined by company policy) - Re-approval required for any extension - Verification that compensatory measures remain effective **Stage 7 — Restoration and Closure:** - Removal of temporary equipment and bypasses in reverse order - **Functional testing** of restored safety systems (PRVs, interlocks, alarms) - **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** with multi-disciplinary team - Lessons-learned documentation and update of PHA database **Roles and Responsibilities (2 Marks):** | Personnel | Key Responsibilities | |---|---| | **Plant Manager** | Final approval for high-risk temporary operations; ensures resources are available | | **HSE Engineer** | Conducts risk assessment, ensures compliance, validates safeguards | | **Area Supervisor** | Day-to-day oversight; verifies permit conditions; reports deviations | | **Operations Crew** | Executes work as per permit; reports anomalies; follows procedures | | **Maintenance Team** | Implements temporary modifications; ensures equipment integrity | | **Process Safety Engineer** | Updates PHA documents; conducts LOPA; verifies MOC completeness | **Conclusion (Hidden in good answers):** A well-defined procedural framework ensures that the **inherent increase in risk** during temporary operations is **compensated by enhanced procedural controls**, **additional safeguards**, and **continuous management oversight**. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Discuss the major categories of temporary operations with suitable examples. Explain the hazards and control measures associated with each category.** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (0.5 Mark):** Temporary operations in process plants can be broadly classified into four major categories based on the nature of the deviation from normal operation. Each category has distinct hazards requiring specific control measures. **Category 1 — Equipment-Related Temporary Operations (2 Marks):** This category involves **physical modifications or additions** to the process equipment. **Examples:** 1. **Bypass of Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs):** Required when PRV is being tested, repaired, or replaced. 2. **Jumper Hoses:** Used to maintain flow when a permanent line is blocked for maintenance. 3. **Temporary Pumps and Heat Exchangers:** Installed to maintain production during primary equipment maintenance. **Hazards:** - Loss of overpressure protection (catastrophic vessel rupture) - Leakage from temporary connections (fire/explosion/toxic exposure) - Thermal/mechanical stress on temporary equipment **Control Measures:** - Reduction of operating pressure to **70%** of design pressure during PRV bypass - Use of **rated flexible hoses** with documented pressure test certificates - Continuous monitoring with portable gas detectors and alarms - Time-limited approval (typically **72 hours** for critical safety bypasses) **Category 2 — Procedural Temporary Operations (1.5 Marks):** This category involves **deviations from approved operating procedures** without physical equipment changes. **Examples:** 1. **Manual Override of Control Loops:** To perform manual control during instrument calibration. 2. **Bypass of Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs):** During fault diagnosis or repair. 3. **Operating Beyond Design Limits:** Required during process upsets or unusual production needs. **Hazards:** - Process upsets leading to off-specification product or runaway reactions - Loss of automatic protection requiring operator intervention - Confusion during shift changes regarding current operating mode **Control Measures:** - Posting of temporary operating instructions at control panel - Enhanced operator presence and supervision - Continuous logging of operating parameters - **Independent verification** by second operator before each action **Category 3 — Maintenance Activities During Operation (1.5 Marks):** This category involves **physical interventions** while the surrounding process remains operational. **Examples:** 1. **Hot Work (Welding/Cutting/Grinding):** Required for emergency repairs in operating areas. 2. **Confined Space Entry:** Maintenance inside vessels/tanks connected to operating units. 3. **Line Breaking:** Opening flanges/connections to insert blinds or replace gaskets. **Hazards:** - Ignition of flammable vapors causing fire/explosion - Toxic gas exposure in confined spaces - Sudden release of trapped pressure during line breaking **Control Measures:** - **Hot Work Permit** with gas testing every 4 hours - **Confined Space Entry Permit** with continuous atmospheric monitoring - **Line Break Permit** with documented isolation, depressurizing, and draining procedures - **Standby rescue team** with SCBA and stretcher for confined space work **Category 4 — Emergency and Start-up/Shut-down Operations (1.5 Marks):** This category includes operations performed **under abnormal or non-steady-state conditions**. **Examples:** 1. **Emergency Depressurization:** To prevent vessel failure during fire or runaway reaction. 2. **Start-up Operations:** Bringing a unit online after maintenance shutdown. 3. **Shut-down Operations:** Bringing a unit offline in an orderly manner for maintenance. **Hazards:** - Rapid pressure/temperature changes causing thermal stress - Incomplete isolation leading to uncontrolled releases - Operator unfamiliarity with abnormal procedures **Control Measures:** - Detailed **emergency response procedures** with clear decision criteria - **Step-by-step startup/shutdown checklists** verified by second operator - Continuous communication between control room and field - **Tabletop drills** conducted quarterly for emergency scenarios **Conclusion (Hidden in good answers):** Effective management of temporary operations requires a **risk-based, category-specific approach** with tailored control measures. The fundamental principle is that **the higher the deviation from normal operation, the more stringent the procedural and engineering controls must be**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2025 — Model Question] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With the help of a suitable case study, explain how inadequate management of temporary operations can lead to a major process safety incident. Discuss the key lessons learned.** **Model Answer:** **Case Study: The Flixborough Disaster, UK (1974) (5 Marks):** **Background:** The Nypro chemical plant at Flixborough, UK, produced **caprolactam** (a precursor to nylon-6) using a series of six cyclohexane oxidation reactors in cascade. On **1st June 1974**, a massive vapor cloud explosion occurred, killing **28 workers**, injuring **36 others**, and destroying the entire plant. **Sequence of Events:** 1. A **cyclohexane oxidation reactor (Reactor No. 5)** developed a leak and was removed for repair. 2. To maintain production, a **20-inch diameter temporary bypass pipe** was installed to connect Reactor No. 4 directly to Reactor No. 6. 3. The temporary pipe was supported only by **wooden scaffolding** and **hose clamps** — a clear engineering failure. 4. The temporary pipe was **not designed for the operating pressure** and **not stress-analyzed** for thermal expansion. 5. On 1st June 1974, the temporary pipe **ruptured catastrophically**, releasing approximately **50 tonnes of hot cyclohexane** at 150°C and 8.5 bar. 6. The vapor cloud ignited, causing a massive explosion equivalent to **15-45 tonnes of TNT**. 7. All 28 fatalities occurred in and around the control room. **Root Causes Related to Temporary Operations:** 1. **No Management of Change (MOC) Procedure:** The temporary pipe installation was not subjected to formal safety review. 2. **No Hazard Analysis:** The bypass arrangement was not subjected to HAZOP or stress analysis. 3. **Inadequate Design:** The temporary pipe was not designed for the process conditions — the only mechanical support was scaffolding. 4. **No Engineering Standards:** Temporary modifications were treated as "expedient" solutions without engineering rigor. 5. **Lack of Authority:** There was no requirement for senior management approval of temporary modifications. 6. **Production Over Safety:** Management prioritized continued production over safety concerns. **Key Lessons Learned (2 Marks):** | Lesson | Application in Modern PSM | |---|---| | **1. Temporary modifications must follow same design standards as permanent installations.** | All temporary equipment must be **engineered, stress-analyzed, and pressure-tested** before commissioning. | | **2. MOC procedures are non-negotiable.** | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 explicitly requires MOC for all changes except "replacement-in-kind." | | **3. Production pressures cannot override safety requirements.** | Senior management commitment to safety is essential; production KPIs must include safety metrics. | | **4. Mechanical integrity of temporary equipment must be assured.** | Pressure testing, material certification, and inspection protocols apply equally to temporary installations. | | **5. Multi-disciplinary review of changes.** | HAZOP, LOPA, and PSSR should be mandatory for all temporary modifications. | **Impact on Industry:** The Flixborough disaster led to: - Establishment of the **UK Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** in its modern form - Development of the **COMAH Regulations** (Control of Major Accident Hazards) - Inclusion of MOC as a core element of OSHA PSM (1992) - Foundation of the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** in 1985 **Conclusion (Hidden in good answers):** The Flixborough disaster remains the **seminal case study** for temporary operations management. It demonstrates that **every deviation from approved design must be subjected to the same level of engineering rigor** as the original design, and that **production convenience must never compromise process safety integrity**. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Describe the key elements that should be included in a Permit-to-Work (PTW) system for temporary operations. Discuss how PTW integrates with Management of Change (MOC) procedure.** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** A Permit-to-Work (PTW) system is a **formal written document** that authorizes specific work activities in areas where normal operating procedures alone are insufficient to control the associated risks. It is a critical component of temporary operations management, ensuring that all hazards have been identified, controls have been implemented, and the work is executed under clearly defined conditions. **Key Elements of a PTW System for Temporary Operations (4 Marks):** **Element 1 — Permit Identification and Validity Period:** - Unique permit number for traceability - Defined start time and expiry time (typically **8-12 hours** for hot work, **24 hours** for general work) - Explicit statement of the **scope of work** and **boundaries** - Name of the **issuing authority** and **work supervisor** **Element 2 — Description of Work:** - Clear, specific description of the temporary operation - Reference to approved procedures and drawings - List of equipment involved (tag numbers, locations) - Identification of any **crossovers** with other simultaneous operations **Element 3 — Hazard Identification and Control Measures:** - Comprehensive list of **all identified hazards** (chemical, mechanical, electrical, thermal) - Specific control measures for each hazard: - Isolation points (valves, blinds, locks) - Atmospheric testing requirements (LEL, O₂, H₂S, CO) - Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) specifications - Communication protocols (radio channels, signals) **Element 4 — Authorization Signatures:** - **Issuer signature** (typically Area Supervisor or Permit Controller) - **Receiver signature** (the person executing the work) - **Approver signature** (HSE Engineer or Manager for high-risk work) - Each signature indicates **acceptance of responsibility** **Element 5 — Pre-Work Verification Checklist:** - Isolation verified (valves locked, blinds inserted, breakers opened) - Equipment depressurized and drained - Atmosphere tested and found safe - Emergency response equipment in place - Communication established between all parties - All personnel briefed and aware of hazards **Element 6 — Suspension and Re-issue Provisions:** - Procedures for **suspending** the permit (e.g., for shift change, weather, unsafe condition) - Requirements for **re-issuance** (re-attestation of conditions) - **Cancellation** criteria and procedures **Element 7 — Hand-back and Closure:** - Confirmation that all temporary modifications have been **removed** - Verification that equipment is **restored to normal configuration** - **Functional testing** of affected safety systems - **Sign-off** by issuing authority and receiver **Integration with Management of Change (MOC) (2 Marks):** The PTW and MOC systems are **complementary but distinct** processes that work together to manage temporary operations: | Aspect | PTW | MOC | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Authorizes specific work activity | Reviews and approves the proposed change itself | | **Duration** | Short-term (hours to days) | Covers the entire change lifecycle | | **Focus** | Safe execution of work | Technical and safety implications of the change | | **Trigger** | Before work begins | Before the change is designed/implemented | **Integration Workflow:** 1. **Step 1:** A change requiring temporary operation is identified → **MOC initiated** 2. **Step 2:** MOC team reviews technical basis, performs risk assessment, approves the change with conditions 3. **Step 3:** Once MOC is approved, **PTW is issued** for the actual work execution 4. **Step 4:** Work proceeds under PTW conditions 5. **Step 5:** Upon completion, PTW is closed and **MOC is closed** only after PSSR verifies successful restoration **Key Synergies:** - PTW provides the **operational control layer** for executing an MOC-approved change - MOC provides the **engineering and management control layer** that ensures the change is sound before work begins - Neither system alone is sufficient for high-risk temporary operations **Conclusion (Hidden in good answers):** A robust PTW system integrated with MOC procedures forms the **operational backbone** of temporary operations management. The combination ensures that every temporary activity is **technically justified, risk-assessed, properly controlled during execution, and fully restored upon completion**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > **1. Writing Generic Answers Without Process Examples (Loss: 2-3 Marks):** > Examiners expect students to cite **specific industry examples** (PRV bypass, hot work, line breaking, etc.). Generic answers like "operations should be safe" receive zero marks for application. > > **2. Confusing PTW with MOC (Loss: 2 Marks):** > Many students treat PTW and MOC as synonyms. They are distinct — **MOC approves the change; PTW authorizes the work**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > > **3. Skipping Numerical Examples in Risk Assessment (Loss: 1-2 Marks):** > Risk calculation questions require **numerical demonstration**. Writing "risk increases by 10 times" without showing the calculation loses marks. > > **4. Not Mentioning the Five Pillars or Specific Standards (Loss: 1 Mark):** > References to **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, CCPS Guidelines, and ISO 31000** demonstrate breadth of knowledge. > > **5. Failing to Discuss Restoration/PSSR (Loss: 1 Mark):** > The closing phases of temporary operations management are often neglected by students but are explicitly tested. > > **6. No Mention of Time Limits or Renewal Procedures (Loss: 1 Mark):** > Temporary operations must be **time-limited** with **renewal procedures**. Examiners penalize answers that treat them as open-ended. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — For Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation:** ### 🔑 Critical Definitions - **Temporary Operation:** Non-routine activity deviating from approved design/procedure - **Bypass:** Deliberate override of a safety function - **PTW:** Permit-to-Work — formal authorization for specific work - **MOC:** Management of Change — review and approval process for changes - **PSSR:** Pre-Startup Safety Review — final verification before restart - **IPL:** Independent Protection Layer — a safeguard with specific PFD - **LOPA:** Layer of Protection Analysis — quantitative risk assessment method - **PFD:** Probability of Failure on Demand — reliability metric ### 🎯 Five Pillars of Temporary Operations Management 1. ✅ **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment** — HAZOP, What-If, LOPA 2. ✅ **Multi-Level Authorization** — Risk-based approval hierarchy 3. ✅ **Time Limitation** — Maximum duration with renewal requirements 4. ✅ **Continuous Monitoring** — Real-time supervision with defined intervals 5. ✅ **Formal Restoration & Verification** — PSSR with multi-disciplinary team ### 📋 Must-Know Categories of Temporary Operations - **Equipment-Related:** Jumper hoses, temporary equipment, PRV bypass - **Procedural:** Control loop overrides, interlock bypass, beyond design limits - **Maintenance:** Hot work, confined space entry, line breaking - **Emergency/Start-up-Shut-down:** Emergency depressurization, abnormal operations ### 🔢 Essential Formulas - **Risk:** $R = P \times C$ - **LOPA Mitigated Frequency:** $F = f_{init} \times \prod PFD_i$ - **PFD (Single Channel):** $PFD = \lambda \times T/2$ - **Time Limit:** $T_{max} = R_{acceptable} / R_{temporary}$ - **Deviation Magnitude:** $\Delta = \vert P_{temp} - P_{design} \vert / P_{design} \times 100\%$ ### ⚠️ Famous Historical Incidents to Cite - **Flixborough (1974):** Temporary pipe rupture, 28 killed - **Bhopal (1984):** Water ingress into MIC storage, 3,800+ killed - **BP Texas City (2005):** Startup operations, 15 killed - **Grangemouth (1987):** Temporary hose failure in bitumen line ### 📜 Key Standards & References - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** — Process Safety Management - **CCPS Guidelines for Safe Process Operations** — Temporary Operations chapter - **EPA Risk Management Program (RMP)** - **UK HSE — Work Permit Systems Guidance** - **API RP 2007** — Safe Practices for Atmospheric Hydrocarbon Service - **NFPA 30** — Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code ### 🚫 Common Student Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Treating PTW and MOC as the same process -
