| **1** | **Construction & Equipment** | Equipment matches P&IDs; vessels / piping labelled; line-up complete |
| **2** | **Process Safety Information** | P&IDs, PFDs, material/energy balances, relief sizing, MSDS available |
| **3** | **Process Hazard Analysis** | All PHA recommendations closed or formally deferred with justification |
| **4** | **Operating Procedures** | Approved, accessible at console, reviewed with operators |
| **5** | **Training** | Operators trained on new process; competency demonstrated and documented |
| **6** | **Mechanical Integrity** | Hydrotest, NDT, leak test, PSV set-pressure test, instrument calibration complete |
| **7** | **Management of Change** | All MOCs for construction phase closed; pre-startup MOC closed |
| **8** | **Emergency Response** | Emergency plan updated; fire protection systems tested; eyewash/shower functional |
| **9** | **Safety Systems** | Alarms, interlocks, trips, ESD, fire & gas detectors function-tested |
| **10** | **Regulatory / Permits** | Hot work permits, confined space permits, lifting permits closed; statutory approvals obtained |

## 2.4 The Linkage: PSSR ↔ Other PSM Elements

> [!NOTE]
> PSSR is **NOT a standalone activity**. It is the *integrating element* that verifies the closure of all upstream PSM elements.

$$\text{PSSR} = f(\text{PSI}, \text{PHA}, \text{MOC}, \text{MI}, \text{Operating Procedures}, \text{Training}, \text{Audit})$$

**Logical Flow Before Start-Up:**

$$\boxed{\text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Procurement} \rightarrow \text{Construction} \rightarrow \text{MI Tests} \rightarrow \text{PHA Close-out} \rightarrow \text{MOC Close-out} \rightarrow \text{PSSR} \rightarrow \text{Chemical Introduction}}$$

Any open item at the PSSR stage **blocks** the introduction of HHCs. Only after formal sign-off by a **competent multi-disciplinary team** is the plant cleared to commence commissioning.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While PSSR is qualitative, the **decision logic** and **compliance logic** can be expressed mathematically for clarity in board exams.

### Decision Logic for PSSR Clearance

Let $S$ = Set of all required checklist items,
Let $s_i$ = Boolean status of item $i$ (1 = closed, 0 = open).

The plant is cleared for chemical introduction **only if**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i = n
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\text{Clearance} = \begin{cases} \text{GO} & \text{if } \forall i, \; s_i = 1 \\ \text{NO-GO} & \text{if } \exists i, \; s_i = 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Risk-Based PSSR Depth

Let $R$ = Residual Risk Score after PHA, $R_{\text{critical}}$ = the threshold risk.

$$
\text{PSSR Depth} = \begin{cases} \text{Comprehensive} & \text{if } R > R_{\text{critical}} \\ \text{Standard} & \text{if } R \le R_{\text{critical}} \end{cases}
$$

### Quantitative Compliance Index (Useful for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

$$
\text{Compliance Index (CI)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i}{n} \times 100\%
$$

A **CI of 100%** is mandatory for HHC introduction.

## 2.6 Who Performs the PSSR?

A **multi-disciplinary team** led by a competent PSSR coordinator:

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Process Engineer** | Verifies PSI, procedures, PHA close-out |
| **Mechanical Engineer** | Verifies MI test records |
| **Instrumentation / Control Engineer** | Verifies loop checks, DCS readiness |
| **Safety Engineer** | Verifies emergency plans, PSSR checklist |
| **Operations Lead** | Verifies operator training, procedure accessibility |
| **Maintenance Lead** | Verifies maintenance procedures, spare parts |
| **Plant Manager** | Final sign-off authority |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Industry Sector | PSSR Application |
|---|---|
| **Petrochemical Refineries** | New crude unit, hydrocracker, FCCU start-ups |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | New API plant or batch reactor campaign change |
| **LNG / Cryogenic Plants** | Liquefaction train commissioning |
| **Fertilizer Plants** | Ammonia / Urea plant start-ups |
| **Power Plants** | Boiler commissioning, turbine start-up |
| **Specialty Chemicals** | Reactor line modifications |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Logic & Worked Examples

## 3.1 PSSR Execution Algorithm — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

Below is the **fully expanded logical derivation** of the PSSR workflow that a KTU board examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

### Step 1 — Trigger Identification

A PSSR trigger event occurs under exactly **two** logical conditions:

$$
\text{Trigger} = \text{New Facility} \;\lor\; \text{MOC-Approved Modification}
$$

**Explanation:**
- A *new facility* trigger applies to green-field or brown-field installations of OSHA-listed HHC processes.
- A *MOC-approved modification* trigger applies when the change alters PSI, P&IDs, operating limits, or safety systems.
- Both conditions require the same PSSR rigor; no exception is permitted under 29 CFR 1910.119 (l).

### Step 2 — Assembly of the PSSR Team

A multi-disciplinary team is chartered under a designated **PSSR Coordinator**. The team's authority, scope, and reporting line are documented in a **Project PSSR Charter**.

**Team Selection Rule (Board Exam Favorite):**

$$
\text{Team Effectiveness} \propto \sum_{k=1}^{m} \left( \text{Discipline Coverage}_k \times \text{Seniority}_k \right)
$$

where $m$ is the number of disciplines represented.

### Step 3 — Development of the PSSR Checklist

The checklist is **customized** for the specific project. It is not a generic template. It must include:

- All PHA action items with closure evidence
- All MOC items with closure status
- All MI test records
- All regulatory permits
- All training records

**Logical Construction:**

$$
\text{Checklist} = \left\{ c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n \right\} \quad \text{where } n = \text{total verification items}
$$

### Step 4 — Document Review (Office Phase)

For each item $c_i$, the team verifies documentary evidence:

$$
\forall c_i \in \text{Checklist}, \quad \text{Document Review: } \text{Status}(c_i) \in \{\text{Verified}, \text{Pending}, \text{N/A}\}
$$

### Step 5 — Physical Walk-Down (Field Phase)

The team physically walks the plant with P&IDs in hand. Each piece of equipment is verified:

$$
\text{Equipment Match} = \begin{cases} \text{Match} & \text{if } \text{As-Built} \equiv \text{As-Designed} \\ \text{Mismatch} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

A **Mismatch** triggers either a corrective action or, if unresolved, a **NO-GO** decision.

### Step 6 — Functional Testing

Safety-critical systems are function-tested:

- **Alarm Test:** Each alarm is simulated and confirmed at the DCS.
- **Interlock / Trip Test:** Cause-and-effect matrix is verified.
- **ESD Test:** Emergency Shutdown is initiated and validated.
- **Relief Device Test:** Set pressure is confirmed against design.

$$
\text{Functional Pass Rate (FPR)} = \frac{\text{Number of Passed Tests}}{\text{Total Number of Tests}} \times 100\%
$$

**Acceptance Criterion:**

$$
\text{FPR} = 100\% \quad (\text{Mandatory for Safety-Critical Systems})
$$

### Step 7 — Decision Logic — GO or NO-GO

Define $s_i \in \{0, 1\}$ as the closure status of checklist item $i$. The clearance rule is:

$$
\text{PSSR Clearance} = \begin{cases} \text{GO} & \text{if } \prod_{i=1}^{n} s_i = 1 \\ \text{NO-GO} & \text{if } \prod_{i=1}^{n} s_i = 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Algebraic expansion:**

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n} s_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{only when } s_1 = s_2 = \ldots = s_n = 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single open item fails the entire PSSR. This is the **AND-gate** logic that board examiners expect.

### Step 8 — Documentation and Sign-Off

The PSSR is closed by a **formal sign-off sheet** signed by all team members. This document becomes a permanent record in the **Process Safety Information** file and is auditable under PSM audits.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Application)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A new ethylene oxide reactor plant has undergone construction. The PSSR checklist contains **8 critical items** (Construction Match, PHA Close-out, MOC Close-out, Operating Procedures, Operator Training, Mechanical Integrity, Safety Systems Test, Emergency Plan Update). The status of each is shown below. Determine the PSSR clearance status using both the AND-gate logic and the Compliance Index approach.

| Item # | Checklist Item | Status |
|---|---|---|
| $c_1$ | Construction matches P&IDs | Closed ($s_1 = 1$) |
| $c_2$ | PHA Action Items Closed | Closed ($s_2 = 1$) |
| $c_3$ | MOC for Construction Phase Closed | Closed ($s_3 = 1$) |
| $c_4$ | Operating Procedures Approved | Closed ($s_4 = 1$) |
| $c_5$ | Operators Trained | Open ($s_5 = 0$) |
| $c_6$ | Mechanical Integrity Tests Complete | Closed ($s_6 = 1$) |
| $c_7$ | Safety Systems Function-Tested | Closed ($s_7 = 1$) |
| $c_8$ | Emergency Response Plan Updated | Closed ($s_8 = 1$) |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — AND-Gate Logic Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i=1}^{8} s_i &= (1)(1)(1)(1)(0)(1)(1)(1) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the product is **0**, the PSSR result is:

$$
\boxed{\text{NO-GO}}
$$

**Reason:** Item $c_5$ (Operator Training) is **open**, which fails the entire review.

**Step B — Compliance Index Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{8} s_i}{8} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{1+1+1+1+0+1+1+1}{8} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{7}{8} \times 100\% \\
&= 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Clearance Verdict:**

$$
\text{CI} = 87.5\% \;<\; 100\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Chemical Introduction BLOCKED}
$$

**Step D — Remediation Action:**

The plant must complete operator training on the new ethylene oxide process, document the training, and **re-perform** the PSSR. Only when $s_5$ becomes **1** can the AND-gate return **1** and CI reach **100%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly say "PSSR = 87.5%, almost done, can introduce chemicals." **This is WRONG.** Under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, the compliance must be **100%** before introducing HHCs. Partial compliance is non-compliance.

---

## 3.3 PSSR vs. Commissioning vs. Start-Up — Analytical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> A frequently confused concept in KTU exams. Memorize the differences.

| Activity | Definition | PSSR Involved? |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Completion** | All equipment physically installed; no chemicals introduced | No |
| **Pre-Commissioning** | Utility tests (air, water, nitrogen) without process chemicals | Partial |
| **Commissioning** | Introduction of actual feed chemicals and operation begins | **YES — PSSR must precede** |
| **Start-Up** | Initial operation of the plant | PSSR already done |
| **Performance Test** | Plant operated at design capacity to demonstrate guarantees | Post-start-up |

The PSSR is the **gateway** between construction and commissioning.

## 3.4 Failure Mode Analysis — What Happens WITHOUT a PSSR?

If PSSR is skipped or rushed:

| Failure Mode | Consequence |
|---|---|
| Untrained operator on duty | Human error → process upset |
| Unverified relief valve sizing | Over-pressure → vessel rupture |
| Open MOC on instrumentation | Mismatched instrument → incorrect reading → wrong action |
| Untested ESD system | Failure during emergency → escalation |
| No updated emergency plan | Delayed response → higher consequence |

> **Historical Reference:** The **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK)** and the **Pasadena, Texas incident (1989)** both had elements of inadequate pre-start-up verification and contributed to the development of modern PSSR requirements under OSHA PSM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PSSR Lifecycle Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Trigger: New Facility or MOC-Approved Modification] --> B[Assemble Multidisciplinary PSSR Team]
    B --> C[Develop Customized PSSR Checklist]
    C --> D[Compile Process Safety Information Package]
    D --> E[Document Review Phase]
    E --> F[Physical Walk-Down Phase]
    F --> G[Functional Testing of Safety Systems]
    G --> H[Verify Training and Procedures]
    H --> I{All Checklist Items Closed?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Formal Sign-Off by PSSR Team]
    I -- No --> K[Generate Action Items for Open Items]
    K --> L[Corrective Action Implementation]
    L --> M[Re-Verify Open Items]
    M --> I
    J --> N[Authorize Introduction of Highly Hazardous Chemicals]
    N --> O[Commissioning Phase Begins]
```

## 4.2 PSSR Inputs and Outputs Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs[Inputs to PSSR]
        PSI[Process Safety Information]
        PHA[PHA Close-Out Register]
        MOC[MOC Close-Out Register]
        MI[Mechanical Integrity Test Records]
        PROC[Operating Procedures]
        TR[Training Records]
        ERP[Emergency Response Plan]
    end

    subgraph PSSR[Pre-Start-Up Safety Review]
        REVIEW[Multi-Disciplinary Verification]
        CHECK[PSSR Checklist Execution]
    end

    subgraph Outputs[Outputs of PSSR]
        GO[PSSR Clearance Certificate]
        RA[Residual Action Items]
        AR[Audit Trail and Records]
    end

    PSI --> REVIEW
    PHA --> REVIEW
    MOC --> REVIEW
    MI --> REVIEW
    PROC --> CHECK
    TR --> CHECK
    ERP --> CHECK
    REVIEW --> GO
    CHECK --> GO
    CHECK --> RA
    GO --> AR
```

## 4.3 PSSR Position in the PSM Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PSM Elements] --> B[Process Safety Information]
    A --> C[Process Hazard Analysis]
    A --> D[Operating Procedures]
    A --> E[Training]
    A --> F[Contractors]
    A --> G[Mechanical Integrity]
    A --> H[Hot Work Permit]
    A --> I[Management of Change]
    A --> J[Incident Investigation]
    A --> K[Emergency Planning and Response]
    A --> L[Compliance Audits]
    A --> M[Pre-Start-Up Safety Review]
    A --> N[Employee Participation]

    M --> O[Final Gate Before Commissioning]
    O --> P[Introduction of Highly Hazardous Chemicals]
```

## 4.4 PSSR Decision Logic Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Construction Verification] --> AND{AND Gate All Closed}
    S2[PHA Closure] --> AND
    S3[MOC Closure] --> AND
    S4[Operating Procedures] --> AND
    S5[Training Records] --> AND
    S6[Mechanical Integrity] --> AND
    S7[Safety Systems Tested] --> AND
    S8[Emergency Plan Updated] --> AND

    AND -- All Yes --> GO[PSSR CLEARED GO]
    AND -- Any No --> NOGO[PSSR NO-GO Block Chemical Introduction]
    NOGO --> CORR[Corrective Action and Re-Review]
    CORR --> AND
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Pre-Start-Up Safety Review (PSSR). When is it mandatory to conduct a PSSR?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Pre-Start-Up Safety Review (PSSR) is a formal, documented, and systematic examination of a new or modified process facility conducted **before** the introduction of highly hazardous chemicals (HHCs). It verifies that construction matches design, operating procedures are in place, personnel are trained, and safety systems are functional.

**When Mandatory (1 Mark):**
PSSR is mandatory under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (l) in two situations:
1. Prior to the **start-up** of a new facility handling HHCs above threshold quantities.
2. Prior to the **introduction of HHCs** into a significantly modified facility where the modification required an MOC approval.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six items that are verified during a Pre-Start-Up Safety Review.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ Mark each):**

1. **Construction matches design specifications** (P&ID verification).
2. **Process Safety Information** (PSI) is complete and accessible.
3. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** action items are closed.
4. **Operating procedures** are approved and available at the workstation.
5. **Operator training** is documented and competency demonstrated.
6. **Mechanical integrity** test records (hydrotest, NDT, relief valve set-pressure) are complete.
7. *(Bonus — any one more)* **Emergency response plan** is updated and communicated.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Explain in detail the **objectives and scope** of the Pre-Start-Up Safety Review (PSSR). Describe the **multi-disciplinary team** involved and their responsibilities. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Discuss the **structured phases** of executing a PSSR, and explain how the PSSR is linked to other Process Safety Management (PSM) elements. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a):

**Definition and Objective (2 Marks):**
PSSR is a formal verification activity performed before introducing highly hazardous chemicals into a new or modified facility. Its primary objective is to confirm that the plant — as built — is safe to operate, all procedures are in place, all personnel are competent, and all emergency response systems are functional.

**Scope of PSSR (2 Marks):**
The scope covers:
- **Hardware verification** (equipment, piping, instrumentation, safety systems).
- **Software verification** (procedures, PSI, PHA, MOC records).
- **People verification** (training, competency).
- **Emergency readiness** (fire protection, ERP, medical response).
- **Regulatory compliance** (permits, statutory approvals).

**Multi-Disciplinary Team (3 Marks):**

| Team Member | Primary Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Process Engineer** | Verifies PSI, PFDs, P&IDs, operating procedures |
| **Mechanical Engineer** | Verifies MI test records, equipment integrity |
| **Instrumentation Engineer** | Verifies loop checks, DCS, alarm/interlock testing |
| **Safety Engineer** | Leads PSSR, verifies PHA close-out, ERP |
| **Operations Supervisor** | Verifies operator training, procedure accessibility |
| **Maintenance Lead** | Verifies maintenance procedures, spare availability |
| **Plant Manager** | Provides final GO / NO-GO authorization |

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Three Structured Phases of PSSR (4 Marks):**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Review Preparation:**
- Compile PSI package, PHA close-out register, MOC register.
- Develop the customized PSSR checklist based on project specifics.

**Phase 2 — Physical Walk-Down (Field Verification):**
- Verify construction matches design (As-Built vs. As-Designed).
- Function-test alarms, interlocks, trips, ESD valves, relief devices.
- Confirm fire protection, gas detection, deluge systems operational.

**Phase 3 — Procedural and Personnel Verification:**
- Operating procedures are issued, accessible, reviewed with operators.
- Training records and competency assessments are verified.
- MOC items closed; emergency response plan communicated.
- Formal **GO / NO-GO** decision and signed documentation.

**Linkage to Other PSM Elements (3 Marks):**

| Upstream PSM Element | PSSR Verification Role |
|---|---|
| Process Safety Information | Confirm PSI is up-to-date |
| Process Hazard Analysis | Confirm PHA recommendations closed |
| Management of Change | Confirm MOCs closed prior to chemical introduction |
| Mechanical Integrity | Confirm all MI tests complete |
| Operating Procedures | Confirm procedures approved and accessible |
| Training | Confirm operators trained and competent |
| Emergency Planning | Confirm ERP updated and communicated |

**Conclusion:**
PSSR is the **integrating PSM element** that ensures no single upstream gap propagates into the start-up phase. It is the final gate before chemical introduction and is mandatory under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **PSSR execution algorithm**. Derive the AND-gate logic used to determine the GO / NO-GO decision. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A new ammonia storage facility has a PSSR checklist with **10 critical items**. Eight items are closed and two are open (operator training incomplete; emergency response plan not updated). Using both the AND-gate logic and the Compliance Index, determine whether the facility can be cleared for chemical introduction. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a):

**Flow Diagram (3 Marks):**
Refer to the **PSSR Lifecycle Flow Diagram** in Section 4.1 of these notes. The flow proceeds as:

$$\text{Trigger} \rightarrow \text{Team Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Checklist} \rightarrow \text{Document Review} \rightarrow \text{Walk-Down} \rightarrow \text{Functional Test} \rightarrow \text{Decision Gate} \rightarrow \text{GO / NO-GO}$$

**Derivation of AND-Gate Logic (4 Marks):**

Let $s_i \in \{0, 1\}$ denote the closure status of checklist item $i$, where:

$$
s_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if item } i \text{ is closed} \\ 0 & \text{if item } i \text{ is open} \end{cases}
$$

The plant is cleared for chemical introduction **only if every** $s_i = 1$. Using Boolean AND logic:

$$
\text{PSSR Clearance} = s_1 \;\wedge\; s_2 \;\wedge\; \ldots \;\wedge\; s_n
$$

In algebraic form:

$$
\text{Clearance} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} s_i
$$

**Truth Table Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n=2: \quad & (1)(1) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{GO} \\
& (1)(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NO-GO} \\
& (0)(1) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NO-GO} \\
& (0)(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NO-GO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single open item **fails** the entire PSSR. The AND-gate enforces a **100% closure requirement**.

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Total critical items: $n = 10$
- Closed items: $8$ → $\sum s_i = 8$
- Open items: $2$ (Training, Emergency Response Plan)

**Step 1 — AND-Gate Logic (3 Marks):**

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{10} s_i = (1)^{8} \times (0)^{2} = 0
$$

Since the product is **0**:

$$
\boxed{\text{PSSR Result} = \text{NO-GO}}
$$

The plant **cannot** be cleared for chemical introduction.

**Step 2 — Compliance Index Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i}{10} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{8}{10} \times 100\% \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification Against Acceptance Criterion (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{CI} = 80\% \;<\; 100\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Chemical Introduction BLOCKED}
$$

**Step 4 — Remediation (1 Mark):**

The facility must:
1. Complete ammonia handling training for all operators and document competency.
2. Update the emergency response plan specific to ammonia (toxic gas scenario with affected population radius).
3. Re-execute the PSSR until CI = 100%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** write "PSSR is 80% complete, almost ready, can start with caution." This is a **fatal answer**. OSHA requires 100%.
> - Do **NOT** confuse PSSR with general "commissioning checklist." PSSR is a **legal PSM requirement** specifically for HHC facilities.
> - Do **NOT** forget to mention the **multi-disciplinary team** — a 14-mark answer without team composition loses at least 2 marks.
> - Always end with the **GO / NO-GO decision** explicitly stated.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** PSSR is a mandatory **pre-commissioning** safety review under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (l)**.
- ✅ **Triggers:** (1) New facility, (2) MOC-approved modification.
- ✅ **Timing:** Strictly **before** introduction of HHCs.
- ✅ **Decision Logic:** AND-gate — **100% closure mandatory**, no partial approval.
- ✅ **Compliance Index formula:** $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\sum s_i}{n} \times 100\%$, must equal **100%**.
- ✅ **Team:** Multi-disciplinary — Process, Mechanical, Instrumentation, Safety, Operations, Maintenance, Plant Manager.
- ✅ **Linked PSM Elements:** PSI, PHA, MOC, MI, Operating Procedures, Training, Emergency Planning.
- ✅ **Key Verification Items:** Construction vs. design, PHA close-out, MOC closure, procedures, training, MI tests, safety system function tests, ERP update.
- ✅ **Output:** Formal **GO / NO-GO** certificate with multi-signature documentation.
- ✅ **Distinguish:** PSSR ≠ Commissioning ≠ Performance Test. PSSR is the **gateway**.
- ✅ **Failure to Conduct PSSR:** A common audit finding and a leading root cause of start-up phase incidents.
- ✅ **Historical Context:** PSSR evolved from lessons of major incidents (Flixborough 1974, Bhopal 1984, Pasadena 1989).
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** "Almost done" is **NOT** acceptable — the regulatory standard is binary (GO or NO-GO).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Integrity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mechanical Integrity (MI)** is a structured, document-controlled element of the Process Safety Management (PSM) program that ensures process equipment, piping systems, instrumentation, and control systems are designed, fabricated, installed, operated, inspected, tested, and maintained in a manner that prevents catastrophic failure and preserves the reliability of the process throughout its operational lifecycle.

According to **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (j)** and the **EPA Risk Management Program (40 CFR §68)**, Mechanical Integrity is one of the **fourteen mandatory PSM elements** that must be implemented at facilities handling **Threshold Quantities (TQ)** of highly hazardous chemicals (HHCs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mechanical Integrity is the **physical-engineering backbone** of PSM. While all other elements (Management of Change, Training, Operating Procedures) are *paper-based governance*, MI is the *physical realization* of safety — it is where engineering meets compliance.

The formal scope, as stipulated by the KTU PECHT864 syllabus, includes:

- **Pressure Vessels** (per ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII)
- **Piping Systems & Pipelines** (per ASME B31.3 / B31.1)
- **Storage Tanks** (per API 650, API 653)
- **Rotating Equipment** (pumps, compressors, turbines)
- **Safety Relief Devices** (rupture discs, pressure relief valves — PRVs)
- **Process Control Instrumentation** (sensors, PLCs, ESD valves)
- **Electrical Systems** (classified/hazardous area equipment)
- **Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **"Mechanical Integrity is the regular health check-up of a chemical plant — the same way you service your car every 5,000 km, you service a pressure vessel every 3–5 years, even if it looks perfectly fine on the outside."**

Imagine you own a car that drives on a mountain highway daily. You don't wait for the **brake pads to fail catastrophically** on a steep curve. Instead, you:

1. **Inspect** brake pads and tires every 10,000 km.
2. **Test** the brake fluid's boiling point annually.
3. **Replace** worn parts *before* they fail.
4. **Document** every service in a logbook.
5. **Train** mechanics on manufacturer procedures.

Now scale this exact logic to a chemical plant containing a reactor operating at **30 bar and 350 °C**, holding **flammable hydrocarbons**. The consequences of failure include **BLEVE**, **VCE (Vapour Cloud Explosion)**, and **toxic release**. Mechanical Integrity is the plant's "service manual" — codifying inspections, tests, preventive maintenance, and quality assurance across the entire **equipment lifecycle** (design → procurement → fabrication → commissioning → operation → decommissioning).

## 1.3 Why Mechanical Integrity is Indispensable

| Plant Incident | Root Cause Category | MI Element Failed |
|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Flixborough Disaster (1974)** | Failed bypass pipe | Piping inspection / modification control |
| **Bhopal Tragedy (1984)** | Corroded storage tank | Tank inspection & maintenance |
| **Texas City Refinery (2005)** | Untested relief valve | PRV testing & inspection |
| **Deepwater Horizon (2010)** | BOP (Blowout Preventer) failure | Critical equipment testing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When explaining Mechanical Integrity in answer scripts, always reference **"prevention through proactive engineering"** — not reactive failure response. Examiners award full marks only when you connect MI to *foreseeable failure modes*.

## 1.4 Statutory Performance Metrics

- **Inspection Coverage Ratio (ICR)** ≥ **95%** of all critical equipment (industry benchmark).
- **On-Time Inspection Compliance** must exceed **90%** annually (CSB and OSHA audits focus on this metric).
- **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) Test Interval** ≤ **12 months** for service in HHC service.
- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) Revalidation** every **5 years** (this drives MI re-prioritization).
- **API 510 / 570 inspection intervals** typically range **5–10 years** depending on corrosion circuits.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Mechanical Integrity Program

The KTU PECHT864 syllabus, in alignment with **OSHA 1910.119(j)**, identifies **seven interconnected work activities** that constitute a compliant MI program. These are:

### Pillar 1: Written Procedures & Training
- **What:** Written inspection, testing, and maintenance procedures for each piece of equipment.
- **Why:** Eliminates "skill-of-the-craft" variability.
- **How:** Trained and qualified personnel (e.g., **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** for NDT inspectors, **API 510/570** certified inspectors).

### Pillar 2: Equipment Inventory & Classification
- **What:** A documented database identifying all PSM-covered equipment.
- **Why:** Defines the universe of equipment subject to MI.
- **How:** Often managed in **SAP-PM, Maximo, or ARCHER (Meridium)** CMMS platforms.

### Pillar 3: Inspection & Testing Frequencies
- **What:** Scheduled activities (visual, NDT, pressure testing).
- **Why:** Detects degradation *before* it reaches critical limits.
- **How:** Driven by **RBI (Risk-Based Inspection)** per **API 580 / API 581**.

### Pillar 4: Deficiency Correction
- **What:** Timely repair of identified defects.
- **Why:** Prevents deferred maintenance backlog.
- **How:** Engineering assessment → fitness-for-service (FFS) per **API 579-1/ASME FFS-1**.

### Pillar 5: Quality Assurance (QA)
- **What:** Procurement, fabrication, and construction quality controls.
- **Why:** Ensures new equipment meets design intent.
- **How:** **ASME stamp holders**, weld procedure specifications (WPS), procedure qualification records (PQR), welder qualification.

### Pillar 6: Management of Change (MOC) Interface
- **What:** Any temporary or permanent equipment change triggers MOC review.
- **Why:** Prevents unapproved modifications from compromising integrity.
- **How:** Documented review by a multidisciplinary team before implementation.

### Pillar 7: Auditing & Continuous Improvement
- **What:** Periodic audits (typically **3-year cycle** for PSM compliance).
- **Why:** Verifies MI program effectiveness.
- **How:** Internal + external audits per **OSHA 1910.119(o)**.

## 2.2 RAGAGEP — The Engineering Benchmark

**RAGAGEP** stands for **Recognized and Generally Accepted Good Engineering Practices**. It is the *engineering rulebook* that defines what is "good enough" for MI compliance.

| RAGAGEP Source | Applicability | Issuing Body |
|----------------|---------------|--------------|
| **ASME BPVC Section VIII** | Pressure vessel design/fabrication | ASME |
| **ASME B31.3** | Process piping | ASME |
| **API 510** | Pressure vessel inspection | API |
| **API 570** | Piping inspection | API |
| **API 653** | Tank inspection, repair, alteration | API |
| **API 580 / 581** | Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) | API |
| **NFPA 25** | Fire protection water systems | NFPA |
| **IEEE 500 / IEC 60079** | Electrical equipment in hazardous areas | IEEE / IEC |
| **ANSI/ISA S84.01** | Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) | ISA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"What is RAGAGEP?"*, answer: *"It is the collective body of industry codes, standards, and practices that an ordinarily prudent engineer familiar with the industry would apply."* — This phrasing is the *verbatim* language used by **OSHA Letter of Interpretation (2009)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for Mechanical Integrity

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Practical Use |
|---|--------------------|---------------------|-------|---------------|
| **1** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | CR = Corrosion Rate; W = mass loss; D = density; A = exposed area; T = exposure time | **mpy** (mils per year) | Predicts metal loss over equipment life |
| **2** | $\text{RL} = \dfrac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\text{min}}}{\text{CR}}$ | RL = Remaining Life; t = thickness | **years** | Determines next inspection date |
| **3** | $\text{API 570 Insp. Interval} = \min\left(\text{F}_{1},\,\text{F}_{2},\,\text{F}_{3}\right)$ | F₁ = corrosion interval; F₂ = CUI interval; F₃ = mechanical damage interval | **years** | Calculates re-inspection due date |
| **4** | $\text{SIF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | SIF = Safety Integrity Factor; PFD = Probability of Failure on Demand | dimensionless | Sizes Safety Instrumented Function |
| **5** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Consequence} \times \text{Probability}$ | Risk-Based Inspection | dimensionless | RBI prioritization |
| **6** | $\text{PRV Set Pressure} = P_{\text{MAWP}} \times 1.05$ (typical) | MAWP = Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | **bar / psi** | Sizes relief devices |
| **7** | $\text{Half-Life (LEL Detector)} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\lambda_{\text{decay}}}$ | Sensor calibration interval | **months** | Calibration frequency |
| **8** | $\text{Inspection Effectiveness (IE)} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Defects Present}} \times 100$ | Inspection quality metric | **%** | Audits NDT vendor performance |
| **9** | $\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder | **MPa / psi** | Pressure boundary integrity check |
| **10** | $\text{Coverage Ratio} = \dfrac{N_{\text{inspected on time}}}{N_{\text{due}}} \times 100$ | MI KPI | **%** | OSHA audit metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet Tip:** Memorize equations **1, 2, 3, 5, and 9** — these appear most frequently in 14-mark derivations.

## 2.4 Degradation Mechanisms (The "Why" Behind Inspections)

| Mechanism | Description | Primary NDT Method |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Uniform / General Corrosion** | Wall thinning across surface | **UT (Ultrasonic Thickness)** |
| **Pitting Corrosion** | Localized deep cavities | **UT + Profilometry** |
| **Crevice Corrosion** | Under gaskets, deposits | **Borescopy / UT** |
| **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Cracking under tensile stress + corrosive environment | **Wet Fluorescent MT, PAUT, TOFD** |
| **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | H₂ diffuses into steel | **Hardness testing, MT** |
| **Erosion** | Mechanical wear by fluid | **UT + Visual** |
| **Fatigue Cracking** | Cyclic loading | **Dye Penetrant, MT, Eddy Current** |
| **High-Temperature Creep** | Time-dependent deformation | **Replica metallography, hardness** |
| **External Corrosion Under Insulation (CUI)** | Corrosion beneath pipe insulation | **Pulsed Eddy Current (PEC), RT, UT** |
| **Mechanical Damage** | External impact, denting | **Visual + UT** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate (mils per year, mpy)

The corrosion rate equation converts laboratory **mass-loss data** into an **engineering-usable rate** that predicts wall thinning over the equipment's service life. This derivation is **foundational** to RBI and remaining-life calculations.

### Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

The mass of metal dissolved during corrosion is directly proportional to the **electric current** flowing through the electrochemical cell.

$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of metal dissolved (grams)
- $I$ = corrosion current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

### Step 1 — Convert Mass to Volume

Since corrosion damage is volumetric, divide by density:

$$
V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
$$

Where $\rho$ = density of the corroding metal (g/cm³).

### Step 2 — Express Volume as Area × Linear Thickness

The corroded volume equals exposed surface area times linear penetration depth:

$$
V = A \cdot \Delta x \implies \Delta x = \dfrac{V}{A}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Rearrange

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Current Density

Define current density $i = I / A$ (A/cm²). Then:

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert Linear Penetration to Annual Rate (mpy)

**1 mil = 0.001 inch = 25.4 μm**
**1 inch = 2.54 cm**
**1 year ≈ 525,600 minutes ≈ 31,536,000 seconds**

The empirically calibrated engineering constant that absorbs these unit conversions is **K = 534**, yielding:

$$
\text{CR}_{\text{mpy}} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A corrosion rate of **10 mpy** on a pipe with **original wall thickness of 12.7 mm (500 mils)** implies a service life of approximately **50 years** — assuming uniform corrosion. RBI programs use this calculation daily.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Remaining Life Calculation

The **Remaining Life (RL)** is the projected number of years until the wall thickness drops to the **minimum allowable thickness (t_min)**. This drives inspection interval scheduling.

### Step 1 — Identify Inputs

- Measured thickness during last inspection: $t_{\text{measured}}$
- Minimum required thickness (from code): $t_{\text{min}}$
- Corrosion rate: $\text{CR}$ (mpy or mm/year)

### Step 2 — Calculate Available Corrosion Allowance

$$
\Delta t = t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\text{min}}
$$

This is the **total allowable metal loss** before the equipment must be retired, replaced, or repaired.

### Step 3 — Divide by Corrosion Rate

$$
\text{RL} = \dfrac{\Delta t}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\text{min}}}{\text{CR}}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the API 570 Inspection Interval Rule

Per **API 570 §6.2**, the inspection interval is the **lesser of**:

$$
\text{Interval} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{RL}}{2},\ 10\,\text{years max}\right)
$$

This **half-life rule** ensures the next inspection occurs with a **50% safety margin** — if RL = 10 years, the next inspection is in 5 years; if RL = 4 years, the next inspection is in 2 years.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A carbon steel pipe (density 7.85 g/cm³) loses **1200 mg** of mass over **180 days** on an exposed area of **20 cm²**. Original wall thickness = **9.53 mm**. Required minimum thickness (per ASME B31.3) = **3.18 mm**.
> **Find:** Corrosion rate (mpy) and Remaining Life (years).

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Apply the corrosion rate formula:

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{534 \times 1200}{7.85 \times 20 \times 180}
$$

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{640{,}800}{28{,}260} = 22.67\,\text{mpy}
$$

Convert mpy to mm/year:

$$
22.67 \times 0.0254 = 0.576\,\text{mm/year}
$$

Step (b) — Calculate Remaining Life:

$$
\text{RL} = \dfrac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\text{min}}}{\text{CR}_{\text{mm/yr}}} = \dfrac{9.53 - 3.18}{0.576}
$$

$$
\text{RL} = \dfrac{6.35}{0.576} = 11.02\,\text{years}
$$

Step (c) — Apply the half-life rule for next inspection:

$$
\text{Interval} = \min\left(\dfrac{11.02}{2},\ 10\right) = \min(5.51,\, 10) = 5.51\,\text{years}
$$

> **Recommendation:** Re-inspect the pipe in approximately **5.5 years**, or sooner if service conditions change.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel

The **hoop (circumferential) stress** determines the required wall thickness for pressure containment. This is the most fundamental equation in pressure vessel engineering.

### Starting Assumption

For a **thin-walled cylinder**, where wall thickness $t \leq D/10$ (i.e., radius-to-thickness ratio ≥ 10).

### Step 1 — Free-Body Analysis

Consider a longitudinal half-section of a cylindrical vessel of length $L$, internal pressure $P$, and diameter $D$. The internal pressure acts on a projected area $D \cdot L$.

### Step 2 — Force Balance

Pressure force pushing the two halves apart:

$$
F_{\text{pressure}} = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

This force is resisted by the **two wall sections** (one on each side) of total cross-sectional area $2 \cdot t \cdot L$:

$$
F_{\text{wall}} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{\text{hoop}}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Forces

$$
P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{\text{hoop}}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Hoop Stress

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Safety Factor and Design Margin

The ASME Code requires that the calculated hoop stress remain **below the allowable stress** $S$ at design temperature, typically:

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop}} \leq S \implies t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot P}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = allowable stress from ASME II-D tables
- $E$ = joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless, 0.85 for spot-examined welded)
- $1.2 P$ = corrosion allowance compensation term

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylinder. This is why **longitudinal welds** in pressure vessels carry higher load and require **full radiographic examination (RT)** — a key ASME Section VIII requirement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — NDT Method Selection

| Damage Type | Best NDT Method | Limitation | Inspection Effectiveness (IE %) |
|-------------|------------------|------------|----------------------------------|
| Surface cracks in ferromagnetic steel | **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | Surface only | **85–90%** |
| Subsurface laminations | **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | Requires couplant | **80–95%** |
| Tight surface cracks | **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** | Surface only | **70–80%** |
| Through-wall thickness profile | **Phased Array UT (PAUT)** | Costly equipment | **95%+** |
| Under-insulation CUI | **Pulsed Eddy Current (PEC)** | Low resolution | **60–75%** |
| Cracking in non-magnetic alloys (SS) | **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | Limited depth | **75–85%** |
| Volumetric weld defects | **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | Radiation hazard | **90%+** |
| Time-of-Flight Diffraction for cracks | **TOFD** | Skilled operators | **95%+** |

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: Define Mechanical Integrity as per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(j). List any four equipment categories covered under the MI program.**

**Model Answer:**
Mechanical Integrity is the PSM element that ensures process equipment is designed, fabricated, installed, operated, inspected, tested, and maintained to prevent catastrophic release of hazardous chemicals. It includes written procedures, trained personnel, equipment inventory, inspection frequencies, defect correction, QA, and audit.

Four equipment categories:
1. Pressure vessels and storage tanks
2. Piping systems (including valves)
3. Pressure relief devices (PRVs, rupture discs)
4. Process control instrumentation (sensors, PLCs, ESD systems)

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: What is RAGAGEP? Give two examples of RAGAGEP standards applicable to pressure vessel inspection.**

**Model Answer:**
**RAGAGEP (Recognized and Generally Accepted Good Engineering Practices)** is the collective set of industry codes, standards, and engineering practices that a prudent, experienced engineer would apply to maintain safe design, operation, and maintenance of process equipment.

Two examples for pressure vessel inspection:
1. **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** (design and fabrication)
2. **API 510** (in-service inspection, rating, repair, and alteration)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 — Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the seven essential elements of a Mechanical Integrity program. Discuss how each element contributes to the prevention of catastrophic process failures.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A carbon steel coupon (density 7.85 g/cm³, exposed area 25 cm²) loses 1850 mg over 240 days of exposure in a refinery overhead system. The pipe original wall thickness is 11.0 mm, and the minimum required thickness per ASME B31.3 is 4.0 mm.
Calculate: (i) the corrosion rate in mpy, (ii) the corrosion rate in mm/year, (iii) the remaining life, and (iv) the next inspection interval per API 570.

---

### Question B2 — Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) per API 580/581. With a neat flowchart, explain how RBI integrates probability of failure (PoF) and consequence of failure (CoF) to prioritize inspection resources.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal cylindrical storage tank (diameter 3.0 m, length 9.0 m) operates at 1.5 bar gauge and 60 °C. The shell is fabricated from SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel with an allowable stress of 138 MPa and joint efficiency of 0.85. Calculate:
(i) the design wall thickness per ASME Section VIII (using $\sigma = PD / 2SE - 0.6P$)
(ii) the maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)
(iii) the required set pressure for the pressure relief valve.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to B1(a) — Seven Elements of MI

The seven elements, each with its contribution to failure prevention, are tabulated below:

| Element | Description | Failure Prevention Role |
|---------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1. **Written Procedures** | Documented inspection, testing & maintenance SOPs | Removes variability; ensures consistency |
| 2. **Trained Personnel** | Certification per ASNT, API 510/570 | Eliminates human error in NDT |
| 3. **Equipment Inventory** | Master database of all PSM equipment | Defines scope; prevents omissions |
| 4. **Inspection & Testing** | Scheduled NDT, PRV testing, calibration | Detects degradation early |
| 5. **Deficiency Correction** | Timely repair & FFS evaluation | Closes the maintenance loop |
| 6. **Quality Assurance** | Procurement, fabrication, construction QA | Prevents defective equipment entering service |
| 7. **Audit** | 3-year compliance audits | Drives continuous improvement |

**Concluding statement:** Integration of all seven elements ensures that degradation is detected and corrected before reaching critical limits — preventing incidents such as Flixborough (1974), Bhopal (1984), and Texas City (2005).

---

### Solution to B1(b) — Worked Numerical

**Given:**
- $W = 1850$ mg
- $D = 7.85$ g/cm³
- $A = 25$ cm²
- $T = 240$ days
- $t_{\text{original}} = 11.0$ mm
- $t_{\text{min}} = 4.0$ mm

**Step (i) — Corrosion Rate in mpy:**

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{534 \times 1850}{7.85 \times 25 \times 240}
$$

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{987{,}900}{47{,}100} = 20.98\,\text{mpy}
$$

**Step (ii) — Corrosion Rate in mm/year:**

$$
\text{CR}_{\text{mm/yr}} = 20.98 \times 0.0254 = 0.533\,\text{mm/year}
$$

**Step (iii) — Remaining Life:**

$$
\text{RL} = \dfrac{11.0 - 4.0}{0.533} = \dfrac{7.0}{0.533} = 13.13\,\text{years}
$$

**Step (iv) — Inspection Interval per API 570:**

$$
\text{Interval} = \min\left(\dfrac{13.13}{2},\, 10\right) = \min(6.57,\, 10) = 6.57\,\text{years}
$$

> **Recommendation:** Schedule the next inspection within **6 years and 7 months**, or sooner if service conditions change.

---

### Solution to B2(a) — RBI per API 580/581

**Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** is a methodology that uses **risk** (a function of **probability of failure** and **consequence of failure**) to prioritize inspection activities — focusing resources on high-risk equipment and reducing unnecessary inspection on low-risk items.

**Key Components:**

- **PoF (Probability of Failure):** Function of damage mechanisms (corrosion, cracking, fatigue), equipment condition, and inspection effectiveness.
- **CoF (Consequence of Failure):** Function of fluid toxicity, flammability, inventory, and proximity to people/assets/environment.
- **Risk = PoF × CoF** — plotted on a 5×5 matrix to categorize equipment as **High, Medium, or Low** risk.

**Flowchart Explanation:**

1. Start with equipment inventory.
2. For each equipment, assess **PoF** based on damage mechanisms, operating history, and inspection history.
3. Assess **CoF** based on financial, safety, and environmental impact categories (Area, Environmental, Equipment damage).
4. Compute **Risk** and plot on the matrix.
5. Allocate inspection resources accordingly — **High risk** gets detailed inspection with shortest interval; **Low risk** gets on-condition monitoring.

> RBI delivers **20–40% reduction** in inspection costs while maintaining or improving safety — this is the core economic argument.

---

### Solution to B2(b) — Pressure Vessel Design Calculations

**Given:**
- $D = 3.0$ m = 3000 mm
- $P_{\text{design}} = 1.5$ bar = 0.15 MPa (gauge)
- $S = 138$ MPa (allowable stress for SA-516 Gr 70 at 60 °C)
- $E = 0.85$ (joint efficiency, spot-examined)
- Corrosion allowance assumed = 0 (for calculation; add 1.6 mm in practice)

**Step (i) — Required Thickness (ASME Section VIII, Div 1):**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + c
$$

With $R = D/2 = 1.5$ m = 1500 mm:

$$
t = \dfrac{0.15 \times 1500}{138 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 0.15} = \dfrac{225}{117.21}
$$

$$
t = 1.92\,\text{mm (without corrosion allowance)}
$$

**With 1.6 mm corrosion allowance:**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = 1.92 + 1.6 = 3.52\,\text{mm (round up to 4 mm standard plate)}
$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP):**

$$
\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6 \cdot t}
$$

Using $t = 4$ mm:

$$
\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{138 \times 0.85 \times 4}{1500 + 0.6 \times 4} = \dfrac{469.2}{1502.4}
$$

$$
\text{MAWP} = 0.3123\,\text{MPa} = 3.12\,\text{bar (gauge)}
$$

**Step (iii) — Relief Valve Set Pressure:**

Per **API 520 / ASME Section VIII**, relief valve set pressure:

$$
P_{\text{set}} = 1.05 \times \text{MAWP} = 1.05 \times 3.12 = 3.28\,\text{bar (gauge)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Round up to the nearest standard value** — typically **3.5 bar gauge** for procurement.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Mechanical Integrity questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Corrosion rate formula uses imperial (mpy). Mixing mm/yr with mpy without conversion is a **favourite 2-mark trap**. Always show both.
> 2. **Confusing MAWP and Design Pressure** — MAWP is the *maximum allowable*, not the *operating*. Set pressure > MAWP × 1.05 (not the other way around).
> 3. **Ignoring the half-life rule** — When asked "when to inspect," never answer with RL itself. Always halve it per API 570.
> 4. **Not mentioning RAGAGEP** — Any answer about MI standards without naming at least one specific RAGAGEP document (API 510, ASME VIII, etc.) loses a full mark.
> 5. **Skipping the FFS / RBI link** — When defects are found, students forget that **API 579 (FFS)** is invoked, not just "replace the equipment."

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanical Integrity**
>
> - **Definition:** MI is the PSM element ensuring equipment reliability through design, fabrication, inspection, testing, and maintenance.
> - **Statute:** **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(j)** and **EPA 40 CFR §68.65**.
> - **Seven Elements:** Procedures, Training, Inventory, Inspection, Deficiency Correction, QA, Audit.
> - **RAGAGEP Standards to Memorize:** ASME VIII (vessels), B31.3 (piping), API 510, 570, 653, 580/581, NFPA 25, ISA S84.
> - **Master Formula:** $\text{CR}_{\text{mpy}} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ — must memorize the constant **534**.
> - **Remaining Life:** $\text{RL} = \dfrac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\text{min}}}{\text{CR}}$ — drive inspection intervals from this.
> - **Half-Life Rule:** Next inspection = min(RL/2, 10 years).
> - **Hoop Stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ — remember longitudinal welds carry twice the load.
> - **NDT Selection:** UT for thickness, MT/DPT for surface cracks, RT/PAUT for weld defects, PEC for CUI.
> - **RBI Link:** Risk = PoF × CoF, plotted on 5×5 matrix; high-risk equipment gets more frequent inspection.
> - **FFS:** When defects are found, invoke **API 579-1/ASME FFS-1** before condemning equipment.
> - **PRV Testing:** Mandatory **at least once every 12 months** for HHC service.
> - **PSM Audit Cycle:** **Every 3 years** as per OSHA 1910.119(o); correct audit deficiencies within **acceptable timeframes**.
> - **Interface with MOC:** Any modification to PSM equipment triggers a documented MOC review **before** implementation.
> - **Key Historical Lessons:** Flixborough (1974), Bhopal (1984), Texas City (2005) — all root-caused to MI failures.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Vessels and Storage Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pressure Vessels — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **pressure vessel** is a closed container designed to hold gases, vapors, or liquids at a pressure substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure. The design pressure is typically defined as the maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP) developed at the designated operating temperature, with strict adherence to design codes such as the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** or the **Indian Standard IS 2825**.

A pressure vessel in the context of process safety management is **not** limited to highly pressurized reactors; even modest pressures (e.g., **0.1 MPa gauge**) are sufficient to classify a containment device as a pressure vessel under the Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO) and Factory Inspectorate regulations operative in India.

### Key Regulatory Thresholds in India

| Vessel Type | Governing Code | Threshold |
|-------------|----------------|-----------|
| Unfired Pressure Vessels | **IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII** | Internal dia. > 150 mm AND design pressure > 0.1 MPa |
| Boilers | **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)** | All steam-generating vessels |
| Transportable Vessels | **Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels (SMPV) Rules** | Gas cylinders, road tankers |
| Refrigeration Vessels | **IS 2192** | Ammonia/halocarbon systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A vessel containing a non-flammable, non-toxic fluid at less than **0.1 MPa (1 bar)** is *exempted* from IS 2825, but it is still a pressure vessel from a process safety perspective and must be designed for mechanical integrity.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balloon Wall" Concept

Imagine a cylindrical balloon inflated with air. The rubber wall must resist two distinct stresses:
- A **circumferential (hoop)** stress trying to split the balloon lengthwise along its side.
- A **longitudinal** stress trying to pull the two circular ends apart.

A steel pressure vessel behaves identically, except the wall is much thicker, made of ductile alloy steel, and the stresses are calculated to remain well below the yield strength with a built-in **factor of safety** (typically **4.0** on ultimate tensile strength for carbon steel per ASME Sec VIII).

> [!TIP]
> **Concept Hook:** The hoop stress in a thin cylinder is always **twice** the longitudinal stress. This 2:1 ratio is the single most important fact a KTU examiner tests in Part B derivations.

---

## 1.3 Storage Tanks — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **storage tank** is a stationary container used to hold liquids (predominantly hydrocarbons, chemicals, or water) at near-atmospheric pressure. Storage tanks are classified by their design pressure, geometry, roof type, and the flash point of the stored liquid.

### Storage Tank Classification (By Pressure)

| Category | Design Pressure | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Atmospheric Storage Tank** | $-0.5$ kPa to $+6.9$ kPa (gauge) | Crude oil, diesel, fuel oil, water |
| **Low-Pressure Tank** | $6.9$ kPa to $103$ kPa (gauge) | Volatile hydrocarbons, gasoline |
| **Pressurized Storage Sphere** | $> 103$ kPa (gauge) | LPG, NGL, butane, propylene |

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Open Bucket" vs. "Sealed Drum"

Think of a **bucket of water** (atmospheric tank) — pressure is essentially atmospheric, and vapors freely escape. Now picture a **pressure cooker** (pressurized sphere) — the contents are sealed, and any leak is catastrophic because of the pressure-energy released. The transition from atmospheric to pressurized storage is, in safety terms, a transition from a *spill hazard* to a *vapor cloud explosion hazard*.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in Process Safety Management

Process industry major accidents (Bhopal 1984, Flixborough 1974, BP Texas City 2005) involved failures in either pressure vessels or storage tanks. The **CSB (US Chemical Safety Board)** and **HSE (UK Health and Safety Executive)** statistics indicate that **mechanical integrity failure of pressure equipment** accounts for nearly **25% of all major process incidents**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "pressure vessel failure" with "explosion." A pressure vessel failure can manifest as a *crack*, a *rupture*, a *brittle fracture*, or a *catastrophic disintegration* — each with different prevention strategies.

---

## 1.5 Core Terminology Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **MAWP** | Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | The pressure stamped on the vessel nameplate |
| **Design Pressure** | Pressure used in thickness calculation | Always $\ge$ MAWP + margin |
| **Corrosion Allowance** | Extra wall thickness reserved for material loss | Typically $1$ mm to $3$ mm |
| **Joint Efficiency (E)** | Factor accounting for weld quality | $0.7$ (spot radiographed) to $1.0$ (fully radiographed) |
| **Hydrostatic Test Pressure** | Field test pressure at $1.3 \times$ MAWP | Proves leak-tightness before commissioning |
| **LOTO** | Lock-Out Tag-Out | Mandatory isolation procedure before maintenance |
| **PMS** | Pressure Management System | Logic system preventing overpressure |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Stresses in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

A vessel is classified as **thin-walled** when the ratio of internal diameter to wall thickness satisfies:

$$\frac{D_o}{t} \ge 20 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{r}{t} \ge 10$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** Always state the thin-wall assumption before using the formulas. If $D/t < 20$, the vessel is **thick-walled** and requires Lamé's equations (covered in Section 3).

### 2.1.1 Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — $\sigma_h$

The hoop stress acts tangentially around the circumference. Visualize cutting the cylinder in half longitudinally: the internal pressure tries to push the two halves apart, and the wall resists with a tensile force.

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

where:
- $p$ = internal design pressure (Pa or MPa)
- $D$ = internal diameter (m or mm)
- $t$ = wall thickness (m or mm)

### 2.1.2 Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — $\sigma_L$

The longitudinal stress acts along the axis of the cylinder. Visualize cutting the cylinder with two transverse cuts: the pressure acting on the circular end-cap tries to push the cap off, and the wall resists.

$$\sigma_L = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$

### 2.1.3 Spherical Vessel Stress — $\sigma_{sp}$

A sphere has no preferred axis, so the membrane stress is uniform:

$$\sigma_{sp} = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why spheres are preferred for pressurized storage:** The wall thickness required for a sphere is **half** that of a cylinder for the same diameter and pressure. This is why LPG bullets are often spherical, not cylindrical.

### 2.1.4 Radial Stress — $\sigma_r$

The radial stress varies from $-p$ (inner wall) to $0$ (outer wall). In thin-walled theory, it is **neglected** because $| \sigma_r | \ll \sigma_h$.

---

## 2.2 Design Thickness Equations (Per ASME / IS 2825)

The governing design equation for a **circumferential** stress-controlled cylindrical shell:

$$t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot f \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot p} + c$$

For a **spherical** shell:

$$t = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot f \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot p} + c$$

Variable glossary (with units per KTU convention):

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) | Notes |
|--------|----------|-----------|-------|
| $t$ | Required wall thickness | mm | Includes corrosion allowance $c$ |
| $p$ | Design pressure | MPa (or N/mm²) | $p_{design} = MAWP + \text{safety margin}$ |
| $D$ | Internal diameter | mm | For pipes, use $D_o$ (outside diameter) |
| $f$ | Allowable design stress | MPa | Typically $\frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{4}$ or $\frac{\sigma_y}{1.5}$ |
| $E$ | Joint efficiency | dimensionless | $0.7 \le E \le 1.0$ |
| $c$ | Corrosion allowance | mm | $1$ to $3$ mm typical |

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Design

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Step KTU Pressure Vessel Design Logic:** Examiners award marks for *each* step shown explicitly. Memorize this sequence.

1. **Determine Design Pressure**: $p_d = MAWP + \text{static head} + \text{surge margin}$
2. **Select Design Temperature**: Maximum sustained operating temperature.
3. **Choose Material**: Refer to ASME Sec II Part D, verify impact toughness for low-T service.
4. **Determine Allowable Stress $f$**: From ASME tables at design temperature.
5. **Select Joint Efficiency $E$**: From weld radiography category.
6. **Add Corrosion Allowance $c$**: Per process service life.
7. **Calculate Thickness** using the governing equation, then round up to next standard plate gauge.

---

## 2.4 Failure Mechanisms of Pressure Vessels

| Failure Mode | Physical Cause | KTU-Style Prevention |
|--------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Ductile Rupture** | Stress exceeds ultimate tensile strength | Adequate thickness, factor of safety |
| **Brittle Fracture** | Low temperature + thick section + stress concentration | Charpy V-notch testing, derating |
| **Creep Rupture** | Sustained high temperature ($>0.4 T_m$) | Use creep-resistant alloys (e.g., Cr-Mo steels) |
| **Fatigue Cracking** | Cyclic pressure / thermal cycling | Define cycle count, use fatigue curves |
| **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Tensile stress + corrosive species + susceptible alloy | PWHT, material upgrade, inhibitors |
| **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | Atomic H diffusion into steel | Use low-H electrodes, bake-out procedures |
| **External Corrosion** | Loss of insulation, CUI under insulation | Coating, regular UT thickness surveys |

---

## 2.5 Storage Tank Geometry & Sizing Equations

For a **vertical cylindrical tank** with a flat bottom and conical/domed roof, the volumetric capacity is:

$$V = \pi r^2 h + V_{roof}}$$

The shell course thicknesses (from bottom course to top course) follow a stepped design because **hydrostatic pressure increases with liquid depth**. The first shell course (at the bottom) is always the thickest.

For a **horizontal cylindrical tank** with hemispherical ends:

$$V = \pi r^2 L + \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$$

---

## 2.6 Bund Wall (Dyke Wall) Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The bund (or dyke) wall must contain **110% of the volume of the largest tank** within the bunded area. This is mandated by the **TPI (Tanks, Pipelines, and Installations) Regulations** and NFPA 30.

$$V_{bund} \ge 1.10 \times V_{largest\ tank}$$

If multiple tanks share a common bund, the capacity rule changes to:

$$V_{bund} \ge 1.10 \times \left( V_{largest\ tank} + 10\% \sum V_{other\ tanks} \right)$$

The bund wall height is typically **$1.0$ m to $1.8$ m**, designed for hydrostatic load + a $0.5$ kPa surcharge for sloshing.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No | Formula | Application |
|------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Thin cylinder hoop stress |
| 2 | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Thin cylinder longitudinal stress |
| 3 | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Thin sphere membrane stress |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{pD}{2fE - 1.2p} + c$ | Cylindrical shell design |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{pD}{4fE - 1.2p} + c$ | Spherical shell design |
| 6 | $D_{max} = \dfrac{2t(fE + 0.6p)}{p}$ | Cylinder MAWP from given thickness |
| 7 | $P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP$ | Hydrostatic test pressure |
| 8 | $V_{bund} \ge 1.10 \times V_{largest}$ | Bund wall capacity |
| 9 | $p_{design} = MAWP + 10\%$ | Quick design pressure margin |
| 10 | $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + \sigma_L^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_L}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress (for 2D biaxial state) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** When asked to find the *minimum* thickness, *always* add the corrosion allowance at the end — students frequently lose 2 marks by forgetting this single addition.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Refineries:** Atmospheric storage tanks for crude, gasoline, diesel — each in a separate bunded area segregated by **fire-resistance rating (typically 2 hours)**.
- **LNG Terminals:** Double-walled cryogenic tanks with $9\%$ Ni-steel inner tank and carbon steel outer tank, vacuum-insulated.
- **Petrochemical Plants:** Reactor pressure vessels designed per ASME Sec VIII Div 1 (with rules) or Div 2 (with stress analysis).
- **Ammonia Plants:** Primary reformer tubes, ammonia converter, high-pressure separator — all thick-walled vessels requiring Lamé analysis.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** Glass-lined steel vessels (GLR) where enamel thickness and stress concentration at nozzles are critical.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Vessel

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logic:** Examiners expect three stages in any stress derivation: (1) Free Body Diagram, (2) Force balance, (3) Stress = Force / Area. Missing any one stage results in loss of 2 to 3 marks.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram
Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and a unit length $L = 1$. The internal pressure $p$ acts radially outward on the inner wall. Imagine cutting the cylinder with a diametrical longitudinal plane. The pressure tries to push the two halves apart.

### Step 2 — Compute the Axial Force Exerted by the Pressure
The projected area on which the pressure acts (per unit length) is:

$$A_{projected} = D \times L = D \times 1 = D$$

The total force pushing the two halves apart is:

$$F_{pressure} = p \times A_{projected} = p \times D$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Resisting Force from the Wall
The wall resists this force through two longitudinal strips of cross-section, each of area $t \times 1$. The total resisting cross-sectional area is:

$$A_{wall} = 2 \times (t \times 1) = 2t$$

### Step 4 — Apply Force Equilibrium
For static equilibrium, the resisting force equals the applied force:

$$\sigma_h \times A_{wall} = F_{pressure}$$

$$\sigma_h \times 2t = p \times D$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Hoop Stress
Rearranging the equilibrium equation:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}}$$

**Unit verification (SI):**
- $p$ in Pa, $D$ in m, $t$ in m
- $\sigma_h$ has units of $\dfrac{\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}} = \text{Pa}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Vessel

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram
Consider the same vessel, but now cut transversely (perpendicular to the axis). The internal pressure acts on the circular end-cap, pushing it outward.

### Step 2 — Compute the Force on the End-Cap
The end-cap projected area is:

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

The axial force exerted by the pressure is:

$$F_{axial} = p \times A_{cap} = p \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Resisting Wall Cross-Section
The wall cross-section resisting the axial pull is an annular ring of mean circumference $\pi D$ and thickness $t$:

$$A_{wall} = \pi D \times t$$

### Step 4 — Force Equilibrium
$$\sigma_L \times A_{wall} = F_{axial}$$

$$\sigma_L \times \pi D t = p \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Longitudinal Stress
Canceling $\pi D$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_L \times t = \frac{p D}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t}}$$

### Verification of the 2:1 Ratio
Dividing the two stress expressions:

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_L} = \frac{pD / 2t}{pD / 4t} = 2$$

This confirms the celebrated 2:1 ratio. **The hoop stress is always twice the longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylinder.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Design Thickness Equation (Cylindrical Shell)

Starting from the hoop stress equation, we incorporate the **factor of safety** through the allowable stress $f$ and the **weld quality** through joint efficiency $E$:

### Step 1 — Equate Hoop Stress to Allowable Design Stress
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} \le \frac{f \cdot E}{1} \quad \text{(allowable stress criterion)}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Effective Thickness (Excluding Corrosion Allowance)
Rearranging for $t$:

$$t_{effective} \ge \frac{pD}{2fE}$$

### Step 3 — Apply ASME Correction Factor (1.2p Term)
The ASME code subtracts a term $1.2p$ from the denominator to account for the difference between the thin-wall formula and the more accurate thick-wall (Clavarino) solution:

$$t_{effective} = \frac{pD}{2fE - 1.2p}$$

### Step 4 — Add Corrosion Allowance
The total ordered plate thickness is:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{pD}{2fE - 1.2p} + c}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Design a cylindrical pressure vessel for the following service:
- Internal diameter: $D = 2000$ mm
- Design pressure: $p = 2.0$ MPa
- Allowable stress: $f = 137$ MPa (SA-516 Gr 70 at 200°C)
- Joint efficiency: $E = 0.85$ (spot radiographed butt weld)
- Corrosion allowance: $c = 2$ mm

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute all values into the design equation.

$$t = \frac{pD}{2fE - 1.2p} + c$$

$$t = \frac{2.0 \times 2000}{2 \times 137 \times 0.85 - 1.2 \times 2.0} + 2$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the denominator term-by-term.

- $2 \times 137 \times 0.85 = 232.9$ MPa
- $1.2 \times 2.0 = 2.4$ MPa
- Denominator $= 232.9 - 2.4 = 230.5$ MPa

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator.

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.0 \times 2000 = 4000$$

**Step 4:** Divide and add corrosion allowance.

$$t = \frac{4000}{230.5} + 2 = 17.35 + 2 = 19.35 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Round up to the next standard plate thickness (per IS 2062 / ASME SA-20).

$$\boxed{t_{ordered} = 20 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6 — Thin-wall check:**

$$\frac{D_o}{t} = \frac{2000 + 2 \times 20}{20} = \frac{2040}{20} = 102$$

Since $102 \gg 20$, the thin-wall assumption is **valid**. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Lamé's Equations for Thick-Walled Cylinders

For vessels where $D_o/t < 20$, the thin-wall theory is invalid. The radial stress varies significantly through the wall thickness, requiring Lamé's equations.

### Step 1 — General Form
At any radial position $r$ from the centerline of a thick-walled cylinder with internal radius $r_i$ and external radius $r_o$, the stresses are:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2) r^2}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2) r^2}$$

### Step 2 — Special Case (Solid-Acting Cylinder, $p_o = 0$)
With external pressure $p_o = 0$ and internal pressure $p_i = p$:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p \cdot r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \left( 1 - \frac{r_o^2}{r^2} \right)$$

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p \cdot r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \left( 1 + \frac{r_o^2}{r^2} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Boundary Conditions Check
- At $r = r_i$ (inner wall): $\sigma_r = -p$ (compressive radial stress) ✓
- At $r = r_o$ (outer wall): $\sigma_r = 0$ ✓
- Maximum hoop stress occurs at $r = r_i$.

### Step 4 — Maximum Hoop Stress (at Inner Radius)
$$\sigma_{\theta, max} = \frac{p(r_o^2 + r_i^2)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

This is the **Bree-Clouston limit** for autofrettaged cylinders. KTU students are expected to *derive* this and *state* the boundary conditions.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Von Mises Equivalent Stress

A pressure vessel experiences biaxial stress ($\sigma_h$, $\sigma_L$, with $\sigma_r \approx 0$). The Von Mises equivalent stress predicts yielding:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + \sigma_L^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_L}}$$

### Substitution Using the 2:1 Ratio
With $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$:

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{(2\sigma_L)^2 + \sigma_L^2 - (2\sigma_L)(\sigma_L)} = \sqrt{4\sigma_L^2 + \sigma_L^2 - 2\sigma_L^2} = \sqrt{3 \sigma_L^2}$$

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \sigma_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{pD}{4t}$$

### Yielding Criterion
The vessel will not yield if:

$$\sigma_{vM} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

where $n$ is the factor of safety (typically $1.5$ to $2.0$ on yield strength).

---

## 3.6 Storage Tank Bund Volume — Worked Derivation

### Problem
A tank farm contains three vertical cylindrical tanks sharing a common rectangular bund. The tank dimensions and contents are:

| Tank | Diameter (m) | Height (m) | Contents |
|------|--------------|------------|----------|
| T-1 | 10 | 8 | Crude oil |
| T-2 | 8 | 8 | Diesel |
| T-3 | 6 | 8 | Kerosene |

Determine the minimum bund capacity per **NFPA 30 / IS 10987**.

### Step 1 — Compute Individual Tank Volumes
- $V_{T-1} = \pi r_1^2 h = \pi (5)^2 (8) = 200 \pi = 628.32$ m³
- $V_{T-2} = \pi r_2^2 h = \pi (4)^2 (8) = 128 \pi = 402.12$ m³
- $V_{T-3} = \pi r_3^2 h = \pi (3)^2 (8) = 72 \pi = 226.19$ m³

### Step 2 — Identify the Largest Tank
$$V_{largest} = V_{T-1} = 628.32 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Bund Capacity Rule
For multiple tanks in a shared bund:

$$V_{bund} \ge 1.10 \times V_{largest} + 0.10 \times \sum V_{others}$$

$$V_{bund} \ge 1.10 \times 628.32 + 0.10 \times (402.12 + 226.19)$$

$$V_{bund} \ge 691.15 + 62.83 = 753.98 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Determine Bund Footprint and Height
Assume a rectangular bund of length $L_b = 20$ m and width $W_b = 10$ m:

$$A_{bund} = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$$

$$h_{bund} = \frac{V_{bund}}{A_{bund}} = \frac{753.98}{200} = 3.77 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Validation Check:** A bund wall height of 3.77 m is structurally unsound and unsafe. The bund footprint must be **redesigned** to keep the wall height between $1.0$ m and $1.8$ m. Increase the bund area:

$$A_{bund,min} = \frac{753.98}{1.8} = 418.88 \text{ m}^2$$

Choose $L_b = 25$ m, $W_b = 17$ m → $A = 425$ m² → $h = 1.77$ m ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pressure Vessel Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PV[Pressure Vessel Cross-Section View]
        direction TB
        nodeA[Inlet Nozzle<br>Reinforced Pad] --> nodeB
        nodeC[Outlet Nozzle<br>with Safe-End] --> nodeB
        nodeD[Manway Flanged<br>Quick-Opening] --> nodeB
        nodeE[Instrument Tee<br>for PI and TI] --> nodeB
        nodeB[Shell Course 1<br>Thickest Section] --> nodeF
        nodeF[Shell Course 2<br>Stepped Thickness] --> nodeG
        nodeG[Shell Course 3<br>Top Course] --> nodeH
        nodeH[Top Head<br>Torispherical or Ellipsoidal]
        nodeI[Bottom Head<br>2 to 1 Ellipsoidal or Hemispherical] --> nodeB
        nodeJ[Skirt Support<br>with Anchor Bolts] --> nodeI
        nodeK[Nameplate and<br>Stamping Plate] --> nodeB
        nodeL[Lifting Lugs<br>4 x 90 deg apart] --> nodeB
        nodeM[External Ladder<br>with Cage] --> nodeB
        nodeN[Platform and<br>Handrails] --> nodeH
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each component in a pressure vessel is a *potential failure point*. The shell courses are tapered to optimize material cost (thicker at the bottom for higher hydrostatic head). Nozzles are reinforced with *weldolet* or *pad-type* reinforcements to compensate for the material removed for the opening.

---

## 4.2 Storage Tank Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Storage Tank] --> B{Pressure Rating}
    B -->|Atmospheric<br>less than 6.9 kPa| C[Fixed Roof Tank]
    B -->|Low Pressure<br>6.9 to 103 kPa| D[Floating Roof Tank]
    B -->|Pressurized<br>above 103 kPa| E[Spherical or Bullet]

    C --> C1[Cone Roof Tank]
    C --> C2[Dome Roof Tank]
    C --> C3[Open Top Tank]

    D --> D1[Internal Floating Roof<br>with Fixed Roof Above]
    D --> D2[External Floating Roof<br>Pontoon or Pan Type]
    D --> D3[Covered Floating Roof<br>with Aluminum Geodesic Dome]

    E --> E1[Horton Sphere<br>Pressurized LPG]
    E --> E2[Spheroid Tank<br>Higher Capacity]
    E --> E3[Bullet Tank<br>Horizontal Cylindrical]

    C1 --> C1A[API 650 Standard]
    D1 --> D1A[API 650 Appendix H]
    E1 --> E1A[ASME Sec VIII Div 1]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The choice of roof type is **not** arbitrary. A **fixed roof tank** is used for low-vapor-pressure liquids (e.g., diesel, lube oil). A **floating roof tank** is mandatory for high-vapor-pressure liquids (gasoline, naphtha) to control VOC emissions and reduce fire hazard from vapor accumulation in the roof space.
