- **Industry Benchmark for OEL Compliance:** $C \leq 0.5 \times OEL$ for sustained operations.
- **LOPA Equation:** $f_{i}^{final} = f_{i}^{initial} \times \prod PFD_{j}$ — each IPL multiplies the protection.
- **HEART Method:** $HEP = 10^{-d/2}$ for simplified human error probability.
- **PSSR:** A **Pre-Startup Safety Review** is mandatory before commissioning any MOC-approved change.
- **Documentation:** Every MOC must be archived in a **permanent register** with 6-month and 12-month reviews.
- **Communication Cascade:** Originator → Supervisor → Plant Manager → MOC Committee → Workforce → Contractors → Audit.
- **Bhopal (1984) lesson:** Lack of MOC over years caused the world's worst industrial disaster.
- **Three concurrent safeguards:** Engineering controls + Administrative controls + PPE must all be evaluated post-change.
- **Human factors priority:** Training gaps must be **closed before**, not after, the change is live.
- **Conditional approval:** Triggered when $\Delta R > 0$ but below threshold; always requires PSSR.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Impact of the Change on Employee Safety and Health**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifications to Operating Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modifications to Operating Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Operating Procedures** are written, step-by-step instructions that document the safe and consistent manner in which a process must be operated under both normal and abnormal conditions. They are a mandatory element under the **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) Process Safety Management (PSM) standard, 29 CFR 1910.119(l)**, and form the operational backbone of the **Process Safety Information (PSI)** and **Management of Change (MOC)** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Pattern):**
> "Modifications to Operating Procedures refer to the systematic, documented, and management-controlled process of updating, revising, or replacing existing written operating instructions to reflect changes in process design, technology, raw materials, equipment, safety requirements, or regulatory standards, while ensuring that all affected personnel are trained and the integrity of the process safety system is preserved."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Checklist Upgrade" Intuition

Imagine an airline that has been flying the **Boeing 737** for 30 years using the same pre-flight checklist. One day, the airline:
1. **Replaces the engines** with a newer, more fuel-efficient model.
2. **Upgrades the avionics** with a glass cockpit.
3. **Changes the fuel supplier** (different fuel grade).

If the pilots continue using the **OLD checklist**, they might:
- Try to start engines using the wrong ignition sequence.
- Miss new warning indicators on the upgraded dashboard.
- Use outdated fuel quality checks.

The result → **catastrophic accident risk!**

This is *exactly* what happens in a chemical plant when operating procedures are NOT modified after a process change. The **Modifications to Operating Procedures** framework is the equivalent of the airline issuing a **revised flight manual**, training all pilots (operators), getting it signed off by the **Chief Pilot (Plant Manager)**, and archiving the old version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a chemical process, an outdated operating procedure is more dangerous than NO operating procedure, because it gives operators a *false sense of security* about a process that has fundamentally changed.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **OSHA PSM Threshold Quantity** | **> 5,000 lbs (2,268 kg)** of a regulated HHV (Highly Hazardous Chemical) | Defines which processes fall under PSM operating procedure rules |
| **EPA RMP Threshold** | **> threshold quantity** (chemical-specific) | Mirrors OSHA PSM for environmental risk |
| **Standard Review Cycle** | **At least every 5 years** | OSHA requires periodic re-validation of procedures |
| **Emergency Procedure Update Window** | **Immediately** | Post-incident SOPs must be revised without delay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 Anchor):**
> Modifications to Operating Procedures is one of the **6 major sub-elements** of the Operating Procedures requirement in PSM and is intricately linked with the **Management of Change (MOC)** element. According to OSHA 1910.119(l)(2), operating procedures must be **reviewed and updated** whenever:
> - A change in process design occurs.
> - A change in operating conditions occurs.
> - A change in process chemicals occurs.
> - A change in equipment occurs.
> - A change in safety/regulatory requirements occurs.
> - Lessons are learned from near-misses or incidents.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 7-Pillar Modification Protocol

Operating procedure modifications follow a **structured 7-pillar protocol** aligned with PSM best practices:

### Pillar 1: **Trigger Identification (Why Modify?)**
- Identification of the change driver — this is the *root cause catalyst*.
- Triggers can be:
  - **Process Change** (new equipment, new chemistry).
  - **Regulatory Change** (new EPA/OSHA rule).
  - **Incident-Driven** (near-miss or accident lessons learned).
  - **Optimization** (throughput, yield, energy efficiency).
  - **PHA/HAZOP Recommendation** (from safety studies).

### Pillar 2: **MOC (Management of Change) Initiation**
- A formal **MOC Form** is raised — this is the *legal authorization document*.
- Without MOC, no procedure modification is valid under PSM.

### Pillar 3: **Technical Drafting**
- The new procedure is drafted by a **Subject Matter Expert (SME)** — typically a Process Engineer.
- Must cover: startup, normal operation, temporary operation, emergency shutdown, normal shutdown, startup following emergency shutdown, and inspection.

### Pillar 4: **Hazard & Risk Re-Assessment**
- A **What-If Analysis** or **mini-HAZOP** is performed on the *new procedure*.
- Ensures the new procedure does not introduce new hazards.

### Pillar 5: **Multi-Disciplinary Review**
- Reviewed by Operations, Safety, Maintenance, Engineering, and Quality teams.
- A **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** may be required.

### Pillar 6: **Approval & Authorization**
- Signed off by the **Plant Manager** or designated authority.
- Documented in the **Procedure Master Log**.

### Pillar 7: **Training, Communication & Implementation**
- Operators are trained on the new procedure.
- Old procedure is **archived** (not destroyed — for audit trail).
- New procedure is **implemented** in the field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 7-pillar protocol reflects the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle of ISO 45001 / 14001 management systems. It is a *closed-loop* mechanism, ensuring that procedure modifications are never ad-hoc.

## 2.2 Types of Modifications

| Type | Duration | MOC Required? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Modification** | Indefinite | **Yes (Full MOC)** | Replacing a reactor with a higher-pressure design |
| **Temporary Modification** | Defined time period (e.g., < 90 days) | **Yes (Abbreviated MOC)** | Bypassing a sensor during a calibration campaign |
| **Emergency Modification** | Immediate | **Verbal MOC, retroactive paperwork** | Rerouting flow to avoid a leaking flange |
| **Trial Modification** | Pilot phase | **Yes (with expiration date)** | Testing a new startup sequence in one unit |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While process safety is largely **qualitative**, certain *quantitative* metrics are tested in KTU exams. These formulas govern the **timing, training, and validation** aspects of procedure modifications.

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **MOC Approval Timeline** | $T_{MOC} = T_{draft} + T_{review} + T_{approval} + T_{training}$ | Total elapsed time from initiation to implementation |
| **Operator Coverage After Training** | $C_{op} = \frac{N_{trained}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % of operators formally trained on the new procedure |
| **Procedure Obsolescence Index** | $POI = \frac{T_{since\_last\_review}}{T_{review\_cycle}}$ | Ratio of elapsed time to standard review cycle (target: $POI \le 1$) |
| **Residual Risk Score (Qualitative Matrix)** | $RR = P \times S$ | Where $P$ = Probability (1–5) and $S$ = Severity (1–5) |
| **Risk Reduction Factor** | $RRF = \dfrac{RR_{before}}{RR_{after}}$ | Quantifies how much the new procedure reduces risk |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Note:** When using `\times` or `\div` symbols in LaTeX, ensure no `|` (pipe) is used as a substitute for multiplication inside the markdown tables. Always use `\times`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The modifications framework is critical in:
- **Refineries** — when switching crude oil feedstock (different sulfur content → different operating limits).
- **Pharmaceutical Plants** — when changing from one API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) to another.
- **Polymer Plants** — when scaling up from pilot to commercial production.
- **LNG Facilities** — when modifying cryogenic handling procedures.
- **Ammonia Plants** — when changing catalyst types in the primary reformer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous Case Study (Bhopal Disaster, 1984):** The MIC (Methyl Isocyanate) storage tank had multiple undocumented operating deviations, including the **failure to maintain refrigeration** and **inoperative vent scrubbers**. While the root cause was multi-factorial, *poor documentation and modification of operating procedures* was cited as a contributing factor by the post-incident investigation. This catalyzed the creation of the OSHA PSM standard in 1992.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure Modification Workflow (Detailed Walk-through)

Below is the **exhaustive analytical derivation** of how an operating procedure modification is executed in a PSM-compliant plant. This is the type of structured answer that earns full marks in a 14-mark KTU question.

### Step 1: **Trigger Event Registration**
The modification request is logged in the **Plant Change Logbook** with a unique MOC reference number:

$$
MOC_{ID} = \text{YYYY-MM-DD} - \text{Sequence}
$$

For example: $MOC_{ID} = 2025-03-15-007$

### Step 2: **Risk Screening**
A **Risk Matrix Screening** is performed using the classic $5 \times 5$ matrix:

$$
\text{Risk Score} (R) = P \times S
$$

Where:
- $P \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ — Probability (1 = Rare, 5 = Frequent)
- $S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ — Severity (1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic)

The Risk Tier is determined:

$$
R_{tier} = \begin{cases} \text{Low} & \text{if } R \le 4 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } 5 \le R \le 12 \\ \text{High} & \text{if } 13 \le R \le 19 \\ \text{Critical} & \text{if } R \ge 20 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: **Technical Drafting of the New Procedure**
The new SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) is structured as:

$$
SOP_{new} = \{ \text{Header, Scope, Definitions, Responsibilities, Procedure Steps, Emergency Response, References} \}
$$

Each step must be:
- **Action-oriented** (verb at the start).
- **Quantified** (use specific setpoints, not "high" or "low").
- **Verifiable** (operator can confirm completion).

### Step 4: **Hazard Re-Assessment (Mini-HAZOP)**

The Mini-HAZOP examines **deviations** from intended operation:

$$
\text{Deviation} = \text{Parameter} + \text{Guide Word}
$$

For example, for the parameter "Temperature" in a reactor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{None} &= \text{No heat input} \\
\text{More} &= \text{Over-temperature} \\
\text{Less} &= \text{Under-temperature} \\
\text{As Well As} &= \text{Impurities entering with feed} \\
\text{Part Of} &= \text{Only one heating medium active} \\
\text{Reverse} &= \text{Flow direction reversed} \\
\text{Other Than} &= \text{Steam instead of hot oil}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each deviation is then evaluated for **Cause–Consequence–Safeguard–Recommendation**.

### Step 5: **Multi-Disciplinary Review (MDR)**
The draft procedure is reviewed by representatives from:
- **Operations** (usability, clarity).
- **Process Engineering** (technical accuracy).
- **Safety/PSM** (regulatory compliance).
- **Maintenance** (equipment-specific steps).
- **Training** (trainability).

Approval is captured via **sign-off matrix**:

$$
\text{Approval} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{Sign-off}_i
$$

Where $\text{Sign-off}_i = 1$ if department $i$ has approved, else $0$. **All** sign-offs must equal 1 for the modification to proceed.

### Step 6: **Training & Competency Verification**
Operators must demonstrate competency on the new procedure:

$$
C_{op} = \frac{N_{trained} \cap N_{competency\_verified}}{N_{total\_operators}} \times 100\%
$$

**Threshold for implementation:** $C_{op} \ge 95\%$ (industry best practice; some plants require 100%).

### Step 7: **Implementation & Old Procedure Archival**
- Old SOP is stamped **"SUPERSEDED — DO NOT USE"**.
- New SOP is placed in field locations.
- Old SOP is archived in the **Document Control System** for a minimum of the **plant's operating life** or as per regulatory requirement (e.g., EPA RMP requires **5 years minimum**).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Modification

| Decision Point | Question | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1:** Is there a change in process design, equipment, chemicals, or operating limits? | — | Trigger full MOC | Routine review only |
| **Q2:** Is the change permanent or temporary (>30 days)? | — | Full MOC + PSSR | Abbreviated MOC |
| **Q3:** Does the change affect a PSM-covered process? | — | Full PSM review | Engineering review |
| **Q4:** Are there any new hazards introduced? | — | Conduct HAZOP/LOPA | Use existing risk assessment |
| **Q5:** Have all operators been trained? | — | Implement | Delay implementation |

## 3.3 Mathematical Worked Example (Typical KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A plant has 40 operators working in 4 shifts (10 per shift). A new startup procedure is being implemented. After training, the records show:
- 38 operators attended the classroom training.
- 36 of those passed the competency assessment.
- 2 operators are on long leave.

Calculate the **Operator Coverage ($C_{op}$)** and determine if the threshold of 95% is met.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify total operators.

$$
N_{total} = 40
$$

Step 2: Identify available operators (excluding long leave).

$$
N_{available} = 40 - 2 = 38
$$

Step 3: Identify trained AND verified operators.

$$
N_{trained \cap verified} = 36
$$

Step 4: Calculate Operator Coverage.

$$
C_{op} = \frac{N_{trained \cap verified}}{N_{available}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
C_{op} = \frac{36}{38} \times 100\%
$$

$$
C_{op} = 0.9474 \times 100\% = 94.74\%
$$

Step 5: Compare with threshold.

$$
C_{op} = 94.74\% < 95\% \quad (\text{Threshold})
$$

**Conclusion:** The threshold is **NOT met**. The implementation must be **delayed** until the 2 long-leave operators return and are trained, or until the 2 operators who failed the competency assessment are re-trained and re-tested.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the **logical interpretation** of the numerical result, not just the math. A board examiner allocates marks for the "engineering judgment" sentence that follows the calculation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Operating Procedure Modification Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Identified: Process Change or Incident] --> B{Is MOC Required?}
    B -- No --> C[Routine Procedure Review Cycle]
    B -- Yes --> D[Open MOC Form with Unique MOC ID]
    D --> E[Risk Screening using P x S Matrix]
    E --> F{Risk Tier = High or Critical?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Conduct Full HAZOP and LOPA]
    F -- No --> H[Conduct What-If Analysis]
    G --> I[Draft New Operating Procedure]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Multi-Disciplinary Review by Operations Safety Engineering Maintenance]
    J --> K{All Departments Approved?}
    K -- No --> L[Revise Draft and Resubmit]
    L --> I
    K -- Yes --> M[Pre-Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    M --> N[Train All Operators on New Procedure]
    N --> O{Operator Coverage Greater than or Equal 95 Percent?}
    O -- No --> P[Delay Implementation and Retrain]
    P --> N
    O -- Yes --> Q[Archive Old Procedure as SUPERSEDED]
    Q --> R[Implement New Procedure in Field]
    R --> S[Audit and Continuous Verification]
    S --> T[Close MOC and Update Master Log]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Hierarchy of Documentation Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1: Plant Safety Policy] --> L2[Level 2: PSM Master Plan]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Operating Procedures SOPs]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Task-Specific Work Instructions]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Operator Logs and Checklists]
    L3 --> MOC[Modifications via Management of Change]
    MOC --> L3
    L1 --> AUDIT[Periodic Compliance Audit]
    AUDIT --> L1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Roles and Responsibilities (RACI Matrix Visualization)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PM[Plant Manager] -- Accountable --> APP[Final MOC Approval]
    PE[Process Engineer] -- Responsible --> DRAFT[Drafting the Procedure]
    SO[Safety Officer] -- Responsible --> RASM[Risk Assessment and HAZOP]
    OPS[Operations Supervisor] -- Responsible --> TRAIN[Operator Training]
    MTC[Maintenance Lead] -- Consulted --> EQUIP[Equipment Specific Steps]
    QA[Quality Assurance] -- Informed --> DOCS[Documentation and Archival]
    AUD[External Auditor] -- Independent --> VER[Verification of Compliance]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Procedure Modification as a Control System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **functional block-level representation** of the procedure modification system, modeled as a **closed-loop feedback control system** (analogous to a classical process control loop).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint: Desired Safe Operating State] --> SUM[(Comparator)]
    PV[Process Variable: Current Operating Reality] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal: Deviation or Change]
    ERR --> CTRL[Controller: MOC Process and Procedure Update]
    CTRL --> OP[Output: New Operating Procedure]
    OP --> PROC[Chemical Process Unit]
    PROC --> FB[Feedback: Operator Logs, Alarms, Incidents]
    FB --> PV
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This block diagram illustrates that the **Modifications to Operating Procedures** system functions as a **negative feedback control loop** — disturbances (process changes, incidents) are detected, a corrective action (new procedure) is implemented, and the system stabilizes at the new desired operating state. This is a **classic analogy** that KTU examiners often use in viva voce questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"What is the role of Management of Change (MOC) in the modification of operating procedures under OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**
- **(1 Mark)** MOC is the formal authorization mechanism that ensures all changes to operating procedures are evaluated for safety, documented, and approved by qualified personnel before implementation.
- **(1 Mark)** Under OSHA 1910.119(l), operating procedures must be updated whenever a process change occurs, and MOC provides the structured workflow to do so.
- **(1 Mark)** MOC ensures that the modification process itself does not introduce new hazards, by mandating hazard analysis, training, and pre-startup safety review.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"List any six conditions that trigger a revision of operating procedures as per PSM standards."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 marks each, list any 6):**
1. Change in process design (equipment replacement or modification).
2. Change in operating conditions (temperature, pressure, flow setpoints).
3. Change in raw materials or process chemicals.
4. Change in safety or environmental regulations.
5. Lessons learned from incidents, near-misses, or PHA recommendations.
6. Periodic review (at least every 5 years as per OSHA).
7. Change in operating personnel roles or shift patterns.
8. Introduction of new technology or instrumentation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse MOC with a simple document update** — remember, MOC is a *process*, not a form.
> - Do **not skip the training step** in your answer — it carries **at least 2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> - Always mention **documentation and archiving** of the *superseded* procedure; many students forget the old SOP and lose 1 mark.
> - For risk-based questions, **show the matrix calculation** (P × S); a vague "high risk" answer gets only partial credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**"Discuss in detail the structured process for modifying an existing operating procedure in a PSM-covered chemical plant. Include the role of MOC, hazard re-assessment, multi-disciplinary review, training, and documentation. (14 Marks)"**

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the step-by-step procedure modification workflow with reference to OSHA 1910.119(l) and MOC.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the hazard re-assessment methodology (What-If / HAZOP) and the training/competency verification process for operators on the modified procedure.

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Step-by-Step Workflow [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The modification process begins with **trigger identification** — a process change, incident, regulatory update, or PHA recommendation is recognized.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** A **Management of Change (MOC) form** is initiated with a unique reference number and a clear scope definition.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** **Risk screening** is performed using the $5 \times 5$ matrix ($R = P \times S$), and the modification is classified as low, medium, high, or critical.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** For high-risk modifications, a **full HAZOP study** is conducted; for low-risk, a **What-If analysis** is sufficient.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** A **multi-disciplinary review** is carried out by Operations, Safety, Engineering, Maintenance, and Training departments.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** The **Plant Manager** provides final approval based on a **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** The old procedure is archived as "SUPERSEDED" and the new procedure is implemented in the field with operator training.

#### (b) Hazard Re-Assessment & Training [7 Marks]

**Hazard Re-Assessment (4 Marks):**
- The new procedure is analyzed using **HAZOP guide words** (None, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) for each process parameter.
- Each deviation is assessed for **cause, consequence, safeguards, and recommendations**.
- A **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** may be applied for high-severity deviations to determine if **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** are sufficient.
- The output of this analysis is a list of **action items** that are integrated into the new procedure.

**Training & Competency (3 Marks):**
- All affected operators receive **classroom training** on the new procedure.
- **Hands-on simulation** in a control room mock-up is conducted.
- Competency is verified via a **written test and oral assessment**.
- **Operator Coverage ($C_{op}$)** is calculated as:

$$
C_{op} = \frac{N_{trained \cap verified}}{N_{available}} \times 100\%
$$

- Implementation is authorized only when $C_{op} \ge 95\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**"A refinery is planning to switch the feedstock from a low-sulfur crude to a high-sulfur crude. Explain how the operating procedures must be modified to safely accommodate this change, covering MOC, documentation, training, and emergency procedures. (14 Marks)"**

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] Identify the modifications required in the operating procedures (startup, normal operation, shutdown) and describe the MOC workflow.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the documentation, training, and emergency procedure updates required, with emphasis on the new hazards introduced (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ exposure, corrosion).

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Procedure Modifications & MOC Workflow [7 Marks]

**Procedure Modifications Required (4 Marks):**
- **Startup Procedure (1 Mark):** Updated pre-heating sequence, modified desulfurization unit startup, new temperature ramps to handle higher viscosity.
- **Normal Operation (1 Mark):** Revised operating temperature windows in the distillation column (due to higher boiling range), updated sampling frequency for sulfur content, modified catalyst regeneration cycles in the hydrotreater.
- **Shutdown Procedure (1 Mark):** Modified purging sequence to remove $\text{H}_2\text{S}$-rich residues, longer nitrogen blanketing duration, updated confined space entry permits.
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) (1 Mark):** New $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ release scenarios, modified isolation logic, updated personal protective equipment (PPE) — e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ monitors on all operators.

**MOC Workflow (3 Marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 Mark):** MOC form raised with full **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** using HAZOP.
- **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Risk matrix assessment — high-sulfur crude increases the $S$ (severity) of fire/explosion scenarios, raising the risk score.
- **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Multi-departmental review and **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** before introducing the new feedstock.

#### (b) Documentation, Training, and Emergency Updates [7 Marks]

**Documentation (2 Marks):**
- New SOPs are drafted, version-controlled, and stamped with a **"NEW — Effective From [Date]"** label.
- Old SOPs are stamped **"SUPERSEDED"** and archived for the **plant's operating life** in the Document Control System.
- The **Material Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** for the new crude is updated and distributed.

**Training (2 Marks):**
- Operators are trained on:
  - New operating limits and why they changed.
  - Updated emergency response for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ exposure.
  - Use of new PPE (respirators, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ monitors).
- Competency is verified using **simulator-based scenarios** in the DCS (Distributed Control System) mock-up.

**Emergency Procedure Updates (3 Marks):**
- New emergency scenarios for **$\text{H}_2\text{S}$ release** are added to the Emergency Response Plan.
- The **flare system** capacity is re-validated for the new feedstock's off-gas composition.
- **Mutual aid** agreements with neighboring facilities are updated to include the new chemical inventory.
- The **Emergency Operations Center (EOC)** is briefed on the new hazards and response protocols.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- ✅ **Modifications to Operating Procedures** is governed by **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119(l)**.
- ✅ Every modification must flow through the **MOC (Management of Change)** process.
- ✅ A **risk matrix** ($R = P \times S$) is used to classify the modification tier.
- ✅ **HAZOP** is used for high-risk modifications; **What-If** for low-risk.
- ✅ **Multi-disciplinary review** is mandatory (Operations, Safety, Engineering, Maintenance, Training).
- ✅ **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** is required before implementation.
- ✅ **Operator training** with a coverage threshold of $C_{op} \ge 95\%$ is the gate to implementation.
- ✅ **Old procedures** are archived (not destroyed) as "SUPERSEDED."
- ✅ The **review cycle** for operating procedures is at least every **5 years**.
- ✅ **Emergency procedures** must be updated *immediately* after a process change.
- ✅ Always show the **engineering interpretation** of any numerical result in your answer.
- ✅ The modification system is a **closed-loop feedback control system** — memorize this analogy for viva questions.
- ✅ The 7-pillar protocol: **Trigger → MOC → Draft → HAZOP → MDR → Approval → Train & Implement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Note:** Questions on Modifications to Operating Procedures are commonly asked in the **ESE (End Semester Exam) Module 4** slot. Allocate **at least 8–10 minutes** for a 14-mark question and aim for a **structured, sub-parted answer** with diagrams (e.g., MOC flowchart) wherever possible. Neat, well-labeled diagrams fetch an easy **1–2 bonus marks** under the KTU valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Necessary Time Period for the Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Necessary Time Period for the Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** under the **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** standard and as adopted within the **KTU PECHT864 syllabus (2024 Scheme)**, the **Necessary Time Period for the Change** is formally defined as:

> **Necessary Time Period for the Change (NTPC)** is the pre-determined, technically justified, and management-authorized duration for which a temporary deviation from approved process design, operating procedures, or equipment specifications may be safely implemented, operated, and subsequently restored to original conditions within a process plant, prior to permanent engineering redesign or full re-validation.

The NTPC concept originates from **Element 8 – Management of Change (MOC)** of the OSHA PSM standard, which mandates that *"Each temporary change shall be limited to a specified time period not to exceed a duration justified by the engineering basis of the change."*

The **Necessary Time Period** is bounded by three critical temporal boundaries:

| Boundary Phase | Description |
|---|---|
| **T₀ (Initiation Time)** | The instant when the change request is formally raised and the MOC procedure is triggered |
| **T₁ (Authorization Time)** | The instant when Technical, Operational, and Safety approvals are granted |
| **T₂ (Restoration Time)** | The instant when the plant must be returned to its original design state |

$$\Delta T_{NTPC} = T_2 - T_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The NTPC is **NOT** an arbitrary time chosen by the operations team. It must be derived from a **documented engineering basis** that justifies why the change is needed, why it is temporary, and the exact technical reasoning for the duration selected.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are renting a house. Your landlord allows you to **temporarily** install a portable air conditioner (a "change" to the electrical system). The landlord says:

> *"You may install this AC unit. However, you must remove it within 90 days, because the building's electrical wiring was not designed for permanent AC loads. After 90 days, if you still need AC, you must apply for a permanent electrical upgrade."*

In this analogy:

- **The house** = Your chemical process plant
- **The original electrical design** = The approved Process & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)
- **The portable AC** = The temporary change (e.g., bypass line, temporary pump, modified operating parameter)
- **The 90 days** = The **Necessary Time Period for the Change**
- **The landlord's justification** = The engineering basis
- **Permanent electrical upgrade** = The eventual Management of Permanent Change

This time limit exists because **temporary changes are engineering compromises** — they reduce safety margins, bypass interlocks, or alter process conditions in ways that cannot be tolerated indefinitely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a time limit exist at all?** Because every temporary change introduces **latent risk**. The longer a deviation from design intent persists, the higher the probability of an incident occurring due to forgotten overrides, degraded documentation, or operator normalization of deviance.

### 1.3 Classification of Changes by Duration

Under the KTU-aligned PSM framework, changes are time-categorized as follows:

$$\text{Change Classification} = f(\Delta T_{NTPC})$$

1. **Emergency Change** ($\Delta T \leq 72$ hours): Imminent hazard-driven, oral authorization permitted, written follow-up within 24 hours
2. **Short-Term Temporary Change** ($72 \text{ h} < \Delta T \leq 30$ days): Full MOC review with simplified basis
3. **Long-Term Temporary Change** ($30 \text{ days} < \Delta T \leq 12$ months): Comprehensive MOC with PHA re-validation
4. **Permanent Change** ($\Delta T > 12$ months or indefinite): Treated as a new design — full Management of Change with MOC → MOC closure → As-built update cycle

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Standard PSM Audit Reference:** **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(l)(2)** — Management of Change element
- **Typical Industry Benchmark for Temporary Changes:** **≤ 6 months** (AIChE/CCPS Guideline)
- **Hard Upper Limit Justification Threshold:** **12 months** before re-justification is mandatory
- **Documentation Retention:** Minimum **5 years** for all MOC records (lifetime of the process for permanent changes)
- **MOC Review Cycle:** Not less than **once per 5 years** for ongoing changes (API RP 754)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** The **Necessary Time Period** must be stated in the MOC document in **calendar days**, NOT in operating hours or production shifts. This prevents ambiguity during audits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Phases of an MOC Lifecycle

The **Necessary Time Period for the Change** is determined within a structured lifecycle. Each phase contributes to the total elapsed time, but only Phase III contributes to the *active* NTPC duration.

#### **Phase I — Request and Screening** (Pre-MOC)
- Change Request (CR) raised by Operations, Maintenance, or Engineering
- **MOC Coordinator** screens the request for PSM applicability
- If applicable → MOC ticket opened; if not → routed to Standard Work Order
- **Typical Duration:** 1 to 5 working days

#### **Phase II — Technical Review and Authorization**
- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) re-evaluation** for the proposed change
- Identification of **affected documents**: P&IDs, PFDs, Cause & Effect matrices, operating procedures
- Multi-disciplinary approval: **Process Engineering, Operations, Maintenance, Safety, HSE**
- **Typical Duration:** 5 to 15 working days for non-emergency changes

#### **Phase III — Implementation and Active Change Period** *(This is the NTPC)*
- Physical implementation of the change in the field
- Active operation under modified conditions
- Continuous monitoring of NTPC expiry
- **This is the phase bounded by $T_1$ (Authorization) and $T_2$ (Restoration)**
- **Typical Duration:** Engineering-justified, generally 30 days to 6 months

#### **Phase IV — Restoration and Verification**
- Removal of temporary equipment (bypass lines, jumpers, blinds removed)
- Restoration to original design conditions
- Pre-startup Safety Review (PSSR) verification
- **Typical Duration:** 1 to 7 working days

#### **Phase V — Closure and Documentation**
- As-built drawing updates
- Operating procedure revisions
- Lessons-learned documentation
- MOC closure sign-off
- **Typical Duration:** 5 to 10 working days

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Time Limitation — Engineering Rationale

The necessity of imposing a time period on a change is rooted in **three core risk principles**:

1. **Degradation of Safety Awareness (Normalization of Deviation):**
   The longer a temporary override remains in place, the more operators and engineers begin to treat it as the "new normal." This phenomenon was a contributing factor in the **Texas City Refinery explosion (2005)** and the **Bhopal disaster (1984)**.

2. **Documentation Drift:**
   Temporary changes often bypass the formal document update cycle. If left indefinitely, field reality diverges from documented reality, creating a "design-incompleteness" hazard.

3. **Compounding Risk Exposure:**
   Risk is cumulative over time. The cumulative hazard exposure is:

$$H_{cumulative} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \lambda(t) \cdot dt$$

   where $\lambda(t)$ is the instantaneous failure/incident rate during the change state. As $T_2 - T_1$ grows, $H_{cumulative}$ grows proportionally.

### 2.3 Engineering Basis for Determining NTPC

The duration is justified using the following decision framework:

| Justification Criterion | Driving Question | Typical Resulting Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Lead Time** | When will the permanent replacement arrive? | NTPC = Lead Time + Installation Buffer |
| **Maintenance Window** | When is the next planned turnaround? | NTPC = Time until Turnaround |
| **Operational Necessity** | Until what date is the deviation functionally required? | NTPC = End of operational need date |
| **Safety Margin** | What is the maximum tolerable exposure at elevated risk? | NTPC = $H_{cumulative, max} / \bar{\lambda}$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Necessary Time Period** | $\Delta T_{NTPC} = T_2 - T_1$ | Days | Defines the active change window |
| **Cumulative Hazard Exposure** | $H_{cum} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \lambda(t)\, dt$ | Incidents (dimensionless) | Risk accumulation during change |
| **Mean Exposure-based NTPC** | $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{H_{cumulative, allowable}}{\bar{\lambda}}$ | Days | Upper bound from risk tolerance |
| **Re-justification Frequency** | $f_{re} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta T_{NTPC}}$ | per day | Triggers re-approval cycles |
| **Emergency Change Limit** | $\Delta T_{emerg} \leq 72$ | Hours | OSHA benchmark for oral authorization |
| **Long-Term Temp. Limit** | $\Delta T_{long} \leq 6$ | Months | AIChE/CCPS guideline |
| **Hard Cap (Before Re-justification)** | $\Delta T_{cap} \leq 12$ | Months | Maximum before new MOC required |
| **Risk Multiplier Check** | $R_{change} = R_{baseline} \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{NTPC})$ | Dimensionless | Linearized risk growth model |
| **PHA Re-validation Trigger** | If $\Delta T_{NTPC} > 30$ days → mandatory PHA update | Boolean | KTU/OSHA compliance gate |
| **Documentation Retention** | $T_{retain} \geq 5$ years (or process lifetime) | Years | Audit compliance |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all the above expressions, the absolute value bars and conditional notation use **\(\vert\)** and **\(\mid\)** LaTeX commands. This preserves markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The NTPC framework is actively used in:

- **Petrochemical refining:** Temporary operating limit modifications during catalyst changeout cycles
- **Pharmaceutical batch processing:** Temporary equipment substitutions to maintain production schedules
- **LNG terminals:** Temporary vapor return system bypasses during maintenance
- **Power plant commissioning:** Temporary alignment configurations before final COD (Commercial Operation Date)

In each case, the NTPC prevents **"temporary forever"** scenarios — the most insidious failure mode of MOC programs worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Allowable NTPC from Risk Tolerance

**Problem Statement:** A process change introduces an elevated incident rate of $\lambda = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ incidents/day. Plant management has set a maximum cumulative hazard exposure budget of $H_{allow} = 0.05$ incidents. Determine the maximum allowable Necessary Time Period for the Change.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the governing relationship between cumulative hazard and incident rate.

$$
H_{cumulative} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \lambda(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2:** Assume a constant (time-invariant) incident rate during the active change period. This is the standard conservative engineering assumption for PSM calculations.

$$
\lambda(t) = \bar{\lambda} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the constant rate into the integral.

$$
H_{cumulative} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \bar{\lambda}\, dt = \bar{\lambda} \cdot (T_2 - T_1)
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $(T_2 - T_1)$ is exactly the Necessary Time Period for the Change.

$$
H_{cumulative} = \bar{\lambda} \cdot \Delta T_{NTPC}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the risk tolerance constraint: cumulative hazard must not exceed the allowable budget.

$$
H_{cumulative} \leq H_{allow}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the expression from Step 4 into the inequality.

$$
\bar{\lambda} \cdot \Delta T_{NTPC} \leq H_{allow}
$$

**Step 7:** Solve algebraically for $\Delta T_{NTPC}$.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq \frac{H_{allow}}{\bar{\lambda}}
$$

**Step 8:** Insert numerical values.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq \frac{0.05}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**Step 9:** Perform the division.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq 200 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 10:** Convert to months for engineering communication (using 30 days/month).

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq \frac{200}{30} \approx 6.67 \text{ months}
$$

**Final Answer:** The maximum Necessary Time Period for the Change is **200 days** (≈ **6.67 months**). For practical implementation, this should be rounded down to **6 months** to maintain a safety margin and align with AIChE/CCPS guidelines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Risk Growth Model During Active Change Period

**Problem Statement:** During a temporary bypass of a relief valve, the baseline process risk is $R_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ incidents/year. The risk growth coefficient due to the temporary change is $\alpha = 0.005$ per day. The proposed NTPC is 90 days. Calculate the risk at the end of the NTPC window.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** State the linearized risk growth model during the active change period.

$$
R(t) = R_0 \cdot \left(1 + \alpha \cdot (t - T_1)\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the time variable convention. Let $t$ be measured from the authorization instant $T_1$, so that $(t - T_1)$ represents days elapsed since the change was authorized.

**Step 3:** Substitute the given numerical values at $t - T_1 = \Delta T_{NTPC} = 90$ days.

$$
R(90) = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \left(1 + (0.005) \cdot (90)\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the product inside the parentheses.

$$
(0.005) \cdot (90) = 0.45
$$

**Step 5:** Add unity to compute the risk amplification factor.

$$
1 + 0.45 = 1.45
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by the baseline risk.

$$
R(90) = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1.45) = 1.74 \times 10^{-3} \text{ incidents/year}
$$

**Step 7:** Express the risk increase as a percentage for engineering interpretation.

$$
\Delta R\% = \left(\frac{R(90) - R_0}{R_0}\right) \times 100\% = (0.45) \times 100\% = 45\%
$$

**Final Answer:** At the end of the 90-day NTPC, the process risk has increased by **45%** over baseline, reaching **$1.74 \times 10^{-3}$ incidents/year**. This calculation provides quantitative justification for selecting (or rejecting) the proposed NTPC duration.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart Logic for NTPC Selection

The following matrix shows how the engineering basis translates into an NTPC value:

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} = \min\left\{
\begin{array}{l}
\Delta T_{equipment\,lead} \\
\Delta T_{turnaround\,gap} \\
\Delta T_{operational\,need} \\
\Delta T_{risk\,budget}
\end{array}
\right.
$$

**Interpretation:** The actual NTPC is the **minimum** of all candidate durations, ensuring that the change is bounded by the most conservative of the four limiting factors. This is a standard safety-engineering "weakest-link" approach.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Emergency Change Scenario

**Scenario:** A pump seal fails at 02:00 hrs. Operations installs a temporary mechanical seal with an estimated lead time of 45 days for the permanent replacement. The process hazard analysis re-rating shows the elevated incident rate is $\bar{\lambda} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per day. The plant risk budget allows $H_{allow} = 0.075$.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1:** Compute the equipment-lead-time-based NTPC.

$$
\Delta T_{lead} = 45 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the risk-budget-based NTPC.

$$
\Delta T_{risk} = \frac{H_{allow}}{\bar{\lambda}} = \frac{0.075}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 150 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the minimum operator.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} = \min(45, 150) = 45 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the emergency-change oral-authorization rule (within 72 hours, written follow-up required).

$$
\text{Authorization at } T_1 = T_0 + 4 \text{ hours (oral)}; \quad \text{Written MOC by } T_1 + 24 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5:** Schedule the restoration deadline.

$$
T_2 = T_1 + 45 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 6:** Schedule a re-justification review at 75% of NTPC elapsed (industry best practice).

$$
T_{review} = T_1 + 0.75 \cdot 45 = T_1 + 33.75 \text{ days}
$$

**Final Answer:** The NTPC is **45 days**, bounded by the equipment lead time. A mandatory re-justification review is scheduled at **Day 34**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MOC Lifecycle and NTPC Position — Block Diagram

The following functional block diagram illustrates where the Necessary Time Period sits within the broader Management of Change lifecycle:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[phase1 request] -->|CR Raised| B[phase2 screen]
    B -->|PSM Applicable| C[phase3 techreview]
    B -->|Not Applicable| D[phase3b stdwo]
    C --> E[phase4 pHA]
    E --> F[phase5 authdept]
    F --> G[phase6 implement]
    G -->|T1 Authorization| H[phase7 activechange]
    H -->|Delta T NTPC Active| I[phase8 monitor]
    I -->|75 percent elapsed| J[phase9 rejustify]
    J -->|Approved| H
    J -->|Rejected| K[phase10 restore]
    H -->|T2 reached| K
    K --> L[phase11 PSSR]
    L --> M[phase12 closure]
    M --> N[phase13 archive]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Node G → H → K** defines the active change window bounded by $T_1$ and $T_2$
- **Node I → J → H** is the re-justification loop at the 75% NTPC milestone
- **Node K → L → M → N** is the mandatory restoration, PSSR, closure, and archive sequence

### 4.2 NTPC Decision Logic — Authorization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Change Initiated] --> Q1{Is it an emergency}
    Q1 -->|Yes| E1[Oral Authorization within 4 hours]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is PSM applicable}
    Q2 -->|No| E2[Standard Work Order]
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Technical Review]
    P1 --> P2[PHA Re validation]
    P2 --> P3[Multi Disciplinary Approval]
    P3 --> P4{All Approvals}
    P4 -->|No| P5[Re work Required]
    P5 --> P1
    P4 -->|Yes| A1[T1 Authorization Recorded]
    E1 --> A1
    A1 --> AC[Active Change Period NTPC Active]
    AC --> M1[Continuous Monitoring]
    M1 --> RJ{Re justification at 75 percent NTPC}
    RJ -->|Approved| AC
    RJ -->|Rejected| RS[Initiate Restoration]
    AC --> TT{Reached T2 Deadline}
    TT -->|Yes| RS
    RS --> PS[PSSR Verification]
    PS --> CL[MOC Closure and Archive]
```

### 4.3 Time-Based NTPC Classification Matrix — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph windows[NTPC Classification Windows]
        W1[Emergency Window 0 to 72 hours]
        W2[Short Term Window 72 hours to 30 days]
        W3[Long Term Window 30 days to 12 months]
        W4[Permanent Change More than 12 months]
    end
    W1 --> D1[Oral Auth Plus Written within 24 hours]
    W2 --> D2[Simplified MOC with Engineering Basis]
    W3 --> D3[Full MOC with PHA Re validation]
    W4 --> D4[New Design MOC and PSSR Required]
```

### 4.4 Cumulative Risk Build-Up Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph riskbuild[Hazard Build Up vs NTPC]
        R0[Baseline Risk R0]
        R1[T1 Authorization Risk Jumps to R1]
        R2[Linear Growth during NTPC]
        R3[T2 Restoration Risk Returns to Baseline]
    end
    R0 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3
```

**Engineering Insight:** The vertical "jump" at $T_1$ represents the immediate risk increase upon change activation. The linear growth during NTPC represents accumulating exposure. The restoration at $T_2$ brings the system back to $R_0$ only if restoration is fully verified.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define the term "Necessary Time Period for the Change" as applied in Process Safety Management. State any two reasons why a time limit is imposed on temporary process changes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Necessary Time Period for the Change (NTPC) is the pre-determined, engineering-justified duration for which a temporary deviation from approved process design, operating procedures, or equipment specifications may be safely implemented and operated, beyond which the plant must be restored to its original design state or upgraded through a permanent change procedure.

**Reasons for Time Limit (1 mark — any two):**

1. **Prevention of Normalization of Deviation:** Operators may psychologically adapt to the temporary change and begin treating it as the permanent state, eroding safety vigilance.
2. **Control of Cumulative Risk Exposure:** Risk accumulates over time during the changed state; bounding the duration limits total hazard exposure.
3. **Documentation Integrity:** Time limits ensure that field reality does not permanently diverge from approved P&IDs, operating procedures, and Cause & Effect matrices.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Differentiate between an Emergency Change and a Short-Term Temporary Change with respect to authorization requirements and maximum allowable duration.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Emergency Change | Short-Term Temporary Change |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Duration** | ≤ 72 hours | 72 hours < ΔT ≤ 30 days |
| **Authorization** | Oral authorization permitted, followed by written MOC within 24 hours | Written MOC required before implementation |
| **PHA Re-validation** | Hazard review concurrent with implementation | Simplified PHA review prior to authorization |

---

### **PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)**

*As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE question from a Module Internal Choice. Two alternatives are provided below.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five phases** of the Management of Change lifecycle, highlighting the phase that corresponds to the active Necessary Time Period for the Change. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A proposed temporary bypass on a reactor cooling water return line introduces an elevated incident rate of $\bar{\lambda} = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ per day. The plant's allowable cumulative hazard budget for this category of change is $H_{allow} = 0.06$ incidents. Determine the maximum allowable Necessary Time Period for the Change and recommend a practical NTPC value with justification. **7 Marks**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The Five Phases of MOC Lifecycle (7 Marks):**

The Management of Change procedure, when mapped to the Necessary Time Period framework, consists of five sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Request and Screening:**
Any modification idea originates from Operations, Maintenance, or Engineering personnel as a Change Request (CR). The MOC Coordinator screens the CR against PSM applicability criteria (process involving TQ threshold quantities, etc.). Duration: typically 1–5 days. **(1 Mark)**

**Phase 2 — Technical Review and Authorization:**
The MOC team conducts a multi-disciplinary review: Process Engineering assesses design impact; Operations reviews operating procedure changes; Maintenance evaluates mechanical implications; HSE conducts a re-screening of the Process Hazard Analysis. Affected documents (P&IDs, PFDs, Cause & Effect, Safe Operating Limits) are listed. Duration: 5–15 days. **(1.5 Marks)**

**Phase 3 — Implementation and Active Change Period [THE NTPC PHASE]:**
This phase is bounded by $T_1$ (authorization) and $T_2$ (restoration deadline). The change is physically installed (e.g., bypass spool, temporary pump, jumper, or modified setpoint). Operations proceed under modified conditions with elevated monitoring. **The active duration $\Delta T_{NTPC} = T_2 - T_1$ IS the Necessary Time Period for the Change.** Duration: engineering-justified, typically 30 days to 6 months. **(2 Marks)**

**Phase 4 — Restoration and Verification:**
At $T_2$, the temporary change is removed. All blinds are reinserted, jumpers are removed, and setpoints are restored. A Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) verifies the restoration. Duration: 1–7 days. **(1.5 Marks)**

**Phase 5 — Closure and Documentation:**
As-built drawings are updated, operating procedures are permanently revised (or reverted), MOC records are archived, and lessons-learned are documented. MOC is formally closed. **(1 Mark)**

---

**(b) Maximum Allowable NTPC Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\bar{\lambda} = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ incidents/day
- $H_{allow} = 0.06$ incidents

**Step 1:** Apply the cumulative hazard model under constant incident rate assumption.

$$
H_{cum} = \bar{\lambda} \cdot \Delta T_{NTPC}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply the risk tolerance constraint.

$$
H_{cum} \leq H_{allow} \implies \bar{\lambda} \cdot \Delta T_{NTPC} \leq H_{allow}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Solve for $\Delta T_{NTPC}$.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq \frac{H_{allow}}{\bar{\lambda}}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq \frac{0.06}{3 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** Perform division.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \leq 200 \text{ days}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 6:** Convert to months and apply engineering safety margin.

$$
\Delta T_{NTPC} \text{ (months)} = \frac{200}{30} \approx 6.67 \text{ months}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 7:** Recommended practical value with justification.

The **recommended NTPC is 180 days (6 months)**. Justification: (i) This is **20 days below the calculated theoretical maximum**, providing a safety margin against assumption errors in $\bar{\lambda}$; (ii) This aligns with the **AIChE/CCPS guideline benchmark of ≤ 6 months** for long-term temporary changes; (iii) A 75% review milestone should be scheduled at **Day 135** to enable orderly restoration planning.

**(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the aid of a flowchart, explain the **decision logic for classifying a change and determining its NTPC window**. Mention the four time-based windows used in PSM practice. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A baseline process has an incident rate of $R_0 = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ incidents/year. A temporary change introduces a risk growth coefficient $\alpha = 0.008$ per day. Calculate the process risk at the end of a 120-day NTPC window. Comment on whether this NTPC duration is acceptable, given a corporate risk tolerance threshold of $R_{max} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ incidents/year. **7 Marks**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Decision Logic and NTPC Windows (7 Marks):**

**Decision Logic (Flowchart — described in narrative for written exam):**

1. Is there an imminent hazard? → **Yes → Emergency Change path**
2. Does the change affect a PSM-covered process? → **No → Standard Work Order**
3. Has a complete PHA re-evaluation been performed? → **No → Return to Phase 2**
4. Have all multi-disciplinary approvals been obtained? → **Yes → Authorize and activate**

**(3 Marks for logic)**

**Four NTPC Windows:**

| Window | Duration | Authorization Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Emergency | ≤ 72 hours | Oral, written follow-up in 24 h |
| Short-Term Temporary | 72 h – 30 days | Written MOC, simplified basis |
| Long-Term Temporary | 30 d – 12 months | Full MOC + PHA re-validation |
| Permanent | > 12 months / indefinite | New design MOC + PSSR |

**(4 Marks — 1 Mark per correct window with key authorization)**

---

**(b) Risk Calculation at End of 120-Day NTPC (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ incidents/year
- $\alpha = 0.008$ per day
- $\Delta T_{NTPC} = 120$ days
- $R_{max} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ incidents/year

**Step 1:** State the risk growth model.

$$
R(t) = R_0 \cdot \left(1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T\right)
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
R(120) = (8 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot \left(1 + (0.008)(120)\right)
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Compute the amplification term.

$$
(0.008)(120) = 0.96
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 4:** Compute the amplification factor.

$$
1 + 0.96 = 1.96
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** Compute the final risk value.

$$
R(120) = (8 \times 10^{-4})(1.96) = 15.68 \times 10^{-4} = 1.568 \times 10^{-3} \text{ incidents/year}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 6:** Compare with corporate threshold.

$$
R(120) = 1.568 \times 10^{-3} > R_{max} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 7:** Conclusion and recommendation.

The risk at the end of the 120-day NTPC **exceeds the corporate threshold by approximately 4.5%**. **The 120-day NTPC is NOT acceptable** in its current form. Recommended action: **Reduce the NTPC to the duration at which risk equals $R_{max}$**:

$$
\Delta T_{max} = \frac{R_{max}/R_0 - 1}{\alpha} = \frac{(1.5/0.8) - 1}{0.008} = \frac{0.875}{0.008} \approx 109 \text{ days}
$$

Therefore, the NTPC should be capped at **approximately 109 days**, with a practical engineering recommendation of **100 days** for safety margin.

**(1 Mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\Delta T_{NTPC}$ with the entire MOC lifecycle duration:** Many students incorrectly include Phases I, II, IV, and V in the NTPC calculation. **NTPC is ONLY Phase III** — the active change period between $T_1$ and $T_2$. (Deduct up to **2 marks**)
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Students often leave answers in days when the question expects months, or omit unit conversion steps entirely. Always show the conversion. (Deduct **1 mark**)
> 3. **Not stating the engineering basis:** When asked to "recommend" an NTPC, merely stating a number without justification (engineering basis, safety margin, or standard guideline) is incomplete. (Deduct **1–2 marks**)
> 4. **Mixing up authorization types:** Emergency changes do NOT require prior written MOC — only oral authorization with written follow-up. Stating otherwise loses marks on the classification question.
> 5. **Skipping the re-justification milestone:** Professional MOC practice requires a re-justification review at 75% of NTPC elapsed. Omitting this from your answer is treated as an incomplete solution.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Necessary Time Period for the Change"**

- **Definition:** The engineering-justified duration for safe implementation and operation of a temporary change, bounded by $T_1$ (authorization) and $T_2$ (restoration).
- **Governing Standard:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 — Management of Change element.
- **Core Equation:** $\Delta T_{NTPC} = T_2 - T_1$.
- **Risk Constraint:** $H_{cum} = \bar{\lambda} \cdot \Delta T_{NTPC} \leq H_{allow}$, giving $\Delta T_{max} = H_{allow} / \bar{\lambda}$.
- **Risk Growth Model:** $R(t) = R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T)$.
- **Four NTPC Windows:**
  * **Emergency:** ≤ 72 hours (oral + 24-h written follow-up)
  * **Short-Term:** 72 h to 30 days (written MOC)
  * **Long-Term:** 30 days to 12 months (full MOC + PHA)
  * **Permanent:** > 12 months (new design MOC)
- **Industry Benchmark Cap:** 6 months (AIChE/CCPS).
- **Hard Re-justification Threshold:** 12 months maximum before a fresh MOC.
- **Re-justification Milestone:** 75% of NTPC elapsed.
- **Documentation:** Minimum 5-year retention (or process lifetime for permanent changes).
- **Failure Mode to Prevent:** "Temporary forever" — normalization of deviation.
- **Real-World Lessons:** Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005 — both involved unauthorized or un-restored temporary changes.
- **Engineering Basis Categories:** Equipment lead time, turnaround gap, operational necessity, risk budget — NTPC = minimum of these.
- **MOC does NOT cover:** Like-for-like replacements, routine maintenance, or administrative changes (these are excluded from PSM MOC).
- **Audit Question to Expect:** "What is the maximum allowable duration for a temporary change in your facility?" → Answer with both your plant's internal cap AND reference to OSHA/CCPS benchmarks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authorization Requirements for the Proposed Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT864 - PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authorization Requirements for the Proposed Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Authorization Requirements for the Proposed Change** constitute a mandatory procedural safeguard within the **Management of Change (MOC)** element of the OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) standard, codified under **29 CFR 1910.119(l)(4)**. These requirements mandate that every proposed modification to a process—whether involving changes in technology, facilities, equipment, procedures, raw materials, operating conditions, or personnel—must receive **written authorization** from a designated, competent approving authority before the change is implemented.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT864 - Module 4):**
> As per the 2024 Scheme syllabus, the authorization phase of MOC is the legally binding step that transforms a *proposed* change into an *approved* change. Without proper authorization, the MOC procedure is considered incomplete and the plant is exposed to catastrophic process hazards.

The authorizing authority is typically the **Plant Manager**, **Engineering Manager**, or a **designated MOC Coordinator** who possesses the technical competence to evaluate safety, health, environmental, and operational implications of the change.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Renovation Permit"

Imagine a homeowner wants to convert the attic into a bedroom. Even if the design is perfect, the construction crew is ready, and the materials are bought, **construction cannot legally begin** until the local municipal authority issues a **building permit**. The permit is the formal, documented sign-off that says:

- The proposed design is **structurally safe**.
- It complies with **electrical, fire, and zoning codes**.
- A **qualified inspector** has reviewed the blueprints.
- The homeowner is **legally protected** if anything goes wrong later.

In the same way, the **Authorization for the Proposed Change** acts as the chemical plant's "building permit." The MOC review process investigates the change; the **authorization signature** is the official green light that commits the organization to proceed—*and* accepts accountability for the consequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Approval* means the change has been judged technically sound. *Authorization* means someone with delegated organizational authority has **formally signed off**, assuming responsibility for allowing the change to proceed.

## 1.3 Core Elements of Authorization (KTU Board-Definition Standard)

The authorization documentation for any proposed change must explicitly address the following six elements:

1. **Identification of the Proposed Change** — Clear, unambiguous description.
2. **Technical Basis for the Change** — Engineering justification, P&ID revisions, mass/energy balance updates.
3. **Safety, Health, and Environmental (SHE) Review** — Hazard Analysis updates, HAZOP revalidation.
4. **Defined Authorization Level** — Tiered approval based on risk classification (low/medium/high).
5. **Time-Bound Authorization Validity** — Start date, end date, and expiration conditions.
6. **Accountable Approver Signatures** — Name, designation, date, and signature of each authority in the chain.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants Used in Authorization Tiers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Risk Thresholds (OSHA PSM Reference):**
> - **Major Hazard Facility Threshold** = **Inventory ≥ Threshold Quantity (TQ)** of any of **137 listed chemicals** in **29 CFR 1910.119 Appendix A**.
> - **Risk Classification Tier 1 (Routine)** = Authorization by **Shift Supervisor** (within 24 hours).
> - **Risk Classification Tier 2 (Moderate)** = Authorization by **Plant Manager** (within 72 hours).
> - **Risk Classification Tier 3 (Major/Process)** = Authorization by **Corporate Process Safety Committee** (within 30 days).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Authorization Requirements

Authorization is not bureaucratic paperwork—it is a **legal, operational, and ethical control mechanism** that performs four critical functions:

### A. Legal Compliance Function
- Satisfies the **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(l)(4)** requirement that *"the employer shall establish and implement written procedures to manage changes... and shall authorize the change."*
- Provides **defensible documentation** in the event of a regulatory inspection, accident investigation, or litigation.

### B. Knowledge Preservation Function
- Forces the **transfer of implicit (tacit) operating knowledge** from experienced personnel into **explicit, written engineering records**.
- Prevents the **"phone-call engineering"** failure mode where a verbal change is made in the field without documentation.

### C. Risk Control Function
- Ensures a **competent authority has formally evaluated** the residual risk before the plant is exposed to it.
- Establishes a **point of no return**—a documented moment where accountability transfers from the proposer to the approver.

### D. Organizational Learning Function
- Creates an **audit trail** for incident investigation, helping future investigators understand *what was approved, by whom, and on what basis*.

## 2.2 Tiered Authorization Hierarchy — The KTU Board-Style Classification

The level of authorization required is **proportional to the risk and complexity** of the proposed change. The standard tiered framework is:

| **Risk Tier** | **Change Type** | **Approving Authority** | **Authorization Time Window** | **Documentation Depth** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Tier 1 — Like-for-Like Replacement** | Identical equipment, same vendor, same specs | **Maintenance Supervisor** | ≤ 24 hours | Short-form MOC checklist |
| **Tier 2 — Minor Procedural** | SOP revisions, P&ID red-line updates | **Shift Supervisor + Plant Engineer** | 24–72 hours | Standard MOC form |
| **Tier 3 — Operational Parameter** | Temperature, pressure, flow, composition changes within ±5% of design | **Plant Manager** | 3–7 days | Full MOC + What-If Analysis |
| **Tier 4 — Process/Technology** | New chemistry, new equipment, new raw materials, capacity expansion | **Corporate Process Safety Committee** + **EHS Head** | 14–30 days | Full MOC + HAZOP + LOPA + Management Review |
| **Tier 5 — Emergency/Emergency Bypass** | Temporary changes to prevent imminent shutdown or hazard | **Plant Manager** (verbal) + **Written follow-up in 24 hrs** | Immediate | Emergency MOC protocol |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Authorization Decision Logic

Although authorization is procedural, it relies on a structured **decision matrix** that can be expressed mathematically. The authorization requirement can be derived from a **Composite Risk Score (CRS)**:

$$CRS = (S \times P \times E) + H$$

Where:

- $S$ = **Severity Score** (1 to 5) — consequence of failure
- $P$ = **Probability Score** (1 to 5) — likelihood of failure
- $E$ = **Exposure Score** (1 to 5) — frequency of personnel exposure
- $H$ = **Hazard Category** modifier (0 for non-PSM, +3 for PSM-listed chemical, +5 for highly hazardous chemical)

**Authorization Tier Determination Table:**

| **CRS Range** | **Authorization Tier** | **Required Approver** |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $0 \le CRS \le 12$ | Tier 1 | Maintenance Supervisor |
| $13 \le CRS \le 25$ | Tier 2 | Plant Engineer |
| $26 \le CRS \le 50$ | Tier 3 | Plant Manager |
| $51 \le CRS \le 75$ | Tier 4 | Corporate PSC + EHS |
| $CRS > 75$ | Tier 5 | **Site Director + Corporate VP** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Composite Risk Score (CRS) framework is widely used in *CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)* guidelines and is the basis for the **Risk-Based Process Safety (RBPS)** pillar of authorization. It transforms subjective judgment into a defensible, quantitative authorization decision.

## 2.4 Mandatory Content of the Authorization Document

A KTU-compliant authorization document must contain the following ten sections:

1. **MOC Reference Number** (unique, traceable, sequential)
2. **Date of Request / Date of Authorization / Date of Implementation**
3. **Title and Description of the Change** (with reference P&ID, ISO drawing, or procedure number)
4. **Reason / Justification** for the change
5. **Technical Evaluation Summary** (results of HAZOP, What-If, LOPA, or similar)
6. **Risk Classification** (CRS calculation and tier assignment)
7. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) Requirements** as per **29 CFR 1910.119(l)(5)**
8. **Authorization Signatures** (with name, designation, signature, date)
9. **Communication Plan** (who has been informed, training requirements)
10. **Validation and Closure Plan** (temporary vs. permanent, time-bound expiry, post-implementation review)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Authorization Workflow & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Authorization Workflow — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

The authorization workflow is a sequential decision process. Each step builds upon the previous, creating an auditable chain of custody for the change. Below is the **complete, KTU-board-grade derivation** of the authorization decision:

---

### **Step 1: Change Initiation**

The change is formally raised through an **MOC Request Form** by the originating discipline (Operations, Maintenance, Engineering, R&D, or Contractor). The request must contain:

$$Request_{fields} = \{Description, Reason, Affected\_Systems, Originator, Date\}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* A change without a written origin cannot be authorized. The MOC number is generated at this stage using a structured format: `MOC-YYYY-XXXX-Tier` (e.g., `MOC-2024-0421-T3`).

---

### **Step 2: Initial Screening and Risk Classification**

The MOC Coordinator performs a **preliminary hazard screening** to classify the change. The Composite Risk Score is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRS &= (S \times P \times E) + H \\
&= (4 \times 3 \times 2) + 3 \\
&= 24 + 3 \\
&= 27
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Here, $S=4$ (major injury, $<\$100K$ damage), $P=3$ (could occur in plant lifetime), $E=2$ (monthly exposure), and $H=3$ (PSM-listed chemical present). The CRS of 27 places this change in **Tier 3 — Plant Manager Authorization**.

---

### **Step 3: Detailed Technical and Safety Review**

The appropriate review technique is selected based on tier:

| **Tier** | **Required Review Technique** | **Reference Standard** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Tier 1 | Walk-down + Checklist | Plant Maintenance Procedure |
| Tier 2 | What-If Analysis | CCPS *Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures* |
| Tier 3 | HAZOP (with updated P&ID) | IEC 61882 |
| Tier 4 | HAZOP + LOPA + SIL Determination | IEC 61511 |
| Tier 5 | Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) | TNO Purple Book / CCPS |

The output of the review is a **Technical Evaluation Report (TER)** containing:
- Identified new hazards
- Modified safeguards
- Updated Safety Instrumented System (SIS) requirements
- Updated operating limits

---

### **Step 4: Authorization Decision — The Logical Gate**

The change enters the **Authorization Gate**, which is a Boolean logical test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Authorization_{granted} = \;&[Originator\_Identified] \;\land \\
&[Technical\_Review\_Complete] \;\land \\
&[Risk\_Tier\_Assigned] \;\land \\
&[Approver\_Authority\_Verified] \;\land \\
&[Documentation\_Complete] \;\land \\
&[Time\_Bound\_Defined]
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* All six Boolean conditions must evaluate to **TRUE** for authorization to be granted. If any condition is FALSE, the MOC is **returned to the originator** for revision, creating a feedback loop until all gates pass.

---

### **Step 5: Multi-Level Signature Cascade**

The authorization cascade is computed by traversing the **Approval Hierarchy Tree (AHT)**. For a Tier 4 change, the cascade is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AHT_{Tier4} = \;&Originator \rightarrow Engineer \rightarrow Plant\_Manager \rightarrow \\
&EHS\_Head \rightarrow Corporate\_PSC \rightarrow Site\_Director
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Each level must sign *sequentially* (not in parallel) to preserve accountability. The corporate process safety committee reviews collectively; their authorization is a **single committee resolution** recorded in meeting minutes.

---

### **Step 6: Implementation Window and PSSR Trigger**

Once authorized, the change enters a **time-bound implementation window**:

$$
\Delta t_{implementation} = t_{start} - t_{authorization} \le t_{window}^{max}
$$

Where $t_{window}^{max}$ is tier-dependent:

| **Tier** | **Maximum Implementation Window** |
|:---:|:---|
| Tier 1 | 7 days |
| Tier 2 | 30 days |
| Tier 3 | 90 days |
| Tier 4 | 180 days |
| Tier 5 | 365 days |

> *Conversion Logic:* If implementation is not completed within the window, the authorization **expires** and a new MOC cycle must be initiated. This prevents the "evergreen change" failure mode.

---

### **Step 7: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**

Before the modified process is started, a **PSSR team** verifies (per **29 CFR 1910.119(l)(5)**):

- Construction matches the authorized design.
- Procedures have been updated and trained.
- Emergency plans reflect the modification.
- All PSSR punch-list items are closed.

$$
PSSR_{pass} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} PSSR\_Item_i
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Only when **every PSSR item** is verified as closed can the process be physically started. The PSSR is the **final authorization gate** before energy is introduced to the modified system.

---

### **Step 8: Authorization Closure and Archival**

The MOC Coordinator closes the authorization, files the MOC packet (request, review, signatures, PSSR, training records) in the **Process Safety Information (PSI) repository** with a minimum retention period of the **plant's operating life + 5 years** (or as per local regulatory requirement).

## 3.2 Authorization Validity Conditions — Mathematical Expression

The authorization remains valid only while all of the following are TRUE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Validity = \;&[Implementation\_Within\_Window] \;\land \\
&[No\_Unapproved\_Field\_Deviation] \;\land \\
&[Training\_Completed\_Before\_Start] \;\land \\
&[PSSR\_Signed\_Off] \;\land \\
&[Operating\_Conditions\_Within\_New\_Design\_Envelope]
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* If *any* of these conditions becomes FALSE, the authorization is **automatically revoked** and the change must be either re-authorized or reversed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Authorization Workflow Flowchart

The complete authorization workflow for a proposed change is depicted below as a **Mermaid Flow Diagram**. This is the KTU board-standard schematic for the Management of Change authorization process:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Change Identified in Plant]) --> stepB[Originator Submits MOC Request Form]
    stepB --> stepC[MOC Coordinator Assigns MOC Number]
    stepC --> stepD[Calculate Composite Risk Score CRS]
    stepD --> stepE{Is CRS Tier 1 or 2?}
    stepE -- YES --> stepF[Light Review Checklist]
    stepE -- NO --> stepG[Detailed HAZOP or LOPA]
    stepF --> stepH[Apply What-If Analysis]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI[Prepare Technical Evaluation Report]
    stepI --> stepJ{All Six Authorization Gates Satisfied?}
    stepJ -- NO --> stepK[Return to Originator for Revision]
    stepK --> stepB
    stepJ -- YES --> stepL[Identify Required Approval Level per Tier]
    stepL --> stepM[Tier 1: Maintenance Supervisor Signs]
    stepL --> stepN[Tier 2: Plant Engineer Signs]
    stepL --> stepO[Tier 3: Plant Manager Signs]
    stepL --> stepP[Tier 4: Corporate PSC Signs]
    stepL --> stepQ[Tier 5: Site Director and Corporate VP Sign]
    stepM --> stepR[Authorization Granted with Time Bound]
    stepN --> stepR
    stepO --> stepR
    stepP --> stepR
    stepQ --> stepR
    stepR --> stepS[Implement Change Within Window]
    stepS --> stepT[Conduct Pre-Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    stepT --> stepU{All PSSR Items Closed?}
    stepU -- NO --> stepV[Hold Process Start Until Closure]
    stepV --> stepT
    stepU -- YES --> stepW[Process Started and Documented]
    stepW --> stepX[Archive MOC Packet in PSI Repository]
    stepX --> endY([Authorization Cycle Closed])
```

## 4.2 Authorization Approval Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** illustrates the tiered approval architecture and the structural relationship between the authorizing authorities:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIER5["TIER 5 - SITE DIRECTOR AND CORPORATE VP"]
        siteDir[Site Director]
        corpVP[Corporate Vice President]
    end

    subgraph TIER4["TIER 4 - CORPORATE PROCESS SAFETY COMMITTEE"]
        pscCh[PSC Chairperson]
        pscEH[EHS Director]
        pscEng[Chief Process Engineer]
    end

    subgraph TIER3["TIER 3 - PLANT MANAGER"]
        plantMgr[Plant Manager]
        prodSup[Production Superintendent]
    end

    subgraph TIER2["TIER 2 - PLANT ENGINEER"]
        plantEng[Plant Engineer]
        procEng[Process Engineer]
    end

    subgraph TIER1["TIER 1 - MAINTENANCE SUPERVISOR"]
        maintSup[Maintenance Supervisor]
        shiftSup[Shift Supervisor]
    end

    origin[Originator - Any Discipline] --> maintSup
    origin --> plantEng
    origin --> plantMgr
    origin --> pscCh
    origin --> siteDir

    maintSup --> plantEng
    plantEng --> plantMgr
    plantMgr --> pscCh
    pscCh --> siteDir
    pscCh --> corpVP
    siteDir --> corpVP

    maintSup -.approves.-> tier1Change[Like for Like Replacement]
    plantEng -.approves.-> tier2Change[Minor Procedural Change]
    plantMgr -.approves.-> tier3Change[Operational Parameter Change]
    pscCh -.approves.-> tier4Change[Process and Technology Change]
    siteDir -.approves.-> tier5Change[Major Capital or Emergency Change]
```

## 4.3 Authorization Decision Logic Block Diagram

This diagram represents the **logical authorization gate** in symbolic block format, useful for KTU board exam sketches:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Originator Identified] --> gateAND{AND Gate - All Six Conditions}
    inputB[Technical Review Complete] --> gateAND
```
